Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in MDT and not on the ward. You also have to clerk an urgent trauma admission arriving in 20 minutes.

options.A: Speak to Dr Grant privately, explain the confidentiality and consent concerns about using a personal phone, ask him to delete the image immediately if consent and secure storage are not in place, and document your discussion and actions taken.
options.B: Ignore the issue for now because the photo was for teaching and focus on clerking the trauma admission arriving shortly.
options.C: Discuss the situation with Mrs Hassan in a private area, check what consent (if any) she gave for clinical photography, apologise for any distress, and explain the steps you will take to ensure her information is handled appropriately.
options.D: Ask Angela to forward you the photo from her email so you can decide later whether it is acceptable to keep for the team.
options.E: Escalate promptly via the ward/Trust process (e.g., inform nurse-in-charge and contact the on-call consultant/clinical supervisor or information governance lead) given potential breach, ensuring the trauma admission is safely handed over or covered.

explanation:
A, C and E best balance immediate patient safety, confidentiality and professional accountability. In A, speaking to Dr Grant privately is proportionate and maintains professionalism while addressing the core risk: an identifiable image on a personal device without clear consent or secure storage. Asking for deletion (if policy/consent not met) and documenting actions aligns with NHS information governance.

C is important because Mrs Hassan has expressed strong privacy concerns, and valid consent for photography must be explicit and informed. Speaking privately reduces distress and respects dignity; an apology for any worry and a clear plan help maintain trust.

E ensures appropriate escalation through Trust channels, recognising you are not the final decision-maker on potential breaches. Involving the nurse-in-charge and contacting Dr Banerjee/on-call consultant or information governance provides oversight and protects staff like Angela.

B minimises a potential breach. D would propagate insecure sharing. F risks humiliating Dr Grant and increasing patient anxiety. G suppresses speaking up. H may help long-term culture but is insufficient without addressing the specific incident and Mrs Hassan’s concerns.
learningPoints: Clinical photography requires explicit consent and secure, policy-compliant storage.
Address concerns promptly and proportionately; speak up and document actions taken.
Support colleagues who raise concerns and use local escalation/information governance pathways.
Maintain patient dignity by discussing sensitive issues privately and transparently.

scenario: You are a DCT in community dental services on a school dental clinic. Sophie (9) attends with her mother, Mrs Khan, for an urgent appointment after the school nurse flagged dental pain. Emily (dental nurse) notices Sophie flinches when her mother raises her voice. On examination, you find gross caries and a dental abscess. Sophie is unusually withdrawn and, when Mrs Khan steps out to take a call, Sophie whispers that she “doesn’t want to go home” and has been left to look after her younger brother overnight. Mrs Khan returns and insists you “just give antibiotics” because she cannot take time off work for an extraction, and she refuses any conversation with the school about the family. Receptionist Olivia says the clinic is overbooked and the next child is already waiting.

options.A: Provide antibiotics only as Mrs Khan requests to preserve rapport, and arrange a routine extraction appointment in a few weeks.
options.B: Explain to Mrs Khan that antibiotics alone may not resolve an abscess; discuss treatment options and analgesia, and offer to involve the community sedation/GA pathway if appropriate, while making clear you must act on any safeguarding concerns.
options.C: Speak with Sophie and Mrs Khan together in the surgery about what Sophie disclosed, so there are no secrets and Mrs Khan understands the seriousness.
options.D: Ensure Sophie is not left alone; inform the safeguarding lead/social care via local pathway promptly, document Sophie’s disclosure verbatim, and seek advice from your supervising clinician, while arranging urgent dental treatment appropriate to symptoms.
options.E: Ask Emily to keep the next child waiting while you finish, but tell Olivia not to document any safeguarding information to avoid upsetting the family.

explanation:
D is most appropriate because Sophie’s disclosure (not wanting to go home, being left to care for a sibling overnight, fearful behaviour) raises safeguarding concerns that must be acted on promptly. NHS and statutory guidance requires you to record the child’s words accurately, involve the safeguarding lead/social care via local pathways, and seek senior advice, while ensuring Sophie is not placed at further risk (e.g., not being left alone).

B is next: it keeps care patient-centred and clinically safe by addressing the abscess with appropriate treatment planning rather than antibiotics-only, and it sets clear expectations that safeguarding duties are non-negotiable. It also helps maintain a workable relationship with Mrs Khan through explanation and options.

C is less appropriate: confronting the disclosure in front of Mrs Khan may increase risk to Sophie and inhibit further disclosure; discussions should usually occur privately with safeguarding guidance.

A is inappropriate because it prioritises parental demand and convenience over clinical safety and fails to address safeguarding. E is least appropriate: asking Olivia not to document safeguarding is unethical and unsafe; accurate documentation is essential even if it causes discomfort or delay.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns must be escalated promptly using local pathways, with clear documentation.
A child’s immediate safety and welfare take priority over clinic time pressures and parental demands.
Antibiotics are not a substitute for definitive management of a dental abscess.
Handle disclosures carefully; avoid increasing risk by confronting carers without safeguarding advice.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is running late in theatre and you are asked to start triaging with dental nurse Sarah. Mr Clarke arrives with facial swelling and reports a “penicillin allergy” but is unsure of the reaction. Receptionist Nadia quietly tells you the electronic record shows ‘anaphylaxis to amoxicillin’ from a previous admission, but the scanned discharge summary is missing. Mr Clarke is agitated and demands antibiotics immediately because he has an interview this afternoon. Sarah points out the waiting room is full and another patient, Mrs Hassan, is complaining loudly about delays. You are confident managing odontogenic infection but are less sure about safe antibiotic choices in uncertain allergy histories and about whether Mr Clarke needs urgent OMFS review. You also notice Mr Clarke smells of alcohol and is struggling to focus on questions.

options.A: Ask Sarah to bring Mr Clarke straight into a surgery, take focused observations and a detailed allergy history, and telephone Dr Kumar for a brief plan while you also request the missing discharge summary from Nadia.
options.B: Prescribe amoxicillin now because Mr Clarke is demanding it and you do not want the swelling to worsen while Dr Kumar is delayed.
options.C: Explain to Mr Clarke that you cannot prescribe until you clarify the allergy, ask Nadia to check any linked hospital systems for the previous anaphylaxis entry, and if red flags are present arrange urgent OMFS review rather than delaying in the waiting room.
options.D: Tell Mr Clarke you will only help if he calms down, and leave him in the waiting area while you see Mrs Hassan first to reduce complaints about delays.
options.E: Prescribe clindamycin immediately to avoid penicillin risk, and document “penicillin allergy” without further detail to save time.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is C: Mr Clarke’s possible anaphylaxis to amoxicillin, intoxication and facial swelling raise safety concerns that outweigh clinic flow. Clarifying allergy details, checking other systems via Nadia, and considering urgent OMFS escalation for red flags (airway compromise, systemic signs, rapidly spreading infection) aligns with NHS patient safety and working within competence.

A is also strong because it brings Mr Clarke to a safe space, obtains observations, and involves Dr Kumar promptly while seeking missing documentation. However, it risks moving too quickly to treatment without first framing the safety rationale and escalation threshold as clearly as C.

E is less appropriate: choosing an alternative antibiotic without a proper assessment may be necessary in some circumstances, but doing it “immediately” and documenting vaguely undermines antimicrobial stewardship and accurate records. It may be reasonable only after assessment and guideline-based choice.

D is poor because it uses a punitive stance and deprioritises a potentially serious infection to manage complaints, risking harm and escalating conflict.

B is least appropriate: prescribing a penicillin when the record suggests anaphylaxis is unsafe, breaches prescribing standards, and exposes Mr Clarke to avoidable harm regardless of time pressure or patient demand.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides time pressure and patient demands.
Uncertain allergy histories require careful clarification and accurate documentation before prescribing.
Escalate promptly when red flags suggest severe infection or compromised capacity (e.g., intoxication).
Teamwork with nurses and administrative staff can support safer decision-making under pressure.

scenario:
You are a DCT2 in a community special care dentistry clinic seeing Mrs Thompson, who has moderate dementia and attends with her son, Daniel. The therapist Laura has already taken an OPG showing a carious lower molar with possible periapical pathology. Mrs Thompson appears calm but keeps looking to Daniel before answering. Daniel insists, “Mum wants it out today,” and becomes irritated when you ask Mrs Thompson questions. Dental nurse Emily quietly tells you that at reception Daniel asked whether “there’s any way to speed this up because we’re late for the bank.” Dr Banerjee (consultant) is off-site at an MDT and has asked you to call if any capacity issues arise.

Mrs Thompson says, “I don’t like dentists,” and then agrees to extraction, but cannot repeat back what will happen or the risks. She also asks you not to tell Daniel about her medications, yet Daniel is holding a list and interrupts. The clinic is running 30 minutes late and the next patient is anxious and waiting.

options.A: Proceed with extraction because Daniel is the next of kin and is requesting it, documenting that Mrs Thompson appeared agreeable.
options.B: Pause the appointment to assess Mrs Thompson’s decision-making capacity specifically for extraction, using simple language and checking understanding, and if capacity is unclear phone Dr Banerjee before any irreversible treatment.
options.C: Ask Emily to take Daniel to the waiting area so you can speak to Mrs Thompson alone about her wishes and medications, explaining to Daniel that you need to confirm consent and confidentiality first.
options.D: Tell Daniel you cannot treat Mrs Thompson unless he signs a consent form as her relative, then proceed if he signs.
options.E: Defer treatment and rebook for a longer slot without exploring capacity today, because the clinic is late and the next patient is waiting.

explanation:
B is essential: before an irreversible extraction, you must assess Mrs Thompson’s capacity for this specific decision and confirm she understands the nature, purpose, risks and alternatives. Involving Dr Banerjee when capacity is unclear reflects recognising limits of competence and ensures senior support.

C supports ethical and lawful practice. Speaking to Mrs Thompson separately reduces undue influence from Daniel and allows you to explore her wishes, including her request about medication confidentiality. Explaining this boundary to Daniel maintains professionalism and helps manage the interpersonal tension.

F is appropriate contingency planning. If she lacks capacity, you should not proceed immediately; instead manage pain/infection appropriately, consider urgent pathways, and document a best-interests process that includes Daniel’s input but is centred on Mrs Thompson’s welfare and least restrictive option.

A and D are unsafe: next of kin cannot consent on behalf of a competent adult, and relative signatures do not replace a capacity/best-interests framework.

E delays care without addressing the immediate consent problem; you should assess capacity today even if treatment is deferred.

G breaches confidentiality without justification.

H distracts from the consent issue and misuses imaging to manage behaviour.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires patient understanding; capacity is decision-specific and must be assessed before irreversible treatment.
Relatives can support communication but cannot override patient autonomy or substitute for consent.
Confidentiality remains important even when carers are present; manage undue influence sensitively.
If capacity is lacking, follow best-interests decision-making and document clearly, seeking senior input when needed.

scenario:
You are a DCT1 attached to an OMFS ward. During the afternoon round, ward nurse Rachel asks you to review Mr Ibrahim, admitted with a deep neck space infection, because he is complaining of increasing pain. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in clinic and the registrar Dr Grant is scrubbed in theatre. You check the drug chart and see that a discharge summary draft has been started for another patient under your login. While you are at the nurses’ station, radiographer Elena calls asking why a CT request was submitted with an incorrect NHS number and no documented creatinine.

Dental nurse Chloe tells you she overheard a junior doctor saying, “Just copy the old details; no one checks,” and she is worried because it sounds like your login was used. Meanwhile Mr Ibrahim’s wife, Mrs Khan, is on the ward demanding to know why no one is updating her, and Rachel is short-staffed and visibly frustrated.

options.A: Go to Mr Ibrahim immediately with Rachel to assess his pain and airway status, then update Dr Grant by phone with any red flags before dealing with the documentation and CT query.
options.B: Ignore Chloe’s comment because challenging colleagues will create conflict; focus only on the CT request to keep radiology satisfied.
options.C: Tell Elena you will cancel the CT request and ask her to re-enter it correctly on radiology’s system, because the ward is too busy to find the right details.
options.D: Secure your account (log out everywhere, change password) and inform Dr O’Connor or the on-call manager about suspected misuse, then work with Rachel to clarify who entered the discharge draft and correct the CT request details with Elena.
options.E: Confront the junior doctor in front of Rachel and Chloe at the nurses’ station, accusing them of using your login and falsifying records, and demand they admit it.

explanation:
A is the most appropriate immediate priority: Mr Ibrahim has a deep neck space infection, so worsening pain could signal deterioration. Assessing with Rachel, checking airway/systemic signs, and escalating to Dr Grant if red flags are present aligns with patient safety and effective teamwork.

D should follow promptly. Potential misuse of your login and inaccurate documentation are serious professionalism and governance issues. Securing your account, informing Dr O’Connor (or appropriate senior/manager), and establishing facts protects patients and you, while correcting the CT request with Elena supports safe imaging and maintains interdepartmental communication.

C is less appropriate: cancelling may be necessary if details are unsafe, but asking Elena to “re-enter it” shifts responsibility inappropriately and risks further errors. You should provide correct identifiers/renal function or formally pause the request until verified.

B is poor because it dismisses a credible concern about record integrity and confidentiality; ignoring it risks ongoing unsafe practice.

E is least appropriate: public confrontation is unprofessional, may be unfair without evidence, escalates conflict, and could undermine the team. Concerns should be handled discreetly through appropriate channels while focusing on patient care.
learningPoints: In acutely unwell patients, clinical deterioration and airway risk must be prioritised and escalated early.
Protecting logins and record integrity is a core professionalism and patient safety duty.
Address concerns about colleagues’ behaviour through appropriate, proportionate channels—not public confrontation.
Clear interdepartmental communication (e.g., radiology identifiers/renal function) prevents avoidable harm and delays.

scenario: You are a DCT in an oral surgery clinic. Dr Kumar (registrar) asks you to extract LR6 for Mrs Thompson, who is anticoagulated with apixaban. The referral letter is brief and the medical history on the electronic record looks out of date. Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you Mrs Thompson mentioned she “sometimes takes warfarin instead” because her GP recently changed her tablets, but she is unsure which she took this morning. The clinic is running 40 minutes late, and Mark (receptionist) is dealing with several frustrated patients. Mrs Thompson is anxious, says she has taken time off work, and insists you “just get it done today.” Dr Kumar is intermittently in theatre and hard to reach. You feel confident with routine extractions but not with managing uncertain anticoagulant status without senior input.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using local haemostatic measures, documenting that Mrs Thompson believes she is on apixaban and that she wants treatment today.
options.B: Ask Sarah to pause the procedure while you verify Mrs Thompson’s current anticoagulant and last dose by calling her GP or anticoagulation clinic, and update the record before making a treatment decision.
options.C: Tell Mark to rebook Mrs Thompson to avoid delays, without discussing details, and move to the next patient immediately.
options.D: Explain to Mrs Thompson that the uncertainty about her anticoagulant means you cannot safely extract today without clarification; offer analgesia advice, a written plan, and an urgent rearranged appointment once medication is confirmed.
options.E: Attempt to contact Dr Kumar and, if he does not answer within a few minutes, continue because the clinic cannot wait longer.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety in the context of uncertain anticoagulant therapy. Sarah has raised a specific concern that Mrs Thompson may have switched between apixaban and warfarin and is unsure about today’s dose. Proceeding (A) without verification risks significant bleeding and is not justified by time pressure or patient preference. The most appropriate action is (B): pause, take a focused medicines history, and verify anticoagulant type and timing with the GP/anticoagulation clinic, updating documentation. This aligns with NHS expectations on safe prescribing/medicines reconciliation and responding to team concerns.

If the medication cannot be confirmed promptly, (D) is next best: clearly explain the risk, respect Mrs Thompson’s frustration while maintaining professional integrity, and provide interim management with a plan for timely treatment once safe. (E) shows some awareness of escalation but sets an unsafe time limit and may lead to proceeding without adequate information. (C) is least appropriate: it prioritises clinic flow and avoids communication, leaving Mrs Thompson without explanation or support and undermining trust and patient-centred care.
learningPoints: Respond promptly to safety concerns raised by dental nurses and other team members.
Patient preference and clinic delays do not justify proceeding when key medical information is uncertain.
Recognise limits of competence and escalate appropriately when managing anticoagulated patients.
Provide clear explanations, interim advice, and a documented plan when treatment must be deferred.

scenario: You are on-call in the hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Khan attends with severe toothache and facial swelling. He is angry and demands antibiotics only, saying he cannot stay because he has to collect his children. Emily (dental nurse) notes he smells of alcohol and keeps interrupting. Dr Chen (consultant) is in clinic next door but is in the middle of a difficult consent discussion with another patient. Mr Khan refuses an intra‑oral exam at first and says, “Just write the prescription—my dentist always does.” Nadia (receptionist) quietly tells you Mr Khan was verbally abusive on the phone earlier and security have been alerted if needed.

options.A: Ask Emily to remain with you, set clear behavioural boundaries with Mr Khan, and explain that assessment is needed to decide if antibiotics or urgent escalation is required.
options.B: Provide an antibiotic prescription immediately to de-escalate the situation, and ask Mr Khan to see his GDP for definitive treatment.
options.C: Offer to arrange a same-day review when Dr Chen is free, and ask Nadia to bring Mr Khan back later without further engagement to avoid confrontation.
options.D: Attempt a brief focused assessment (including vital signs/red flags) and, if Mr Khan continues to refuse examination, document refusal and consider escalation to Dr Chen or medical team based on risk.
options.E: Ask security to remove Mr Khan immediately because he was rude on the phone, and record him as ‘did not cooperate’.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions prioritise safety, capacity, and staff protection while maintaining professionalism. (A) is appropriate because it uses Emily’s support, sets boundaries, and explains the need for an assessment—this is patient-centred but firm, and helps manage the interpersonal dynamic and staff safety. (D) is also key: you should attempt a focused clinical assessment for red flags (e.g., airway compromise, systemic signs) and document any refusal. If risk is high or cooperation is limited, escalation to Dr Chen or the medical team is justified; this reflects clinical governance and recognising limits of competence.

(F) addresses the ethical tension around possible intoxication and capacity. Exploring support and checking understanding before prescribing or undertaking procedures supports valid consent and safe discharge planning.

In contrast, (B) gives antibiotics without assessment and reinforces inappropriate expectations, potentially missing sepsis. (C) avoids engagement and delays risk assessment. (E) and (G) are punitive and risk discrimination; security involvement should be proportionate to immediate threat, not prior rudeness alone. (H) wrongly prioritises hierarchy and convenience over timely escalation when concerned.
learningPoints: Antibiotics should follow appropriate assessment; avoid prescribing to manage conflict.
Assess capacity and understanding, especially when intoxication is suspected.
Use clear boundaries and team support to maintain staff safety and professionalism.
Escalate promptly when red flags or non-cooperation create clinical risk.

scenario: You are a DCT in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Hassan attends with her adult son, Mr Petrov, who insists on answering every question for her. Mrs Hassan appears withdrawn and avoids eye contact. Laura (hygienist) tells you privately she overheard Mr Petrov saying, “Don’t tell them about the bruises or they’ll make trouble.” Chloe (dental nurse) says Mrs Hassan’s consent forms were signed by her son at a previous visit because “she was slow.” The appointment is for urgent denture adjustments, but you also notice oral ulceration that could need referral. The clinic is short-staffed and running behind; Olivia (receptionist) says transport is booked to take Mrs Hassan home in 20 minutes.

options.A: Ask Mr Petrov to step into the waiting area while you speak to Mrs Hassan alone, using a calm explanation about routine confidentiality and consent checks in special care clinics.
options.B: Proceed with denture adjustments only, avoid raising bruises or consent issues today, and ask Laura to ‘keep an eye’ at the next visit.
options.C: Explain to Mrs Hassan and Mr Petrov that you have safeguarding concerns and will make a referral today, without first speaking to Mrs Hassan alone.
options.D: Ask Chloe to document Laura’s comment verbatim and check previous notes, then contact the safeguarding lead for advice while you continue with only minimal urgent care.
options.E: Tell Olivia to cancel Mrs Hassan’s transport so you have time to address everything, even if it means she may struggle to get home.

explanation:
This scenario contains potential adult safeguarding concerns, questionable consent practices, and time/transport pressures. The most appropriate first step is (A): speaking to Mrs Hassan alone, in a routine and non-confrontational way, supports autonomy, allows you to assess whether she can consent, and may enable disclosure without Mr Petrov’s influence. It also maintains a workable relationship by framing this as standard practice.

(D) is next: Chloe should contemporaneously document Laura’s specific concern and you should review prior notes about consent, then seek advice from the service safeguarding lead. This uses teamworking and appropriate escalation while allowing you to prioritise immediate safety.

(C) is less appropriate than A/D because declaring a safeguarding referral in front of Mr Petrov could increase risk to Mrs Hassan and undermines confidentiality; however, if there is immediate danger it may become necessary.

(E) is low because cancelling transport without agreement could disadvantage Mrs Hassan and is disproportionate; time should be managed through prioritisation and escalation rather than removing support. (B) is least appropriate: ignoring safeguarding indicators and problematic consent to focus on dentures fails duty of care and NHS safeguarding responsibilities.
learningPoints: Create opportunities to speak to patients privately when consent or coercion is suspected.
Document concerns accurately and escalate to the safeguarding lead promptly.
Manage safeguarding disclosures in a way that minimises risk to the patient.
Time and service pressures should not override safeguarding and valid consent.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in a busy NHS oral surgery emergency clinic. Your consultant, Dr Ahmed, is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan is booked for extraction of a lower molar and looks pale; he says he is on “blood thinners” but doesn’t know which. The referral letter is missing from the notes, and the electronic record shows atrial fibrillation but no current medication list. Nadia on reception says there are six walk-ins waiting and the clinic is already running 40 minutes late. Mr Khan is frustrated and asks you to “just get it done today” because he has travelled far. Sarah quietly adds she overheard Mr Khan mention a recent hospital admission.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using local measures for haemostasis, document that Mr Khan reported “blood thinners,” and ask him to bring his medication list next time.
options.B: Ask Nadia to call the GP and hospital ward for an urgent medication list and recent discharge summary, while you take a focused history from Mr Khan and check vital signs with Sarah.
options.C: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot safely extract without confirming his anticoagulant status, offer analgesia advice and an early rebook, and document the decision clearly.
options.D: Escalate to Dr Ahmed via switchboard for urgent guidance, and if unavailable, contact the on-call OMFS registrar (Dr Kumar) before making any irreversible decision.
options.E: Ask Sarah to prepare the LA and instruments while you try to check the summary care record quickly, to avoid further delay if the medication list appears.

explanation:
The safest priority is to clarify Mr Khan’s anticoagulation status and recent admission before any extraction. Option B is most appropriate because it uses the team (Nadia and Sarah) to obtain objective information (GP/hospital confirmation), while you assess Mr Khan clinically (history and observations) to identify red flags. Option D is next best: seeking timely senior input from Dr Ahmed or, more realistically, the on-call registrar Dr Kumar aligns with recognising limits of competence and protecting patient safety when documentation is incomplete.

Option C is reasonable and patient-centred if information cannot be obtained promptly; it respects Mr Khan’s wishes through explanation and offers an alternative plan, but it should follow active attempts to gather medication details and senior advice rather than being the first move.

Option E risks premature preparation that may signal commitment to proceed and could pressure you to act without adequate information; it is lower priority though checking the record is sensible.

Option A is least appropriate: proceeding with an invasive procedure when anticoagulant type/dose and recent admission are unknown breaches clinical safety standards and exposes Mr Khan to avoidable harm, regardless of time pressure and clinic delays.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides clinic flow and time pressure when key medical information is missing.
Use the team effectively (reception, nursing staff) to gather collateral history and documentation.
Escalate early when limits of competence or uncertainty about anticoagulation/medical status exist.
Document the decision-making process and communicate clearly with the patient about delays or rebooking.

scenario: You are a DCT2 in a community special care dentistry clinic. Mrs Thompson, who has mild learning disability, attends with her sister, Olivia, who insists on speaking for her. Dr Chen (specialty dentist) is running between rooms and asks you to “keep things moving.” Emily (dental nurse) mentions Mrs Thompson previously became distressed during treatment and that the last consent form was incomplete. Today Mrs Thompson needs a restoration under local anaesthetic, but she appears anxious and keeps looking at Olivia before answering. Olivia asks you not to mention that Mrs Thompson has recently started an antidepressant because “it will worry her,” and she presses you to proceed quickly because they have a taxi waiting. Mrs Thompson quietly says, “I don’t like needles,” but also says she wants “the tooth fixed.

options.A: Ask Olivia to step out briefly so you can speak to Mrs Thompson alone, with Emily present, to assess her understanding and preferences without coercion.
options.B: Proceed with the restoration as Olivia requests to avoid distress and delays, documenting that consent was obtained with family support.
options.C: Use a simple, structured explanation with visual aids and check-back questions to confirm Mrs Thompson understands the procedure, risks, and alternatives, and document the capacity/consent discussion.
options.D: Tell Olivia you will not treat unless she discloses all medications, and threaten to cancel the appointment if she refuses.
options.E: Offer alternatives to reduce anxiety (topical anaesthetic, pause signals, possible inhalation sedation referral) and agree a stepwise plan that Mrs Thompson can stop at any time.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions prioritise valid consent, supported decision-making, and anxiety management while maintaining confidentiality. Option A is appropriate because it reduces undue influence: speaking to Mrs Thompson privately (with Emily as chaperone) helps you assess capacity and whether her agreement is voluntary, which is essential when a relative dominates the conversation. Option C directly addresses the quality of consent by using accessible communication and documenting understanding, risks, and alternatives; this supports NHS standards on informed consent and reasonable adjustments.

Option E is also key: acknowledging Mrs Thompson’s needle fear and offering practical strategies (topical, pause signals, and considering sedation referral) is patient-centred and improves the likelihood of a successful, consensual procedure.

Options B and H are unsafe and unethical: family pressure and time constraints do not justify bypassing proper consent, and a sister cannot sign consent unless legally authorised and appropriate. Option F breaches confidentiality by discussing sensitive information in a public corridor. Option D escalates conflict and is disproportionate; you should explain why medication history matters without threats. Option G can be appropriate if doubt remains, but first steps are to assess and support consent yourself; calling Dr Chen immediately may be unnecessary and could reinforce Olivia’s pressure to “get it done quickly.”
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s understanding and voluntary agreement; relatives can support but must not coerce.
Make reasonable adjustments for patients with learning disability, using accessible explanations and check-back.
Manage anxiety proactively and offer options, including staged treatment and sedation referral when indicated.
Maintain confidentiality when discussing sensitive medical information and document capacity/consent clearly.

scenario: You are the DCT on OMFS ward cover during a hectic afternoon. Dr Grant (registrar) asks you to update discharge paperwork quickly for Mr Petrov, who is due to leave in an hour. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you Mr Petrov’s post-op bleeding episode overnight was “downplayed” in the notes, and she is worried the discharge advice doesn’t mention when to seek help. When you open the draft summary, you notice it states “no complications,” and it lists an antibiotic that Mr Petrov did not receive. Dr Grant says, “Just copy the template—pharmacy will sort it,” and adds that the bed is needed for an incoming trauma admission. Mr Petrov’s daughter phones Mark (ward clerk) angrily, saying they were not told about the bleeding and want to complain.

options.A: Amend the discharge summary to accurately reflect the overnight bleeding episode and correct medications, then ask Dr Grant to review and countersign before it is sent.
options.B: Leave the summary as drafted to avoid conflict with Dr Grant, and rely on Rachel to give verbal advice to Mr Petrov about bleeding risks.
options.C: Discuss Rachel’s concern with Dr Grant, explaining the inaccuracies you found, and propose a brief conversation with Mr Petrov (with Rachel present) to explain the bleeding and safety-netting advice.
options.D: Send the discharge summary immediately to meet the deadline, and plan to email an amended version later once the trauma admission is settled.
options.E: Document Rachel’s concern in the notes and tell Mark to direct the daughter’s complaint to PALS, without contacting Mr Petrov before discharge.

explanation:
Option C is most appropriate because it addresses patient safety and professionalism in real time: you raise Rachel’s concerns with Dr Grant and propose clear communication and safety-netting to Mr Petrov, which also helps manage the daughter’s anger. It balances the pressure for beds with the duty of candour and accurate discharge information.

Option A is also highly appropriate: correcting factual errors (complications and antibiotics) and ensuring senior review/countersignature is essential, especially when Dr Grant is the supervising registrar. Accurate documentation supports continuity of care and reduces harm after discharge.

Option E is a lower priority but still reasonable: recording Rachel’s concern and signposting to PALS helps ensure the complaint is managed appropriately; however, it should not replace direct communication with Mr Petrov and his family.

Option D is unsafe: sending inaccurate information with the intention to fix it later risks the wrong advice being acted upon and undermines trust.

Option B is least appropriate: leaving known inaccuracies because of hierarchy pressure breaches professional integrity and compromises patient safety; verbal advice cannot compensate for a misleading discharge summary and incorrect medication list.
learningPoints: Discharge documentation must be accurate, especially regarding complications and medicines.
Raise concerns respectfully but assertively when senior pressure conflicts with patient safety.
Duty of candour and good communication can reduce escalation of complaints.
Safety-netting advice should be clear, documented, and consistent with the clinical record.

scenario:
You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mrs Thompson, booked for extraction of LL6, looks “puffy” and says she once had a serious reaction to antibiotics but can’t recall which. The triage note from Mark (receptionist) lists “NKDA” because the electronic record failed to load earlier. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and has asked you to keep the clinic moving because there are four waiting patients and radiology is closing soon.

Mrs Thompson is anxious, repeatedly asks for “whatever antibiotics you normally give”, and says she needs to collect her grandson from school in an hour. Emily (another nurse) quietly tells you the patient mentioned “swelling and wheeze” last time but she isn’t sure.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using local anaesthetic, avoid prescribing antibiotics, and document “possible allergy” after the procedure to prevent delaying the waiting room.
options.B: Pause the procedure, take a focused allergy history with Sarah present, check the GP summary/previous letters on the spine system, and if uncertainty remains, discuss with Dr Kumar before prescribing or extracting.
options.C: Ask Mark to rebook Mrs Thompson to a later date when the record system is stable, and move immediately to the next patient to keep clinic flow.
options.D: Prescribe amoxicillin now because it is standard, advise Mrs Thompson to stop it if she develops symptoms, and continue with treatment to meet her time constraint.
options.E: Tell Mrs Thompson you cannot treat her today due to her anxiety, provide only analgesia advice, and ask Emily to escort her out so you can catch up.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because the immediate risk is preventable harm from an unverified allergy history and potentially unnecessary antibiotics. Taking a structured allergy history with Sarah as a witness, checking available records, and escalating to Dr Kumar if uncertainty persists aligns with NHS patient safety standards and recognising limits of competence. It also supports valid consent: Mrs Thompson cannot make an informed decision if key risks (e.g., anaphylaxis) are unknown.

Option A may be acceptable only if, after clarification, extraction is low risk and no antibiotics are indicated; however, proceeding before resolving the allergy uncertainty is unsafe, so it ranks below B. Option C addresses system failure and time pressure but postponing without attempting to clarify risks today may not be patient-centred and could increase suffering.

Option E is inappropriate: anxiety alone is not a reason to refuse care, and asking Emily to “escort her out” risks poor communication and complaint escalation. Option D is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin “because it is standard” disregards the reported reaction, undermines professional integrity, and could cause serious harm. Time pressure and radiology closing do not justify unsafe prescribing.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides time pressure and clinic flow pressures.
A documented “NKDA” entry is not reliable when systems fail; verify allergies directly and corroborate with records.
Antibiotics should not be prescribed to meet patient expectations; prescribe only when clinically indicated.
Escalate to a supervisor when uncertainty could lead to significant harm.

scenario:
You are in a community special care dentistry clinic seeing Mr Petrov, who has mild learning disability and attends with his support worker, Olivia. He needs restorations and possible extraction under sedation next month. Maria (hygienist) tells you Mr Petrov confided he is frightened of Olivia because she “shouts” and takes his bank card, but he begged Maria not to tell anyone. During your appointment, Olivia answers most questions for him and pressures you to “just get him booked quickly” because transport is difficult. Mr Petrov avoids eye contact and nods to everything.

Dr Chen (consultant) is running late and you have a full waiting list.

options.A: Arrange a brief private conversation with Mr Petrov (with an appropriate chaperone) to explore his wishes, assess capacity for the proposed plan, and explain why you may need to share concerns to keep him safe.
options.B: Tell Olivia directly in the waiting area that you suspect financial abuse and that you will be reporting her to safeguarding immediately.
options.C: Document Maria’s disclosure and your observations, then follow local safeguarding policy by discussing promptly with the safeguarding lead/Dr Chen, ensuring Mr Petrov is supported appropriately.
options.D: Do nothing today because Mr Petrov asked Maria to keep it secret; proceed with booking sedation to avoid delaying his care.
options.E: Ask Maria to speak to Olivia to “sort it out informally” because Maria has good rapport with both of them.

explanation:
A, C and F are the three most appropriate actions. A is crucial because Mr Petrov’s engagement suggests possible coercion; a private, supported conversation helps you assess his capacity and obtain valid consent, in line with the Mental Capacity Act principles and NHS consent standards. It also allows you to be transparent that confidentiality may be overridden where there is risk of harm.

C ensures professional safeguarding practice: clearly documenting Maria’s disclosure, your own observations (Olivia speaking for him, pressured booking), and escalating to Dr Chen and/or the safeguarding lead follows local policy and supports accountable decision-making under time pressure.

F addresses consent and communication: using accessible information and considering an independent advocate reduces undue influence and supports patient-centred care, especially if sedation and extractions are planned.

B is poorly handled because it risks confrontation and escalation in a public area and may endanger Mr Petrov. D ignores a potentially vulnerable adult safeguarding concern and undermines professional duty of care. E inappropriately delegates safeguarding to informal mediation and risks compromising Maria’s professional boundaries. G may be necessary only if there is immediate danger, but bypassing internal safeguarding routes is usually disproportionate. H is not patient-centred and could deny necessary care without a plan to keep him safe.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns can justify sharing information beyond the care team, but it must be proportionate and policy-led.
Valid consent requires the patient’s own understanding and freedom from coercion; assess capacity and support communication.
Document disclosures and observations contemporaneously and escalate to the safeguarding lead/supervisor.
Manage potentially abusive dynamics calmly and privately to avoid increasing risk to the patient.

scenario:
You are the on-call DCT covering the OMFS ward overnight. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Amira, a 9-year-old admitted after facial trauma. Her father, Mr Khan, is angry because he believes the team “missed a broken jaw” and is demanding a CT scan now. The earlier clerking note by Dr Grant (registrar) mentions “dental injury, observe, CT if worsening”, but the neuro obs chart is incomplete and the radiology request system is down. Rachel says Amira has vomited once and is more sleepy, but she is also due analgesia.

Mr Khan is filming on his phone and insists you give him a clear answer immediately.

options.A: Assess Amira immediately using an ABCDE approach, ask Rachel to administer due analgesia and complete observations, and escalate urgently to Dr Grant/consultant on-call with your findings and the limitation of the broken IT system.
options.B: Tell Mr Khan you cannot discuss details while he is filming, refuse to assess Amira until he stops, and ask security to remove him from the ward.
options.C: Reassure Mr Khan that Dr Grant’s plan is already in place, advise him to wait until morning ward round, and focus on catching up on other ward jobs.
options.D: Contact the on-call radiographer Elena by phone to discuss urgent imaging pathways given the down system, and document any verbal requests and advice received.
options.E: Order a CT scan on paper without senior input to meet Mr Khan’s demand and avoid a complaint.

explanation:
A is the most appropriate because Amira’s vomiting and increasing sleepiness could indicate clinical deterioration; an immediate ABCDE assessment and prompt escalation to Dr Grant aligns with patient safety and recognising limits of competence overnight. Delegating practical actions to Rachel (analgesia, observations) supports teamwork and addresses the incomplete neuro obs that currently undermines safe decision-making.

D is also appropriate: with the radiology system down, phoning Elena to clarify urgent pathways is pragmatic and maintains continuity, provided you document the discussion and outcomes clearly.

C is less appropriate but preferable to confrontational responses; however, simply deferring to the morning round ignores potential red flags and risks harm.

B is inappropriate because it delays assessment and escalates conflict. While filming can be addressed professionally, safeguarding the child’s immediate clinical needs comes first; involving security should be proportionate and usually after urgent care is underway.

E is least appropriate: ordering CT solely to appease Mr Khan bypasses clinical judgement and senior oversight, and risks unnecessary radiation exposure. Complaints risk does not justify unsafe or unindicated investigations; decisions should be based on Amira’s condition and agreed escalation routes.
learningPoints: Escalate promptly when a patient may be deteriorating, especially out of hours and with incomplete documentation.
Use the multidisciplinary team effectively: delegate observations/analgesia and document clearly.
System failures require proactive communication (e.g., phone radiology) with robust documentation.
Manage distressed relatives professionally without delaying urgent patient assessment.

scenario:
You are a DCT in a hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah, the dental nurse, quietly tells you that Mr Khan (58) in the next chair looks “puffy around the neck” and has been struggling to swallow. The clinic is already running 50 minutes late and Receptionist Nadia is dealing with a queue of irritated patients. Mr Khan was booked for “toothache” and the triage note is brief; it mentions diabetes and “penicillin allergy?” with a question mark. He is agitated and repeatedly asks for “strong antibiotics now” because he has a family wedding tomorrow.

Dr Kumar (registrar) is tied up in an oral surgery procedure upstairs and has asked not to be interrupted unless it is urgent. At the same time, Mrs Thompson is in the waiting room complaining loudly that her sedation appointment has been delayed twice.

options.A: Ask Sarah to take Mr Khan’s full set of observations (including oxygen saturations) immediately while you step in to apologise to Mrs Thompson and ask Nadia to rebook her if necessary.
options.B: Bring Mr Khan straight into a room, assess his airway and swallowing, clarify the penicillin allergy history, and escalate urgently to Dr Kumar or the on-call OMFS team if there are any red flags.
options.C: Prescribe a non-penicillin antibiotic based on the “penicillin allergy?” note to avoid delay, and arrange review later in the day when Dr Kumar is free.
options.D: Tell Nadia to move Mr Khan to the end of the list because the clinic is behind, and see the next routine patient to reduce waiting-room tension.
options.E: Ask Mr Khan to wait while you check the electronic record for previous antibiotic prescriptions, then decide whether to treat or refer once the record is complete.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to prioritise immediate clinical safety. Mr Khan’s neck swelling and dysphagia are red flags for a spreading odontogenic infection with potential airway compromise, so bringing him in, assessing airway and swallowing, and escalating urgently to Dr Kumar/on-call OMFS if needed is essential (B). This reflects NHS priorities of patient safety over clinic flow and working within limits by involving senior support.

Delegating observations to Sarah while you manage competing pressures is also appropriate (A), provided you do not allow the complaint from Mrs Thompson to distract from urgent assessment. Asking Nadia to rebook can be reasonable, but only after Mr Khan is safe.

Checking records (E) can support safe prescribing and allergy clarification, but it must not delay assessment of potential airway risk.

Prescribing “just in case” based on an uncertain “penicillin allergy?” note (C) is unsafe: it bypasses assessment, may be inadequate without source control, and normalises prescribing under pressure.

Deprioritising Mr Khan to manage waiting-room tension (D) is least appropriate as it risks deterioration and breaches duty of care, regardless of clinic delays or patient complaints.
learningPoints: Airway or swallowing concerns in dental infections require immediate assessment and urgent escalation.
Delegation (e.g., observations by a dental nurse) can help manage competing demands without compromising safety.
Antibiotic prescribing must be clinically justified; uncertainty about allergies requires clarification, not assumptions.
Patient complaints and clinic delays must not override urgent clinical priorities.

scenario:
You are in a community special care dentistry clinic seeing Mrs Hassan (34), who has learning disabilities and attends with her support worker, Mark. Dr Chen (consultant) is in another surgery and asked you to “keep things moving” because the afternoon list is overbooked due to staff sickness. Emily, the dental nurse, mentions that Mrs Hassan has previously become distressed during injections.

Mrs Hassan needs an extraction of a painful lower molar. Mark answers most questions for her and keeps looking at his watch, saying transport will leave in 20 minutes. When you try to explain risks and alternatives, Mrs Hassan avoids eye contact and says “I don’t know” repeatedly. Mark insists, “She always says that—just do it, she needs it out,” and asks you to proceed quickly. You also notice bruising on Mrs Hassan’s forearm; when you ask gently, she becomes quiet and Mark says she “bumps into doors.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using local anaesthetic, relying on Mark’s agreement because he is her support worker and transport is waiting.
options.B: Pause the procedure, speak directly to Mrs Hassan using simple language and visual aids, and check whether she can understand, retain and weigh information before deciding.
options.C: Ask Mark to step out briefly so you can speak to Mrs Hassan alone, explaining you do this routinely to ensure she can express preferences privately.
options.D: Ignore the bruising because it is not related to dentistry, and focus on completing the extraction to relieve pain quickly.
options.E: Document that consent was obtained from Mark and Mrs Hassan, then continue to avoid delaying the overbooked clinic.

explanation:
The best actions prioritise valid consent and safeguarding while managing pressure. You must assess Mrs Hassan’s capacity for this specific decision and support her understanding (B). Speaking directly to her with simplified information and appropriate aids helps her participate and aligns with patient-centred care.

Creating space for her to express herself without Mark’s influence is appropriate (C). Asking Mark to step out briefly can reduce coercion and is consistent with good practice, particularly when a patient appears unable to speak freely.

Given the bruising and evasive explanation, you should escalate concerns in line with safeguarding policy and seek senior input from Dr Chen on capacity/consent and the best treatment plan (F). This protects Mrs Hassan and supports you to work within competence.

Proceeding based on Mark’s agreement (A) or documenting consent as if it were valid (E) is unsafe and unethical; a support worker is not automatically a legal decision-maker. Ignoring bruising (D) misses a potential safeguarding issue. Offering IV sedation immediately (G) may be outside remit and requires assessment, consent and appropriate staffing. Delaying transport without discussion (H) undermines trust and autonomy; practical arrangements should be negotiated transparently.
learningPoints: Consent must be valid: support understanding and assess decision-specific capacity.
Where there is potential coercion, offer opportunities to speak to the patient privately.
Safeguarding concerns should be acted on and escalated according to local policy.
Time pressure and service targets do not justify bypassing consent or ignoring welfare concerns.

scenario:
You are a DCT on an OMFS ward. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov (72), admitted overnight with a facial abscess. He is due for theatre later and is anxious, asking repeatedly when he can eat and whether his “heart tablets” will be given. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in theatre and Dr Grant (registrar) is in an MDT meeting.

While checking the drug chart, you notice an antibiotic prescription that appears to have been written by another DCT, Dr Fernandes, but the dose is unusually high for Mr Petrov’s renal function, which is documented in the notes. Rachel says the last dose was already administered and asks you to rewrite the chart because pharmacy is querying it. At the same time, Mr Petrov’s daughter phones the ward demanding details and saying she will “go to PALS” if she is not updated.

options.A: Immediately amend the antibiotic dose on the chart to what you believe is correct and sign it, then message Dr Fernandes later to let them know.
options.B: Ask Rachel to hold further doses until the prescription is clarified, review the notes and renal function, and contact Dr Grant or the on-call pharmacist (Mr Evans) for urgent advice before rewriting.
options.C: Tell Mr Petrov’s daughter the current antibiotic and theatre plan over the phone to reduce conflict, because she is a close relative and he seems confused.
options.D: Speak to Dr Fernandes directly (by phone if needed) to clarify their rationale for the dose, and document the discussion and agreed plan in the notes.
options.E: Reassure Rachel that the prescription must be correct because Dr Fernandes wrote it, and continue the current dosing to avoid disrupting theatre plans.

explanation:
Patient safety and appropriate escalation come first. Holding further doses while you clarify the prescription (B) is most appropriate: Mr Petrov has documented renal impairment and an unusually high dose could cause harm. Involving Dr Grant, the on-call team, or pharmacist Mr Evans supports safe prescribing and recognises your limits.

Contacting Dr Fernandes to understand the rationale and agree a plan (D) is also important for teamwork and professionalism; it avoids assumptions and allows learning. Clear documentation of the conversation and changes is essential for continuity of care.

Unilaterally amending and signing a new dose without discussion (A) may be necessary in an emergency, but here it risks confusion, undermines governance, and could be incorrect without confirming indication, microbiology advice, and theatre timing.

Disclosing details to Mr Petrov’s daughter without confirming consent (C) risks breaching confidentiality. You should check whether Mr Petrov agrees to sharing information or whether she has legal authority. Finally, deferring to hierarchy and continuing a potentially unsafe dose (E) is least appropriate; professional integrity requires you to challenge and escalate concerns even if it risks inconvenience to the service.
learningPoints: If a prescription seems unsafe (e.g., renal dosing), pause and escalate rather than continuing by default.
Clarify concerns directly with the prescriber and document decisions to support teamwork and governance.
Maintain confidentiality: share information with relatives only with patient consent or appropriate authority.
Professionalism includes challenging errors respectfully, even under service pressure.

scenario: You are the DCT1 in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. The waiting room is full and reception (Nadia) is warning about multiple complaints. Your dental nurse, Sarah, flags that the next patient, Mr Clarke, is booked for an incision and drainage of a swelling but his notes are thin and the medical history sheet is half-complete. Mr Clarke looks flushed and says he “just needs it sorted quickly” because he must collect his child from school. He mentions a previous rash with “a penicillin” but can’t recall details. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in theatre and not immediately contactable. A junior doctor from ED has phoned asking you to “just give antibiotics” because ED is overloaded. Mr Clarke is increasingly impatient, and Nadia asks whether you can “keep things moving” to reduce delays.

options.A: Ask Sarah to take full observations and complete the medical history with Mr Clarke, while you assess the swelling and document the reported penicillin rash before deciding on treatment.
options.B: Phone the OMFS registrar, Dr Lewis, to discuss the unclear penicillin reaction and whether Mr Clarke needs ED assessment for systemic involvement before you proceed.
options.C: Prescribe amoxicillin now to satisfy the ED request and re-check the allergy details later, so you can start drainage quickly.
options.D: Explain to Mr Clarke that you may need to delay treatment briefly to clarify his allergy and assess severity, and ask Nadia to update the waiting room about a short clinical safety delay.
options.E: Proceed with incision and drainage immediately to avoid further delay, and avoid any antibiotics today given the uncertainty about allergy.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety despite pressure from Nadia and the ED junior. Option A is the most appropriate first step: completing a proper medical history, recording observations, and documenting the reported penicillin rash with Sarah supports safe decision-making and aligns with standards on accurate records and safe prescribing. Option B is also highly appropriate because Dr Kumar is unavailable and the combination of an unclear allergy history and possible systemic signs (flushed appearance) warrants senior input from Dr Lewis and consideration of ED assessment if sepsis is a concern. Option D helps manage the interpersonal dynamic and competing priority (clinic flow vs safety) by setting expectations with Mr Clarke and involving Nadia appropriately without breaching confidentiality.
Option E may be reasonable only after assessment: incision and drainage can be definitive, but proceeding immediately without clarifying severity, observations, or consent implications is unsafe, so it ranks lower. Option C is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin with an unclear penicillin allergy risks harm, is poor antimicrobial stewardship, and prioritises convenience over clinical judgement and professional integrity.
learningPoints: Complete and document medical history and allergy status before prescribing.
Escalate to an appropriate senior when risk is unclear and supervision is limited.
Manage waiting-room pressure transparently while maintaining patient safety.
Antibiotic requests from other departments should not override dental clinical judgement and stewardship.

scenario: You are in a community special care dentistry clinic. Mrs Hassan attends with her son, Daniel (9), for extractions under inhalation sedation next week. The referral letter is vague and the consent form is incomplete. Daniel is quiet and avoids eye contact. When you ask about medical history, Mrs Hassan answers for him and becomes defensive when you try to speak to Daniel directly. Your dental nurse, Chloe, quietly tells you she overheard Mrs Hassan on the phone in the waiting area saying Daniel “must not tell them anything” because “social will get involved again”. The interpreter service is unavailable today, and Mrs Hassan’s English is limited. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is running late and the clinic is already behind. Receptionist Olivia asks if you can “just get the forms signed” to keep the list moving.

options.A: Ask Chloe to stay with Daniel and Mrs Hassan while you immediately phone the safeguarding lead for advice, documenting Chloe’s overheard comment verbatim.
options.B: Proceed to obtain Mrs Hassan’s signature on the consent form today to avoid delaying the sedation list, and plan to clarify details on the day of treatment.
options.C: Arrange a follow-up pre-assessment appointment with a booked interpreter and explain to Mrs Hassan that you cannot finalise consent today because Daniel’s understanding and history need to be confirmed.
options.D: Ask to speak to Daniel alone in an age-appropriate way (with Chloe present) to explore his understanding and whether he feels safe, while explaining to Mrs Hassan that it is routine practice.
options.E: Tell Olivia to inform the waiting room that you are dealing with a safeguarding concern and that the clinic will be delayed.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions prioritise safeguarding and valid consent while remaining professional and proportionate. A is appropriate because Chloe’s overheard statement raises a potential safeguarding red flag; contacting the safeguarding lead promptly and documenting the information accurately (including that it was overheard) follows NHS safeguarding duties and supports defensible decision-making. D is also appropriate: speaking to Daniel in an age-appropriate way, with Chloe present, helps assess his understanding and any concerns about safety, and it can be framed as routine practice to reduce confrontation with Mrs Hassan.
F is appropriate because this situation has multiple risks—sedation planning, unclear referral/consent, language barrier, and possible coercion—so escalating to Dr O’Connor ensures senior oversight and a coordinated plan.
C is sensible for consent quality but, on its own, could delay action on a potential safeguarding issue and misses immediate assessment. B and H compromise consent and safety. E breaches confidentiality by sharing safeguarding details with the waiting room; delays can be explained without specifics. G is accusatory, escalates conflict, and may put Daniel at further risk; safeguarding referrals should be handled calmly and through appropriate pathways.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns should be escalated promptly and documented accurately.
Valid consent requires understanding; language barriers and coercion risks must be addressed.
Children should be given opportunities to speak in an age-appropriate way.
In complex ethical situations, involve seniors and follow local safeguarding pathways rather than confronting or delaying.

scenario: You are a DCT in the oral surgery radiology department for a joint clinic. Radiographer Elena refuses to take a CBCT for Mrs Thompson, saying the request form is incomplete and the justification is unclear. Dr Chen (consultant) is in clinic with the next patient and has asked you to “sort the imaging quickly”. The patient, Mrs Thompson, is anxious and says she has already taken time off work twice; she insists the scan must happen today. Dental nurse Emily whispers that the referral from restorative mentions a possible impacted canine but there is no recent OPT on the system. Elena is frustrated because several junior staff have been sending poor requests, and she says she will “incident report the team” if it continues. You are aware the clinic is running late and reception (Mark) is fielding calls from patients asking why appointments are delayed.

options.A: Apologise to Elena, ask Emily to locate any prior imaging or referral details, and complete a clearer clinical justification on the request form in line with IR(ME)R before re-submitting.
options.B: Tell Elena that Dr Chen is the consultant and that she must proceed with the CBCT today because the patient has been waiting and the clinic is late.
options.C: Explain to Mrs Thompson why Elena cannot perform the CBCT yet, offer to contact restorative for missing details, and give her an honest timeframe so she can decide whether to wait or rebook.
options.D: Ask Mark to tell Mrs Thompson that radiology is refusing to help, so she can complain to them directly and put pressure on Elena.
options.E: Escalate promptly to Dr Chen with the specific missing information Elena needs, and propose either obtaining an OPT first or re-justifying the CBCT, depending on clinical necessity.

explanation:
Under IR(ME)R, imaging must be appropriately justified and documented; time pressure cannot override this. A is the most appropriate because it addresses Elena’s legitimate concern, involves Emily in locating prior imaging, and improves the request quality—supporting patient safety and constructive interprofessional working. E is next: escalating to Dr Chen with concrete details allows the referrer/practitioner decision-maker to agree whether CBCT is necessary or whether an OPT should be obtained first, keeping responsibility with the appropriate clinician and reducing conflict.
C is also appropriate: it manages Mrs Thompson’s anxiety and expectations through transparent communication and choice, without blaming radiology. B is less appropriate because it undermines Elena’s professional role and IR(ME)R compliance, escalating the interpersonal dynamic and risking unsafe imaging. D is least appropriate: it inappropriately triangulates the complaint process, damages working relationships, and misrepresents the situation to the patient, potentially breaching professionalism and trust.
Overall, the best response is to improve documentation, collaborate with radiology, and involve Dr Chen for decision-making while keeping the patient informed.
learningPoints: IR(ME)R justification and documentation are mandatory even under time pressure.
Maintain respectful interprofessional communication when another team raises safety concerns.
Escalate to the appropriate referrer/senior with specific information, not demands.
Manage patient expectations transparently without blaming other departments.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Clarke arrives with facial swelling and trismus. The receptionist, Nadia, says he has already waited 90 minutes and is angry. Your dental nurse, Sarah, flags that his triage sheet lists “penicillin allergy,” but the previous ED note in the shared record suggests he tolerated amoxicillin last month. Dr Kumar (registrar) is scrubbed in theatre and not immediately reachable. Mr Clarke insists he “just needs antibiotics” and refuses drainage because he has a job interview this afternoon. Meanwhile, two children are waiting in pain and the clinic is running late. Sarah looks worried and asks what to prescribe because the electronic prescribing system is asking you to select an allergy status before it will proceed.

options.A: Ask Nadia to book Mr Clarke into the next available slot tomorrow and give him standard penicillin antibiotics today to avoid further delay.
options.B: Explain to Mr Clarke, with Sarah present, why urgent assessment and likely drainage are recommended; assess airway/red flags, examine, and if he still refuses, document refusal and offer safety-netting and appropriate non-penicillin antibiotic only if clinically indicated.
options.C: Tell Sarah to select “no known allergy” on the prescribing system based on the ED note and issue amoxicillin now, then review Mr Clarke after you have seen the two children.
options.D: Ask Sarah to call theatre to interrupt Dr Kumar for an immediate antibiotic choice, while you start writing up a prescription to save time.
options.E: Pause prescribing, clarify the allergy directly with Mr Clarke (reaction type, timing), check available records, and if uncertainty remains, choose a non-penicillin option and escalate to Dr Kumar as soon as available; prioritise clinical assessment over clinic flow.

explanation:
The safest first step is to clarify and manage risk around the documented penicillin allergy while still addressing Mr Clarke’s acute infection. Option E is most appropriate because you stop the process that could lead to harm, take a focused allergy history, cross-check records, and choose a non‑penicillin antibiotic if uncertainty remains. It also recognises limits of competence by escalating to Dr Kumar when available, without delaying initial assessment.

Option B is also strong: it prioritises assessment of airway/red flags in a swollen, trismus patient, supports informed consent by explaining drainage, and respects Mr Clarke’s autonomy if he refuses—provided you document clearly and give safety-net advice. However, without explicitly addressing the allergy/system constraint first, it is slightly less immediately risk-focused than E.

Option D shows appropriate escalation but is less appropriate because interrupting theatre should be reserved for emergencies; you also should not pre-write a prescription before confirming allergy details. Option C is unsafe and unprofessional: instructing Sarah to override allergy status risks anaphylaxis and places inappropriate pressure on a nurse. Option A is least appropriate: giving “standard” penicillin antibiotics without verification and deferring care ignores patient safety and duty of care despite time pressure and anger.
learningPoints: Patient safety and allergy verification take priority over time pressure and patient demand.
Valid consent includes explaining options and consequences; refusals must be documented with safety-netting.
Escalate appropriately: seek senior input without creating unnecessary disruption, and never bypass safety systems.
Support team members by giving clear, safe instructions and not asking them to act outside safe practice.

scenario: You are on an OMFS ward during a hectic afternoon round. Dr O’Connor (consultant) asks you to discharge Mrs Hassan, who had an incision and drainage yesterday, to free a bed. The ward nurse, Angela, quietly tells you Mrs Hassan’s husband has been “answering for her” and she seems frightened when he is present. Mrs Hassan speaks limited English; today the interpreter service is unavailable. At the bedside, Mr Hassan insists on staying and demands to know “exactly what was done,” while Mrs Hassan avoids eye contact and says softly she is “fine to go.” Your registrar, Dr Fernandes, is dealing with a trauma call and messages that you should “just get the discharge done quickly.” Meanwhile, a junior nurse asks you to sign a discharge summary that contains copied text suggesting the swelling was “resolved,” although you have not examined Mrs Hassan yet.

options.A: Ask Angela to arrange a private conversation with Mrs Hassan away from her husband, and use a phone/video interpreting service if available; explain confidentiality and check for safeguarding concerns before proceeding with discharge.
options.B: Complete the discharge summary as written to meet bed targets, and advise Mrs Hassan to return if symptoms worsen.
options.C: Tell Mr Hassan you cannot discuss details without Mrs Hassan’s consent, and ask him to step out briefly while you assess her clinically and confirm she understands the discharge plan.
options.D: Message Dr O’Connor directly that you suspect domestic abuse and refuse to discharge Mrs Hassan until a safeguarding referral is made.
options.E: Sign the discharge summary now but plan to amend it later after you have examined Mrs Hassan.

explanation:
A, C and F best balance safeguarding, confidentiality and accurate records under pressure. Option A is key because it creates a safe opportunity for Mrs Hassan to speak privately, uses interpreting support where possible, and allows you to explore concerns raised by Angela without making assumptions. This aligns with NHS safeguarding principles: act on worrying indicators and ensure the patient can communicate freely.

Option C appropriately manages confidentiality and consent by making clear to Mr Hassan that disclosure requires Mrs Hassan’s agreement, and it facilitates a private clinical assessment. It also de-escalates an interpersonal challenge without confrontation.

Option F addresses immediate patient safety and professional integrity: you must examine Mrs Hassan and only sign documentation you believe is accurate. Correcting copied text and documenting communication limitations protects the patient and the team.

B and E prioritise flow over safety and are unsafe: signing inaccurate documentation or “fixing later” breaches professional standards. D is disproportionate and risks damaging team relationships; you should escalate safeguarding concerns, but you need a factual basis and usually follow local safeguarding pathways rather than issuing ultimatums to Dr O’Connor. G inappropriately defers responsibility and ignores confidentiality. H is unsafe and tokenistic; covert leaflets can increase risk if discovered and do not replace a proper safeguarding approach.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns should be explored promptly and sensitively, creating privacy and communication support.
Confidentiality applies even with relatives present; involve family only with patient consent.
Only sign clinical documents that are accurate and based on your own assessment.
Escalate concerns via appropriate pathways while maintaining professional, proportionate communication.

scenario: You are a DCT in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson, who has Parkinson’s disease, attends with her daughter for an urgent denture sore spot review. The clinic is overbooked due to staff sickness. Your dental nurse, Emily, tells you the referral letter mentions “on warfarin,” but the medical history on the electronic record is blank because the system was down yesterday. Maria (therapist) is running late and asks if you can “quickly adjust” Mrs Thompson’s lower denture so Maria can start her next patient. Mrs Thompson is visibly fatigued and her daughter becomes frustrated, saying, “She’s been in pain all week—just do something now.” You realise the adjustment might require smoothing a sharp flange near an ulcer, and there’s a possibility of needing a small soft-tissue assessment to exclude trauma versus something more concerning.

options.A: Proceed with the denture adjustment immediately to reduce pain, and ask Emily to chase the warfarin information after the appointment to avoid further delays.
options.B: Explain to Maria and the daughter that you need to confirm key medical details and examine the ulcer properly first; ask Emily to contact the GP/anticoagulation clinic and check Mrs Thompson’s yellow book/INR record before any invasive soft-tissue intervention.
options.C: Ask Maria to see Mrs Thompson instead since she is already delayed, while you continue with other patients to keep the clinic moving.
options.D: With Emily present, take a focused history and examine the ulcer; if it appears traumatic, make a conservative denture adjustment and provide clear advice and a short review, documenting the incomplete medical history and actions taken.
options.E: Tell the daughter that her frustration is unacceptable and warn that you will cancel the appointment if she continues, then proceed as planned.

explanation:
Option D is most appropriate because it prioritises patient-centred care and clinical safety: you take a focused history, examine the ulcer, and, if it looks consistent with trauma, carry out a conservative adjustment and arrange timely review. You also document the incomplete medical record and your decision-making, which is vital in a system failure situation.

Option B is next best because it manages team expectations (Maria) and the relative’s pressure while ensuring key medical information is sought. Checking warfarin/INR is particularly relevant if any soft-tissue intervention or bleeding risk is anticipated. However, it may delay simple, non-invasive adjustment that could be safely done after assessment, so it sits just behind D.

Option A is less appropriate: acting before clarifying medication status and examining the lesion risks missing red flags or creating avoidable harm, even if the intention is to reduce pain quickly.

Option C is inappropriate because it inappropriately delegates a potentially more complex assessment to Maria primarily for flow, rather than matching the task to competence and continuity; it may also undermine teamwork by shifting risk.

Option E is least appropriate: it escalates conflict with the daughter and threatens care. While boundaries matter, you should de-escalate and refocus on Mrs Thompson’s needs, not use cancellation as leverage under stress.
learningPoints: Assess and document properly before intervening, especially when records are incomplete.
Manage time pressure by communicating clear, safety-based reasons to patients and colleagues.
Consider anticoagulants when planning any intervention with bleeding risk; verify key details where possible.
De-escalation and respectful communication support teamwork and patient-centred care.

scenario: You are a DCT in a hospital sedation clinic. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is running two rooms because a colleague has called in sick. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mrs Thompson is listed for IV sedation and an upper molar extraction, but her pre-assessment form is incomplete and the medical history in the electronic record contradicts the paper notes: the e-record shows severe asthma with a recent admission, while the paper form says “mild, inhaler occasionally.” Mrs Thompson is anxious and says she travelled far and “can’t face coming back.” Dr Kumar looks stressed and says, “We’ll just go with what’s on the paper—she was fine last time.” Meanwhile, reception (Nadia) says three patients are already waiting and one has complained about delays. You cannot reach the anaesthetist for advice, and Dr Kumar is due to start the next case in five minutes.

options.A: Proceed with IV sedation using the paper history only, and ask Sarah to “keep a closer eye on her breathing” during the extraction to avoid further delay.
options.B: Tell Dr Kumar you are not comfortable proceeding until the discrepancy is resolved; ask Nadia to pause further patient arrivals, and start verifying the asthma history by calling Mrs Thompson’s GP/using summary care record and checking recent admission details.
options.C: Offer Mrs Thompson local anaesthetic extraction without sedation immediately, explaining that IV sedation cannot be confirmed safely today, and document that she “declined to wait.”
