C is most appropriate because it addresses immediate patient safety: Mr Khan has swelling and trismus, so a structured urgent assessment (airway, systemic signs) and confirming allergies are essential before any prescribing or discharge. Involving Chloe and updating Dr Grant ensures safe triage within your competence and maintains escalation pathways while the registrar is in theatre.

D is the next best action because Dr Fernandes’ use of a personal phone raises confidentiality and consent concerns. Addressing it discreetly maintains professionalism and reduces conflict in front of Mr Khan, while still taking prompt action. Escalation and documentation are appropriate if there is non-compliance.

A is less appropriate: although the concern is valid, challenging Dr Fernandes in front of an already angry patient risks undermining trust and escalating confrontation; it may also distract from urgent assessment. B is inappropriate because ignoring a potential data protection breach fails professional responsibility. E is least appropriate: prescribing ‘strong antibiotics’ to manage frustration without adequate history and allergy checks is unsafe, undermines antimicrobial stewardship, and could cause harm if Mr Khan has an allergy or needs urgent surgical management rather than antibiotics alone.
learningPoints: In facial swelling with trismus, prioritise assessment of airway risk, systemic illness and allergies before prescribing.
Address confidentiality and consent breaches promptly, but choose an approach that maintains patient trust and team professionalism.
Escalate to seniors when managing beyond your competence or when urgent risk is identified.
Do not prescribe antibiotics to manage pressure or dissatisfaction; ensure indications and safety checks first.

scenario: You are a DCT in an oral surgery clinic. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is running late and has stepped out to take an urgent call. Your next patient, Mr Khan, is booked for an extraction under local anaesthetic. Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you she saw “apixaban” on his GP summary last month but today’s medical history form is incomplete and the anticoagulant box is unchecked. Mr Khan appears anxious and insists he needs the tooth out today because he is flying tomorrow. Nadia (receptionist) says two patients are already complaining about delays. You cannot access the GP record immediately because the system is slow, and Dr Lewis (registrar) is covering another clinic. Mr Khan becomes irritated when you mention postponing, saying he "told someone already" and that cancelling will "ruin everything".

options.A: Proceed with the extraction now using local haemostatic measures, explaining to Mr Khan that you will be extra careful, and document that the patient requested urgent treatment.
options.B: Ask Sarah to re-check Mr Khan’s medications with him and his phone list, and call the anticoagulation clinic/GP urgently; if apixaban use cannot be clarified promptly, offer a temporary dressing/analgesia plan and rebook with Dr Ahmed’s input.
options.C: Ask Nadia to fit Mr Khan into the end of the session and start the next patient, hoping Dr Ahmed returns by then to advise on anticoagulation management.
options.D: Tell Mr Khan you cannot treat him today due to missing paperwork, ask him to leave, and instruct Nadia to book him another appointment without further discussion.
options.E: Continue with the extraction but ask Mr Khan to stop his apixaban tonight and tomorrow, and give standard post-op instructions, as he is travelling.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is B because Sarah has raised a credible safety concern and the medical history is incomplete. For an extraction, confirming whether Mr Khan is taking apixaban (and when it was last taken) is essential for risk assessment and informed consent. Contacting the GP/anticoagulation service and directly verifying with the patient are proportionate steps, and offering interim measures (temporary dressing, analgesia advice, clear safety-netting) prioritises patient safety while respecting his wish for symptom relief.

C is reasonable as a secondary step: managing clinic flow by moving Mr Khan later may buy time for Dr Ahmed to return and for checks to be completed, while remaining transparent with Mr Khan.

A is less appropriate because proceeding without clarifying anticoagulation undermines clinical safety and consent; documenting patient pressure does not justify unsafe care.

D is unprofessional and risks escalation; you still owe Mr Khan an explanation and an alternative plan.

E is least appropriate: advising a patient to stop apixaban without appropriate medical oversight is outside competence and may expose him to thromboembolic harm, breaching professional integrity and NHS safety standards.
learningPoints: Act on concerns raised by dental nurses and verify incomplete medical histories before invasive procedures.
Patient safety and informed consent take priority over time pressure and patient demands.
Know the limits of competence: do not advise anticoagulant interruption without appropriate clinical authority.
Offer interim symptom control and clear safety-netting when definitive care must be delayed.

scenario: You are on a community dental service domiciliary visit to a care home with Laura (dental therapist) and Chloe (dental nurse). You are reviewing Mrs Thompson, who has moderate dementia and recurrent ulceration from a broken lower denture. Her son, Mr Clarke, is present and repeatedly answers for her. He insists you “just take the denture away” today because he is worried she will choke, and he asks you not to mention anything to the GP because “they overreact”. Mrs Thompson looks distressed when her son speaks loudly, then quietly tells Chloe she is "scared" at night but stops when Mr Clarke returns to the room. Laura is concerned about delay because there are two more residents to see and transport is booked.

options.A: Ask Olivia to find any documentation about capacity and decision-making authority, and if absent, carry out and document a brief capacity assessment specific to denture removal and ulcer management.
options.B: Agree with Mr Clarke to remove the denture immediately and reassure him you will not inform the GP to avoid upsetting the family.
options.C: Arrange to speak with Mrs Thompson alone (with Chloe present) to explore her wishes and the comment about being scared, and consider safeguarding pathways if concerns persist.
options.D: Defer all treatment until a future visit when the paperwork is available, and end the visit now to stay on schedule for the transport.
options.E: Explain to Mr Clarke that you must act in Mrs Thompson’s best interests and may need to share relevant information with the GP/care home team; discuss a proportionate plan (e.g., smoothing denture, soft lining, or temporary removal) based on consent/capacity findings.

explanation:
A, C and E are the three most appropriate actions because they centre Mrs Thompson’s rights, safety and lawful decision-making. A is essential: in domiciliary care, paperwork may be missing, but you still must assess and document capacity for this specific decision and clarify whether Mr Clarke has legal authority (e.g., health and welfare LPA). Acting without this risks unlawful treatment and poor consent.

C appropriately manages the interpersonal dynamic: Mr Clarke is dominating, and Mrs Thompson has hinted she is “scared” at night. Speaking with her privately (with Chloe) supports valid consent and enables you to consider safeguarding if there are indicators of neglect, coercion or abuse. Safeguarding should be proportionate but cannot be ignored.

E sets clear professional boundaries: you cannot promise to withhold relevant information from the GP if it is necessary for best interests, ongoing care or safeguarding. It also offers a practical, least-restrictive plan (repair/adjustment, temporary removal, pain control) aligned with consent/capacity findings.

B and G are inappropriate as “next of kin” does not equal decision-making authority, and colluding to avoid GP involvement undermines patient safety. D and H prioritise convenience over care and miss potential safeguarding concerns. F may compromise safety and lone-working standards for Laura.
learningPoints: Capacity is decision-specific and must be assessed and documented when in doubt, especially in dementia.
Dominant relatives can undermine patient autonomy; create space for the patient’s voice.
Safeguarding concerns should be explored sensitively and escalated appropriately when indicated.
You cannot agree to conceal relevant information if it affects ongoing care or safety.

scenario: You are a DCT in the radiology department requesting an OPG for Tyler (10) from the emergency dental clinic. Tyler fell at school and now has a suspected mandibular fracture. His mother, Mrs Hassan, is upset and says Tyler is "too scared" for any X-rays. Elena (radiographer) refuses the request because the electronic referral is missing a justification and the pregnancy status field is blank; she says she has been criticised for poor IR(ME)R compliance. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and not answering his bleep. Rachel (ward nurse) is waiting to take Tyler for analgesia review and wants you to “hurry up” because the ED cubicles are full. Mrs Hassan starts filming on her phone, saying she will "show how the hospital treats kids".

options.A: Ask Elena to perform the OPG anyway because the clinical suspicion is high, and offer to amend the referral after the image is taken to save time.
options.B: Step aside with Mrs Hassan and Tyler to acknowledge their anxiety, explain why imaging is needed and what will happen, ask her to stop filming due to staff privacy, and seek Tyler’s assent while assessing whether he can cooperate.
options.C: Complete the missing referral details immediately with the information you have (mechanism of injury, suspected fracture, urgency), confirm pregnancy status is not applicable to Tyler, and document your conversation with Elena about IR(ME)R compliance.
options.D: Tell Rachel that radiology is refusing and ask her to take Tyler back to ED to wait until Dr Kumar is free, as you do not want conflict with radiology staff.
options.E: Escalate to the duty radiology superintendent or on-call clinician authorised under IR(ME)R for justification if Dr Kumar remains unavailable, explaining the trauma concern and ED pressures.

explanation:
B is most appropriate because it addresses the immediate interpersonal and ethical tensions: Tyler’s fear, Mrs Hassan’s distress, and the filming. Supporting a child’s understanding and cooperation, seeking assent, and maintaining staff privacy are key to patient-centred care and professionalism. Asking her to stop filming is reasonable when it involves staff privacy and may escalate conflict.

C is next: Elena’s concern about IR(ME)R compliance is legitimate. Completing a clear clinical justification and correcting the irrelevant pregnancy field (as Tyler is a child) supports lawful, safe imaging and good cross-department communication.

E is appropriate escalation when the referrer/justifier (Dr Kumar) is unavailable and the clinical risk is significant. Using the proper pathway protects patient safety and supports radiology governance.

D is less appropriate: it delays care without attempting to resolve the barriers and risks Tyler being lost in the ED system, though it is safer than bypassing governance.

A is least appropriate because it asks Elena to breach IR(ME)R processes and undermines a safety culture; “fixing it later” is not acceptable in NHS radiology workflows.
learningPoints: Manage anxiety and consent/assent in children while maintaining professionalism under provocation.
Respect and support IR(ME)R governance: clear justification and correct referral details matter.
Escalate appropriately when key decision-makers are unavailable and time-critical imaging is needed.
Do not pressure colleagues to bypass safety processes to meet time targets.

scenario: You are the DCT1 in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. The waiting room is full and reception has warned of a rising number of complaints about delays. Sarah, the dental nurse, tells you that Mr Ibrahim is booked for extraction of UR6 but says he “reacts badly to antibiotics”. In his electronic record, an allergy is recorded as “penicillin ?anaphylaxis” from five years ago, but today’s referral letter from his GDP (Dr Grant) states “no known drug allergies”. Mr Ibrahim is anxious and wants “whatever makes it quick” so he can get back to work. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and not answering calls. Radiology is also running behind, and you cannot immediately access previous discharge summaries. Sarah is pressing you to start because the next patient, Mrs Thompson, is already in the chair and upset.

options.A: Explain to Mr Ibrahim that you will proceed with the extraction without any antibiotics and document that the GDP letter states no allergies, so the risk is minimal.
options.B: Pause the procedure, take a focused allergy history from Mr Ibrahim (including reaction details and timing), check the summary care record/previous hospital notes if available, and document the discrepancy before deciding on any prescribing.
options.C: Ask Sarah to call radiology and request they prioritise printing any prior discharge summaries while you start preparing local anaesthetic to save time.
options.D: Tell Mr Ibrahim that the clinic cannot treat him today due to inconsistent records and ask reception (Nadia) to rebook him for another day when Dr Kumar is available.
options.E: Proceed with the extraction and prescribe amoxicillin post-operatively because it is the standard protocol; if he objects you will switch to metronidazole.

explanation:
The safest and most professional response is to pause and clarify the allergy history and documentation (B). The record suggests possible penicillin anaphylaxis, which is a high-risk red flag, and relying on Dr Grant’s letter alone would not meet NHS expectations for safe prescribing. Taking a structured history from Mr Ibrahim, checking available records (e.g., SCR/hospital notes), and documenting the discrepancy demonstrates clinical safety, good record keeping, and patient-centred consent.

C is a pragmatic supporting action: asking Sarah to help obtain prior information while you prepare can reduce delay without compromising safety, provided you do not administer or prescribe anything before clarifying the allergy.

A is less appropriate because it minimises risk and frames the discrepancy as “minimal” without adequate verification; while not giving antibiotics may be reasonable, the decision must be based on a proper assessment and explanation.

D is disproportionate: cancelling care due to record inconsistency increases harm and service burden; lack of immediate registrar availability is not, by itself, a reason to defer a straightforward extraction if you can practice safely.

E is unsafe and ignores potential anaphylaxis, undermining informed consent and professional integrity.
learningPoints: Discrepancies in allergy documentation must be actively clarified and recorded before prescribing.
Patient safety and informed consent take priority over time pressure and complaints about delays.
Use the dental team effectively (e.g., delegating record retrieval) while maintaining responsibility for clinical decisions.
Avoid routine antibiotic prescribing; decisions should be individualised and risk-based.

scenario: You are a DCT2 in community dental services seeing Sophie (8) for repeated extractions under inhalation sedation due to severe caries. Her mother, Mrs Hassan, arrives late and appears tearful and irritable. During the pre-op checks, Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you she noticed bruising on Sophie’s upper arms when helping her put on a bib, and Sophie flinched when Mrs Hassan raised her voice. Sophie whispers to you that she “doesn’t like going home when mum’s boyfriend is there” but stops talking when her mother comes closer. The interpreter booked for Mrs Hassan (who has limited English) has not arrived, and the clinic is already running 40 minutes behind with another child waiting. Dr Chen (consultant) is off-site at an outreach clinic and can only be contacted intermittently. Mrs Hassan demands you “just do the teeth today” and says she will complain if you “make problems”.

options.A: Proceed with sedation and extractions to avoid worsening Sophie’s dental pain, and make a safeguarding note after the clinic ends so you do not escalate the situation in front of Mrs Hassan.
options.B: Ask Emily to keep Sophie in the surgery while you ask Mrs Hassan to step to reception (Olivia) to complete paperwork, creating a short opportunity to speak to Sophie alone and assess her disclosure further.
options.C: Contact the safeguarding lead on the day (or duty social worker if required) and document the bruising and Sophie’s comment, explaining to Mrs Hassan that you have a duty to act on any concerns.
options.D: Insist that Mrs Hassan leaves the building immediately so you can speak to Sophie, and threaten to call security if she refuses.
options.E: Defer sedation and any irreversible treatment until you have appropriate interpreter support to ensure Mrs Hassan understands the plan and risks, and document the consent limitation.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions prioritise safeguarding, valid consent, and patient safety. Creating a brief, non-confrontational opportunity to speak to Sophie alone (B) is appropriate because Sophie has made a partial disclosure and appears fearful; it uses Emily and reception (Olivia) to manage the interaction without escalating conflict. You must then act on safeguarding concerns the same day (C): unexplained bruising plus behavioural cues and Sophie’s statement warrant documenting clearly and contacting the safeguarding lead/duty service in line with NHS safeguarding responsibilities. This may be uncomfortable with Mrs Hassan, but professional duties override fear of complaints.

Deferring sedation and irreversible treatment without an interpreter (E) supports valid consent and sedation safety; proceeding when Mrs Hassan has limited English risks invalid consent and later dispute. 

A is unsafe: delaying safeguarding action and proceeding with sedation could expose Sophie to ongoing harm and risks an invalid consent process. D is unnecessarily aggressive and likely to inflame the situation. F misunderstands limits of competence and the role of supervision—experience does not replace safeguarding/consent requirements. G may be helpful operationally but is secondary to the core safeguarding/consent steps. H is punitive and may bias documentation rather than focusing on Sophie’s welfare.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns must be acted on promptly and documented objectively.
Children should be given opportunities to speak without a potentially influencing adult present when concerns arise.
Valid consent requires understanding; interpreter absence can make consent invalid, especially for sedation.
Service pressures and fear of complaints do not justify proceeding when safeguarding or consent is compromised.

scenario:
You are on OMFS ward cover as a DCT1. Mr Khan is post-op day 1 following incision and drainage of a facial swelling. Angela (ward nurse) asks you to re-prescribe his analgesia because the electronic order has expired, but you notice the last dose of morphine was given early and the pain score documentation is incomplete. At the same time, Lucy (dental nurse) phones from the oral surgery clinic: Dr O’Connor wants you to “quickly consent” Mrs Singh for a biopsy because clinic is overbooked, and the consultant is running late. You have not met Mrs Singh before and her notes include an unclear anticoagulant history. Mark (pharmacist) has also left a message querying a clindamycin prescription written overnight for Mr Khan because the indication is not stated.

Angela is visibly stressed and says the ward is short-staffed. Lucy sounds frustrated and implies you are “not being helpful”.

options.A: Go straight to oral surgery clinic to consent Mrs Singh as requested by Dr O’Connor, because delays in theatre lists have major knock-on effects.
options.B: Ask Angela to give Mr Khan non-opioid analgesia from the PRN protocol and tell Mark you will reply after clinic; then go to consent Mrs Singh immediately.
options.C: Review Mr Khan at the bedside to assess pain, check observations and sedation score, clarify the early morphine administration with Angela, and amend analgesia safely while documenting; then call Mark to clarify and update the clindamycin indication.
options.D: Call Lucy back to explain you cannot consent Mrs Singh without reviewing her history and discussing anticoagulants; offer to come after stabilising the ward issue or ask if Dr O’Connor/Dr Lewis can consent when available.
options.E: Write a new morphine prescription for Mr Khan based on yesterday’s chart to save time, and leave the missing documentation for Angela to complete later.

explanation:
C is most appropriate because it addresses immediate patient safety on the ward. Mr Khan has had early morphine with incomplete pain documentation; reassessing him, checking observations/sedation, and clarifying administration with Angela reduces risk of oversedation and supports safe prescribing. Updating the clindamycin indication with Mark also reflects good antimicrobial stewardship and record keeping.

D is next best: consenting Mrs Singh without reviewing her notes and anticoagulant history risks invalid consent and peri-procedural harm. Communicating clearly with Lucy, setting boundaries, and proposing alternatives (Dr O’Connor or Dr Lewis consenting, or you attending once safe) demonstrates teamwork while recognising limits of competence.

B is partially reasonable for workload management but unsafe if it defers pharmacist queries and relies on protocol without assessing Mr Khan first; PRN pathways do not replace clinical review when documentation and dosing are concerning.

A prioritises service flow over consent quality and anticoagulant safety, and could lead to harm and professional issues.

E is least appropriate: copying opioid prescribing without assessment and delegating documentation undermines clinical safety and professional accountability.
learningPoints: Prioritise immediate clinical risk (e.g., opioid safety) over convenience and list pressures.
Valid consent requires adequate information, assessment of risks (including anticoagulants), and appropriate senior involvement.
Communicate boundaries respectfully when colleagues apply pressure; propose safe alternatives.
Good prescribing includes clear indications and timely liaison with pharmacy.

scenario:
You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Khan is booked for an urgent extraction of a painful lower molar. The waiting room is full and reception (Nadia) is asking you to “keep things moving” because several patients are already late. Your dental nurse, Sarah, quietly tells you she saw “penicillin” written on a previous discharge letter for Mr Khan and he looked unsure when asked about allergies at check-in.

The notes are incomplete: the medical history page is unsigned and the electronic record is temporarily down. Mr Khan is anxious and says he “doesn’t want any needles or tablets that make him swell again,” but he cannot recall the drug name. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and not immediately contactable. Sarah looks concerned and asks whether you can still prescribe amoxicillin after the extraction “like we usually do.

options.A: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan to a later slot so you can catch up, and proceed to extract another patient’s tooth first.
options.B: Pause the appointment to take a focused allergy history with Mr Khan, document it clearly, and if uncertainty remains avoid prescribing beta‑lactams and plan an alternative analgesia/management strategy.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction immediately to relieve pain, and decide about antibiotics afterwards once the electronic record is back online.
options.D: Call the on-call switchboard to bleep Dr Kumar for urgent advice on antibiotics, keeping Mr Khan in the chair until you get a response.
options.E: Prescribe amoxicillin “just in case” because it is standard after extractions, and ask Sarah to add a note about possible allergy later.

explanation:
The safest and most appropriate action is to clarify and document the allergy history before prescribing or administering any medication (B). Mr Khan’s statement about swelling suggests a possible previous allergic reaction; NHS standards require medicines safety, accurate documentation, and respecting patient-centred concerns even under time pressure. If uncertainty remains, you should avoid beta‑lactams and manage pain with appropriate local measures and analgesia, reserving antibiotics for clear indications.

Seeking senior input is appropriate when risk is higher and records are unavailable; bleeping Dr Kumar via switchboard (D) demonstrates recognising limits and escalation. However, keeping Mr Khan “in the chair” could worsen anxiety, so this is second.

Rebooking via Nadia (A) may be reasonable to allow safe assessment, but prioritising another patient first risks leaving an anxious patient untreated and can feel dismissive; better to communicate expected delays and offer interim analgesia.

Proceeding with extraction then “deciding later” (C) fails to address consent and medicines planning and may increase risk if antibiotics are given reflexively afterwards.

Prescribing amoxicillin “just in case” and documenting later (E) is unsafe and unprofessional: prophylactic antibiotics are not routine, and potential allergy makes this the least appropriate.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides clinic flow pressures and targets.
A focused allergy history and contemporaneous documentation are essential before prescribing.
Escalate to a senior when uncertainty increases risk or records are unavailable.
Avoid routine or defensive antibiotic prescribing; prescribe only when clinically indicated.

scenario:
You are a DCT in a community special care dentistry clinic. Mrs Thompson, who has mild learning disability, attends with her sister, Olivia, for multiple restorations. The appointment is already running late because a previous patient required extra decontamination. The dental nurse, Emily, notes that Mrs Thompson keeps looking to Olivia before answering and has not brought her usual communication passport.

Halfway through the discussion, Olivia insists you “just do the fillings quickly” and asks you to avoid mentioning sedation because it “sets her off.” Mrs Thompson quietly says she is frightened of the drill and asks if she can “sleep through it.” Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in a meeting off-site and has previously advised you to consider inhalation sedation assessment for complex care.

At the same time, reception (Mark) messages that transport has arrived early and will leave in 20 minutes.

options.A: Ask Olivia to step out briefly (with Mrs Thompson’s agreement) so you can assess Mrs Thompson’s wishes and capacity without pressure, using simple language and checking understanding.
options.B: Proceed with local anaesthetic and start the restorations immediately to make sure transport is not missed, reassuring Mrs Thompson as you go.
options.C: Explain to both Mrs Thompson and Olivia the options (including sedation assessment), benefits/risks, and likely timelines, and document the discussion and Mrs Thompson’s preferences.
options.D: Tell Olivia firmly that family members are not allowed to contribute to consent discussions, and refuse to speak with her for the rest of the appointment.
options.E: Arrange a short “holding plan” for today (e.g., temporary dressing/fluoride advice if needed), and rebook for a dedicated sedation assessment if Mrs Thompson prefers this and it is clinically appropriate.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions prioritise valid consent, patient autonomy, and reasonable risk management. Separating undue influence is important: asking Olivia to step out briefly with Mrs Thompson’s agreement (A) allows you to assess Mrs Thompson’s wishes and capacity without coercion, while maintaining respect for family involvement. You must still communicate sensitively to avoid escalating conflict.

Providing a clear, accessible explanation of options—including sedation assessment—(C) supports informed consent and aligns with NHS duties around shared decision-making and documentation. It also addresses Emily’s concern about reliance on Olivia and the missing communication passport.

Given Mrs Thompson’s expressed fear and request to “sleep through it,” creating a proportionate holding plan and arranging an appropriate sedation assessment (E) balances patient-centred care with safety and realistic service constraints.

Proceeding rapidly to meet transport (B) risks invalid consent and distress. Excluding Olivia entirely (D) is disproportionate and may damage rapport; family can support communication when appropriately managed. Threatening discharge due to transport constraints (F) is punitive and unethical. Waiting for Dr Banerjee before discussing sedation (G) delays patient-centred discussion; you can discuss options within your competence. Recording Olivia as consenting on Mrs Thompson’s behalf (H) is unacceptable unless there is legal authority; it undermines autonomy and professional integrity.
learningPoints: Consent requires the patient’s understanding and voluntary decision-making; manage undue influence.
Use accessible communication and document capacity/consent discussions clearly.
Time and transport pressures should not drive unsafe or non-consensual care.
Create interim plans when definitive care is not appropriate on the day.

scenario:
You are a DCT on OMFS ward cover. A post-op patient, Mr Petrov, had a difficult extraction under GA yesterday and is in significant pain. The ward nurse, Rachel, asks you to rewrite the analgesia chart because the current prescription is unclear. When you review the drug chart, you notice Dr Lewis (registrar) has prescribed a high dose of an opioid with an unusually frequent dosing interval, and the renal function results from this morning are still pending. Mr Petrov’s partner, Mrs Hassan, is at the bedside and is angry that “no one is listening,” demanding stronger medication and threatening to complain.

At the same time, your dental nurse, Chloe, tells you she overheard two junior doctors joking about Mr Petrov being “drug-seeking” at the nurses’ station. Rachel looks uncomfortable and says the ward is short-staffed and needs a quick decision before the next medication round.

options.A: Rewrite the chart exactly as Dr Lewis prescribed to avoid delaying the medication round, and hand over concerns to the night team.
options.B: Review Mr Petrov’s observations, current analgesia use, and relevant history with Rachel, and if the opioid dosing seems unsafe pause rewriting and contact Dr Lewis to clarify and agree a safe plan.
options.C: Tell Mrs Hassan that complaints are pointless and she should let staff get on with their jobs, then leave to deal with other ward tasks.
options.D: Challenge the junior doctors immediately and publicly at the nurses’ station about the “drug-seeking” joke to make an example.
options.E: Document the prescribing concern and the overheard comments, support Chloe and Rachel, and raise the professionalism issue through an appropriate route (e.g., speaking to the individuals privately and/or informing the supervising consultant) after immediate patient safety is addressed.

explanation:
The most appropriate priority is patient safety. Working with Rachel to review observations and analgesia needs, and pausing to clarify the unusual opioid dosing with Dr Lewis before rewriting (B), demonstrates medicines governance, recognising limits of competence, and safe escalation. Rewriting an unclear or potentially unsafe prescription simply to meet the medication round would be risky, especially with pending renal function.

Once immediate safety is addressed, documenting concerns and dealing with unprofessional behaviour appropriately (E) is next best. Chloe’s report about “drug-seeking” jokes risks bias and undermines dignity. Addressing it via a proportionate, constructive route—often privately first and escalating if needed—aligns with NHS professionalism and maintaining effective teamwork.

Copying the prescription and handing over (A) may reduce short-term delay but fails to act on a credible safety concern you have identified.

Publicly confronting the junior doctors (D) may escalate conflict and disrupt the ward; it is better handled professionally and, where possible, in private unless there is immediate risk.

Dismissive communication to Mrs Hassan (C) is least appropriate: it inflames the situation, damages trust, and fails to provide patient-centred explanations or a plan for pain control.
learningPoints: Do not transcribe or rewrite prescriptions you believe may be unsafe; clarify and escalate.
Prioritise immediate clinical safety before addressing secondary issues.
Unprofessional comments can bias care; address concerns proportionately and document where appropriate.
De-escalate angry relatives with clear explanations and a plan, maintaining dignity and respect.

scenario:
You are a DCT in a busy NHS oral surgery clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan is booked for an upper molar extraction today, but his referral letter lists “penicillin allergy – anaphylaxis” while the electronic hospital record shows “no known drug allergies.” Mr Khan looks anxious and says his GP once told him to “avoid antibiotics,” but he cannot recall the name. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is running late and asks you to “just start the consent and get him prepped” to avoid delaying the waiting room.

Meanwhile, Nadia (receptionist) tells you two emergency patients have arrived and the consultant, Dr Ahmed, is in theatre and not contactable for 30 minutes. Mr Khan says he wants to “get it done today” because he has taken unpaid leave, but he also asks whether he will need antibiotics afterward.

options.A: Proceed with consent and local anaesthetic as planned, and if antibiotics are required later choose an alternative to penicillin based on your judgement without further checks.
options.B: Pause preparation and ask Sarah to contact Mr Khan’s GP and the hospital pharmacy for clarification of the reported anaphylaxis, while you explain to Mr Khan that safety checks may delay treatment.
options.C: Tell Nadia to rebook Mr Khan to a later date immediately so the list stays on time, and document that allergy information was conflicting.
options.D: Begin the consent discussion focusing on the extraction risks, but explicitly state you will not prescribe or administer antibiotics until Dr Kumar has reviewed the allergy discrepancy and agreed a plan.
options.E: Ignore the referral letter because the electronic record is more reliable, and reassure Mr Khan that antibiotics are routine so he should not worry.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety when allergy information conflicts. Option D is most appropriate: you can start a proportionate consent discussion for the extraction, but you clearly set a boundary that prescribing/administration decisions (particularly antibiotics) will wait for senior review by Dr Kumar. This manages time pressure without compromising safety and respects limits of competence.

Option B is also appropriate: asking Sarah to contact the GP/pharmacy helps reconcile records and supports safe prescribing, while keeping Mr Khan informed. It may cause delay, but transparency maintains trust and aligns with NHS duty of candour.

Option C is less appropriate because immediate rebooking prioritises flow over Mr Khan’s needs and may be premature before exploring options (e.g., proceeding with extraction without antibiotics). However, documenting the discrepancy is sensible.

Option A is risky: choosing an alternative antibiotic “based on your judgement” without clarifying the allergy or senior agreement increases harm risk and exceeds a DCT’s remit under pressure.

Option E is least appropriate: dismissing the referral and normalising antibiotics undermines informed consent and could lead to severe adverse reactions. It also disregards Sarah’s concern and professional standards on medicines safety.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy histories must be resolved before prescribing or administering medicines.
You can balance efficiency with safety by progressing appropriate steps (e.g., consent) while deferring high-risk decisions to seniors.
Team members’ safety concerns (e.g., from the dental nurse) should be taken seriously and acted upon.
Be transparent with patients about delays caused by safety checks and document discrepancies clearly.

scenario:
You are covering a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Daniel (aged 22), who has a learning disability and usually communicates with short phrases. Emily (dental nurse) tells you Daniel appeared withdrawn in the waiting room and flinched when his son touched his shoulder. The appointment is for urgent caries-related pain. Mrs Thompson insists on staying in the surgery and answers most questions for him, saying, “He doesn’t understand, just do what you need.”

During a brief moment when Mrs Thompson steps out to take a phone call, Daniel quietly says, “Don’t tell Mum… I don’t like when she’s angry,” and points to fading bruises on his upper arm. He then asks for “the tooth to stop hurting” and says he wants to go home quickly. The interpreter service is not available today.

options.A: Proceed with urgent treatment immediately with Mrs Thompson present, as Daniel has requested pain relief and delays will worsen his distress.
options.B: Ask Mrs Thompson to remain in the waiting area for part of the consultation so you can assess Daniel’s wishes and capacity privately, explaining this is routine practice.
options.C: Document Daniel’s disclosure verbatim, assess immediate risk, and contact the safeguarding lead for advice the same day, updating Dr O’Connor by phone.
options.D: Tell Emily to quietly take photographs of the bruising on her phone for evidence in case Mrs Thompson denies it later.
options.E: Confront Mrs Thompson directly about the bruises in front of Daniel to gauge her reaction and deter further harm.

explanation:
This scenario has dual priorities: managing Daniel’s pain and responding to a potential safeguarding concern while respecting autonomy and confidentiality. The three best actions are B, C and F.

B creates a safer space to assess Daniel’s wishes, capacity and any coercion; it also reduces undue influence from Mrs Thompson and is consistent with person-centred care. F ensures valid consent by adapting communication to Daniel’s needs, rather than accepting Mrs Thompson’s blanket permission. This supports legal/ethical requirements that Daniel’s consent is central if he has capacity.

C is essential: Daniel’s disclosure and bruising raise safeguarding concerns. Recording his words verbatim, assessing immediate risk, and contacting the safeguarding lead the same day aligns with NHS safeguarding duties and professional integrity. Informing Dr O’Connor provides senior oversight.

A risks proceeding without proper consent and may miss abuse indicators. D is inappropriate and breaches confidentiality and information governance; photos should only be taken under local policy with consent and secure storage. E may escalate risk and intimidate Daniel. G and H inappropriately delay safeguarding action and prioritise time pressures over safety.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns require timely escalation and clear documentation, even when evidence is uncertain.
Valid consent depends on the patient’s understanding; adapt communication for learning disability and assess capacity.
Create opportunities to speak with vulnerable patients privately to reduce coercion and support autonomy.
Maintain confidentiality and information governance—avoid informal evidence gathering (e.g., personal phones).

scenario:
You are the DCT on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) calls you to review Mrs Singh, who had a surgical extraction under GA yesterday and is due for discharge. Mrs Singh reports increasing swelling and says her mouth tastes “foul.” Her temperature is 38.1°C. The drug chart shows IV co‑amoxiclav was started overnight, but the indication box is blank and there is no documented allergy status. Lucy (dental nurse) finds in the pre-assessment paperwork that Mrs Singh previously reported a “rash with amoxicillin,” but the anaesthetic note states “NKDA.”

Dr Grant (registrar) is in clinic and asks you to “sort it out” because bed management is pressuring for discharge before noon. Mark (pharmacist) phones to query the antibiotic choice and incomplete documentation, saying he cannot supply the next dose without clarification.

options.A: Ask Mark (pharmacist) to withhold further antibiotics until Dr Grant is free to review, and focus on completing discharge paperwork to meet the noon target.
options.B: Reassess Mrs Singh clinically, review the notes with Rachel and Lucy, and escalate promptly to Dr Grant with a clear SBAR summary including the allergy discrepancy and current sepsis markers.
options.C: Tell Mrs Singh’s daughter that complaints should go through PALS and ask Rachel to remove her from the bay so you can work without interruption.
options.D: Update the drug chart yourself by writing an indication for co‑amoxiclav and confirming “no allergy” based on the anaesthetic note, so Mark can release the next dose.
options.E: Explain to Mrs Singh and her daughter that you are concerned about possible infection and unclear allergy status, apologise for the confusion, and outline the immediate plan while you seek senior input.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it prioritises clinical safety: Mrs Singh has red flags (fever, swelling, malodour) and conflicting allergy documentation. A prompt reassessment and structured escalation to Dr Grant using SBAR supports timely decision-making and shared situational awareness with Rachel and Lucy.

Option E is next: communicating openly with Mrs Singh and her daughter, acknowledging uncertainty and explaining the plan demonstrates professionalism and patient-centred care. It can also de-escalate the anger and supports the duty of candour.

Option A is less appropriate but defensible: Mark is right to query supply without documentation. However, simply withholding antibiotics and focusing on discharge risks harm unless paired with urgent clinical review and alternative prescribing. Discharge targets must not override safety.

Option C is generally unhelpful: while setting boundaries is sometimes necessary, asking to remove the daughter without first addressing concerns may escalate conflict and undermine trust.

Option D is least appropriate: you must not “confirm” no allergy based on a single note when Lucy has contrary pre-assessment information. Altering documentation to facilitate supply without proper verification breaches medicines governance and could cause serious adverse reactions.
learningPoints: Clinical deterioration and possible infection require prompt reassessment and escalation, regardless of discharge pressures.
Conflicting allergy documentation must be reconciled before prescribing/continuing antibiotics.
Use structured communication (e.g., SBAR) with seniors and the multidisciplinary team to support safe decisions.
Open, honest communication with patients/relatives can de-escalate conflict and uphold professionalism.

scenario:
You are a DCT1 in an oral surgery urgent clinic. Dr Kumar (specialty registrar) is in theatre and has asked you to keep the list moving. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Clarke is booked for an upper molar extraction but his notes are inconsistent: the medical history summary says “warfarin for AF,” while the scanned referral letter mentions “apixaban started last month.” Mr Clarke is annoyed about waiting and says he “just wants it out today” because he has a job interview tomorrow. Receptionist Olivia says two more emergencies have arrived and the consultant, Dr Ahmed, is tied up in a cancer MDT.

Mr Clarke cannot recall the name of his anticoagulant and becomes defensive when you ask further questions. You can see bruising on his forearms. The last recorded INR is 2.8 from three weeks ago, but it is unclear if he is still on warfarin.

options.A: Ask Olivia to rebook Mr Clarke to the next available slot without further assessment so the clinic can run on time.
options.B: Explain to Mr Clarke that you cannot proceed safely until his anticoagulant is confirmed, and ask Sarah to help you phone his GP/pharmacy urgently while you assess local measures and discuss options.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction as planned using local haemostatic measures, documenting that Mr Clarke was unsure of his medication and that the clinic was busy.
options.D: Interrupt Dr Kumar in theatre via bleep to confirm a plan and whether Dr Ahmed needs to be involved, explaining the conflicting anticoagulant information.
options.E: Ask Mr Clarke to sign the consent form immediately so you can start, then try to clarify the medication while setting up the room.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is B: patient safety overrides time pressure, and you need accurate anticoagulant information before an extraction. Involving Sarah to contact the GP/pharmacy is practical and uses the team effectively, while you keep Mr Clarke informed and explore safer alternatives (e.g., deferral, temporary measures, or senior review). D is next best because escalating to Dr Kumar is justified when there is a significant bleeding risk and unclear documentation; seeking supervision is consistent with recognising limits of competence and ensuring safe care, even if it disrupts theatre briefly.

C is unsafe: proceeding despite uncertainty about warfarin versus apixaban risks significant haemorrhage and does not meet NHS expectations for safe prescribing/operative decision-making. Documentation does not legitimise an unsafe action. E undermines informed consent: asking Mr Clarke to sign before he understands the risks related to anticoagulation is poor practice and can feel coercive. A is least appropriate: rebooking without assessment or explanation disregards Mr Clarke’s concerns, fails to manage clinical risk, and may escalate dissatisfaction without addressing the underlying safety issue.
learningPoints: Patient safety takes priority over clinic flow when key medical information is unclear.
Valid consent requires that material risks are explained based on accurate clinical details.
Use the wider team (e.g., nurse, GP/pharmacy) to verify critical medication histories promptly.
Escalate early when uncertainty creates significant risk; recognise limits of competence.

scenario:
You are on-call for OMFS trauma. Daniel, a 15-year-old, attends with his aunt after an assault. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in a delayed theatre case; Dr Fernandes (registrar) is covering two areas. Rachel (ED nurse) tells you Daniel has disclosed he “doesn’t want the police involved” and keeps checking his phone. His aunt insists on staying in the cubicle and answers questions for him. Daniel has a fractured incisor, lip lacerations and appears frightened but medically stable.

When you try to take a history, Daniel becomes withdrawn. He quietly says he can’t go home tonight and asks you not to tell his aunt. The aunt becomes angry, saying, “We don’t do social services—just fix his teeth.” The ED department is crowded, and Rachel warns there is pressure to move patients through.

options.A: Ask Rachel to help you find a quiet space and speak to Daniel alone, explaining to the aunt that you routinely speak to young people privately for part of the assessment.
options.B: Reassure the aunt that you will not involve any other services and focus only on dental treatment to keep the interaction calm.
options.C: Document Daniel’s disclosure verbatim and contact the hospital safeguarding team for advice, informing Dr Fernandes of the concern as soon as possible.
options.D: Call the police immediately without discussing anything further with Daniel or the ED team, as an assault automatically requires police involvement.
options.E: Proceed with suturing and splinting first, and postpone any safeguarding discussion to the outpatient review because the ED is busy.

explanation:
A, C and F best balance patient-centred care, safeguarding, and clinical priorities. A is appropriate because adolescents should routinely be offered time alone, improving disclosure and supporting autonomy, while managing the aunt’s behaviour professionally. F is essential to maintain trust: Daniel needs a clear explanation that confidentiality is not absolute if there is risk of harm, consistent with NHS safeguarding principles and informed consent.

C is appropriate because Daniel’s statement that he can’t go home and does not want his aunt told raises safeguarding concerns. Documenting his words accurately and contacting the safeguarding team provides timely specialist support; informing Dr Fernandes ensures senior awareness and shared decision-making within your limits as a DCT.

B is inappropriate because it promises secrecy and may prevent necessary safeguarding. D may be disproportionate and can breach confidentiality; police involvement may be needed but should follow safeguarding pathways and senior input unless immediate danger requires emergency action. E delays safeguarding without justification. G focuses on throughput over welfare and may invalidate consent if Daniel’s wishes and capacity are not considered. H is unsafe and breaches professional boundaries and data governance.
learningPoints: Offer young people the opportunity to speak without accompanying adults as part of routine assessment.
Explain confidentiality and its limits clearly and age-appropriately.
Escalate safeguarding concerns promptly via local pathways and involve seniors.
Do not make promises that prevent necessary information-sharing to protect a patient.

scenario:
You are a DCT1 in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson, who has moderate learning disability and anxiety, is attending for multiple restorations. Laura (dental therapist) has already started treatment under a shared care plan. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you that Laura looks stressed and has quietly said she may have placed a temporary dressing on the wrong tooth because Mrs Thompson kept moving and the tooth notation on the chart was smudged.

At the same time, receptionist Mark says the transport driver is refusing to wait beyond 10 minutes, and Mrs Thompson’s support worker is asking to leave early for another appointment. Mrs Thompson is now upset and says she doesn’t want “any more drilling.” Laura asks you not to mention her worry to the patient because it will “make things worse” and she fears being blamed.

options.A: Ask Chloe to escort Mrs Thompson and the support worker to a quiet waiting area, then speak with Laura to clarify exactly what was done and check the tooth against notes and any available radiographs.
options.B: Tell Laura to correct the record later when the clinic is less busy, and continue with the next planned restoration to avoid losing transport.
options.C: Explain to Laura that you must prioritise patient safety, and agree a plan for prompt review and transparent communication with Mrs Thompson and the support worker if an error is confirmed.
options.D: Immediately tell Mrs Thompson that Laura treated the wrong tooth, apologise, and offer to stop treatment, without first confirming the facts.
options.E: Ask Mark to persuade the driver to wait and, if that fails, arrange alternative transport while you pause treatment to resolve the clinical uncertainty safely.

explanation:
A is most appropriate because it creates space to establish the facts promptly and calmly, using Chloe to support Mrs Thompson while you and Laura check tooth notation and what was placed. This manages the interpersonal dynamic (Laura’s anxiety and fear of blame) while focusing on clinical safety. C follows: you set expectations about duty of candour and accurate records, supporting Laura while making clear that patient welfare comes first. Planning how to communicate with Mrs Thompson and her support worker if an error is confirmed aligns with professional integrity and patient-centred care.

E is appropriate because transport pressure should not drive unsafe decisions; involving Mark to negotiate and arranging alternatives helps maintain service flow without compromising safety. D is less appropriate than E because immediate disclosure without confirming details risks unnecessary distress and may spread misinformation; however, if an error is confirmed, prompt honest communication is required. B is least appropriate: delaying correction and continuing treatment with unresolved uncertainty risks harm, undermines documentation standards, and places Laura and the service at greater professional risk.
learningPoints: Resolve clinical uncertainty immediately before continuing treatment, even under time pressure.
Support colleagues to speak up, while maintaining duty of candour and accurate documentation.
Service pressures (e.g., transport) must not override patient safety.
Plan communication to minimise distress while remaining honest and transparent.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. The waiting room is full and reception has already warned about delays. Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you that Mr Khan, booked for an urgent extraction of LL6, mentioned “a bad reaction” to a local anaesthetic years ago but it isn’t in his notes. Mr Khan looks anxious and asks you to “just get it done quickly” because he has to pick up his child. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and not immediately available. Nadia (receptionist) is pushing you to keep to time because a patient has threatened to complain.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using the planned lidocaine with adrenaline, documenting that Mr Khan reported an unclear past reaction and that you proceeded due to clinical urgency.
options.B: Ask Mr Khan to describe the previous reaction in detail (timing, symptoms, treatment) and check any available hospital records; if uncertainty remains, defer extraction and discuss an alternative plan and safety-netting with him.
options.C: Tell Nadia to rebook Mr Khan for another day and ask her not to mention the allergy concern to avoid escalating his anxiety in the waiting room.
options.D: Ask Sarah to contact Dr Kumar between theatre cases for immediate advice while you begin preparing the room and obtain consent for extraction as usual.
options.E: Offer to prescribe antibiotics and strong analgesia instead, explaining this will “settle things” until Dr Kumar is free to extract later.

explanation:
The most appropriate first step is to clarify risk and ensure patient safety. Option B prioritises a focused allergy/adverse reaction history (often vasovagal, intravascular injection, or adrenaline-related palpitations rather than true allergy) and checks records before exposing Mr Khan to potential harm. It also supports informed consent by ensuring he understands why there may be delay and provides safety-netting. Option D is appropriate next: involving Dr Kumar promptly respects your limits of competence and uses the team effectively, while preparation can continue without committing to an unsafe intervention.

Option C is weaker because instructing Nadia not to mention the concern risks undermining transparency and informed consent; it also dismisses Sarah’s valid safety concern and damages teamwork. Option E is inappropriate: antibiotics are not indicated for pain alone and using them to “buy time” conflicts with antimicrobial stewardship and patient-centred care; it may also delay definitive treatment. Option A is least appropriate because proceeding with an extraction and local anaesthetic despite unresolved allergy uncertainty exposes Mr Khan to avoidable risk and falls short of NHS expectations around patient safety and documentation integrity.
learningPoints: Take and document a structured history when an adverse drug reaction is reported, and distinguish true allergy from side effects/anxiety.
Patient safety and valid consent override time pressure and complaint risk.
Escalate appropriately and recognise limits of competence when uncertainty could lead to harm.
Support team members who raise safety concerns and communicate transparently with patients and admin staff.

scenario: You are on an OMFS ward review round. Mrs Thompson, 78, is admitted with a spreading odontogenic infection and mild confusion. Her daughter is at the bedside and is insistent that you “don’t mention cancer or operations” because it will upset her mother. Angela (ward nurse) tells you Mrs Thompson has repeatedly pulled off her oxygen and is trying to leave the ward. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in clinic and has asked you to consent Mrs Thompson for incision and drainage under GA today if possible, as theatres are limited. When you speak to Mrs Thompson, she asks you directly, “Am I going to be put to sleep? I don’t want anyone deciding without me.” The daughter becomes angry and says she will complain if you talk to her mother about risks.

options.A: Ask Angela to arrange a quiet space and carry out a focused capacity assessment for the specific decision about GA and surgery, using clear language and checking understanding.
options.B: Agree with the daughter to avoid discussing GA and document that the family requested non-disclosure to prevent distress.
options.C: Telephone Dr O’Connor to update him on the daughter’s objections and Mrs Thompson’s questions, and seek guidance on timing and consent approach.
options.D: Proceed to complete the consent form with the daughter acting as next of kin, as the infection is urgent and theatre time will be lost.
options.E: Ask the daughter to step outside briefly so you can speak to Mrs Thompson alone, clarifying what information she wants and whether she consents to her daughter being involved.

explanation:
Options A, E and F best align with UK consent law and NHS professional standards. A is essential because confusion does not equal incapacity; you must assess Mrs Thompson’s capacity for this specific decision (GA and incision/drainage) and support her understanding. E manages the interpersonal dynamic: speaking with Mrs Thompson alone can reduce undue influence and allows you to clarify her preferences about information sharing and her daughter’s involvement, supporting autonomy and confidentiality.

If Mrs Thompson lacks capacity, F is the correct pathway: a documented best-interests decision involving relevant clinicians (including Dr O’Connor) and ward staff such as Angela, and considering urgency and least restrictive alternatives. This maintains patient safety given the time-critical infection.

B and D are inappropriate: relatives cannot demand non-disclosure when the patient is asking directly, and “next of kin” cannot provide consent for an adult with capacity; even without capacity, decisions must be best interests, not proxy consent. C is helpful but not among the top three compared with completing capacity/undue influence steps first; it can follow. G escalates prematurely and risks harming relationships. H risks avoidable clinical deterioration and is not proportionate given urgency.
learningPoints: Capacity is decision-specific and must be assessed and documented when there is uncertainty.
Valid consent requires the patient to receive information they want/need and be free from undue influence.
Relatives cannot consent on behalf of a competent adult; if capacity is lacking, use a best-interests process.
Time-critical infection management still requires lawful, patient-centred decision making.

scenario: You are a DCT in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Hassan, who has learning disabilities and uses a support worker, is booked for multiple restorations. The morning has been overbooked due to staff sickness, and Chloe (dental nurse) tells you the appointment book has been changed several times. When you open the record, the last clinician’s note is brief and the consent section is incomplete. Olivia (receptionist) says the support worker is threatening to leave if they are not seen within 10 minutes because transport is prepaid. Dr Chen (consultant in special care) is off-site at a safeguarding meeting and only reachable intermittently. Mrs Hassan appears calm but avoids eye contact, and the support worker answers questions for her. Chloe looks uncomfortable and says quietly, “I’m not sure she understands what we’re doing today.

options.A: Proceed with the planned restorations, relying on the support worker’s agreement as implied consent because Mrs Hassan attended the appointment.
options.B: Pause the clinical work and use accessible communication (simple language, visual aids) to confirm Mrs Hassan’s understanding and consent for today’s treatment, documenting capacity and consent clearly before starting.
options.C: Ask Olivia to tell the support worker that delays are their problem and that transport arrangements are not the clinic’s responsibility.
options.D: Ask Chloe to explain to the support worker why you need extra time for consent and to explore whether transport can be adjusted, while you focus on Mrs Hassan directly.
options.E: Limit treatment to a non-invasive check-up and temporary measures only, and rebook all restorations for a day when Dr Chen is present, regardless of Mrs Hassan’s wishes.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it addresses the core safety and ethical issue: ensuring Mrs Hassan can understand and consent to restorations. Under NHS standards, valid consent requires appropriate communication and documentation, especially with patients who may need reasonable adjustments. Chloe’s concern that Mrs Hassan may not understand must be taken seriously.

Option D supports teamwork and manages the interpersonal dynamic with the support worker: Chloe can explain the need for extra time and negotiate practicalities (e.g., transport flexibility) while you maintain patient-centred communication with Mrs Hassan, reducing the risk of the support worker dominating the conversation.

Option E may be necessary if, after support, consent cannot be obtained or capacity is unclear and Dr Chen’s input is needed; limiting to non-invasive or temporary care protects safety. However, an automatic rebook “regardless of wishes” is why it sits below D.

Option C is unprofessional and likely to escalate conflict, undermining access and rapport. Option A is least appropriate: implied consent via attendance and reliance on a support worker’s agreement risks invalid consent and breaches professional integrity and patient autonomy.
learningPoints: Take team concerns about consent seriously and prioritise patient understanding over time pressure.
Provide reasonable adjustments and communicate directly with the patient, not only the support worker.
Document capacity and consent clearly; do not rely on implied consent for invasive treatment.
Use the wider team to manage logistics and conflict while maintaining patient-centred care.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan is booked for extraction of LL6. The electronic record shows “penicillin allergy – rash,” but the triage note from yesterday (written by Dr Kumar, registrar) states “no known drug allergies.” Mr Khan is in pain and keeps asking to “just get it out quickly” because he needs to pick up his child. The waiting room is full and Nadia (receptionist) says three patients are already complaining about delays. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in theatre and not answering bleeps. Sarah is worried because the LA chart template auto-populates an amoxicillin prescription for “post-op cover,” and she has seen it printed. Mr Khan is irritated by repeated questions and says he “might have had a reaction years ago but can’t remember.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using local anaesthetic, avoid prescribing amoxicillin, and document that allergy status is unclear based on Mr Khan’s uncertainty.
options.B: Ask Sarah to hold the printed prescription, take a focused allergy history with Mr Khan (including reaction, timing and severity), and check the GP summary on the shared care record; if still unclear, avoid penicillin and document clearly before treatment.
options.C: Tell Nadia to rebook Mr Khan because allergy documentation conflicts and you cannot proceed safely without Dr O’Connor’s input.
options.D: Phone Dr Kumar to clarify what was discussed at triage and whether he updated the allergy status, then decide whether to proceed based on his recollection.
options.E: Continue as planned and issue the auto-populated amoxicillin prescription because the triage note says no allergies, to keep the clinic moving.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it prioritises patient safety while keeping care moving: you respond to Sarah’s concern, obtain a specific allergy history directly from Mr Khan, and use available systems (shared care record/GP summary) to reconcile conflicting documentation. You also prevent an unsafe “default” prescription and ensure clear documentation for continuity—aligned with NHS duties to prescribe safely and to make sure the record is accurate.

Option D is helpful as a secondary step: contacting Dr Kumar may clarify why the triage note conflicts, but you should not rely solely on a colleague’s memory instead of confirming with the patient and records.

Option A is less appropriate because proceeding while the allergy remains unresolved leaves avoidable risk, particularly around prescribing and future encounters; documenting uncertainty alone does not mitigate harm.

Option C is overly defensive: rebooking due to a manageable documentation discrepancy delays urgent pain relief and does not use proportionate risk management.

Option E is least appropriate: issuing amoxicillin for “clinic flow” ignores uncertainty and could cause harm, breaching professional integrity and safe prescribing standards.
learningPoints: Take concerns raised by dental nurses seriously and act promptly.
Reconcile conflicting documentation using patient history and available records before prescribing.
Avoid ‘default’ or routine antibiotics; prescribe only when clinically indicated and safe.
Clear contemporaneous documentation supports continuity and patient safety.

scenario: You are on a restorative clinic in a community dental service seeing Mrs Thompson, who has learning disability and attends with her support worker, Mark. She needs a crown re-cemented and is visibly anxious. Mark answers most questions for her and says, “Just do whatever you did last time; she doesn’t understand this stuff.” Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you that Mrs Thompson whispered in the waiting room that she does not want Mark in the surgery, but looked frightened when he approached. The appointment is already running late, and Dr Chen (consultant) is off-site at an outreach meeting. When you try to speak directly to Mrs Thompson, Mark becomes defensive and says you are “making it a big deal” and threatens to complain that you are refusing treatment. Mrs Thompson avoids eye contact and nods at everything.

options.A: Ask Mark to step outside for part of the consultation, explaining this is routine, and use simple language and visual aids to check Mrs Thompson’s understanding and preferences about treatment and who she wants present.
options.B: Proceed with re-cementation quickly while Mark remains present, because it is low risk and Mrs Thompson is nodding, to avoid escalating the situation.
options.C: Document that Mark is the decision-maker because he is the support worker, and obtain his consent for the procedure.
options.D: Pause non-urgent treatment and contact your safeguarding lead for advice, given Mrs Thompson’s request for privacy and her frightened behaviour; agree a plan with Emily about immediate safety.
options.E: Tell Mark you suspect abuse and that you will be reporting him unless he leaves immediately.

explanation:
A is appropriate because it supports valid consent and patient-centred care: asking Mark to step out (framed as routine) allows Mrs Thompson to speak freely, and using simple language/visual aids helps you assess understanding and preferences. This aligns with NHS principles of involving the patient as far as possible and respecting autonomy.

D is also appropriate: Emily’s information and Mrs Thompson’s frightened behaviour raise a potential safeguarding concern and possible coercion. Seeking advice from the safeguarding lead is proportionate and supports immediate safety planning without making accusatory statements.

F is appropriate because time pressure risks poor consent. Arranging more time and considering an independent advocate reduces reliance on Mark and supports a less coercive environment.

B is inappropriate because nodding in the presence of a potentially controlling companion is not reliable consent, even for “low risk” care. C is wrong: a support worker cannot automatically consent; you must assess capacity and follow best-interests processes if needed. E escalates and could increase risk to Mrs Thompson. G breaches professionalism by ignoring a concern raised by Emily. H delays care unnecessarily when you can take safeguarding/consent steps now and escalate appropriately.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s understanding and freedom from undue influence.
Support workers do not automatically have authority to consent; assess capacity and use best-interests processes when required.
Respond to safeguarding indicators proportionately and seek advice through local pathways.
Time pressure is not a reason to compromise consent, dignity, or safety.

scenario: You are a DCT attached to the OMFS ward. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, post-op day 1 after incision and drainage for a facial swelling. He is requesting to go home and is angry about being kept nil by mouth for a possible return to theatre. While you are assessing him, Lucy (dental nurse) shows you a WhatsApp message in the staff group where Dr Grant (specialty dentist) has posted a photo of Mr Petrov’s swollen face with the caption: “Classic ‘ignored it for a week’—nightmare drain.” The photo includes part of the patient’s wristband. Lucy looks uncomfortable but says, “Everyone does it for teaching.” At the same time, Mr Petrov’s pain score is rising and Rachel is chasing you for a plan because the ward round with Dr Ahmed (consultant) starts in 10 minutes.

options.A: Focus solely on Mr Petrov’s clinical needs now; make a note to speak to Dr Grant sometime next week about the WhatsApp photo when the ward is quieter.
options.B: Ask Lucy to delete the message from her phone and not discuss it further, then continue with Mr Petrov’s assessment.
options.C: Continue the assessment and pain plan with Rachel, but immediately message Dr Grant to remove the photo from the group and remind him that identifiable patient images must not be shared on WhatsApp; then document the concern and escalate via the appropriate senior (Dr Ahmed) or information governance route.
options.D: Confront Dr Grant in the corridor in front of the team, accuse him of breaching confidentiality, and demand he apologises to Lucy and the ward staff.
options.E: Tell Rachel you cannot see Mr Petrov until the confidentiality issue is resolved, and ask her to manage his pain meanwhile.

explanation:
C is most appropriate because it balances competing priorities: you continue addressing Mr Petrov’s deteriorating pain with Rachel (patient safety), while taking prompt action to limit harm from a likely confidentiality breach. Asking Dr Grant to remove the image immediately and reminding him about appropriate channels reflects professional integrity and protects patient trust. Documenting and escalating to Dr Ahmed and/or information governance follows NHS expectations for raising concerns.

B is partially helpful—reducing onward sharing by asking Lucy to delete it—but it is insufficient on its own and risks implying secrecy rather than proper reporting.

A is less appropriate because delaying action prolongs the risk of further dissemination of an identifiable image (wristband visible), even though clinical care is urgent.

D is inappropriate as it escalates conflict, risks humiliation, and may reduce openness to learning; concerns should be raised respectfully and through appropriate processes.

E is least appropriate because it inappropriately withholds timely clinical review of Mr Petrov; confidentiality concerns should not prevent essential care, especially when the patient’s pain is worsening and a theatre decision is pending.
learningPoints: Maintain patient safety while addressing professionalism concerns in parallel where possible.
Do not share identifiable patient information on informal messaging platforms; act quickly to limit harm.
Raise concerns respectfully and escalate via appropriate senior/information governance pathways.
Avoid conflict escalation that undermines teamwork and a culture of openness.

scenario:
You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Dr Kumar (registrar) is delayed in theatre, and you are covering triage with dental nurse Sarah. Mr Petrov attends with facial swelling and trismus. He says he is “allergic to penicillin” but cannot describe the reaction; the ED discharge summary in the electronic record lists “rash ?amoxicillin” from years ago. Receptionist Nadia is pressuring you because the waiting room is full and Mrs Thompson has already complained about delays. Sarah quietly adds that Mr Petrov smells of alcohol and seems confused, and she is worried about his capacity to consent for incision and drainage.

Mr Petrov insists on “strong antibiotics only” and refuses hospital admission because he needs to pick up his child.

options.A: Ask Sarah to keep Mr Petrov in a side room, take a focused history about the alleged penicillin reaction and current symptoms, check observations, and page Dr Kumar/consultant on-call to discuss management before prescribing or attempting drainage.
options.B: Prescribe clindamycin immediately for Mr Petrov, document “penicillin allergy,” and ask Nadia to book him into an urgent review slot tomorrow to avoid delaying other patients.
options.C: Explain to Mr Petrov that swelling with trismus may require admission and IV antibiotics, assess his capacity given possible intoxication, and if capacity is lacking involve ED/medical team for urgent assessment and safeguarding/next-of-kin as appropriate.
options.D: Proceed with local anaesthetic incision and drainage now because it is time-critical, using implied consent given the risk of deterioration, and ask Sarah to prepare the instruments.
options.E: Ask Nadia to tell the waiting room there will be further delays due to a potentially serious infection, and request a translator line because Mr Petrov’s English is limited, before making any treatment decision.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety and valid consent. Mr Petrov has red flags (facial swelling, trismus) and Sarah raises concern about confusion/alcohol, so capacity must be assessed before any invasive treatment. Option C is most appropriate: explain admission need, formally assess capacity, and escalate to ED/medical team if capacity is impaired. This balances respect for autonomy with duty of care and sepsis risk.

A is next: moving him to a side room, taking a structured allergy history, checking observations, and contacting Dr Kumar/consultant ensures safe prescribing and recognises limits of competence when senior support is unavailable.

E is helpful but secondary: managing clinic communication and addressing language barriers supports informed consent and reduces conflict with Nadia/the waiting room, but should not delay urgent clinical assessment.

B is less appropriate: clindamycin “to keep things moving” risks inappropriate antibiotic use and misses the need for potential admission and airway/sepsis assessment; documenting an uncertain allergy without clarification is poor practice.

D is least appropriate: implied consent is not justified when the patient is conscious and potentially lacks capacity; proceeding without proper consent and senior input risks harm and professional criticism.
learningPoints: Assess and document capacity when intoxication/confusion is suspected before treatment.
Red-flag odontogenic infections require urgent escalation; patient safety outweighs clinic flow.
Clarify and document drug allergy history before prescribing whenever possible.
Recognise limits of competence and seek senior/medical support when required.

scenario:
You are on a restorative clinic seeing Mrs Hassan for assessment before complex crown and bridge work. Dr Banerjee (consultant) has asked you to obtain updated consent and ensure all diagnostics are complete. Mrs Hassan arrives with her adult son, Mr Clarke, who answers most questions and repeatedly says, “Mum doesn’t need all the details—just do what you did last time.” Dental therapist Laura has already taken updated photographs but notes the old consent form in the record is unsigned and the medical history is from 18 months ago.

Mrs Hassan speaks limited English and seems anxious. The booked telephone interpreter has not connected. Mr Clarke becomes impatient and tells receptionist Olivia he will “take this further” if the appointment is wasted. Dr Banerjee is running late in another clinic.

options.A: Ask Olivia to rebook Mrs Hassan for a longer appointment with face-to-face interpretation, and send her home today so the clinic stays on time.
options.B: Explain to Mr Clarke that you need to speak directly with Mrs Hassan, ask him to wait outside for part of the appointment, and check with Mrs Hassan if she wants him involved in discussions.
options.C: Proceed with the consent discussion using Mr Clarke as interpreter, because he knows her history and it avoids wasting the slot.
options.D: Update Mrs Hassan’s medical history yourself using closed questions and ask her to sign the old consent form so the consultant can start treatment later.
options.E: Ask Laura to document her concerns about the unsigned consent and language barrier in the notes, and brief Dr Banerjee as soon as he becomes available.

explanation:
Valid consent requires that Mrs Hassan understands the information, has capacity, and is free from undue influence. With limited English and an interpreter failing to connect, proceeding using Mr Clarke as interpreter (C) risks inaccurate translation, breached confidentiality, and coercion. The most appropriate actions are to protect patient autonomy while keeping the pathway moving safely.

B is essential: you set boundaries with Mr Clarke, speak directly with Mrs Hassan, and check her preference for his involvement. This addresses potential undue influence and supports confidentiality.
