Option A is appropriate because speaking to Mrs Thompson alone helps ensure her voice is heard and reduces undue influence from Mr Clarke; it can be framed as routine, maintaining professionalism and de-escalation. Option C supports informed consent by checking Mrs Thompson’s understanding and preferences, and documenting capacity/consent considerations—particularly important given inconsistent answers and anxiety. Option F is appropriate escalation: if concerns persist, involving the safeguarding lead/senior clinician and following local policy protects Mrs Thompson while ensuring actions are proportionate and well documented.

Option B is inappropriate because antibiotics are not a tool for conflict management and should only be prescribed when clinically indicated; it risks antimicrobial stewardship breaches. Option D is disproportionate at this stage and may escalate distress without first assessing Mrs Thompson privately. Option E undermines consent and shared decision-making. Option G may be necessary in extreme cases but is premature and could disadvantage Mrs Thompson’s access to care. Option H dismisses Emily’s concern and misses an opportunity to explore potential risk appropriately.
learningPoints: Consent must be voluntary and based on patient understanding; consider undue influence.
Antibiotics should not be prescribed to manage expectations or conflict.
Use private conversation and careful documentation when relatives dominate consultations.
Escalate safeguarding concerns via local pathways when indicators persist.

scenario:
You are a DCT on an OMFS ward. Mr Petrov is post-op day 1 after drainage of a deep neck space infection. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review him because he is more swollen and says swallowing feels harder. At the same time, Dr Kumar (registrar) messages you: “Need discharge summary done now for bed pressures.” You also notice a junior colleague, Dr Grant, taking a photo of Mr Petrov’s neck on his personal phone, saying it’s “for learning” and that he will “crop the face out.”

Mr Petrov looks worried and asks if he is getting worse. Rachel is concerned about airway risk and wants a senior review. Dr Grant seems defensive when you question the photo and says everyone does it. The ward is busy, and you are aware that challenging a colleague may strain relationships.

options.A: Ask Dr Grant to delete the photo immediately, explain that personal-phone clinical images are not acceptable without proper consent and approved systems, and document the incident and escalate to Dr Kumar or the consultant as per policy.
options.B: Prioritise assessing Mr Petrov with Rachel, including airway red flags and observations, and urgently escalate to Dr Kumar/consultant if there are signs of deterioration before focusing on discharge paperwork.
options.C: Complete the discharge summary first to help bed flow, then review Mr Petrov once the ward round is finished.
options.D: Tell Dr Grant to keep the photo but ensure he does not share it, as it is useful for teaching and the face is not visible.
options.E: Reassure Mr Petrov that swelling is normal after surgery and ask Rachel to monitor him, so you can finish the discharge summary quickly.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because Mr Petrov may be deteriorating after a deep neck space infection; airway compromise is time-critical. Assessing him promptly with Rachel, checking observations and red flags, and escalating to Dr Kumar/consultant aligns with patient safety and recognising urgency despite bed pressures.

Option A is next: Dr Grant’s use of a personal phone risks confidentiality and breaches trust and policy. Asking him to delete the image, clarifying standards around consent and approved imaging systems, and escalating/documenting appropriately demonstrates professional integrity and protects the patient and organisation.

Option E is less appropriate: while reassurance can be helpful, dismissing symptoms and delegating to monitoring without assessment risks missing deterioration and undermines Rachel’s concern. Option C is inappropriate because administrative tasks should not delay urgent clinical review. Option D is least appropriate: “cropping the face” does not remove confidentiality risk, and retaining images on personal devices without proper consent and governance is unacceptable; it normalises poor practice and could harm Mr Petrov’s trust if discovered.
learningPoints: Prioritise potential airway/clinical deterioration over administrative pressures.
Address confidentiality and information governance breaches promptly and proportionately.
Listen to and act on concerns raised by ward nursing staff.
Escalate to seniors when patient risk exceeds your immediate capacity or certainty.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you Mr Khan has arrived as a walk-in with facial swelling and “can’t open properly”. The waiting room is full and Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and not answering calls. Mr Khan is frustrated and says he needs “strong antibiotics now” because he has a flight tonight. On triage you note trismus, feverish appearance and dysphagia, but the medical history on the electronic record is incomplete and the last entry suggests a penicillin allergy “as a child” with no details. Nadia (receptionist) is asking you to keep to appointment times because two patients have already complained. Mr Khan refuses to go to A&E, saying he “just wants tablets”.

options.A: Ask Sarah to keep Mr Khan in the waiting room while you finish the next booked patient, and prescribe amoxicillin based on the most likely cause to avoid delays.
options.B: Explain to Mr Khan that his symptoms may indicate a spreading infection, ask Sarah to bring him straight into a surgery for immediate assessment, and arrange urgent senior review via the on-call OMFS team if Dr Kumar remains unavailable.
options.C: Tell Nadia to rebook Mr Khan for tomorrow morning when Dr Kumar is back, as you cannot safely manage him without a registrar present.
options.D: Offer Mr Khan metronidazole only, document the uncertain penicillin allergy, and discharge him with safety-netting to attend A&E if worse.
options.E: Call the hospital switchboard to bleep the on-call OMFS registrar, start immediate observations with Sarah (including temperature and oxygen saturations), and if dysphagia persists arrange transfer to A&E/ED via hospital policy even if Mr Khan is reluctant.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety: Mr Khan’s trismus, dysphagia and systemic features raise concern for a deep space infection with potential airway compromise. The most appropriate action is to escalate urgently and initiate basic observations while arranging transfer in line with local policy (E). This recognises limits of competence and the need for timely senior input when Dr Kumar is unavailable, and it manages risk even if the patient is reluctant.

B is also highly appropriate: bringing Mr Khan in immediately, explaining the seriousness, and arranging urgent senior review supports informed decision-making and de-escalates conflict while prioritising safety.

D is less appropriate: metronidazole alone and discharge may under-treat a potentially severe infection and relies too heavily on safety-netting, though documenting the uncertain allergy is good practice.

C is inappropriate because delaying to tomorrow ignores red flags and could lead to deterioration; lack of immediate registrar availability is not a justification to defer urgent care.

A is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin despite an unclear penicillin allergy and leaving a potentially unstable patient in the waiting room prioritises clinic flow over safety and breaches professional standards around safe prescribing and escalation.
learningPoints: Red flags (trismus, dysphagia, systemic signs) require urgent escalation and may warrant ED transfer.
Patient safety overrides clinic flow pressures and complaints about waiting times.
Recognise limits of competence and seek senior support promptly when serious infection is suspected.
Safe prescribing includes clarifying allergy history and documenting uncertainty while managing risk.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson for extraction of a painful lower molar. She has mild learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Clarke, who answers most questions and insists she “doesn’t understand anything” and wants you to “just get it done quickly”. Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you Mrs Thompson looked anxious in the waiting room and said she didn’t want her brother in the room. The referral letter is brief and doesn’t mention capacity. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is running late and has asked you to keep the list moving. Mrs Thompson nods when her brother speaks but avoids eye contact; when you ask her directly about the extraction she says, “I’m not sure… he gets angry.” Mr Clarke becomes defensive and says he will complain if you “waste time”.

options.A: Ask Emily to invite Mr Clarke to wait outside for part of the consultation, explain to Mrs Thompson you need to speak with her alone, and assess her understanding of the extraction and alternatives using simple language.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction as Mr Clarke appears to be acting in her best interests, documenting that her brother provided consent on her behalf.
options.C: Pause the procedure, inform Mr Clarke that consent must come from Mrs Thompson if she has capacity, and offer to rebook with Dr O’Connor present if she remains unsure today.
options.D: Ask Mr Clarke to sign a written consent form as next of kin, and reassure him this will protect the clinic from complaints.
options.E: Explore privately with Mrs Thompson what she means by “he gets angry,” consider whether there is a safeguarding concern, and follow local safeguarding escalation pathways if indicated.

explanation:
Valid consent requires that Mrs Thompson understands, retains, weighs information and communicates a decision, free from coercion. A is appropriate because it creates space for Mrs Thompson to speak without Mr Clarke’s influence and allows you to assess capacity using accessible communication, with Emily supporting a calm environment.

E is also appropriate: Mrs Thompson’s comment “he gets angry” and her wish for her brother not to be present raise potential safeguarding and coercion concerns. Exploring this privately and following local safeguarding pathways aligns with NHS duties to protect vulnerable adults while maintaining confidentiality and appropriate documentation.

F is appropriate because the situation is complex and time-pressured; involving Dr O’Connor promptly supports shared decision-making, ensures a robust capacity/consent process, and helps manage the interpersonal conflict with Mr Clarke.

B, D and G are unsafe and unethical: “next of kin” cannot consent for an adult with capacity, and proceeding based on nodding risks invalid consent and harm. C may be reasonable if she remains unsure, but rebooking without first assessing capacity and safeguarding misses immediate concerns. H is disproportionate; a complaint threat alone does not justify security involvement unless there is imminent risk.
learningPoints: Consent must be obtained from the patient if they have capacity; relatives cannot consent as “next of kin.”
Create opportunities to speak to vulnerable patients privately to reduce coercion and assess capacity.
Safeguarding concerns should be explored sensitively and escalated via local pathways.
Seek senior support when consent/capacity is uncertain or when interpersonal conflict threatens safe care.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover as the DCT. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, post-op day 1 after drainage of a submandibular abscess. He is due for discharge today, but his discharge summary is incomplete and the antibiotic plan is unclear. Dr Chen (consultant) has left for clinic, and Dr Grant (registrar) is in a difficult MDT meeting. Mr Petrov’s wife, Mrs Petrov, is upset and says no one has explained the plan; she is also filming on her phone “for evidence”. Rachel is worried because Mr Petrov’s observations show a rising temperature and his swallow seems worse than earlier. Meanwhile, Mark (pharmacist) is calling repeatedly because the TTO prescription is missing an indication and allergy status.

options.A: Ask Rachel to stop Mrs Petrov filming immediately and threaten to cancel discharge unless she complies, then continue writing the discharge summary.
options.B: Review Mr Petrov urgently at the bedside with Rachel, reassess airway/swallow and observations, and if concerned about deterioration escalate to Dr Grant or the on-call team rather than proceeding with discharge.
options.C: Complete the TTO prescription quickly using the last antibiotic on the drug chart, and tell Mark you will update the indication later to avoid delaying discharge.
options.D: Explain to Mrs Petrov that you will provide an update after assessing Mr Petrov, ask her to pause filming while you discuss confidential information, and offer a private space to talk once immediate clinical checks are done.
options.E: Document the current concerns, contact Dr Chen’s secretary to request guidance when he is free, and proceed with discharge if Mr Petrov says he feels ‘okay’.

explanation:
Mr Petrov has red flags for deterioration (rising temperature, worsening swallow) after a deep neck infection. The most appropriate action is to assess him immediately with Rachel and escalate to senior support rather than discharging (B). This prioritises patient safety and recognises limits of competence when the plan is unclear and the patient may be becoming unwell.

D is next: it manages the interpersonal dynamic with Mrs Petrov by setting respectful boundaries around filming and confidentiality, while not delaying urgent clinical assessment. Offering a private discussion supports patient-centred communication and reduces complaint escalation.

E is less appropriate: documenting concerns is good, but waiting for Dr Chen’s secretary and proceeding with discharge based on Mr Petrov feeling “okay” risks missing clinical deterioration and does not address the immediate observations.

A is inappropriate because it escalates conflict and uses threats rather than proportionate boundary-setting; it also distracts from urgent clinical review.

C is least appropriate: rushing an incomplete prescription and promising to add indication/allergy later undermines safe prescribing and creates risk for Mark dispensing incorrectly, breaching documentation standards and teamwork expectations.
learningPoints: Post-operative deterioration signs should prompt immediate review and escalation before discharge.
Maintain confidentiality and set boundaries around recording in a calm, proportionate way.
Accurate prescribing and documentation are essential; do not ask pharmacy to dispense on incomplete information.
Use teamwork with ward staff and escalate early when senior clinicians are unavailable.

scenario:
You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you Mr Khan has arrived with facial swelling and trismus. The triage note says “penicillin allergy – rash”, but the GP letter in the system is missing. Mr Khan is frustrated because he has waited 90 minutes and says he “just needs antibiotics” so he can return to work. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Receptionist Nadia quietly asks if you can “keep it quick” because the waiting room is full and one patient is already complaining.

On examination you suspect an odontogenic infection with possible spreading cellulitis; Mr Khan is febrile and struggles to open. He refuses hospital admission, saying he cannot afford time off, and asks you to prescribe “whatever is strongest”. Sarah looks concerned and reminds you that the allergy documentation is unclear.

options.A: Prescribe amoxicillin immediately to avoid further delay, document that the triage note mentioned a rash, and advise Mr Khan to see his GP if it worsens.
options.B: Explain to Mr Khan why you are concerned about a spreading infection, assess airway/red flags, and arrange urgent senior review via Dr Kumar’s on-call route while Sarah contacts the OMFS ward for escalation support.
options.C: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan into a later slot because the clinic is running behind, and tell him to attend A&E if he feels worse.
options.D: Clarify the allergy history directly with Mr Khan (reaction, timing, severity), check available records, and if allergy is uncertain avoid penicillins and discuss alternative antibiotics alongside definitive management and safety-netting.
options.E: Agree to prescribe antibiotics only if Mr Khan signs that he is refusing admission, then discharge him with minimal discussion to keep the clinic moving.

explanation:
The most appropriate priority is patient safety. Option B is best because Mr Khan has systemic features and possible spreading cellulitis; assessing airway/red flags and escalating for urgent senior input via the on-call route is appropriate when Dr Kumar is unavailable. It also uses the team effectively (Sarah contacting the OMFS ward) and resists pressure from Nadia to rush.

Option D is next: unclear “penicillin allergy – rash” requires a structured allergy history and record check. Avoiding penicillins if uncertain and considering alternatives supports safe prescribing and antimicrobial stewardship, alongside planning definitive management and robust safety-netting.

Option E is less appropriate: documenting refusal can be part of good practice, but using a “sign and discharge” approach risks coercion and undermines informed consent. Refusal of admission should trigger careful capacity assessment, explanation of risks, and escalation.

Option A is unsafe: prescribing amoxicillin despite uncertain allergy and without addressing severity/need for admission prioritises speed over safety.

Option C is least appropriate: rebooking a potentially serious infection delays care and inappropriately shifts responsibility to A&E, contrary to duty of care and NHS patient-centred standards.
learningPoints: Escalate promptly when red flags suggest spreading infection; patient safety overrides clinic flow pressures.
Take and document a detailed drug allergy history; do not prescribe penicillins when allergy status is unclear.
Informed refusal requires capacity assessment, clear explanation of risks/benefits, and appropriate senior involvement.
Use the wider team (nursing/ward/on-call pathways) to manage risk when supervisors are unavailable.

scenario:
You are a DCT in a community special care dentistry clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Daniel, who has moderate learning disability and communicates with short phrases. Emily (dental nurse) notes Daniel has multiple bruises on his forearms and flinches when his mother raises her voice. Mrs Thompson insists on staying in the room and answers most questions for him. Daniel needs an extraction under local anaesthetic; he appears anxious but nods when you ask if he wants the tooth removed.

Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in another clinic and can be contacted by phone. Receptionist Olivia tells you the transport is booked to leave in 20 minutes and Mrs Thompson is demanding you “get it done today” because she cannot take more time off. Emily quietly says she feels “something isn’t right” and asks whether you should speak to safeguarding.

options.A: Ask Mrs Thompson to step out briefly, explaining you need to check Daniel’s wishes privately, and use simple language/visual aids to assess his understanding and capacity for the extraction.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction immediately because Daniel nodded, and document that his mother provided collateral history and consented on his behalf.
options.C: Call Dr O’Connor for advice, outlining Emily’s observations and your concerns, and agree a plan for safeguarding escalation before proceeding.
options.D: Tell Olivia to cancel the transport and rebook Daniel without explanation to avoid confrontation with Mrs Thompson.
options.E: Ask Emily to stay with Daniel while you speak to Mrs Thompson in the corridor about the bruises and your duty to consider safeguarding, keeping the discussion factual and non-accusatory.

explanation:
The best actions prioritise valid consent and safeguarding while maintaining professionalism. Option A is appropriate because Daniel’s consent must be informed and voluntary; a private conversation (where feasible) helps assess his wishes, capacity, and whether he is being coerced. Using accessible communication supports patient-centred care.

Option C is also appropriate: the combination of bruising, flinching, and a controlling dynamic warrants senior discussion. Contacting Dr O’Connor demonstrates recognising limits of competence and ensures safeguarding decisions are proportionate and documented.

Option E is appropriate because it manages the interpersonal tension with Mrs Thompson while exploring concerns. Speaking factually and non-accusatorily, with Emily present to support Daniel, helps maintain safety and de-escalate.

Options B, G and H are unsafe: a nod is not sufficient evidence of understanding, and a parent cannot simply “take responsibility” for an adult’s consent. These options risk treating without valid consent and missing potential abuse.

Option F is problematic: safeguarding referrals should generally be transparent unless doing so increases risk; proceeding “as normal” may also be inappropriate until consent and safety are clarified. Option D is unhelpful because it avoids communication and may worsen trust and access to care.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires patient understanding and freedom from coercion; capacity is decision-specific.
Safeguarding concerns should be considered and escalated appropriately, using senior support and local pathways.
Manage challenging relatives professionally: be factual, non-accusatory, and prioritise the patient’s voice.
Service pressures (transport/time) should not override consent and safeguarding duties.

scenario:
You are a DCT on an OMFS ward during a busy afternoon. Dr Grant (registrar) asks you to discharge Mr Petrov after incision and drainage of a submandibular abscess. The electronic discharge summary is partly completed, but the antibiotic section is blank and the allergy box is inconsistent: one page says “NKDA”, another lists “clarithromycin – anaphylaxis”. Ward nurse Angela tells you Mr Petrov’s partner is at the nurses’ station demanding to leave immediately and is filming on her phone, saying she will “post how slow the NHS is”.

Radiographer Elena has also phoned to say Mr Petrov missed his follow-up CT slot because porters were unavailable, and she wants to know whether to rebook. Dr Grant is about to go into theatre and says, “Just send him—he’s fine.

options.A: Complete the discharge summary using the “NKDA” entry to save time, prescribe clarithromycin as per the usual post-op protocol, and ask Angela to remove the partner from the ward.
options.B: Pause the discharge, reconcile the allergy status with Mr Petrov and the notes, and contact Dr Grant before prescribing or finalising the discharge medications.
options.C: Ask Angela to speak to the partner about filming and confidentiality, and offer a private update area while you continue to finalise safe discharge paperwork.
options.D: Tell Elena to rebook the CT for the next available slot and document the missed appointment, ensuring the plan is communicated to Mr Petrov at discharge.
options.E: Discharge Mr Petrov without antibiotics and advise him to contact his GP if he develops symptoms, because the allergy information is unclear.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate: inconsistent allergy documentation (including “clarithromycin – anaphylaxis”) makes prescribing high risk. Reconciling allergies directly with Mr Petrov and the record, and involving Dr Grant before finalising medications, prioritises patient safety and stays within your competence.

Option C is next: Angela can address the partner’s filming and confidentiality, and offering a private space helps de-escalate while maintaining dignity for other patients. You should not personally confront while critical safety tasks remain, but the behaviour must be managed.

Option D is appropriate because missed imaging affects ongoing care. Rebooking and documenting, then communicating the plan to Mr Petrov, supports continuity and reduces risk of lost follow-up.

Option E is less appropriate: withholding antibiotics might be clinically reasonable in some cases, but doing so purely due to documentation uncertainty without senior discussion or a clear plan risks undertreatment after a serious infection.

Option A is least appropriate and unsafe: it ignores conflicting allergy information and could cause severe harm by prescribing clarithromycin. It also inappropriately focuses on removing the partner rather than proportionate management of confidentiality and communication.
learningPoints: Always reconcile and document drug allergies before prescribing; conflicting records require clarification.
Safe discharge includes accurate medications, follow-up plans, and clear communication to the patient.
Manage confidentiality breaches (e.g., filming) through ward processes and de-escalation while prioritising clinical safety.
Escalate to seniors when time pressure conflicts with safe prescribing and discharge decisions.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in an NHS oral surgery clinic. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is running late after an emergency call. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan is booked for extraction of UR6 and the referral letter mentions a “previous reaction to penicillin,” but the electronic record lists “NKDA.” Mr Khan is anxious and says he “just wants it out today” because he has taken unpaid leave. Receptionist Nadia tells you two urgent walk-ins have arrived and the waiting room is full. Sarah quietly adds that the last clinician sometimes “ticks NKDA to save time.” Mr Khan’s wife is on speakerphone insisting you prescribe antibiotics “like last time” before any extraction. You have not met Mr Khan before, and the consent form is partially completed by a student observer. You can do the extraction, but you are unsure about the allergy history and whether antibiotics are indicated.

options.A: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan to the next available slot and send him home with advice, without clarifying the allergy discrepancy, to reduce today’s pressure on the clinic.
options.B: Pause the procedure, take a focused allergy history directly from Mr Khan (including the penicillin reaction details), check previous correspondence, and document the discrepancy before proceeding with any prescribing or extraction.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction as planned to avoid further delays, and ask Sarah to update the allergy status afterwards once the clinic is quieter.
options.D: Prescribe a course of amoxicillin now to reassure Mr Khan’s wife and reduce the risk of post-operative infection, then proceed with the extraction.
options.E: Explain to Mr Khan that you need senior input due to conflicting records, ask Sarah to help contact Dr Ahmed or the on-call registrar Dr Lewis, and if not available, defer treatment safely with analgesia advice and clear safety-netting.

explanation:
The most appropriate priority is patient safety and accurate documentation. Option B directly addresses the conflicting allergy information by taking a focused history from Mr Khan and checking available correspondence, then documenting clearly. This aligns with NHS expectations around safe prescribing and informed consent; you cannot rely on a potentially inaccurate “NKDA” tick-box, especially when Sarah suggests shortcuts may have occurred.

Option E is also highly appropriate because it recognises limits of competence and the need for supervision when records conflict and prescribing is being requested. Involving Dr Ahmed or Dr Lewis supports safe decision-making and protects both patient and trainee; if senior advice is unavailable, deferring with safety-netting is safer than proceeding uncertainly.

Option C is less appropriate: proceeding first and “fixing” the allergy record later exposes Mr Khan to avoidable risk and undermines consent.

Option A reduces pressure but fails to resolve the safety issue or provide a clear plan; it may also be unfair to Mr Khan.

Option D is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin despite an unclear penicillin reaction is unsafe and antibiotics are not indicated simply to reassure a relative. It breaches antimicrobial stewardship and professional integrity.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy documentation must be clarified and recorded before prescribing or treatment.
Patient safety and antimicrobial stewardship outweigh time pressure and external demands.
Recognise limits of competence and seek timely senior support when risk is uncertain.
Valid consent requires accurate information and patient understanding, not family pressure.

scenario: You are on a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Daniel, who answers most questions for her. Laura (dental therapist) tells you Mrs Thompson has early dementia noted on a previous letter, but today there is no capacity assessment documented. Mrs Thompson needs a painful lower molar extracted. She appears calm but keeps looking to Daniel before speaking. Daniel insists you “just do it quickly” and refuses an interpreter, saying he will translate, although Mrs Thompson occasionally switches into Polish. Receptionist Olivia says transport has arrived early and the driver will leave in 20 minutes. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in another surgery with a distressed patient and asks you to “keep the list moving.” Daniel also asks you not to mention the dementia diagnosis because it “upsets her.

options.A: Ask Daniel to step out briefly so you can speak to Mrs Thompson alone, using simple questions to assess understanding and voluntariness, and document your findings before deciding on extraction.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction because Mrs Thompson is not resisting and Daniel is her next of kin, then document consent as ‘verbal’ to avoid missing the transport window.
options.C: Ask Olivia to contact the transport driver to request a short delay, explaining you need time to ensure consent and communication support for Mrs Thompson.
options.D: Use Daniel as the sole interpreter to save time, and avoid discussing dementia to maintain rapport and reduce distress.
options.E: Call Dr Kumar for immediate review and ask him to take over the consent discussion, even if it means leaving his distressed patient waiting.

explanation:
Options A, C and F best balance patient-centred care, consent, and service pressures. A is crucial because Mrs Thompson’s reliance on Daniel and the prior note of dementia raise concerns about capacity and voluntariness. Speaking to her alone (where appropriate) helps identify undue influence and supports a proportionate capacity assessment, which must be documented.

F is also appropriate: using a professional interpreter protects accuracy and confidentiality and reduces the risk of Daniel filtering information. It supports informed consent and aligns with NHS standards on communication and equality of access.

C appropriately manages the system pressure by asking Olivia to negotiate a short transport delay, rather than compromising consent. This is a practical, patient-safety-focused step.

B is unsafe and legally/ethically flawed: next of kin cannot consent for an adult with capacity, and lack of resistance is not consent. D risks miscommunication and coercion. E may be disproportionate because it pulls Dr Kumar from another distressed patient; you should escalate if needed, but not as the first step. G is punitive and not patient-centred. H breaches confidentiality by sharing sensitive information with non-clinical staff without a need-to-know basis.
learningPoints: Consent must be obtained from the patient and requires understanding, voluntariness and adequate communication support.
Family members should not be used as sole interpreters for consent-sensitive discussions when alternatives exist.
Manage time pressures by adjusting logistics rather than lowering consent standards.
Document capacity/consent assessments clearly, especially in patients with cognitive impairment.

scenario: You are the DCT on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, post-op day 1 after incision and drainage for a deep neck space infection. He is stable but in pain and wants to go outside to smoke. Dr Chen (consultant) has asked for strict observations and early warning score escalation. While you are checking the notes, you overhear Dr Grant (registrar) joking loudly at the nurses’ station about Mr Petrov being “a frequent flyer” and showing a photo on his phone of the patient’s swollen face taken in theatre. Emily (dental nurse) looks uncomfortable and whispers that Dr Grant often posts “clinical banter” in a private group chat. Mr Petrov’s partner arrives and angrily demands to know why “everyone is laughing” and threatens to complain. At the same time, Rachel tells you the ward is short-staffed and she needs you to prescribe stronger analgesia quickly.

options.A: Focus only on prescribing analgesia for Mr Petrov and ignore Dr Grant’s behaviour because challenging a registrar could damage team relationships.
options.B: Ask Dr Grant privately to stop sharing the photo immediately, remind him of confidentiality and professional standards, and request he deletes the image and does not post it; then document your concern and escalate to Dr Chen or the clinical supervisor if needed.
options.C: Confront Dr Grant loudly at the nurses’ station so the team sees you take confidentiality seriously, and demand he hands over his phone for inspection.
options.D: Reassure Mr Petrov’s partner that no one is laughing at him, apologise for the distress, and explain you will look into what happened without discussing staff details; then address Mr Petrov’s pain and smoking request safely.
options.E: Tell Emily to report Dr Grant if she feels uncomfortable, but take no further action yourself because you did not take the photo.

explanation:
Option D is most appropriate because it immediately de-escalates the interpersonal conflict with Mr Petrov’s partner while maintaining confidentiality about staff matters. It also keeps focus on patient-centred care: addressing pain promptly and managing the smoking request in the context of Dr Chen’s instructions and post-operative risk.

Option B is next best: it tackles the potential confidentiality breach proportionately and professionally by speaking to Dr Grant privately, asking him to stop and delete the image, and escalating appropriately if needed. This aligns with NHS expectations on information governance and raising concerns, even when the colleague is senior.

Option E is less appropriate but still better than inaction: encouraging Emily to report is supportive, yet you still have a duty to act on what you observed.

Option A is inappropriate because it prioritises team harmony over professional integrity and patient trust; ignoring the behaviour risks further breaches and undermines the ward team.

Option C is least appropriate: a public confrontation may escalate conflict, damage working relationships, and is unlikely to be an effective or proportionate way to manage a concern. It could also worsen the complaint situation and distract from Mr Petrov’s care.
learningPoints: Address immediate patient needs while also managing professionalism concerns that affect trust and safety.
Confidentiality breaches (including photos) must be challenged and escalated appropriately, regardless of hierarchy.
De-escalate complaints with empathy and transparency without disclosing staff details inappropriately.
Support colleagues to raise concerns, but do not delegate away your own responsibility to act.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you that Mr Khan has arrived as a walk-in with facial swelling and trismus. The clinic is already running 45 minutes late and Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Mr Khan is frustrated and says he needs “strong antibiotics” because he has a flight tonight. While you are preparing to assess him, Nadia (receptionist) hands you a paper triage sheet from yesterday that appears to list a penicillin allergy, but the electronic record shows “no known drug allergies”. Mr Khan speaks limited English and his adult son is translating, but the son keeps answering for him and is pushing for a prescription without examination.

options.A: Ask Nadia to re-check the allergy status by phoning Mr Khan’s GP and, while waiting, ask Sarah to bring Mr Khan straight into a surgery for immediate assessment and observations (including airway red flags), using a telephone interpreter rather than relying on his son.
options.B: Issue a broad-spectrum antibiotic prescription immediately to avoid delay, advise Mr Khan to see a dentist abroad if it worsens, and document that the son translated.
options.C: Bring Mr Khan in urgently, explain to his son that you need to hear from Mr Khan directly, arrange a telephone interpreter, and clarify allergy history with Mr Khan before prescribing anything; if airway compromise is suspected, escalate to on-call OMFS/ED without waiting for Dr Kumar.
options.D: Tell Sarah to rebook Mr Khan into the next available slot tomorrow because the clinic is running late, and ask him to attend A&E if he becomes short of breath.
options.E: Proceed with assessment using the son as interpreter to save time, prescribe amoxicillin if indicated, and ask Mr Khan to confirm verbally that he has no allergies.

explanation:
Option C is most appropriate because it prioritises immediate patient safety (potential deep space infection/airway risk) while ensuring valid consent and accurate prescribing. Using a telephone interpreter reduces the risk of miscommunication and undue influence from Mr Khan’s son, and clarifying the allergy discrepancy before prescribing aligns with safe medicines management. Escalating to on-call OMFS/ED if red flags are present is appropriate even if Dr Kumar is unavailable.

Option A is also strong: it combines urgent assessment with attempts to verify allergy status and improves communication. However, waiting for GP confirmation should not delay necessary escalation if Mr Khan is unwell.

Option D is less appropriate because rebooking a potentially serious swelling to protect clinic flow risks harm; advising A&E only “if it worsens” is too passive.

Option E is weaker: relying on the son as interpreter and a quick verbal check increases the risk of inaccurate allergy history and compromised consent.

Option B is least appropriate: prescribing without examination and with unclear allergy status breaches patient safety standards and professional integrity, and could delay definitive care.
learningPoints: Potential airway compromise overrides clinic time pressures and requires prompt assessment/escalation.
Valid consent and accurate history-taking may require a professional interpreter rather than family members.
Resolve discrepancies in allergy documentation before prescribing; document clearly.
Escalate appropriately when senior support is unavailable and patient safety is at risk.

scenario: You are on an OMFS ward reviewing post-op patients. Mrs Thompson had a difficult extraction under GA yesterday and is now complaining of numbness and severe pain. Dr Lewis (registrar) asks you to update the discharge summary quickly because beds are needed. While you are writing, you notice that the operation note states “inferior alveolar nerve intact” but the nursing notes from Angela (ward nurse) record that Mrs Thompson reported numbness immediately in recovery. Mrs Thompson’s partner is at the bedside and is angry, saying “someone has messed up and you’re covering it up.” He demands to see the consultant and wants a copy of all notes now. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is in clinic and has asked not to be interrupted unless urgent. You feel pressure to discharge, but also to be honest, manage expectations, and ensure appropriate follow-up and documentation.

options.A: Tell the partner that nerve injuries are a known risk and that the operation note proves everything was normal, then complete discharge as planned to free the bed.
options.B: Ask Angela to step outside with you briefly to clarify the timeline of symptoms and ensure her nursing documentation is complete, then update your discharge summary to reflect the reported numbness and the plan for review.
options.C: Speak directly with Mrs Thompson (not just her partner) to confirm what she understands and what she wants shared, check her capacity and consent for her partner’s involvement, and offer a private conversation if she prefers.
options.D: Immediately interrupt Dr Ahmed in clinic and ask him to come to the ward to speak to the partner, regardless of Mrs Thompson’s condition or wishes.
options.E: Alter the discharge summary to match the operation note so the documentation is consistent, and arrange outpatient review later if she complains again.

explanation:
The best actions are B, C and F because they address patient safety, honesty in documentation, and confidentiality while managing an escalating interpersonal situation.

B is appropriate: clarifying the timeline with Angela and ensuring nursing notes are complete helps reconcile the discrepancy between the operation note and recovery symptoms. Updating the discharge summary to reflect what was reported (and the plan) supports continuity of care and professional integrity.

C is essential: Mrs Thompson is the patient, and her partner should not dominate the conversation. Checking what Mrs Thompson consents to share and offering privacy protects confidentiality and supports patient-centred care, especially when she appears anxious.

F is appropriate: it sets boundaries about record access while still providing an explanation and signposting the correct NHS process for notes and complaints, reducing conflict without being defensive.

A and G minimise symptoms and risk unsafe discharge. E involves falsifying documentation, which is serious misconduct. D may be disproportionate if there is no immediate clinical emergency and ignores Mrs Thompson’s wishes. H inappropriately shifts responsibility and perpetuates inaccurate records.
learningPoints: Document symptoms and discrepancies honestly; never alter records to ‘match’ another note.
Speak to the patient directly and confirm consent for relatives’ involvement.
Manage complaints and requests for records professionally, signposting formal processes.
Balance bed pressures with safe discharge planning and appropriate follow-up.

scenario: You are a DCT in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Hassan attends with her support worker, Rebecca (therapist), for urgent care. Mrs Hassan has mild learning disability and becomes distressed when procedures change. The referral letter is missing from the electronic system due to an IT outage, and Olivia (receptionist) can only find a brief note: “pain LR6”. In the chair, Lucy (dental nurse) quietly tells you she overheard Mark (radiographer) earlier saying the OPG machine is down and “bitewings will have to do”, but Mrs Hassan previously refused intraoral radiographs. Rebecca insists you “just take the tooth out today” because transport is arranged and she cannot bring Mrs Hassan back easily. You are not confident extracting LR6 without imaging and a clear medical history, and Dr O’Connor (consultant) is off-site but contactable by phone. Mrs Hassan starts crying and says she wants to go home.

options.A: Explain to Rebecca that you cannot proceed without confirming Mrs Hassan’s wishes and capacity, then speak directly to Mrs Hassan using simple language and visual aids to explore options (analgesia, temporary measures, radiographs, or rebooking), documenting her preferences.
options.B: Proceed with extraction of LR6 without radiographs to avoid distress and because Rebecca says transport cannot be rearranged; ask Lucy to hold Mrs Hassan’s hands to keep her still.
options.C: Call Dr O’Connor for advice about managing without the referral and imaging, and discuss whether temporary measures or referral to another site for imaging is safer today.
options.D: Ask Olivia to print any previous clinic letters and medication lists from the last attendance, and ask Rebecca to provide contact details for Mrs Hassan’s GP/pharmacy to verify key medical history before any irreversible treatment.
options.E: Tell Mrs Hassan that she must have bitewings or you will not treat her, and ask Rebecca to sign a consent form on her behalf so you can proceed quickly.

explanation:
A is most appropriate because it centres Mrs Hassan’s autonomy and supports valid consent. In special care settings, using simple language and aids helps ensure understanding, and documenting her preferences is essential—especially when she is distressed and wants to leave.

C is next: contacting Dr O’Connor recognises your limits of competence and helps you make a safe plan when referral information and imaging are unavailable. It also supports shared decision-making under service pressure.

D is also appropriate: gathering prior letters and verifying medical history through reliable sources reduces risk before any irreversible procedure, particularly when the electronic referral is missing due to IT failure.

E is less appropriate: issuing an ultimatum and asking Rebecca to sign consent is not valid; consent must come from Mrs Hassan if she has capacity, and refusal of radiographs should be explored rather than overridden.

B is least appropriate: extracting without adequate assessment/imaging and using restraint is unsafe and unethical, risks harm, and breaches professional standards and safeguarding principles.
learningPoints: Consent must be obtained from the patient with capacity; support workers cannot consent on their behalf.
Recognise limits of competence and seek senior advice when information/imaging is missing.
System failures (IT outages, equipment downtime) require safe workarounds, not shortcuts.
Avoid coercion or restraint; manage distress with communication and appropriate alternatives.

scenario:
You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you Mr Ibrahim has arrived early for an extraction and is “definitely allergic to penicillin,” but the triage sheet lists “NKDA.” Mr Ibrahim is anxious and says he “can’t miss work,” pushing to be treated immediately. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in another room with a difficult patient and asks you to “just get on with it” to keep the waiting room moving. Receptionist Nadia adds that the clinic is already running 40 minutes late and there are two patients complaining.

Mr Ibrahim’s medical history in the electronic record is incomplete; the last update was two years ago. He mentions a past “rash” after antibiotics but can’t recall the name. You are considering prescribing post‑extraction antibiotics because he reports swelling, but you have not examined him yet. Sarah looks uncomfortable and repeats that she heard the allergy from Mr Ibrahim’s wife on the phone.

options.A: Tell Nadia to rebook Mr Ibrahim to a later slot so the clinic can catch up, and proceed with other patients without clarifying the allergy today.
options.B: Pause the appointment to take a focused medical and allergy history directly from Mr Ibrahim, document it in the record, and examine him before deciding whether any antibiotic is indicated.
options.C: Ask Sarah to phone Mr Ibrahim’s wife again to confirm the allergy details while you start local anaesthetic and prepare for extraction to avoid delays.
options.D: Explain to Dr Kumar that you cannot prescribe or proceed until the allergy discrepancy is resolved, and request brief senior input if uncertainty remains after your history.
options.E: Prescribe a non‑penicillin antibiotic “to be safe” based on Sarah’s concern, and proceed with extraction without further delay.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to take responsibility for patient safety by clarifying and documenting the discrepancy yourself. Option B prioritises a direct, focused allergy and medical history from Mr Ibrahim, then examination before any prescribing decision. This aligns with NHS expectations for safe prescribing and accurate records; relying on outdated electronic data or second‑hand information risks harm.

Option D is next: it appropriately manages the interpersonal pressure from Dr Kumar by being clear that you cannot proceed until the discrepancy is addressed, and it recognises limits of certainty—seeking brief senior input if the history remains unclear.

Option C is less appropriate because delegating a phone call to Sarah may help gather information, but starting local anaesthetic and preparing for extraction before resolving an allergy/medical history discrepancy undermines informed consent and safe decision‑making, especially if you later discover a relevant contraindication.

Option E is weak: prescribing “to be safe” without examination or indication promotes inappropriate antibiotic use and still fails to establish the true allergy status.

Option A is least appropriate because rebooking to manage flow avoids the immediate safety issue, leaves inaccurate records uncorrected, and does not address Mr Ibrahim’s anxiety or clinical need.
learningPoints: Resolve discrepancies in medical history and allergies before treatment or prescribing.
Safe prescribing requires clinical assessment and a clear indication; avoid defensive antibiotic use.
Escalate appropriately when uncertainty persists and communicate boundaries under time pressure.
Accurate documentation supports continuity of care and reduces future risk.

scenario:
You are in a community special care dentistry clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Daniel, who insists on staying in the room and answers questions for her. Mrs Thompson has mild learning disability and appears nervous. Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you she has noticed bruising on Mrs Thompson’s forearm when helping her put on a bib. Mrs Thompson is booked for multiple restorations, but the clinic is short‑staffed and Dr O’Connor (consultant) is running an outreach session off‑site and is only intermittently reachable.

During consent discussion, Mrs Thompson looks at Daniel before speaking and says she “doesn’t want any injections,” yet Daniel interrupts, saying she “always says that” and wants you to “just get it done today.” The interpreter service you requested has not arrived; Mrs Thompson’s speech is clear but she struggles to understand complex explanations.

options.A: Ask Daniel to step outside for part of the consultation, explaining you need to speak with Mrs Thompson alone to confirm her wishes and assess capacity and consent.
options.B: Proceed with treatment as planned because Daniel is her carer and the clinic is short‑staffed, documenting that he agreed on her behalf.
options.C: Ask Emily to document the bruising in the notes and, with Mrs Thompson’s agreement where possible, follow local safeguarding procedures and seek advice from the safeguarding lead.
options.D: Call Dr O’Connor repeatedly until you reach him, and delay all other patients until you have explicit consultant instruction.
options.E: Offer Mrs Thompson a staged plan: focus today on urgent care and acclimatisation, revisit injections with clear, simple explanations, and consider alternative pain control options if appropriate.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions prioritise Mrs Thompson’s autonomy, valid consent, and safeguarding while remaining proportionate. Option A is key: asking Daniel to step out enables you to speak directly with Mrs Thompson, reduce coercion, and assess capacity and understanding. This addresses the interpersonal dynamic and supports patient‑centred care.

Option C is appropriate because Emily’s observation of bruising is a potential safeguarding indicator. Documenting objectively and following local safeguarding pathways—seeking advice from the safeguarding lead—meets NHS duties to protect vulnerable adults. Gaining Mrs Thompson’s agreement where possible respects confidentiality, while recognising that safeguarding concerns may justify information sharing.

Option E supports ethical decision making under pressure: a staged approach with simple explanations helps ensure consent is informed and voluntary, and avoids forcing injections against expressed wishes. It also balances clinic flow by focusing on urgent needs.

Less appropriate options include B and H, which disregard consent and safeguarding. D is disproportionate and risks delaying care for others; you can follow established policies without immediate consultant contact. F is confrontational and unsafe, potentially escalating risk. G may be reasonable for language support, but sending her away without addressing consent and safeguarding concerns misses immediate responsibilities.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s understanding and freedom from undue influence.
Safeguarding concerns should be documented objectively and escalated via local pathways.
Use proportionate, staged treatment plans when anxiety or understanding limits immediate consent.
Manage relatives/carers professionally while keeping the patient at the centre of decisions.

scenario:
You are a DCT on an OMFS ward. Dr Grant (registrar) asks you to write up discharge medications for Mr Petrov after incision and drainage of a facial swelling. Mr Petrov is keen to leave quickly because his partner is waiting outside and he is frustrated about delays. Ward nurse Angela tells you the electronic discharge summary still shows an old anticoagulant dose and that Mr Petrov’s allergy status is “unknown.”

While you are checking the chart, you notice Dr Grant has already printed a prescription and left it on the desk for you to sign, saying, “It’s standard—just sign so he can go.” The prescription includes an antibiotic and analgesia, but you are not sure the antibiotic choice matches the microbiology advice documented earlier. Rachel (ward nurse) adds that beds are needed and asks you to “help us move him on.”

You feel pressured by hierarchy and bed pressures, but you are accountable for what you sign.

options.A: Refuse to sign the prescription until you have verified Mr Petrov’s allergy status, current anticoagulant dose, and the documented microbiology advice; then amend and document changes before signing.
options.B: Sign the prescription as Dr Grant requested to avoid delaying discharge, and plan to correct any errors later if contacted.
options.C: Ask Angela to update the allergy and anticoagulant details on the discharge summary while you independently check the notes and microbiology plan before signing anything.
options.D: Sign only the analgesia section and leave the antibiotic unsigned, telling Rachel that the medical team can sort the antibiotic after discharge.
options.E: Escalate to Dr Grant immediately, explaining the specific discrepancies you’ve found and asking him to review the prescription with you before discharge if you cannot verify details promptly.

explanation:
Option A is most appropriate because signing a prescription makes you professionally and legally accountable. Verifying Mr Petrov’s allergy status, anticoagulant dose, and the microbiology plan addresses immediate patient safety risks (allergy reaction, bleeding risk, inappropriate antibiotics) and supports accurate discharge documentation.

Option C is also appropriate and supports teamwork: asking Angela to update the discharge summary while you check the notes ensures parallel processing under time pressure without compromising safety. It uses ward resources appropriately and improves continuity of care.

Option E is next: it manages the hierarchy pressure by clearly escalating concerns to Dr Grant with specifics. If you cannot promptly verify key details, senior review is appropriate and protects the patient and you.

Option D is less appropriate because partial signing creates fragmented care and risks Mr Petrov leaving without necessary antibiotics or with unclear responsibility, potentially leading to deterioration and readmission.

Option B is least appropriate: signing first and correcting later normalises unsafe practice, undermines professional integrity, and could cause immediate harm. Bed pressures and patient frustration do not justify bypassing safety checks.
learningPoints: You are accountable for any prescription you sign; verify key safety information first.
Use teamwork to resolve documentation issues quickly without compromising patient safety.
Challenge hierarchy respectfully and escalate when pressured to act unsafely.
Avoid fragmented discharge plans that leave treatment responsibilities unclear.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you Mr Khan is in cubicle 2 with facial swelling and “penicillin allergy” written on a triage sheet. The electronic record, however, lists “no known drug allergies”. Mr Khan is in pain and says he “just needs antibiotics” because he has a flight tomorrow. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is tied up with a trauma case and has asked you to “keep the list moving”. Nadia (receptionist) warns there are four patients waiting who are already complaining. Mr Khan’s wife is filming on her phone and says she will post online if he is not treated quickly. You examine Mr Khan: he is febrile, trismus is worsening, and you suspect spreading infection that may need admission.

options.A: Tell Mr Khan you will prescribe amoxicillin as the record says no allergies, ask Sarah to book a review tomorrow, and move on to the next patient to reduce delays.
options.B: Pause the clinic flow to clarify the allergy directly with Mr Khan (reaction type and timing), ask Sarah to check previous hospital letters, and document the discrepancy before prescribing anything.
options.C: Ask Nadia to tell Mr Khan’s wife filming is not allowed and threaten to stop treatment unless she deletes the video.
options.D: Escalate immediately to Dr Kumar with your clinical findings (fever, trismus, spreading swelling) and discuss urgent referral to OMFS/on-call for possible admission, while keeping Mr Khan monitored in clinic.
options.E: Explain to Mr Khan that antibiotics alone may be unsafe and offer to drain if possible, even though you have not done an incision and drainage independently before, to avoid admission and delays.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to prioritise patient safety and escalation. Mr Khan’s fever, worsening trismus and spreading swelling suggest a potentially serious odontogenic infection; D appropriately involves Dr Kumar promptly and considers urgent OMFS/on-call assessment and admission, which aligns with NHS expectations to recognise deterioration and work within escalation pathways. B is next: the conflicting allergy documentation is a direct prescribing risk. Clarifying the history with Mr Khan, asking Sarah to check prior correspondence, and documenting the discrepancy supports safe prescribing and defensible records.

C addresses an interpersonal issue (filming) but is heavy-handed. You should manage confidentiality and staff safety calmly, but threatening to stop treatment risks escalating conflict and delaying urgent care.

E is inappropriate because it pushes you beyond your competence: offering incision and drainage when you have not done it independently risks harm and undermines informed consent. A is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin without resolving the allergy discrepancy and without addressing possible sepsis/spreading infection prioritises clinic flow over safety and could lead to serious adverse outcomes.
learningPoints: Patient safety and escalation override time pressure and waiting-room complaints.
Resolve and document discrepancies in allergy status before prescribing.
Recognise limits of competence and avoid undertaking procedures you cannot safely deliver independently.
Manage challenging behaviour (e.g., filming) proportionately without delaying urgent care.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has mild learning disability and attends with her support worker, Mark. She needs extraction of a painful lower molar. Mrs Thompson says she wants it “out today” but becomes distressed when you discuss local anaesthetic and asks Mark to “decide for me”. Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you Mrs Thompson previously disclosed she is scared of Mark “getting angry” if appointments run over. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in another building and only available by phone. The clinic is running late and Olivia (receptionist) says patient transport will leave in 20 minutes. Mark insists you proceed quickly and asks to sign the consent form “to save time”. You suspect Mrs Thompson may have capacity but is being influenced, and you also need to manage pain and transport constraints.

options.A: Ask Mark to wait outside for part of the consultation, and explore Mrs Thompson’s wishes and understanding in a calm, supported way, using simple language and checking comprehension.
options.B: Allow Mark to sign the consent form as Mrs Thompson requested, and proceed with the extraction to prevent her missing transport.
options.C: Phone Dr Banerjee for immediate advice on capacity, consent, and possible safeguarding concerns, and document the discussion and plan.
options.D: Tell Olivia to cancel Mrs Thompson’s transport as a consequence of Mark’s behaviour, and rebook when the clinic is quieter.
options.E: Proceed with the extraction but ask Emily to hold Mrs Thompson’s hands firmly if she becomes distressed, to keep things safe and efficient.

explanation:
A, C and F best balance consent, vulnerability and timely care. A is appropriate because separating Mark for part of the discussion reduces undue influence and allows you to assess Mrs Thompson’s own preferences and understanding. Using accessible communication supports valid consent and patient-centred care.

F is essential: capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. You should assess whether Mrs Thompson can understand, retain, weigh and communicate the decision about extraction under local anaesthetic. If she lacks capacity, you must follow best-interests decision-making and involve appropriate advocacy/support services rather than defaulting to Mark.

C is appropriate because Dr Banerjee is your senior and can advise on capacity, consent and whether Emily’s concern suggests safeguarding. Documenting your rationale is important in ethically complex situations.

B is unsafe and unlawful: Mark cannot simply sign to “save time” if Mrs Thompson has capacity, and even if she lacks capacity, consent is not transferred to a support worker. D is punitive and risks harming Mrs Thompson. E involves inappropriate restraint. H uses antibiotics as a convenience measure, risking poor antimicrobial stewardship and delaying definitive care. G dismisses a potential safeguarding indicator and undermines team concerns.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s own informed decision when they have capacity.
Capacity assessments are decision-specific; undue influence must be considered.
Seek senior advice and document reasoning in ethically complex cases.
Safeguarding concerns raised by colleagues should be explored, not dismissed.

scenario: You are a DCT on an OMFS ward during a busy afternoon. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to rewrite a discharge prescription for Mr Petrov, who had incision and drainage under GA and is due to go home. The junior doctor has left, and the ward is short-staffed. Rachel says the pharmacist, Dr Shaw, is refusing to dispense because the chart is incomplete and the antibiotic dose seems high for Mr Petrov’s weight. Dr Lewis (registrar) is in theatre and has previously told the team to “sort discharges without interrupting”. Mr Petrov is frustrated and says he will leave without medication. Meanwhile, Angela (another nurse) tells you a new admission with facial cellulitis has just arrived and needs clerking. You have not prescribed independently on this ward before and are unsure of the local antibiotic protocol.

options.A: Rewrite the prescription exactly as the incomplete chart shows so Dr Shaw will have something to dispense, and ask Rachel to “sort the rest” later.
options.B: Contact Dr Lewis in theatre with a concise SBAR summary, explain you are not confident with the dose/protocol, and ask for confirmation or alternative plan before prescribing.
options.C: Ask Dr Shaw to dispense the antibiotics “as a one-off” and promise you will correct the paperwork after Mr Petrov has left.
options.D: Prioritise clerking the new cellulitis admission immediately and tell Rachel discharge can wait until the registrar is free.
options.E: Work with Rachel and Dr Shaw to verify Mr Petrov’s weight, allergies and local protocol (e.g., ward guideline), and only prescribe once details are confirmed and documented.

explanation:
E is most appropriate because it addresses the immediate safety risk: Dr Shaw has identified a potentially incorrect dose and incomplete documentation. Collaborating with Rachel and Dr Shaw to confirm weight, allergies and the relevant guideline supports safe prescribing, good interprofessional working and defensible records.

B is next: you recognise limits of competence and escalate appropriately to Dr Lewis using a structured summary. Although Dr Lewis prefers not to be interrupted, patient safety and safe prescribing justify escalation.

D is reasonable but less appropriate than E/B because delaying discharge may frustrate Mr Petrov, yet the new cellulitis admission could deteriorate. However, you should not abandon the discharge issue without ensuring someone is addressing it; prioritisation must consider both patients.

C is inappropriate: asking Dr Shaw to dispense “as a one-off” undermines pharmacy safety checks and creates governance risk. A is least appropriate because copying an incomplete or potentially unsafe prescription breaches professional standards and could directly harm Mr Petrov. Overall, the safest approach is to verify key information, use guidelines, and escalate when unsure rather than rushing to maintain flow.
learningPoints: Safe prescribing requires accurate patient details, allergy status and clear documentation.
Pharmacy queries are safety interventions and should be taken seriously.
Recognise limits of competence and escalate despite hierarchy or workflow pressure.
Prioritise multiple patients by clinical risk, not convenience or frustration.

scenario: You are on a restorative clinic in a dental hospital. Dr Chen (consultant) is running behind and asks you to start Mr Clarke’s appointment while she finishes a difficult case. Mr Clarke is scheduled for crown preparation but arrives upset, saying he has read online that “dentists overcharge the NHS” and wants a guarantee the crown will be done in one visit. Lucy (dental nurse) shows you the notes: the consent form is unsigned and the medical history was last updated 18 months ago. Mr Clarke’s partner is in the room and keeps answering questions for him. Midway through your discussion, Laura (hygienist) quietly tells you she overheard Dr Chen joking in the staff room about Mr Clarke being “a nightmare complainer” and worries it could escalate if he complains. You need to maintain professionalism, ensure valid consent, and keep the clinic moving without undermining your consultant or inflaming the patient’s distrust.

options.A: Update Mr Clarke’s medical history and confirm his understanding of the planned procedure, risks, benefits and alternatives, and ensure consent is obtained before any irreversible treatment.
options.B: Reassure Mr Clarke that NHS dentistry is not about money and tell him Dr Chen said he is “overreacting”, to put him in his place.
options.C: Ask Mr Clarke privately whether he wants his partner present, and direct questions to him to support autonomy and reduce undue influence.
options.D: Tell Laura to raise her concern about Dr Chen’s comment directly with Mr Clarke so he knows the team is on his side.
options.E: Start crown preparation immediately to catch up time, and ask Lucy to get the consent form signed at the end once Mr Clarke has calmed down.

explanation:
A, C and F are most appropriate because they focus on patient-centred care and professionalism. A is essential: Lucy has identified an unsigned consent form and outdated medical history. You must update the history and obtain informed consent before any irreversible steps such as crown preparation.

C supports Mr Clarke’s autonomy and confidentiality by checking whether he wants his partner present and ensuring questions are directed to him, reducing the risk of undue influence. This also helps de-escalate an already distrustful interaction.

F appropriately manages expectations. Guarantees about single-visit completion may be unrealistic; explaining limitations honestly and involving Dr Chen supports shared decision-making without undermining your consultant.

E is unsafe and breaches consent standards. B is unprofessional and likely to escalate conflict. D is inappropriate: involving the patient in internal staff comments risks undermining trust and does not follow appropriate routes for raising concerns. G dismisses a professionalism issue raised by Laura. H is defensive and biased documentation; notes should be factual and objective, not pejorative, even when anticipating a complaint.
learningPoints: Do not begin irreversible treatment without updated medical history and valid informed consent.
Support patient autonomy by managing third-party presence and directing communication to the patient.
Be honest about realistic outcomes and involve seniors when expectations are challenging.
Maintain professional integrity: document objectively and handle staff concerns through appropriate channels.

scenario: You are the on-call DCT covering trauma and emergency dental overnight. At 02:00, Sophie (10) arrives in ED after a fall, with an avulsed upper incisor in milk. Her father is angry, saying the wait is “unacceptable”, and he refuses X-rays because “radiation is dangerous”. Elena (radiographer) says she can fit Sophie in now, but only if the request is completed correctly; she has had repeated incorrect dental requests tonight. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is off-site and reachable by phone, while Dr Grant (registrar) is managing a mandibular fracture in theatre. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you Sophie looks very anxious and keeps pulling away, and the ED nurse is asking when you will discharge the family because cubicles are needed. You are confident with splinting but less experienced with managing distressed children and consent disputes.

options.A: Proceed to replant and splint immediately without radiographs to avoid delays and because the tooth is already out, documenting the father’s refusal.
options.B: Call Dr O’Connor for urgent advice on consent, safeguarding considerations if the father’s refusal risks harm, and the clinical plan for avulsion management, and document the discussion.
options.C: Ask Chloe to spend time using age-appropriate reassurance and distraction with Sophie, and consider topical/local anaesthetic options to reduce distress before attempting treatment.
options.D: Tell the father that if he refuses X-rays you will not treat Sophie at all, and ask ED security to remove him from the cubicle.
options.E: Complete the radiograph request carefully with Elena, explain to the father why imaging is recommended and how dose is minimised, and explore his concerns while keeping Sophie comfortable.

explanation:
E is most appropriate because it balances clinical safety, consent and teamworking. For dental trauma, imaging may be important to assess root development, intrusion, fractures or foreign bodies. Working with Elena to complete the request correctly respects radiology governance, and explaining dose minimisation helps address the father’s concerns and support informed consent.

B is next: this is a high-stakes, time-pressured situation with a consent dispute. Calling Dr O’Connor demonstrates recognising limits, seeking senior input and considering whether refusal could place Sophie at risk. Clear documentation of advice and decisions is essential.

C is also appropriate: Chloe’s support with reassurance and pain control can improve cooperation and reduce the need for coercion, enabling safer care.

A is less appropriate: proceeding without imaging may be justifiable in exceptional circumstances, but here radiography is available immediately and the father’s refusal should be explored and escalated first. D is least appropriate: threatening to withhold all treatment and involving security is disproportionate and likely to escalate conflict; it does not reflect patient-centred care. Any safeguarding or capacity concerns should be managed through appropriate clinical and organisational pathways, not punitive confrontation.
learningPoints: Manage consent disputes by explaining risks/benefits and documenting discussions; escalate when needed.
Work effectively with radiology and follow request governance to support patient safety.
Use child-centred communication and anxiety management to enable safe treatment.
Under pressure, avoid coercive or punitive responses; prioritise proportionate de-escalation and escalation pathways.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in an NHS oral surgery clinic. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is running late after an emergency call, and Dr Lewis (registrar) asks you to keep the list moving. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Clarke is booked for an extraction but the referral letter mentions “previous anaphylaxis to penicillin” while the electronic record shows “no known drug allergies”. Mr Clarke is irritated about waiting and says he has “never had a problem with antibiotics” and wants the tooth out today because he starts a new job tomorrow. The chart also shows a prescription for amoxicillin from an urgent care visit last year, but there is no reaction documented. Dr Lewis suggests you proceed and “sort the allergy later” because two sedation patients are waiting. You feel pressure to avoid delays, but you are unsure which record is correct and whether any prophylactic antibiotics were planned.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction as planned but avoid prescribing any antibiotics, and ask Sarah to update the allergy status after the appointment.
options.B: Pause the procedure and take a focused allergy history from Mr Clarke, then document it and, if uncertainty remains, contact the urgent care provider or GP before proceeding.
options.C: Ask Dr Lewis to take over immediately and, while he extracts, you call Dr Banerjee to inform him of the discrepancy in the allergy records.
options.D: Tell Mr Clarke the extraction cannot be done today due to missing information, rebook him, and ask reception (Nadia) to obtain the correct allergy details later.
options.E: Proceed with local anaesthetic and start the extraction, planning to clarify the allergy status during the procedure to minimise delay.

explanation:
The safest priority is resolving the conflicting allergy information before an invasive procedure and any potential prescribing. Option B is most appropriate: taking a focused allergy history from Mr Clarke, documenting clearly, and seeking corroboration if uncertainty remains aligns with patient safety, accurate records, and informed consent. It also supports Sarah’s concern and reduces the risk of harm if antibiotics or other drugs are needed.

Option C is reasonable because it escalates the safety concern to Dr Lewis/Dr Banerjee and ensures senior oversight when you are under pressure; however, it inappropriately suggests Dr Lewis proceeds while you chase information, which could still expose the patient to risk.

Option A is less appropriate: avoiding antibiotics does not fully address the discrepancy, and allergic risk is not limited to antibiotics alone; proceeding without clarity undermines documentation standards.

Option D may be necessary if information cannot be clarified, but immediately cancelling without attempting an on-the-spot history and senior discussion is disproportionate and not patient-centred given Mr Clarke’s circumstances.

Option E is least appropriate because starting treatment before clarifying key safety information prioritises throughput over safety and consent, and it normalises bypassing concerns raised by Sarah and the records.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy documentation must be clarified and recorded before treatment or prescribing.
Patient safety and informed consent take precedence over time pressure and list targets.
Support and act on concerns raised by dental nurses and other team members.
Escalate to seniors when uncertainty persists or pressure risks unsafe decisions.

scenario: You are in an NHS community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Hassan attends with her adult son, who insists on speaking for her. Laura (hygienist) has just completed periodontal charting and reports that Mrs Hassan winced repeatedly and whispered that she “doesn’t want him to know everything”. Mrs Hassan has moderate learning disability and limited English; the usual interpreter has not arrived due to sickness. The son demands an immediate extraction of a painful molar and asks you to “just give antibiotics too” because she is “always better with them”. He becomes defensive when you ask to speak to Mrs Hassan directly and says he has power of attorney, but he cannot produce documents. Receptionist Olivia quietly tells you the son has previously complained about staff “wasting time”. You are balancing pain management, consent capacity, confidentiality, and the risk of delaying care.

options.A: Ask Olivia to arrange a telephone/video interpreter urgently and, while waiting, take Mrs Hassan to a private room with Laura present to assess her understanding and wishes.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction based on the son’s request because he appears to know her preferences and the clinic is overbooked.
options.C: Offer analgesia advice and a temporary dressing if appropriate, and explain to the son that antibiotics are not routinely indicated; document the clinical reasoning.
options.D: Ask the son to leave immediately and threaten to call security if he refuses, before you have spoken to Mrs Hassan alone.
options.E: Contact the safeguarding lead for advice because of the controlling behaviour and the patient’s statement about not wanting him to know everything.

explanation:
The most appropriate actions prioritise valid consent, confidentiality, and safety while managing pain. A is appropriate because arranging an interpreter and creating privacy (with Laura present) helps you communicate directly with Mrs Hassan, explore what she meant by not wanting her son to know details, and reduce coercion. It is also a practical step under NHS equality duties to make reasonable adjustments for language and learning disability.
