It partially addresses the risk while still meeting Mr Clarke’s urgent pain needs, but it is weaker than B because it does not resolve the discrepancy before decisions are made.

Rebooking (C) may be necessary if you cannot obtain a reliable history or consent, but it risks leaving Mr Clarke in pain and may be disproportionate if the issue can be resolved promptly.

Asking Sarah to proceed despite her concern (A) undermines teamwork and ignores a safety red flag.

Prescribing based on NKDA alone (E) is least appropriate; it risks preventable harm and falls short of professional standards on safe prescribing and documentation.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy documentation must be actively clarified and recorded before prescribing.
Valid consent requires an up-to-date, completed medical history and patient understanding.
Team members’ safety concerns (e.g., from the dental nurse) should be taken seriously and addressed.
Patient safety takes priority over clinic throughput and external pressure.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Hassan for multiple extractions under local anaesthetic. Emily (dental nurse) tells you the interpreter booked for Arabic has not arrived. Mrs Hassan speaks limited English and is accompanied by her adult son, who insists on translating and repeatedly answers questions for her. He becomes irritated when you ask Mrs Hassan directly and says, “Just do it—she’s been waiting weeks.” Dr Chen (consultant) is in another room with a medical emergency and cannot attend immediately. Mrs Hassan looks confused, nods frequently, and flinches when you mention “removing teeth,” but her son says she understands. The clinic is already overrunning and Olivia (receptionist) warns that transport is waiting to take Mrs Hassan home within 30 minutes.

options.A: Explain to the son that you cannot proceed without confirming Mrs Hassan’s understanding, and attempt to use the hospital telephone interpreting service immediately.
options.B: Proceed with extractions because the son is present and appears to understand the plan, documenting that he translated.
options.C: Offer to complete only a simple check-up today and rearrange treatment when a professional interpreter is available, providing interim pain management advice.
options.D: Ask Emily to bring in another bilingual patient from the waiting room to interpret so you can keep to time.
options.E: Ask Mrs Hassan to sign the consent form now to avoid losing the transport slot, then clarify details during the procedure.

explanation:
Using a professional interpreting route (A) is central to valid consent: Mrs Hassan must understand the nature, risks, benefits, and alternatives. Reliance on her son risks inaccurate translation, coercion, and breaches of confidentiality, particularly as he is answering for her and applying pressure. Attempting telephone interpreting is a practical NHS solution when face-to-face interpreting fails.

Escalating to an appropriate senior (F) is also appropriate. With Dr Chen unavailable, involving Dr Lewis supports safe decision-making and demonstrates recognising limits of competence in a high-stakes consent situation.

Addressing the system constraint (G) helps prioritise patient-centred care without unnecessarily cancelling treatment. Liaising with Olivia to delay transport creates time to complete consent properly and reduces the pressure that could lead to unsafe shortcuts.

Proceeding with the son translating (B) or signing first and clarifying later (E) undermines informed consent and exposes Mrs Hassan to harm and the team to complaint/medico-legal risk. Using another patient to interpret (D) is inappropriate and breaches confidentiality. Rearranging (C) may be necessary if interpreting cannot be secured, but it should follow attempts to obtain professional support and senior input. Asking the son to leave to “go faster” (H) is disrespectful and does not solve the communication barrier.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires patient understanding; use professional interpreters rather than family members when possible.
Escalate consent and capacity concerns to an appropriate senior when supervision is limited.
Manage practical barriers (e.g., transport timing) to avoid unsafe shortcuts.
Confidentiality and avoidance of coercion are key when relatives are involved in communication.

scenario: On an OMFS ward, you are asked to review Mr Petrov, who had surgery yesterday. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you she overheard Dr Grant (registrar) joking at the nurses’ station about Mr Petrov’s “smell” and showing a photo of the surgical site on his personal phone to two junior staff. Mr Petrov’s partner is visiting and looks upset because she thinks staff are “laughing at him.” Dr Grant is otherwise clinically competent and has been supportive of you, but he is currently in theatre and not immediately reachable. Angela (ward sister) says she is worried this could escalate into a formal complaint and asks what you plan to do. You also need to assess Mr Petrov’s pain control and review his post-op antibiotics before the next drug round.

options.A: Prioritise reviewing Mr Petrov’s clinical needs now, then promptly inform Angela of the concern and document a factual account of what Rachel reported.
options.B: Confront Dr Grant in theatre immediately and demand he deletes the photo in front of you, regardless of disruption.
options.C: Reassure Mr Petrov’s partner that it was “just banter” to calm her down and avoid a complaint.
options.D: Ask Rachel to submit a DATIX and leave the matter with her because she witnessed it directly.
options.E: Speak to Dr Grant privately at the earliest safe opportunity, explain the concern about dignity/confidentiality, and escalate via the consultant (Dr O’Connor) or governance route if needed.

explanation:
Action A is most appropriate because it balances immediate patient safety with professional responsibilities. Mr Petrov needs pain and antibiotic review before the drug round; delaying could cause harm. After addressing urgent care, informing Angela and documenting a factual record of Rachel’s report supports transparency and appropriate escalation.

E is also highly appropriate: speaking to Dr Grant privately at the earliest safe opportunity allows you to address unprofessional behaviour, reinforce standards on patient dignity and confidentiality, and seek remediation. If the concern is substantiated or not resolved, escalating to Dr O’Connor or governance aligns with NHS expectations and professional integrity.

D has some merit because incident reporting may be required, but asking Rachel to do it and “leaving it with her” risks abdicating responsibility; as the recipient of the concern, you should ensure it is handled appropriately.

B is less appropriate: confronting Dr Grant in theatre could compromise patient safety and is unlikely to be constructive, even though deletion of inappropriate images is important.

C is least appropriate: minimising the issue as “banter” dismisses the partner’s concern, undermines trust, and fails to address potential breaches of confidentiality and dignity.
learningPoints: Address immediate clinical safety needs while ensuring professionalism concerns are escalated appropriately.
Patient images must not be shared on personal devices; confidentiality and dignity are non-negotiable.
Document and report concerns factually through appropriate governance routes.
Challenge unprofessional behaviour in a timely, proportionate, and safe manner.

scenario: In the radiology department, you have requested a dental panoramic radiograph for Tyler, a 9‑year‑old referred to paediatric oral surgery. Elena (radiographer) refuses, saying the request form is missing justification and the clinical question is unclear. Tyler’s mother is becoming angry in the waiting area and says Mark (receptionist) told her it would be “quick.” You are aware Dr Banerjee (consultant) wants the OPG today to plan a GA list, and the paediatric clinic will close in 20 minutes. Chloe (dental nurse) is with you and says Tyler is very anxious and may not cooperate if he has to come back. You feel pressured to persuade Elena, but you also recognise radiation safety rules and the need for proper documentation.

options.A: Clarify the clinical indication and question on the request immediately, updating the form with relevant history and discussing it with Elena to meet IR(ME)R requirements.
options.B: Tell Elena that Dr Banerjee “needs it today,” and ask her to proceed as an exception because the clinic is closing.
options.C: Ask Chloe to stay with Tyler to use reassurance techniques while you contact Dr Banerjee or Dr Fernandes (registrar) for the required justification wording.
options.D: Tell Tyler’s mother that radiology are being difficult and advise her to complain to PALS.
options.E: Offer to take Tyler straight to the scanner and position him yourself to speed things up, leaving Elena to press the button.

explanation:
A is appropriate because it directly addresses Elena’s valid concern: under IR(ME)R, imaging must be justified with a clear clinical question and relevant history. Completing the request properly supports patient safety and professional accountability.

C is also appropriate: involving a senior such as Dr Banerjee or Dr Fernandes to confirm the indication and wording is efficient and demonstrates good teamwork, while Chloe supports Tyler’s anxiety and cooperation. It also helps manage competing priorities between radiology, clinic time, and patient distress.

F is appropriate patient-centred communication. A clear apology and explanation to Tyler’s mother maintains trust, reduces conflict, and sets expectations while you work on a safe solution.

B and H inappropriately pressure radiology or provide inadequate justification, risking unsafe practice. E is unsafe and outside role boundaries. D escalates conflict and undermines interdepartmental working. G abandons the issue without attempting to resolve it, potentially delaying necessary care and increasing Tyler’s anxiety.
learningPoints: Radiographic requests must include clear justification and clinical questions to meet IR(ME)R standards.
Work collaboratively with radiology and seniors rather than applying pressure to bypass safety processes.
Manage anxious children and distressed relatives with honest, calm explanations and a clear plan.
Delegate appropriately within the team to balance competing clinical demands.

scenario: During an oral surgery sedation clinic, you are the DCT assisting Dr Ahmed (consultant) with IV sedation. A last-minute cancellation means you are asked to start the next patient, Mrs Thompson, while Dr Ahmed is called to review a deteriorating patient in recovery. Lucy (dental nurse) says Mrs Thompson is extremely anxious and is asking whether she can “have the same sedation” as her friend did. The notes include a brief medical history but the pre-sedation assessment form is incomplete, and the last recorded BP was taken six months ago. Mrs Thompson mentions she started a new beta-blocker last week but is unsure of the dose. The clinic is running late and Mark (receptionist) warns that transport for another patient will leave soon. Lucy says, “We can just get the cannula in to save time.

options.A: Ask Lucy to cannulate Mrs Thompson while you prepare drugs, so you can start promptly before Dr Ahmed returns.
options.B: Explain to Mrs Thompson that you cannot proceed without a full assessment and up-to-date observations, and wait for Dr Ahmed or an appropriately trained clinician before initiating sedation.
options.C: Take and document current observations, complete the pre-sedation assessment as far as you can, and contact Dr Ahmed with the beta-blocker uncertainty before any sedation steps.
options.D: Proceed with cannulation and begin titrating sedation slowly, planning to update Dr Ahmed once he is back from recovery.
options.E: Ask Mark to tell the next patient that there will be a delay due to safety checks, and request that transport timings are adjusted if possible.

explanation:
C is the most appropriate initial action: you can safely gather essential information within your competence—current observations, medication details, and completion of the assessment form—and then escalate the unresolved beta-blocker uncertainty to Dr Ahmed. This supports clinical safety and good documentation.

B is also appropriate because initiating sedation without appropriate supervision and a complete assessment is unsafe. Recognising limits of competence is crucial in sedation, particularly with incomplete paperwork and medication changes.

E appropriately manages system pressures by communicating delays and attempting to adjust transport, rather than allowing time pressure to drive unsafe practice.

A is less appropriate: asking Lucy to cannulate may be within her skills in some settings, but it risks drifting into “getting started” without the required assessment and supervision, and may pressure staff into unsafe shortcuts.

D is least appropriate: starting titration without senior oversight and without a complete assessment breaches safe sedation standards and exposes Mrs Thompson to avoidable risk, especially given the unknown beta-blocker dose and outdated observations.
learningPoints: Do not initiate sedation without a full, current assessment and appropriate supervision.
Work within your competence: gather observations and history, then escalate uncertainties promptly.
Time pressure and transport constraints should be managed transparently, not by bypassing safety steps.
Accurate documentation is integral to safe sedation practice and informed consent.

scenario: You are the DCT in an oral surgery clinic. Dr Kumar (registrar) is running late after an emergency call, and the waiting room is full. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan’s notes list a penicillin allergy, but the referral letter from his GDP suggests he “tolerated amoxicillin last year”. Mr Khan is anxious and insists he needs antibiotics today because “my wedding is this weekend”. He has facial swelling but is afebrile and can open 25 mm. Dr Kumar has texted: “Start him on co‑amoxiclav and I’ll review later.” Sarah looks uncomfortable and says she is not happy dispensing without clarifying the allergy. Meanwhile, Emily (another nurse) asks you to help with a post‑op bleed in the next surgery.

options.A: Ask Sarah to phone Mr Khan’s GDP immediately for clarification while you write a co‑amoxiclav prescription so it is ready if the allergy is confirmed as incorrect.
options.B: Explain to Mr Khan that antibiotic choice depends on a confirmed allergy history, take a focused allergy history yourself, and if uncertainty remains, contact Dr Kumar or the on‑call consultant before prescribing.
options.C: Follow Dr Kumar’s text instruction, prescribe co‑amoxiclav now to avoid delay, and document that you acted on senior advice.
options.D: Tell Mr Khan you cannot help today due to the post‑op bleed next door, ask reception (Nadia) to rebook him, and move to the next patient.
options.E: Prescribe metronidazole as a compromise because it avoids penicillin, and advise Mr Khan to return if worse; you can update Dr Kumar later.

explanation:
The priority is safe prescribing and working within your competence under NHS standards. Option B is most appropriate: you take ownership of a focused allergy history with Mr Khan (what reaction, when, severity), explain the rationale, and escalate promptly to Dr Kumar or the on‑call consultant if uncertainty persists. This balances patient-centred care with clinical safety and respects Sarah’s concern.

A is helpful but second-best because writing a prescription before confirming the allergy risks anchoring the team toward an unsafe plan; however, contacting the GDP for corroboration is reasonable alongside your own history.

E may be appropriate if antibiotics are genuinely indicated and penicillin allergy is suspected, but it is weaker because it bypasses senior input and clinical assessment of whether antibiotics are needed at all (swelling without systemic signs may require operative management, not antibiotics).

C is unsafe: acting on a text to prescribe co‑amoxiclav with an unresolved allergy concern exposes Mr Khan to potential harm and undermines professional accountability.

D is least appropriate: deferring care without safety-netting or assessment ignores a potentially worsening infection and does not appropriately manage competing priorities; you should triage the bleed but ensure Mr Khan is assessed and escalated.
learningPoints: Allergy status must be actively clarified before prescribing; uncertainty requires escalation.
Patient pressure and time constraints do not justify unsafe prescribing.
Team members’ safety concerns (e.g., Sarah’s) should be taken seriously and addressed respectfully.
Antibiotics should be prescribed only when clinically indicated, with appropriate documentation and safety-netting.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson for multiple extractions under IV sedation. Laura (hygienist) has been working with her for months and says Mrs Thompson becomes very distressed with unfamiliar staff. Today, the interpreter booked for Mrs Thompson’s preferred language has not arrived, and reception (Olivia) says the agency cannot provide anyone for at least an hour. Dr Chen (consultant) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Mrs Thompson’s adult son insists he can translate and is pushing to “just get it done” because he has taken time off work. Mrs Thompson looks withdrawn and avoids eye contact; when you ask if she is happy for her son to translate, she nods but does not speak. Chloe (sedation nurse) reminds you the list is overbooked and another medically complex patient is waiting.

options.A: Proceed with sedation using the son as interpreter, documenting that Mrs Thompson agreed, to avoid wasting the list time.
options.B: Ask Olivia to re-contact the interpreting agency and explore telephone/video interpreting immediately, explaining to the son that you need an independent interpreter for consent.
options.C: Speak to Mrs Thompson alone with Laura present, using simple English and visual aids, to assess her understanding and whether she feels pressured by her son.
options.D: Cancel the whole list to avoid any consent risk and ask Chloe to send all patients home with new dates.
options.E: Delay sedation and contact Dr Chen’s secretary to request urgent advice, then proceed only if Dr Chen replies before the next patient is due.

explanation:
Valid consent requires that Mrs Thompson understands the proposed sedation and extractions and is free from undue influence. B is appropriate because arranging telephone/video interpreting via Olivia maintains independence and supports informed consent while trying to preserve clinic flow.

C is also appropriate: speaking to Mrs Thompson away from her son (with Laura as a familiar, supportive staff member) helps you assess capacity, comprehension, and potential coercion. It addresses the interpersonal dynamic without being confrontational and aligns with patient-centred care.

F is appropriate because it offers meaningful care today (assessment, pain management, planning) without proceeding to an irreversible sedation/extraction when communication is compromised.

A and G are inappropriate: relying on the son to interpret or sign consent risks inaccurate translation and coercion, and next-of-kin cannot consent for a capacitous adult. H is unprofessional and may escalate conflict and distress. D is disproportionate; you should manage risk without cancelling everyone. E may help but is too passive and time-dependent; you still need an interpreter solution and a plan even if Dr Chen is unavailable.
learningPoints: Consent is only valid when the patient can understand, retain, and weigh information without undue pressure.
Family members should not be used as interpreters for consent-sensitive discussions except in exceptional circumstances with safeguards.
Offer safe alternatives (assessment/pain control) when definitive treatment cannot proceed safely.
Manage interpersonal dynamics calmly while prioritising the patient’s autonomy and dignity.

scenario: On OMFS ward cover, you review Mr Petrov post-operatively after drainage of a deep neck space infection. Angela (ward nurse) asks you to rewrite the drug chart because the antibiotic dose is unclear. While you are checking the chart, Dr Grant (registrar) arrives and quietly tells you he “adjusted the dose last night” but did not document it because he was “too busy” and didn’t want to “get pulled into questions”. Rachel (staff nurse) overhears and looks concerned. Mr Petrov’s wife is at the bedside and asks what antibiotics he has been given, as she read online about kidney damage. Dr Grant says, in front of her, “Just tell her it’s standard—don’t worry about details.” You feel pressure to protect the registrar relationship, but the documentation gap could affect patient safety and trust.

options.A: Clarify the intended antibiotic regimen with Dr Grant, update the drug chart accurately, and ensure the change is documented with date/time and prescriber details; then inform Angela and Rachel of the confirmed plan.
options.B: Tell Mr Petrov’s wife you cannot discuss medications and ask her to wait for Dr Grant on the next ward round.
options.C: Document in the notes that Dr Grant verbally informed you of a dose change, and ask him to countersign later; if he refuses, escalate to the consultant on call.
options.D: Ignore the missing documentation to avoid conflict, rewrite the chart with what you believe is correct, and move on to the trauma bleep.
options.E: Challenge Dr Grant loudly at the bedside about unsafe practice so that the team understands documentation matters.

explanation:
A is most appropriate: it addresses immediate patient safety by confirming the correct antibiotic regimen, updating the chart properly, and communicating clearly with Angela and Rachel. Accurate prescribing documentation is essential for continuity of care and governance.

C is next: recording that you received a verbal instruction is better than leaving an undocumented change, and it sets a clear expectation for Dr Grant to countersign. If he refuses, escalation is justified to protect Mr Petrov and the wider team.

B is less appropriate but preferable to misinformation: you should not refuse discussion entirely, but if you are unsure due to unclear documentation, it is reasonable to defer detailed answers until you have confirmed the regimen; you should still acknowledge her concerns and offer to return with accurate information.

E is unhelpful: challenging Dr Grant publicly at the bedside risks undermining trust and escalating conflict in front of Mr Petrov’s wife; concerns should be raised professionally and privately unless there is an immediate danger.

D is least appropriate: rewriting based on assumption and ignoring the documentation gap risks medication error and breaches professional integrity, especially under time pressure from the trauma bleep.
learningPoints: Medication changes must be clearly documented and traceable to a prescriber.
Address professionalism concerns with colleagues constructively while prioritising patient safety.
Be honest with patients/relatives: provide accurate information or explain you will confirm and return.
Time pressure and hierarchy do not justify unsafe shortcuts.

scenario: In the radiology department, you have brought Mrs Singh from the restorative clinic for a CBCT requested by Dr O’Connor (consultant) to plan an implant. Elena (radiographer) reviews the request and says the justification is insufficient and the referral lacks a recent periapical radiograph. Mrs Singh is already in a gown and becomes upset, saying she took unpaid leave and “can’t come back”. Mark (radiographer) adds that the CBCT list is running behind and they cannot “squeeze in repeats” if paperwork is wrong. You phone Dr O’Connor’s clinic, but Nadia (receptionist) says he is with a patient and “won’t take calls”. Mrs Singh asks you to “just tell them to do it” and starts crying. Elena remains firm but not rude; she says she is happy to discuss if you can provide clinical details.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed with the CBCT as a one-off, explaining the patient has already attended and it will save another visit.
options.B: Provide Elena with the clinical justification you know (implant planning, suspected proximity to the sinus/IAN), and ask if a limited field-of-view CBCT could be justified to minimise dose if criteria are met.
options.C: Offer Mrs Singh a private space, acknowledge her frustration, and explain why radiology must follow justification rules; then agree a plan for next steps and timings.
options.D: Email Dr O’Connor after the clinic with the issue and ask radiology to rebook Mrs Singh without further action today.
options.E: Ask Mark to take a quick periapical radiograph in radiology now, attach it to the request, and then re-present the CBCT request for justification.

explanation:
B supports effective interdepartmental communication and radiation safety: you provide Elena with specific clinical justification and explore dose-minimising alternatives (e.g., limited FOV) consistent with IR(ME)R principles. You are not overruling the radiographers; you are enabling a safe decision.

C is appropriate because Mrs Singh is distressed and feels trapped by the system. Offering privacy, acknowledging inconvenience, and explaining governance requirements maintains trust and patient-centred care while avoiding blame.

F is appropriate when resolution is time-critical and the referrer is unavailable. Escalating to the radiology duty lead allows an authorised decision on whether the scan can proceed or must be rebooked.

A is unsafe and undermines radiology governance. D is too passive and leaves the patient unsupported today. E may be possible in some pathways, but asking radiology to take additional imaging purely to “fix paperwork” risks unnecessary exposure and may not be within their protocol. G damages working relationships and misrepresents the situation. H abandons the patient and escalates distress.
learningPoints: Radiation requests must be justified; patient inconvenience does not override governance.
Communicate specific clinical details to support safe imaging decisions and consider dose reduction.
Manage patient distress with empathy and clear explanations without blaming other departments.
Escalate appropriately when cross-department resolution is needed and the referrer is unavailable.

scenario: During an emergency dental clinic, you assess Daniel, a 7‑year‑old with a swollen cheek and fever. His mother, Mrs Hassan, is anxious and says he has not eaten all day. Lucy (dental nurse) reports Daniel’s medical history includes asthma and a previous hospital admission for an allergic reaction, but the details are vague in the notes. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in an MDT meeting and has asked not to be disturbed unless “critical”. Dr Lewis (specialty dentist) is covering another clinic and is not immediately available. Daniel is crying and will not allow you to examine properly; Mrs Hassan demands antibiotics and says she will “go to A&E” if you refuse. The waiting room is full, and Mark (receptionist) tells you two more children are booked in the next 15 minutes.

options.A: Attempt to complete treatment by extracting the likely source tooth despite limited examination, to avoid delay and reduce infection risk.
options.B: Ask Lucy to obtain more detail on the previous allergic reaction from Mrs Hassan while you assess Daniel’s vital signs and look for red flags (trismus, dysphagia, voice change), then decide on escalation.
options.C: Prescribe a broad-spectrum antibiotic immediately to satisfy Mrs Hassan and move to the next patient, advising review in 48 hours.
options.D: Call Dr Banerjee briefly with a focused summary of red flags and your concerns, and agree whether Daniel needs same-day OMFS/paediatric assessment or can be managed in clinic with safety-netting.
options.E: Advise Mrs Hassan to take Daniel to A&E without assessment because he is febrile and uncooperative, and document that she requested antibiotics.

explanation:
B is most appropriate: you gather essential information quickly (including allergy history) and assess for clinical red flags and physiological compromise. This is safe triage under pressure and uses Lucy effectively.

D is next: escalating to Dr Banerjee with a concise, risk-focused summary respects his request while recognising your limits and prioritising Daniel’s safety. Senior input is appropriate given potential airway risk and unclear allergy history.

E can be appropriate if red flags or inability to assess safely persists, but it is weaker because you should not default to A&E without attempting an initial assessment and clear safety-netting; however, safeguarding patient safety may require emergency escalation.

C is inappropriate: prescribing to satisfy a parent, especially with unclear allergy history and without adequate assessment, risks harm and delays definitive care.

A is least appropriate: attempting extraction in a distressed, uncooperative child without adequate examination, consent, or appropriate behaviour management/sedation support exceeds safe competence and could worsen outcomes. Managing the waiting room must not override safe care for a potentially unwell child.
learningPoints: Recognise red flags and prioritise triage in unwell children; assess airway risk early.
Use the team to obtain critical history (including allergies) under time pressure.
Escalate to seniors appropriately when assessment is limited or risk is uncertain.
Do not provide antibiotics or invasive treatment to appease relatives when safety is unclear.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. The waiting room is full and reception (Nadia) tells you there are two walk‑ins. Your dental nurse, Sarah, quietly flags that Mr Khan in chair 2 has “penicillin allergy” written on an old GDP letter, but today’s triage sheet lists “NKDA”. Mr Khan is in pain with a swelling around a lower molar and is demanding antibiotics because he has a job interview tomorrow. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in another surgery and has asked you to “keep things moving” because the consultant clinic is overrunning. Mr Khan becomes irritated when Sarah asks about allergies again and says staff are “wasting time”. You are considering whether to prescribe and reappoint, or attempt drainage/extraction today. Ethical tensions include patient safety versus time pressure, and patient wishes versus clinical judgement.

options.A: Prescribe amoxicillin immediately based on the triage sheet stating NKDA, to reduce swelling quickly, and ask Nadia to book Mr Khan back in 48 hours.
options.B: Pause treatment to take a focused allergy history with Mr Khan, clarify the discrepancy with Sarah present, and document the outcome before deciding on analgesia, antibiotics or operative care.
options.C: Tell Sarah you will “sort it later” and continue with local anaesthetic and extraction to avoid delaying the clinic, as antibiotics are unlikely to help anyway.
options.D: Ask Dr Kumar to step into the room briefly to confirm the safest plan given the conflicting allergy information and Mr Khan’s insistence on antibiotics.
options.E: Explain to Mr Khan that because he is being uncooperative you will not treat him today, and ask Nadia to arrange a routine appointment with his GDP.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to clarify the allergy discrepancy with Mr Khan and document it (B). Conflicting records (old GDP letter vs triage) create a significant patient safety risk; prescribing or administering drugs without confirmation breaches safe prescribing and NHS duty of care. Involving Sarah in the discussion supports teamwork and shows you are responding appropriately to a team member’s concern.

Escalating to Dr Kumar for a brief review (D) is also appropriate because you are under time pressure and the situation involves prescribing risk and competing priorities; seeking timely supervision is consistent with recognising limits and maintaining safety.

Proceeding with extraction while deferring the allergy issue (C) is unsafe: local anaesthetic, analgesics and potential antibiotics still require an accurate allergy history, and rushing undermines consent and safety.

Prescribing amoxicillin based solely on the triage sheet (A) is poor practice; it risks harm if the allergy is real and also gives antibiotics to satisfy patient demand rather than clinical need.

Refusing care due to frustration (E) is least appropriate. Even if Mr Khan is challenging, you must manage behaviour professionally and ensure urgent needs are addressed or appropriately redirected, not withheld punitively.
learningPoints: Act on concerns raised by dental nurses and verify discrepancies in key safety information.
Safe prescribing requires an accurate allergy history and clear documentation.
Seek senior input when time pressure and risk factors make decision-making complex.
Patient demands should not drive antibiotic prescribing; prioritise clinical need and safety.

scenario: You are in a restorative clinic seeing Mrs Thompson for a planned crown preparation. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is running late and has asked you to start patients to avoid cancellations. Your dental nurse, Emily, mentions Mrs Thompson previously complained about “not being listened to”. Mrs Thompson arrives visibly anxious and says she has read online that crowns “poison the body” and wants you to place a temporary crown only, then “see how it feels”. She also refuses any radiographs because she is worried about radiation, but her last periapical is over three years old and the tooth had a deep filling. Meanwhile, the next patient is a complex referral and reception (Olivia) is asking whether to rebook them if you overrun. Ethical tensions include respecting patient autonomy versus ensuring informed consent and clinical safety, and clinic efficiency versus addressing anxiety and misinformation.

options.A: Explore Mrs Thompson’s concerns and understanding, explain risks/benefits of proceeding without a current radiograph, and check capacity to consent before deciding whether to continue today.
options.B: Proceed with preparation and fit a temporary crown as requested, documenting that Mrs Thompson declined radiographs and permanent treatment.
options.C: Ask Emily to fetch Dr O’Connor to speak to Mrs Thompson immediately so you can start the next patient while they talk.
options.D: Offer written information and a follow‑up appointment, suggesting she takes time to consider options; inform Olivia early that the next patient may need rescheduling.
options.E: Tell Mrs Thompson that NHS policy requires a radiograph and a permanent crown today, otherwise you will discharge her back to her GDP.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions focus on informed, patient-centred decision-making while maintaining safety. Exploring Mrs Thompson’s concerns, understanding and consent (A) is essential: her anxiety and misinformation mean you must ensure she understands options and implications, including the risks of proceeding without up-to-date imaging. This supports autonomy through valid consent rather than simply agreeing to demands.

Providing information and offering time to decide with a planned follow-up (D) is appropriate if consent cannot be achieved today or if safety prerequisites (like imaging) are not met. Informing Olivia early helps manage service pressures transparently and avoids last-minute disruption.

Addressing radiograph fears with clear, evidence-based explanation and offering the smallest appropriate image (F) supports shared decision-making and clinical safety. It respects autonomy while making a reasonable effort to overcome barriers.

Options B and G prioritise throughput and patient preference over safety and informed consent; documenting refusal does not make an unsafe plan acceptable. Option C inappropriately offloads the interaction to Dr O’Connor and risks undermining continuity; you can seek senior input, but not as a way to avoid patient communication. Option E is coercive. Option H is unsafe and unethical: sedation requires assessment, consent and appropriate indication, not convenience.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires patient understanding; anxiety and misinformation must be addressed, not bypassed.
Radiographs should be justified and explained; refusal may necessitate deferring treatment if safety is compromised.
Manage clinic pressures proactively (e.g., early communication with reception) without compromising patient care.
Senior support can help, but the trainee must still communicate and document appropriately.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover as a DCT2. Nurse Angela asks you to review Mr Petrov, admitted overnight with a facial cellulitis. His notes include a consent form for incision and drainage signed late last night. Mr Petrov now says he does not remember signing anything and is worried because his English is limited; his adult daughter is insisting you “just do it” because she has taken time off work. Dr Grant (registrar) is in theatre and has messaged that the list is tight and you should “get him ready”. When you look at the consent form, the risks section is incomplete and there is no record of an interpreter. Ethical tensions include respecting autonomy versus family pressure, and service efficiency versus valid consent/documentation.

options.A: Proceed to prepare Mr Petrov for theatre and reassure him that the registrar will explain everything again once he is anaesthetised.
options.B: Explain to Mr Petrov that you need to confirm consent, arrange an interpreter (or approved telephone interpreting), and update the team that surgery may be delayed until consent is valid.
options.C: Ask the daughter to translate the consent discussion to save time, and document that she interpreted.
options.D: Call Dr Grant to explain the consent concerns and incomplete documentation, and request they review Mr Petrov before proceeding if possible.
options.E: Tell Nurse Angela to proceed with pre-op checks and cannulation as planned, because consent is already signed and it is not your role to question it.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to ensure Mr Petrov has valid, informed consent (B). He does not recall signing, has limited English, and the form is incomplete with no interpreter documented. NHS standards require consent to be informed, voluntary, and given with understanding; if these conditions are not met, you must pause and rectify, even under time pressure.

Contacting Dr Grant (D) is also appropriate. As the registrar responsible for the procedure, they need to know the consent concerns and should review the patient. Escalation demonstrates professionalism and prioritises patient safety and legal/ethical requirements.

Option E is inappropriate because it dismisses your duty to raise patient safety and governance issues. While you may not have taken the original consent, you are obliged to act when you identify a problem.

Using the daughter as interpreter (C) is poor practice: it risks inaccuracies, coercion, and undermines voluntariness, especially given her strong preference to proceed. Professional interpreting should be used.

Option A is least appropriate. Reassuring Mr Petrov that explanations can wait until he is anaesthetised is ethically unacceptable and could amount to treatment without valid consent.
learningPoints: Consent must be informed, voluntary and based on understanding; a signature alone is not sufficient.
Use professional interpreters rather than family members, particularly when there is potential pressure or conflict.
Escalate promptly to the responsible clinician when documentation or consent is inadequate.
Service pressures do not justify compromising ethical and legal standards.

scenario: You are in the radiology department requesting an OPG for a trauma patient, Mr Clarke, who attended A&E after an assault. The referral from OMFS is handwritten and difficult to read. Radiographer Elena says the form does not clearly justify the exposure and the patient’s date of birth is missing, so she cannot proceed. Mr Clarke is agitated, pacing, and says he will leave if he waits any longer. Your dental nurse, Chloe, is trying to calm him but he is becoming verbally aggressive. Dr Chen (consultant) is expecting the imaging before deciding whether to admit, and the porter is waiting to take Mr Clarke back. Ethical tensions include patient safety/IR(ME)R compliance versus urgency, and staff safety versus patient-centred care.

options.A: Work with Elena to complete a clear justification and patient identifiers, checking Mr Clarke’s details on the hospital system before resubmitting the request.
options.B: Ask Chloe to physically block the exit so Mr Clarke cannot leave before the OPG is taken.
options.C: Phone Dr Chen to explain the delay and request a revised electronic referral or verbal justification that can be documented to satisfy radiology requirements.
options.D: Tell Elena that because this is trauma she must proceed immediately, and you will “sort the paperwork later” to avoid complaint.
options.E: De-escalate Mr Clarke by acknowledging his frustration, explaining the need for correct checks, and offering a realistic timeframe and pain relief plan while waiting.

explanation:
The best actions balance urgency with legal and safety requirements. Completing the referral properly with Elena (A) is appropriate because IR(ME)R requires correct patient identifiers and a clear justification before exposure. Collaborating rather than blaming supports teamwork and speeds resolution.

Escalating to Dr Chen for a revised referral or documented justification (C) is also appropriate. Dr Chen is the responsible senior and can clarify clinical need; documenting their justification helps radiology proceed safely and defensibly.

De-escalating Mr Clarke while explaining the checks (E) addresses staff safety and patient-centred care. Chloe’s efforts can be supported by calm communication, setting expectations, and ensuring pain is managed while delays occur.

Options D and H undermine professional relationships and could lead to unsafe or unlawful imaging. Option B is unsafe and inappropriate; restraining a patient without authority escalates risk. Option F and G compromise care continuity and fail to communicate with Dr Chen, risking missed injuries and poor handover. Overall, the correct choices demonstrate respectful interdepartmental communication and prioritise both patient and staff safety under pressure.
learningPoints: Radiographic exposures require correct identifiers and justification; urgency does not remove IR(ME)R duties.
Use collaborative communication to resolve cross-department barriers quickly.
De-escalation and staff safety are priorities when patients become aggressive.
Escalate appropriately to the responsible senior and document decisions and delays.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in a community dental service special care clinic. You and therapist Laura are seeing Sophie, a 7‑year‑old, for multiple caries. Sophie’s mother, Mrs Hassan, appears distracted and repeatedly answers for Sophie. During a fluoride varnish discussion, Sophie quietly tells Laura that she “doesn’t like staying at mum’s boyfriend’s house” and that he comes into her room at night. Laura looks shaken and tells you privately she is unsure if she misheard. Mrs Hassan then becomes defensive, saying Sophie “makes things up”, and insists you finish quickly because she needs to leave. Receptionist Mark says the next patient has arrived early and the clinic is short-staffed. Dr Banerjee (consultant in special care) is in another building and not immediately available. Ethical tensions include safeguarding versus maintaining rapport/confidentiality with the parent, and acting promptly versus uncertainty about the disclosure.

options.A: Reassure Mrs Hassan that Sophie is joking, complete varnish quickly, and make a note to mention it to Dr Banerjee at the next team meeting.
options.B: Ask Mrs Hassan to step into the waiting area briefly while you and Laura speak to Sophie alone, explaining this is routine to help children talk openly.
options.C: Document Sophie’s exact words and Laura’s account contemporaneously, and follow local safeguarding procedures by contacting the on-call safeguarding lead for immediate advice.
options.D: Confront Mrs Hassan directly, accusing her boyfriend of abuse, and state you will call the police now in front of Sophie.
options.E: Tell Laura not to worry because you did not hear it yourself, and continue the appointment as normal to avoid upsetting the family.

explanation:
The most appropriate priority is to act on the potential safeguarding disclosure by documenting accurately and following local safeguarding procedures (C). Sophie’s statement about an adult entering her room at night is concerning; even if details are unclear, NHS safeguarding guidance emphasises that suspicions and disclosures must be recorded verbatim and escalated promptly to the safeguarding lead for advice.

Speaking to Sophie without Mrs Hassan present (B) is appropriate to clarify what she meant in a child-centred way and to reduce parental influence, while maintaining a calm, routine framing. This also supports Laura, who is distressed, by taking the concern seriously.

Option D is less appropriate than the above because confronting Mrs Hassan with accusations in front of Sophie risks escalating conflict, compromising Sophie’s safety, and may prejudice any subsequent safeguarding assessment. However, it at least recognises seriousness, which is why it is not the very lowest.

Option E is inappropriate: you must not dismiss Laura’s concern or require personal certainty before acting. Option A is least appropriate because delaying action to a future meeting and minimising the disclosure fails your safeguarding duty and may leave Sophie at risk.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns should be documented contemporaneously using the child’s exact words and escalated via local pathways.
Do not allow time pressure or parental defensiveness to delay safeguarding action.
Create opportunities to speak to children privately when appropriate and safe.
Support colleagues who raise concerns; do not require absolute certainty before seeking safeguarding advice.

scenario: You are the DCT in an NHS oral surgery clinic. Your supervisor, Dr Ahmed, is in theatre and asked you to start the afternoon list. The first patient, Mr Petrov, is booked for extraction of a lower molar. Dental nurse Sarah quietly tells you she has found an old letter in the scanned notes mentioning a “severe reaction to penicillin,” but today’s medical history form is incomplete and Mr Petrov speaks limited English. Receptionist Nadia says two patients have already complained about delays. Mr Petrov looks anxious and keeps nodding, and his adult son insists they are “in a rush” and offers to translate. You also notice the consent form is pre-printed for local anaesthetic plus sedation, but the appointment book says “LA only.

options.A: Proceed with LA extraction as booked, and ask Mr Petrov’s son to translate the key risks while Sarah prepares the instruments so you can catch up time.
options.B: Pause the appointment, use a professional telephone interpreter to confirm Mr Petrov’s medical history (including the penicillin reaction) and what anaesthetic/sedation he is expecting, and document the discrepancy before deciding whether to proceed.
options.C: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Petrov to a later date when Dr Ahmed is available, explaining only that “there’s a paperwork issue,” and move to the next patient immediately.
options.D: Call Dr Ahmed in theatre for a quick instruction, then continue with the extraction based on his advice even if an interpreter is not available.
options.E: Ignore the old scanned letter as it may be outdated, complete the extraction, and tell Mr Petrov to update his medical history at reception afterwards.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to pause and establish a safe, informed plan. Option B prioritises patient safety and valid consent by using a professional interpreter rather than relying on Mr Petrov’s son, who may filter information under time pressure. It also addresses the ethical tension of clinic delays versus safe care and resolves the LA/sedation discrepancy before proceeding.

Option D is next best because escalating to Dr Ahmed recognises limits of competence and seeks senior input; however, it is weaker than B if it leads to proceeding without ensuring Mr Petrov understands and agrees to the correct plan. Senior advice does not replace valid consent or accurate history.

Option C may be reasonable if safe care cannot be delivered today (e.g., no interpreter and unresolved documentation), but rebooking without clear explanation risks undermining trust and does not address immediate safeguarding of consent; you should still communicate appropriately and document.

Option A is unsafe: using a relative to translate for consent and risks is poor practice, especially with anxiety and pressure. Option E is least appropriate because it dismisses a potentially serious allergy and defers essential history-taking, risking harm and breaching professional standards.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires patient understanding; use professional interpreting services rather than relatives where possible.
Patient safety overrides pressure to reduce delays or meet targets.
Resolve discrepancies in planned treatment (e.g., LA vs sedation) before proceeding and document clearly.
Seek senior support when information is unclear, but do not use senior advice to bypass consent processes.

scenario: You are on-call in the hospital emergency dental clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with severe dental pain and demands antibiotics, saying she “always gets them” from her GDP. She is visibly upset and tells dental nurse Emily that she cannot afford to miss work again. On examination you suspect irreversible pulpitis with no swelling or systemic signs; definitive treatment would be a pulpotomy or extraction, but the clinic is overbooked and Dr Kumar (registrar) is dealing with a facial swelling in another room. Receptionist Mark informs you that the next patient, Mr Clarke, has been waiting 90 minutes and is becoming angry at the desk. Mrs Thompson refuses local anaesthetic, stating she is “terrified of needles,” and asks you to “just prescribe something.

options.A: Explain to Mrs Thompson why antibiotics are not indicated, offer analgesia advice and topical measures, and re-offer LA with supportive techniques; if she still refuses, provide a clear safety-net and arrange urgent definitive care.
options.B: Prescribe a short course of antibiotics to Mrs Thompson to calm the situation and keep the clinic moving, documenting that she requested them.
options.C: Ask Emily to sit with Mrs Thompson to explore her needle phobia and discuss options such as LA techniques, while you briefly update Dr Kumar about the situation and ask if he can review when free.
options.D: Tell Mark to move Mr Clarke ahead of Mrs Thompson because he has waited longer, and ask Mrs Thompson to return another day when she is calmer.
options.E: Offer to arrange inhalation sedation immediately and proceed, even though the service is not available in the emergency clinic today.

explanation:
The best actions focus on safe, patient-centred care while upholding antimicrobial stewardship. Option A directly addresses Mrs Thompson’s request by explaining why antibiotics are not indicated for suspected irreversible pulpitis without systemic involvement, while still offering pain-management advice, re-offering LA, and providing safety-netting. This respects autonomy without giving inappropriate medication.

Option C is appropriate because Emily can support Mrs Thompson’s needle phobia and de-escalate distress, while you keep Dr Kumar informed and seek timely senior input given competing clinical priorities. This supports teamwork and coping with pressure.

Option F is also key: offering definitive options available now and documenting refusal ensures informed decision-making and protects patient safety and professional integrity.

Option B is inappropriate: prescribing to “keep things moving” breaches antimicrobial stewardship and could expose Mrs Thompson to harm and false reassurance. Option D risks unfairness and could be perceived as punitive; triage should be based on clinical need, not anger at reception. Option E promises a service you cannot deliver. Option G is disproportionate without evidence of aggression and may escalate. Option H is unnecessary and risks confidentiality and poor consent practice; standard documentation is sufficient.
learningPoints: Do not prescribe antibiotics for dental pain without signs of infection; explain reasoning and safety-net.
Address anxiety and phobias with supportive communication and team help to enable definitive care.
Offer and document definitive treatment options and refusal, ensuring informed consent.
Manage competing demands fairly through clinical triage and calm de-escalation.

scenario: You are a DCT in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Hassan, who has mild learning disability, attends with her support worker, Laura (therapist), for multiple restorations. During the appointment, dental nurse Chloe shows you a message in the staff group chat where Dr Grant (specialty dentist) has posted a photo of today’s diary with patient names visible, joking about “DNA regulars.” Chloe looks uncomfortable and says she worries this breaches confidentiality but is afraid to challenge Dr Grant. Meanwhile, Mrs Hassan becomes agitated by the noise from the next surgery and asks to stop, but Laura insists you “just get it done” because transport is arranged. The clinic is running late and the receptionist Olivia is asking whether Mrs Hassan will be finished soon.

options.A: Ask Chloe to delete the message from her phone and remind her not to share it further, then continue treating Mrs Hassan to avoid further delays.
options.B: Pause treatment to reassure Mrs Hassan and check her capacity and consent to continue today, offering breaks or rescheduling if needed, and then separately raise the group chat concern with Dr Grant or your supervisor following local policy.
options.C: Reply in the group chat telling everyone that Dr Grant has breached GDPR and that you will be reporting him, so the message is publicly addressed immediately.
options.D: Ignore the group chat issue because Dr Grant is senior, and focus on completing the restorations quickly as Laura requested.
options.E: Save a screenshot of the chat as evidence, ensure Mrs Hassan is settled and safe, and then escalate the confidentiality concern through the appropriate route (e.g., line manager/clinical governance) rather than confronting Dr Grant in front of staff.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it prioritises Mrs Hassan’s immediate needs: her agitation and request to stop raise issues of consent and capacity, especially in special care dentistry. You should reassure her, offer breaks, and ensure she understands and agrees to proceed; transport convenience and Laura’s pressure cannot override patient autonomy. B also proposes addressing the confidentiality concern separately and appropriately.

Option E is also strong: preserving evidence can be necessary for governance, and escalating via the correct route supports professional integrity and patient confidentiality. It avoids a public confrontation that could worsen team dynamics.

Option A is less appropriate: asking Chloe to delete the message may reduce dissemination but does not address the underlying breach or learning for the team. It also risks placing responsibility on Chloe rather than on the person who posted it.

Option C, while well-intentioned, is too confrontational and may breach professionalism by publicly accusing Dr Grant; it can escalate conflict and does not follow local reporting pathways. Option D is least appropriate because it ignores both the confidentiality breach and Mrs Hassan’s expressed wish to stop, risking harm and violating consent standards.
learningPoints: Confidentiality breaches (including in group chats) must be addressed through appropriate governance routes.
Patient autonomy and valid consent override convenience pressures from carers or transport arrangements.
Support junior staff who raise concerns and avoid shifting responsibility onto them.
Manage concerns about senior colleagues professionally, focusing on patient safety and learning culture.

scenario: You are in the radiology department for an urgent OPG for Mr Ibrahim, referred from the OMFS ward after a fall. The ward note from Dr Chen (consultant) requests an OPG “today” to assess possible mandibular fracture. Radiographer Elena reviews the request and says it is missing pregnancy status and clinical justification details, so she cannot proceed under IR(ME)R. Mr Ibrahim is in pain and accompanied by ward nurse Rachel, who is frustrated because the porter is booked to return him in 10 minutes. You phone Dr Lewis (registrar) for clarification, but he is in clinic and sounds irritated, saying “just get it done.” Meanwhile, reception in radiology is busy and other patients are waiting.

options.A: Work with Elena to complete the missing IR(ME)R information by checking the OMFS ward notes and asking Rachel to confirm key details, then request Dr Lewis or Dr Chen to authorise/justify the imaging appropriately.
options.B: Ask Elena to take the OPG now and promise to send the missing details later, because the request is from a consultant and the patient is in pain.
options.C: Escalate to the radiology superintendent immediately, stating Elena is obstructing urgent trauma care.
options.D: Explain to Rachel and Mr Ibrahim why the imaging cannot proceed until the request is compliant, and ask Rachel to rebook portering while you resolve the documentation with OMFS.
options.E: Write the justification yourself on the form and sign Dr Chen’s name to avoid delay, since the clinical indication is obvious.

explanation:
Option A is appropriate because it supports Elena in meeting IR(ME)R requirements and focuses on getting the request compliant using available information, while ensuring that the imaging is properly justified and authorised by an appropriate clinician (e.g., Dr Lewis or Dr Chen). This protects patient safety and legal compliance.

Option D is also important: explaining the delay to Mr Ibrahim and Rachel maintains transparency and reduces frustration, while arranging portering avoids an unsafe rush. It balances timely care with correct process.

Option F appropriately escalates within OMFS: asking Dr Lewis to attend or identifying another authorised referrer ensures the right person provides justification, rather than pressuring radiology to bypass rules.

Option B undermines IR(ME)R and puts Elena in an unsafe position. Option C is disproportionate and damages interdepartment relationships. Option E is dishonest and potentially fraudulent. Option G delays necessary trauma imaging without clinical justification. Option H is outside radiology’s usual remit and could be unsafe without prescribing authority and appropriate monitoring; pain management should be coordinated via OMFS/ward team.
learningPoints: IR(ME)R compliance and proper justification are essential even under time pressure.
Use respectful, collaborative communication with radiology colleagues to resolve missing information.
Be transparent with patients and ward staff about delays and practical steps (e.g., portering) to reduce harm.
Never falsify documentation or pressure others to bypass legal safety processes.

scenario: You are a DCT in a paediatric GA theatre list. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is delayed in traffic and the list is already behind. The first child, Sophie (7), is in the anaesthetic room with her mother, Mrs Khan, and is crying and refusing to open her mouth. Anaesthetic nurse Angela asks whether you can quickly confirm which teeth are planned for extractions because the consent form is unclear: it lists “deciduous molars UR/UL” but the charting in the notes mentions different teeth. Dental nurse Lucy tells you the pre-op radiographs are not on the system, and the theatre coordinator is pressing to start or risk cancelling later cases. Mrs Khan says she signed “whatever they told me” and now wants reassurance you are not removing the wrong teeth.

options.A: Delay starting and request the radiographs urgently, clarify the treatment plan against the records, and if uncertainty remains, wait for Dr O’Connor to confirm before proceeding, explaining the reasons to Angela and Mrs Khan.
options.B: Proceed with extracting the most carious-looking teeth based on your clinical judgement so the list can start, and document that the consent form was unclear.
options.C: Ask Lucy to search other systems for the radiographs while you speak to Mrs Khan to confirm what she understood, and contact Dr O’Connor to agree the definitive plan before any irreversible treatment.
options.D: Tell Angela to proceed with anaesthesia and you will confirm the teeth once Sophie is asleep, as it will be easier to examine.
options.E: Ask the theatre coordinator to swap Sophie with a later case to buy time, while ensuring Mrs Khan is kept informed and Dr O’Connor is contacted about the consent discrepancy.

explanation:
Option C is most appropriate because it combines immediate practical steps (Lucy searching for radiographs) with communication and escalation. Speaking to Mrs Khan helps assess what she consented to and addresses her anxiety, while contacting Dr O’Connor recognises your limits and ensures the definitive plan is agreed before irreversible treatment.

Option A is also appropriate: delaying to obtain radiographs and clarify the plan prioritises patient safety and valid consent, even under theatre pressure. However, C is stronger because it actively parallel-processes tasks and communication.

Option E is a reasonable systems-based response: swapping cases may reduce cancellations while you resolve the discrepancy, provided Sophie and Mrs Khan are kept informed and the clinical decision remains safe.

Option D is less appropriate because inducing anaesthesia before confirming the exact teeth risks proceeding without clear consent and increases the chance of wrong-site surgery; examination under GA does not replace pre-operative planning and documentation. Option B is least appropriate: extracting based on appearance with unclear consent is unsafe, breaches professional standards, and could cause significant harm and complaint.
learningPoints: Do not proceed with irreversible treatment when consent or treatment plan is unclear; wrong-site prevention is critical.
Recognise limits of competence and seek senior confirmation, especially in theatre and paediatric settings.
Use parallel tasking (gather records, contact senior, communicate with family) to manage time pressure safely.
Theatre efficiency is important, but patient safety and valid consent take priority.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah, the dental nurse, flags that Mr Khan is booked for an urgent extraction but his triage form lists “penicillin allergy—rash,” while the electronic record shows “no known drug allergies.” Mr Khan is in pain and repeatedly asks for antibiotics “like last time” and says he needs to leave soon to pick up his child. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in another surgery and has asked you to keep the list moving because two walk-in patients are waiting. Receptionist Nadia tells you Mr Khan is becoming frustrated in the waiting area and has already complained about delays. You are considering prescribing amoxicillin and arranging extraction today, but the consent form from a previous visit is scanned and partly illegible.

options.A: Ask Nadia to tell Mr Khan you will prescribe antibiotics now to speed things up, and proceed to book the extraction without clarifying the allergy discrepancy.
options.B: Call Mr Khan into the surgery, clarify his allergy history directly, and ask Sarah to check any previous discharge letters; if uncertainty remains, discuss an alternative antibiotic or no antibiotic plan with Dr Kumar before prescribing.
options.C: Prescribe amoxicillin based on the electronic record showing no allergy, and document that the patient “denies allergy” without further discussion to avoid delaying the clinic.
options.D: Defer all decisions until Dr Kumar is free, leave Mr Khan waiting, and ask Nadia to manage his frustration without giving any timeframe.
options.E: Rely on the scanned previous consent form as evidence of consent, and proceed with extraction without re-checking understanding because Mr Khan is in pain and wants treatment.

explanation: Option B is most appropriate because it prioritises patient safety and informed decision-making while still aiming to keep the clinic moving. Mr Khan has conflicting allergy documentation; clarifying the history directly and checking prior correspondence with Sarah reduces the risk of a serious adverse drug reaction. If uncertainty persists, involving Dr Kumar before prescribing aligns with recognising limits of competence and safe prescribing standards. D is less appropriate but safer than prescribing blindly: waiting for senior input may be necessary, but leaving Mr Khan without communication worsens patient experience and could escalate conflict; you should still give updates and manage pain. C is unsafe because it relies on one record and encourages inadequate documentation, risking harm and breaching professional integrity. E is inappropriate as consent must be current, informed, and based on the patient’s present understanding; an illegible, historic form cannot substitute for a new consent discussion, particularly if the plan differs. A is least appropriate: it prioritises speed and complaint avoidance over safety, involves misleading reassurance via Nadia, and risks prescribing a contraindicated antibiotic.
learningPoints: Resolve discrepancies in allergy documentation before prescribing.
Valid consent must be contemporaneous and based on patient understanding, not historic paperwork.
Patient safety and professional integrity outweigh time pressure and complaint risk.
Communicate delays and decisions clearly to patients and the team to reduce escalation.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has mild learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Clarke. Emily (dental nurse) notes that Mr Clarke answers most questions and keeps interrupting when you try to speak to Mrs Thompson. Mrs Thompson quietly says she is “not sure” about having multiple extractions today and looks to her brother before responding. The referral letter from Dr Banerjee (consultant) recommends extractions under local anaesthetic to reduce repeated infections, but the notes also mention previous distress during treatment. The clinic is running late and Maria (hygienist) is waiting to use the room for a domiciliary planning call. Mr Clarke becomes irritated and says, “Just do what the hospital said—she can’t decide.

options.A: Ask Emily to invite Mr Clarke to wait in reception for a few minutes, then speak to Mrs Thompson alone to explore her wishes, understanding, and whether she feels pressured.
options.B: Proceed with the extractions as per Dr Banerjee’s referral because it is clinically indicated, documenting that her brother consented on her behalf.
options.C: Explain to Mr Clarke that you must assess Mrs Thompson’s capacity for this decision and, if she has capacity, her choice will be respected even if it differs from the referral plan.
options.D: Cancel the appointment immediately and discharge Mrs Thompson back to her GDP to avoid conflict and delays to Maria’s call.
options.E: Offer a short break and a simplified explanation with visual aids, checking Mrs Thompson’s understanding and giving her time to ask questions, even if it delays the clinic.

explanation: A, C and E best reflect patient-centred, lawful consent and capacity practice in the NHS. A is appropriate because it creates space for Mrs Thompson to speak freely; the dynamic suggests possible undue influence from Mr Clarke, and a brief one-to-one discussion (with Emily nearby for safety) supports autonomy and safeguarding awareness. C is essential: you must explain professional duties calmly, maintain respect, and set boundaries that consent cannot be provided by a relative unless there is legal authority and lack of capacity. E supports valid consent by adapting communication, checking understanding, and allowing time—particularly important given the notes of previous distress and her expressed uncertainty. B and F are unsafe and unethical: “next of kin” cannot consent for an adult with capacity, and proceeding would breach consent standards and professional integrity. D is disproportionate and risks abandonment of care. G may be helpful later, but it avoids your responsibility to assess capacity and communicate directly with the patient. H pre-empts a pathway without shared decision-making and could be inappropriate if Mrs Thompson could proceed with adjustments or prefers a different plan.
learningPoints: Consent for adults requires assessing capacity and supporting decision-making with accessible communication.
Relatives cannot consent on behalf of a capable adult without legal authority.
Be alert to undue influence; consider speaking to the patient separately when appropriate.
Manage time pressure without compromising autonomy and patient-centred care.

scenario: During an OMFS ward evening cover, you notice Dr Grant (registrar) has written up a discharge prescription for Mr Ibrahim that includes codeine, despite the ward notes stating he had severe nausea and confusion with opioids post-op. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you she queried it earlier but Dr Grant said, “Just give it—he’s going home and I’m busy.” Mr Ibrahim’s daughter, Mrs Hassan, is at the bedside and insists they need “strong painkillers” because she has a long drive. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in theatre and not immediately available. You are a DCT and feel uncomfortable challenging the registrar, but you are also aware the discharge is meant to happen within the hour to free a bed. The tensions include patient safety versus hierarchy and bed pressures, and balancing family expectations with appropriate prescribing and clear documentation.

options.A: Co-sign the prescription as written to avoid delaying discharge, and advise Rachel to monitor for side effects at home and call if concerned.
options.B: Speak to Dr Grant privately, highlight the documented opioid reaction and Rachel’s concern, and propose an alternative analgesia plan; if unresolved, escalate to the on-call consultant or duty senior before discharge.
options.C: Tell Mrs Hassan that her request is unreasonable and ask her to leave the ward so you can complete discharge paperwork without interference.
options.D: Ask Rachel to withhold the codeine and discharge Mr Ibrahim without any analgesia to avoid risk, documenting “opioid intolerance.”
options.E: Review Mr Ibrahim’s notes and speak with him (and Mrs Hassan) about his previous reaction and current pain, then document a clear, patient-specific analgesia plan and communicate it to Rachel and Dr Grant.

explanation: B is most appropriate because it addresses the immediate safety risk while respecting hierarchy and using appropriate escalation. Dr Grant’s plan conflicts with documented adverse effects; raising this privately and offering alternatives supports teamwork and reduces defensiveness. If Dr Grant does not amend the prescription, escalating to the on-call consultant/duty senior is justified given potential harm. E is also highly appropriate: engaging Mr Ibrahim directly, acknowledging Mrs Hassan’s concerns, and documenting a tailored plan improves informed decision-making and continuity for Rachel and the discharge team. D is safer than giving codeine but still suboptimal: discharging without analgesia risks unmanaged pain and poor outcomes; safer non-opioid options or antiemetic support should be considered. C is unprofessional and likely to escalate conflict; relatives can be challenging, but you must remain respectful and manage expectations. A is least appropriate: co-signing a prescription you believe unsafe breaches professional integrity and could cause avoidable harm, and “monitor at home” is inadequate mitigation for a foreseeable adverse reaction.
learningPoints: Challenge and escalate prescribing concerns when patient safety is at risk, regardless of hierarchy.
Engage the patient in analgesia planning and document a clear, individualised plan.
Use private, respectful communication to address colleague errors and preserve teamwork.
Do not co-sign or enable prescriptions you believe are unsafe.

scenario: In the radiology department, you have brought Mrs Singh from the restorative clinic for a CBCT requested by Dr Chen (consultant) to assess an impacted canine before surgical exposure. Elena (radiographer) says the request form is incomplete: it lacks justification and does not specify whether a smaller field of view could be used. Mrs Singh is anxious and says she has already taken time off work twice; she also mentions she might be pregnant but is “not sure” and has not done a test. Lucy (dental nurse) whispers that the restorative clinic is overbooked and Dr Chen will be annoyed if you return without imaging. Elena is firm that she cannot proceed without proper information and pregnancy status clarified.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed with the CBCT anyway because Dr Chen requested it, and document afterwards that the patient ‘may be pregnant’.
options.B: Call Dr Chen or the registrar Dr Lewis to clarify the clinical question, confirm the smallest appropriate field of view, and update the request with clear justification before imaging.
options.C: Explain to Mrs Singh that possible pregnancy changes the risk–benefit discussion, and offer to reschedule after a pregnancy test or consider alternative imaging if appropriate.
options.D: Argue with Elena that radiology is delaying patient care and threaten to make a formal complaint if the scan is not done today.
options.E: Ask Lucy to take Mrs Singh back to the restorative clinic immediately and book a new radiology slot without informing radiology of the pregnancy uncertainty.

explanation: B, C and F are most appropriate because they protect patient safety, meet IR(ME)R principles, and maintain effective cross-department working. B ensures the referrer’s clinical question and justification are explicit and helps select the smallest suitable field of view, reducing radiation exposure. F complements this by collaborating with Elena to correct the request properly rather than pressuring radiology; it respects radiographers’ governance responsibilities and supports teamwork. C addresses Mrs Singh’s possible pregnancy and anxiety: you must discuss how this affects risk–benefit and offer a safe plan such as deferring until pregnancy status is known or considering alternative imaging if clinically acceptable. A, H and proceeding without clarification increase radiation risk and breach governance. D damages interdepartment relationships and is unprofessional. E avoids the issue and risks repeated delays and poor communication. G is coercive and undermines valid consent; patients must not be threatened into investigations, particularly when pregnancy is uncertain.
learningPoints: Radiation requests must meet justification and optimisation requirements; incomplete requests should be corrected, not bypassed.
Possible pregnancy requires careful risk–benefit discussion and may warrant deferral or alternatives.
Maintain respectful interdepartmental communication; avoid blame and escalation threats.
Patient consent must be voluntary and informed, even under time pressure.

scenario: On a school dental clinic outreach session, you are examining Sophie (8) for pain. Chloe (dental nurse) quietly tells you Sophie flinches when adults approach and has several bruises on her upper arms. Sophie’s mother, Mrs Khan, is present and answers abruptly, saying Sophie is “clumsy” and demanding antibiotics instead of treatment because she “can’t miss work again.” Sophie whispers to you that she is scared to go home but stops when her mother looks at her. The school safeguarding lead is off-site today. Receptionist Mark calls to say the next class is waiting and the mobile clinic must leave on time. You are concerned about possible non-accidental injury and emotional coercion, but you also need to manage Sophie’s dental pain and maintain rapport with Mrs Khan to avoid Sophie being withdrawn from care.

options.A: Prescribe antibiotics to satisfy Mrs Khan, avoid confrontation, and advise her to see the GDP if pain persists.
options.B: Continue the dental assessment calmly, ask age-appropriate questions to Sophie with Chloe present, and document injuries and disclosures objectively while planning appropriate dental care.
options.C: Ask Mrs Khan to step outside briefly so you can speak to Sophie alone, explaining it is routine to talk to children directly; if Mrs Khan refuses, note this and proceed with safeguarding escalation.
options.D: Wait until the end of the session to mention your concerns to anyone, because the safeguarding lead is off-site and you do not want to delay the clinic.
options.E: Follow local safeguarding policy: contact the on-call safeguarding team/social care via the clinic’s protocol, and inform your supervising clinician (e.g., Dr Ahmed) promptly, even if it disrupts the timetable.

explanation: E is most appropriate because suspected safeguarding concerns require timely action according to local policy. The safeguarding lead being off-site does not remove your duty; contacting the on-call safeguarding team/social care and informing your supervisor (e.g., Dr Ahmed) ensures appropriate escalation and shared responsibility. B is also highly appropriate: you still need to address Sophie’s pain while gathering information sensitively, documenting bruises and Sophie’s words factually, and maintaining a calm environment with Chloe as a chaperone. C is appropriate because speaking to Sophie directly can clarify concerns and supports child-centred care; managing Mrs Khan’s reaction professionally is important, and refusal to allow private conversation can be relevant information. D is unsafe: delaying escalation risks ongoing harm and misses the opportunity for prompt safeguarding action. A is least appropriate: prescribing antibiotics without indication compromises clinical safety and enables avoidance of necessary assessment, while failing to act on potential abuse breaches professional and safeguarding responsibilities.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns require prompt escalation via local pathways, even when clinics are busy.
Document injuries and disclosures objectively, including who said what and observed behaviours.
Balance dental pain management with child-centred communication and safety planning.
