Do not use inappropriate prescribing to manage conflict or time pressure.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Your next patient, Mrs Thompson, is booked for an extraction of an upper molar. The dental nurse, Sarah, quietly tells you she overheard Mrs Thompson mention to reception (Olivia) that she “reacted badly to penicillin” in the past, but the electronic record lists “NKDA”. The clinic is running 40 minutes late and Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Mrs Thompson looks anxious and says she needs to leave in 30 minutes to collect her grandson. She asks you to “just get it done” and requests antibiotics “to stop it flaring again”. Sarah is worried about proceeding without clarifying the allergy and says the consent form hasn’t been printed. Olivia is asking if you can see the next patient early because the waiting room is full.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction but avoid prescribing anything today; ask Sarah to document the suspected penicillin reaction in the notes after the appointment.
options.B: Pause the appointment to take a focused allergy history from Mrs Thompson, update the record, and if uncertainty remains, defer non-urgent treatment and contact Dr Kumar or the on-call consultant for guidance.
options.C: Ask Olivia to rebook Mrs Thompson for another day and tell Mrs Thompson it is her responsibility to ensure her medical history is correct before treatment.
options.D: Continue with the extraction to prevent further delay, and ask Sarah to print the consent form while you administer local anaesthetic and begin.
options.E: Offer Mrs Thompson antibiotics as requested to manage her anxiety and reduce the chance of post-operative issues, using a non-penicillin option just in case.

explanation:
The safest and most professional response is to pause and clarify the allergy history (B). Sarah has raised a credible concern and NHS practice requires accurate medicines/allergy documentation before prescribing or treatment decisions. If Mrs Thompson’s reaction is unclear and the procedure is not immediately life-saving, deferring and seeking senior input from Dr Kumar/on-call is appropriate and demonstrates working within limits under pressure.

A is less optimal but still safer than rushing: it avoids inappropriate antibiotics and at least ensures the suspected allergy is recorded; however, it does not address the immediate need for valid consent and a safe plan before proceeding.

C appropriately recognises the need to rebook if safety cannot be assured, but the wording is blaming and risks damaging trust; responsibility for checking histories sits with the clinical team.

E is inappropriate: prescribing to reduce anxiety is not justified, and “just in case” antibiotic choice breaches antimicrobial stewardship and may still be unsafe without a clear history.

D is least appropriate: starting anaesthetic/extraction while consent is incomplete and allergy status is uncertain prioritises throughput over patient safety and undermines Sarah’s concern and professional standards.
learningPoints: Take and document an allergy history before prescribing or undertaking treatment that may be affected by it.
Valid consent requires time, information and appropriate documentation; it cannot be rushed to meet clinic targets.
Patient safety and antimicrobial stewardship override pressure to reduce waiting times.
Respond constructively to concerns raised by dental nurses and escalate appropriately when seniors are unavailable.

scenario: You are on an OMFS ward reviewing Mr Ibrahim, admitted overnight with a spreading odontogenic infection. Dr Chen (consultant) asked you to ensure consent is completed for incision and drainage under GA later today. Mr Ibrahim speaks limited English and his adult daughter, Layla, has been translating. Layla insists on staying and answers questions for him, saying he “doesn’t need all the scary details”. Mr Ibrahim nods but avoids eye contact and looks uncomfortable. The ward nurse, Angela, tells you the theatre coordinator is pushing for paperwork within 20 minutes or the slot may be given away. Meanwhile, Dr Grant (registrar) messages that he is tied up in clinic and can’t come immediately. You are concerned about whether Mr Ibrahim understands the procedure, risks (including airway compromise), and alternatives, and whether Layla is filtering information.

options.A: Ask Angela to arrange a professional interpreter urgently and explain to Layla that you need to speak directly with Mr Ibrahim to ensure valid consent.
options.B: Complete the consent form quickly using Layla as translator, focusing on the benefits so the theatre slot is not lost.
options.C: Document your concerns about communication and capacity, and escalate to Dr Chen or the on-call consultant if an interpreter cannot be arranged in time for valid consent.
options.D: Ask Layla to step outside for part of the conversation and check with Mr Ibrahim privately whether he wants her to translate and whether he feels pressured.
options.E: Tell the theatre coordinator you will sign the consent now and “go through the details later” on the way to theatre.

explanation:
The three best actions prioritise valid consent, patient autonomy and safety. A is appropriate because a professional interpreter reduces the risk of misunderstanding and meets NHS standards for informed consent; it also sets clear boundaries with Layla while maintaining respect. D is also important: speaking with Mr Ibrahim privately helps assess whether he wants Layla involved, whether he feels pressured, and supports confidentiality and autonomy. C appropriately recognises limits of competence and the urgency/complexity; documenting concerns and escalating to Dr Chen/on-call ensures senior oversight if valid consent cannot be achieved in time.

B, E, F and H are unsafe and unethical. Rushing consent to keep the theatre slot (B/E) undermines informed decision-making and could invalidate consent. “Protecting yourself” with vague documentation (F) is poor professional integrity and does not address the underlying risk. Withholding serious risks (H) breaches the duty to disclose material risks.

G may be reasonable to involve Dr Grant, but simply waiting without actively arranging interpretation or escalation risks delay and ongoing inadequate communication. The focus should be enabling Mr Ibrahim to understand and decide, not list management alone.
learningPoints: Use professional interpreters for consent when language barriers exist; family members may filter information.
Check patient preferences and potential coercion by speaking privately when appropriate.
If valid consent cannot be obtained, document concerns and escalate to a senior clinician.
Time pressure and theatre targets do not justify compromised consent or incomplete risk discussions.

scenario: You are a DCT in a community dental service special care clinic. Your patient, Tyler (8), attends with his mother, Mrs Khan, for caries management. During the appointment, Tyler quietly tells you he doesn’t want to go home because his mother’s new partner “gets angry” and he was hit last night. He shows a fading bruise on his upper arm. Mrs Khan, who appears stressed, interrupts and says Tyler is “making stories” and demands you finish quickly because she needs to get to work. The dental nurse, Emily, looks alarmed and later whispers that Mrs Khan refused to let Tyler speak alone at reception. Your supervisor, Dr O’Connor, is off-site at a meeting but reachable by phone. The receptionist, Mark, says the next families are already waiting and complaints have been made about delays.

options.A: Tell Mrs Khan you must follow safeguarding procedures, arrange to speak to Tyler briefly with Emily present, and contact Dr O’Connor and the safeguarding lead for advice on next steps today.
options.B: Finish the dental treatment as quickly as possible and ask Mark to book Tyler for a longer appointment next week to discuss the disclosure in private.
options.C: Confront Mrs Khan directly and accuse her partner of assaulting Tyler, warning that social services will be called if she doesn’t cooperate.
options.D: Document Tyler’s words verbatim, your observations of the bruise, and Emily’s concern, and follow local safeguarding pathways even if it delays the clinic.
options.E: Reassure Mrs Khan that you will keep Tyler’s comments confidential and not tell anyone, to avoid escalating the situation during the appointment.

explanation:
A is most appropriate because it acts promptly on a safeguarding disclosure, involves appropriate support (Dr O’Connor and the safeguarding lead), and sets a clear, calm boundary with Mrs Khan while maintaining professionalism. It also enables a brief, appropriate conversation with Tyler (with Emily present) to clarify immediate risk without conducting an in-depth investigation.

D is also highly appropriate: accurate contemporaneous documentation (verbatim disclosure, bruise description, who was present, and Emily’s observations) is essential for safeguarding and continuity of care. Following local pathways may cause delay, but patient safety overrides clinic flow.

B is less appropriate because deferring action risks ongoing harm and fails to respond to potential immediate risk, though it recognises the need for privacy.

C is inappropriate: confrontation and accusations can escalate risk to Tyler and damage cooperation; safeguarding requires a measured approach.

E is least appropriate: promising confidentiality is unsafe and misleading. You should explain information will be shared on a need-to-know basis to keep Tyler safe. Mark’s waiting-room pressures must not deter appropriate safeguarding action.
learningPoints: Act immediately on safeguarding concerns; do not delay because of clinic pressures.
Do not promise absolute confidentiality—explain need-to-know information sharing to keep the child safe.
Document disclosures and clinical observations accurately and verbatim.
Escalate to the safeguarding lead/senior clinician and follow local pathways rather than confronting carers.

scenario: You are a DCT in the radiology department supporting an oral surgery clinic. Mr Petrov has been referred for removal of an impacted lower third molar. Dr Banerjee (consultant) has requested a CBCT due to proximity to the inferior alveolar nerve. The radiographer, Elena, reviews the request and says the referral form lacks justification and doesn’t document that a recent OPG was considered; she is reluctant to expose the patient without proper IR(ME)R-compliant information. Mr Petrov is already in the waiting area and is frustrated, telling the receptionist, Nadia, that he took unpaid leave and wants “whatever scan is needed today”. Dr Lewis (registrar) is in clinic and says curtly by phone that radiology is “being difficult” and asks you to “sort it out” as he is running behind.

options.A: Work with Elena to gather the missing clinical details from Dr Lewis and the notes (symptoms, OPG findings, treatment plan), then update or re-submit the request so justification is clear before scanning.
options.B: Ask Elena to proceed with the CBCT because Dr Banerjee requested it, and deal with the paperwork later to avoid upsetting Mr Petrov.
options.C: Explain to Mr Petrov that imaging must be justified and that you are coordinating with radiology and the surgical team; offer an estimated timeline and alternatives (e.g., proceed with OPG review today and book CBCT promptly if still needed).
options.D: Complain to Elena in front of Nadia and Mr Petrov that radiology is delaying care, and tell Mr Petrov to raise it with PALS.
options.E: Document the delay as radiology’s fault and advise Dr Lewis to write a complaint email so it doesn’t happen again.

explanation:
A, C and F best balance patient-centred care with legal and safety requirements. A is appropriate because it respects Elena’s IR(ME)R responsibilities and focuses on obtaining the necessary justification (e.g., OPG findings and intended surgical plan) before exposure. C manages Mr Petrov’s frustration transparently, maintains trust, and offers practical alternatives while avoiding blame. F is appropriate escalation: if justification cannot be provided promptly, involving Dr Banerjee or the duty radiologist supports timely decision-making without pressuring Elena to act outside governance.

B is unsafe and undermines radiology regulation and patient safety by prioritising speed over justified exposure. D and E damage interdepartmental relationships and professionalism; public criticism and blame do not solve the immediate problem and can escalate complaints. G abdicates responsibility and misrepresents governance. H is premature and not patient-centred because it cancels without attempting to resolve the justification issue or provide a plan.

Overall, the best actions show respectful teamwork across departments, clear communication with Nadia and Mr Petrov, and adherence to imaging governance while managing clinic pressures.
learningPoints: Imaging must be justified and documented; time pressure does not override IR(ME)R governance.
Manage patient frustration with clear explanations, realistic timelines and alternatives.
Maintain professional relationships across departments; avoid blame and public criticism.
Escalate appropriately when there is disagreement about justification rather than pressuring colleagues.

scenario: You are a DCT on a sedation clinic list. Mrs Hassan is scheduled for IV sedation for multiple extractions. The sedation nurse, Chloe, tells you the pre-assessment notes are incomplete: the recorded weight is from two years ago and the medical history mentions “sleep apnoea query” but no sleep study result. Mrs Hassan is tearful and says she has waited months and is terrified of dental treatment. She admits she sometimes uses her partner’s diazepam to sleep. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is in another room starting a case and asked you to “get the next patient ready”. The clinic coordinator, Olivia, is pressing to avoid overruns because an afternoon list is fully booked. Chloe is uneasy about proceeding without clarifying the sleep apnoea and benzodiazepine use, and she asks what you want to do.

options.A: Proceed with cannulation and baseline observations while asking Mrs Hassan targeted questions about snoring, daytime sleepiness, and diazepam use, and immediately update Dr Ahmed before any sedative is administered.
options.B: Reassure Chloe that pre-assessment is often imperfect and continue as planned so the list stays on time; mention the missing details to Dr Ahmed after the case.
options.C: Ask Olivia to inform Mrs Hassan there will be a delay, and request Dr Ahmed review the case before proceeding because of possible sleep apnoea and unprescribed benzodiazepine use.
options.D: Advise Mrs Hassan to take diazepam before her appointment next time to help her cope, and document it as part of the plan.
options.E: Cancel the sedation on the spot without explanation, tell Mrs Hassan to leave, and ask Olivia to fill the slot with another patient.

explanation:
C is most appropriate because suspected sleep apnoea and unprescribed benzodiazepine use increase sedation risk and require senior review and potentially altered planning. It also manages expectations by asking Olivia to communicate the delay respectfully. This prioritises patient safety over list targets.

A is also appropriate: obtaining focused information and preparing the patient (observations/cannulation) can be efficient, but crucially it includes updating Dr Ahmed before any sedative is given. This demonstrates working within competence and using time productively without committing to unsafe sedation.

B is less appropriate because it normalises incomplete assessment and delays escalation until after the case, risking harm and undermining Chloe’s concern.

D is inappropriate and unsafe: advising use of someone else’s diazepam is unethical, outside prescribing governance, and could interact dangerously with IV sedation.

E is least appropriate due to poor patient-centred care and lack of explanation or safety-netting. While cancellation may ultimately be necessary, it must be communicated compassionately with clear reasons and a plan for reassessment. Mrs Hassan’s anxiety should be acknowledged, but not at the expense of sedation governance.
learningPoints: Potential sleep apnoea and benzodiazepine use are sedation red flags requiring senior review and possible postponement.
Use team concerns (e.g., Chloe’s) as prompts to reassess risk rather than to maintain throughput.
You can gather key information and prepare the patient, but do not proceed to sedation without appropriate assessment and authorisation.
Communicate delays or cancellations compassionately, with clear reasons and a plan for next steps.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you that Mr Khan has arrived for an urgent extraction and looks unwell. The notes from yesterday’s triage are brief and the medical history section is partly blank. Mr Khan says he is “fine” but mentions he takes “blood thinners” and cannot recall the name; he is frustrated because he has waited 2 hours. Dr Lewis (registrar) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Nadia (receptionist) is asking whether you can “just crack on” because two more patients are waiting and a complaint is brewing. Mr Khan insists he needs the tooth out today and asks you not to contact his GP because “it always delays things”. You are concerned about bleeding risk and capacity for informed consent under pressure, but also about worsening infection if you postpone.

options.A: Ask Sarah to call Dr Lewis in theatre for urgent advice while you take Mr Khan’s BP, check for red flags, and clarify the anticoagulant name using his phone/medication list if available.
options.B: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot proceed safely without confirming his anticoagulant and recent INR/DOAC timing, and ask Nadia to rebook him into the next urgent slot while you contact his GP/pharmacy for details.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction using local measures for haemostasis, documenting that Mr Khan could not recall the medication name and that he accepted the bleeding risk.
options.D: Ask Nadia to bring the next patient in while you quickly search the electronic record for any hospital anticoagulation clinic letters and ask Sarah to check if Mr Khan has a repeat prescription list in his wallet.
options.E: Tell Mr Khan that because he refused GP contact you will discharge him with antibiotics and advise him to see his GDP when convenient.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety: proceeding with an extraction when Mr Khan cannot identify his anticoagulant creates an unacceptable bleeding risk. Option A is most appropriate because it combines immediate clinical assessment (vitals/red flags), active information-gathering, and escalation to Dr Lewis despite theatre pressures, while supporting Sarah’s concern. Option D is also appropriate as a parallel step to retrieve reliable information without abandoning the patient; it uses available systems and maintains clinic flow without compromising safety.

Option B is reasonable but ranks lower because rebooking before making best efforts to obtain same-day information may delay necessary care; however, it appropriately sets boundaries and involves GP/pharmacy to enable safe treatment. Option E is less appropriate: prescribing antibiotics simply to avoid definitive care risks antimicrobial stewardship issues and may not address the source; it also penalises the patient for refusing contact rather than exploring reasons and explaining necessity. Option C is least appropriate because documenting uncertainty does not mitigate harm; consent is not valid if key risks cannot be quantified and the patient is under time pressure. NHS professionalism requires acting within competence, escalating appropriately, and prioritising safety over throughput and complaints.
learningPoints: Do not undertake invasive procedures without adequate medical history and risk assessment, especially around anticoagulants.
Escalate early and support team members who raise safety concerns; use parallel information sources (records, pharmacy, GP).
Valid consent requires the patient to understand material risks; time pressure and frustration can impair decision-making.
Avoid using antibiotics as a substitute for definitive treatment and follow antimicrobial stewardship principles.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has severe dental anxiety and mild learning disability. Emily (dental nurse) has booked a longer slot, but the clinic is running late after a medical emergency earlier. Mrs Thompson now refuses a bitewing radiograph, saying she “can’t cope with anything in her mouth”. Her sister, Olivia, speaks over her and demands you “just do the filling today” and says she will consent on her behalf. Dr Chen (consultant) is in another room supervising a sedation assessment and has asked not to be interrupted unless urgent. Mrs Thompson has intermittent pain from a heavily restored molar; without radiographs you are unsure whether there is recurrent caries close to the pulp. Emily quietly tells you that last visit Mrs Thompson agreed to radiographs with desensitisation, but today she looks overwhelmed.

options.A: Ask Olivia to step into the waiting area for a few minutes while you speak directly with Mrs Thompson, using simple language to check her understanding and preferences.
options.B: Proceed with the filling without radiographs to avoid distress, documenting that radiographs were declined and that Olivia requested treatment.
options.C: Offer a short break and a graded desensitisation approach (show the sensor, practice positioning), and agree a ‘stop signal’ with Mrs Thompson, even if it means the appointment may overrun.
options.D: Explain to Olivia that she cannot consent on Mrs Thompson’s behalf unless there is legal authority and Mrs Thompson lacks capacity, and set expectations about respectful communication.
options.E: Interrupt Dr Chen immediately to ask whether you can proceed without radiographs, because the clinic is running late.

explanation:
The best actions prioritise Mrs Thompson’s autonomy, capacity-sensitive communication, and safe care. A is appropriate because it reduces the interpersonal pressure from Olivia and allows you to assess Mrs Thompson’s understanding and wishes directly; this supports valid consent and patient-centred care. D is also appropriate: it sets professional boundaries with Olivia, clarifying that she cannot override Mrs Thompson’s consent unless there is a lawful basis and lack of capacity, and it addresses the dominating dynamic.

F is appropriate because, without radiographs and with uncertainty about pulpal involvement, definitive restorative treatment may be unsafe; interim measures and prompt review manage pain while respecting her refusal today.

C is a good supportive strategy but is not in the top three here because the clinic is already delayed and Mrs Thompson appears overwhelmed; graded exposure may still be attempted, but if she continues to refuse, forcing an overrun may not be proportionate. E is unnecessary escalation; Dr Chen’s input is helpful but you can manage consent/capacity and interim care without interrupting. B and G prioritise throughput or self-protection over safety and shared decision-making. H is unacceptable: restraint/coercion breaches consent and could be traumatic. NHS standards require informed consent, reasonable adjustments, and safeguarding dignity.
learningPoints: Consent must come from the patient with capacity; relatives cannot consent unless legally authorised and capacity is lacking.
Use reasonable adjustments and communication strategies to support anxious or learning-disabled patients.
If key diagnostics are declined, consider interim measures and safety-netting rather than unsafe definitive treatment.
Manage challenging relatives professionally while keeping the patient’s voice central.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, admitted with facial swelling. During the review, you notice the drug chart includes penicillin despite a documented allergy in the electronic record. Mr Petrov’s English is limited and he looks confused; his son is on speakerphone translating and insists “no allergy, just give it”. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in clinic and has asked you to “sort ward jobs” because he is behind. When you approach Chloe (dental nurse) to help confirm details, she says she overheard a junior doctor earlier joking that Mr Petrov “always claims allergies” and they prescribed quickly to avoid delays. Meanwhile, Mr Petrov’s swelling appears to be worsening and he is febrile.

options.A: Hold the penicillin dose, assess Mr Petrov for sepsis indicators, and ask Rachel to start observations/escalate per NEWS while you arrange an interpreter or language line to clarify the allergy history.
options.B: Administer the prescribed penicillin now because the son denies the allergy and delaying antibiotics could worsen the infection.
options.C: Contact Dr Kumar to inform him of the allergy discrepancy and clinical deterioration, and agree an alternative antibiotic plan consistent with local guidelines.
options.D: Document in the notes that the junior doctor made an inappropriate joke and email the clinical lead immediately before addressing the immediate prescribing issue.
options.E: Ask Rachel to give the penicillin but stay with Mr Petrov in case of a reaction, as a compromise between safety and urgency.

explanation:
A is most appropriate: patient safety comes first. With a documented penicillin allergy and limited communication, you should withhold the dose until the allergy history is clarified using appropriate interpretation, while simultaneously treating the deterioration by escalating observations and sepsis screening with Rachel. This addresses urgent clinical risk without exposing Mr Petrov to potential anaphylaxis.

C follows closely: informing Dr Kumar and agreeing an alternative antibiotic plan ensures senior oversight and guideline-based prescribing, recognising your limits and the registrar’s responsibility despite clinic pressures.

D is appropriate but after immediate safety steps. The reported joking attitude suggests unprofessional behaviour and potential pattern of unsafe practice; documenting factually and raising concerns through appropriate channels supports professional integrity. However, emailing the lead should not delay urgent care.

E is less appropriate because asking Rachel to administer a drug you believe may be unsafe shifts responsibility and still risks harm; “monitoring” is not a mitigation for anaphylaxis. B is least appropriate: a relative’s denial via speakerphone is not a reliable substitute for patient history or records, especially with language barriers. NHS standards require safe prescribing, effective communication, and raising concerns about colleagues while prioritising immediate patient care.
learningPoints: Do not administer medications when a documented allergy conflict is unresolved; clarify using interpreters and reliable sources.
Escalate deteriorating patients promptly using NEWS/sepsis pathways and involve seniors early.
Raise concerns about unprofessional or unsafe colleague behaviour through appropriate channels, after stabilising the patient.
Avoid delegating unsafe actions to others; accountability and patient safety remain paramount.

scenario: You are a DCT in the radiology department supporting an oral surgery clinic. Dr O’Connor (consultant) has requested an OPG for Mrs Hassan before a surgical extraction. Elena (radiographer) refuses to take the image because the request form is missing a pregnancy status and the justification is written as “routine”. Mrs Hassan is visibly upset, saying she took unpaid leave and travelled far; she asks you to “make them do it”. Mark (receptionist) tells you the clinic is already overbooked and Dr O’Connor is “not in the mood for delays”. When you check the electronic record, you find a recent OPG from another hospital 3 weeks ago, but it is not available on your system yet. Elena says she can image today if a clear clinical justification is documented and pregnancy status is confirmed, but she has other patients waiting.

options.A: Apologise to Mrs Hassan, explain that radiographs require legal justification and pregnancy checks, and ask her privately about pregnancy possibility before proceeding.
options.B: Ask Elena to take the OPG immediately and promise to update the justification and pregnancy status afterwards so the clinic can stay on time.
options.C: Call Dr O’Connor to clarify the clinical question and whether the 3-week-old OPG would be acceptable, and document a specific justification on the request.
options.D: Ask Mark to complain to the radiology manager about Elena’s refusal because it is delaying patient care.
options.E: Request the external OPG urgently via image transfer and, if it will arrive promptly and is adequate, avoid repeat exposure; keep Mrs Hassan updated about the plan and timescales.

explanation:
C, E and A best balance legal compliance, patient-centred communication and interdepartmental teamwork. C is appropriate because it directly addresses Elena’s valid IR(ME)R concerns: you clarify the clinical question with Dr O’Connor and document a specific justification, supporting safe and lawful imaging while maintaining a professional relationship with radiology.

E is also appropriate: you have identified a recent OPG from another hospital, so requesting image transfer may avoid unnecessary radiation exposure. Keeping Mrs Hassan informed manages expectations and reduces distress.

A is appropriate because pregnancy status is a required check and should be discussed sensitively and privately; explaining the reason helps maintain trust.

B is unsafe and breaches IR(ME)R principles—imaging cannot be justified retrospectively. D and F undermine teamwork and escalate conflict rather than solving the problem. G is dishonest and unsafe, and could constitute serious professional misconduct. H may compromise clinical safety if imaging is required for surgical planning; it also bypasses radiology governance rather than addressing the documentation issue. NHS standards emphasise patient safety, accurate records, respectful cross-team communication, and avoiding unnecessary exposure.
learningPoints: Radiographic exposures must be justified and documented in line with IR(ME)R; you cannot ‘fix paperwork later’.
Avoid repeat imaging when recent adequate images may be obtainable; request transfers and document rationale.
Communicate delays transparently to patients and maintain respectful interdepartmental relationships under pressure.
Never falsify pregnancy status or justification; honesty and accurate documentation are professional duties.

scenario: During a prison dental service session, you are treating Mr Clarke for recurrent pain under an old crown. Lucy (dental nurse) notes that Mr Clarke’s consent form from last month is unsigned, but the clinical notes suggest a discussion took place. The prison officer, Mr Lewis, is impatient and tells you to “keep it quick” because staffing is short and movement times are strict. Mr Clarke asks for strong painkillers and says he will “kick off” if you don’t sort it today. He also quietly tells you he is being pressured by another inmate to get antibiotics to trade. You are unsure whether he is disclosing coercion or manipulating you, and you are conscious of confidentiality limits in a custodial setting. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is off-site but contactable by phone.

options.A: Pause treatment to re-take consent properly with Mr Clarke, ensuring he understands options and documenting clearly, even if this means the prison officer has to rearrange movement times.
options.B: Prescribe antibiotics and a strong opioid to de-escalate Mr Clarke’s threats, documenting that the prison environment limited your options.
options.C: Ask Mr Lewis (officer) to stay within earshot so you feel safer while Mr Clarke explains the coercion, and then decide whether to prescribe antibiotics.
options.D: Explain to Mr Clarke that you cannot prescribe antibiotics without clinical indication and discuss alternative pain management; contact Dr Banerjee for advice given the coercion disclosure and threat of violence.
options.E: Proceed with treatment based on last month’s notes, and ask Lucy to sign the consent form as a witness to save time.

explanation:
D is most appropriate because it addresses multiple ethical tensions: patient safety vs intimidation, and confidentiality vs security. You set clear prescribing boundaries (no antibiotics without indication; cautious analgesia), manage pain appropriately, and seek senior support from Dr Banerjee due to the coercion disclosure and potential violence risk—recognising limits and using supervision.

A is next: valid consent cannot be assumed from unsigned paperwork, particularly in a high-pressure environment. Re-taking consent protects Mr Clarke’s autonomy and your professional accountability, even if it inconveniences prison logistics.

C is less appropriate but may be necessary for immediate safety; however, keeping the officer within earshot can compromise confidentiality and may inhibit disclosure. If used, it should be proportionate and explained to Mr Clarke, with efforts to speak privately if safe.

B is inappropriate: prescribing antibiotics or opioids to manage threats breaches professional integrity, risks diversion/misuse, and undermines antimicrobial stewardship. E is least appropriate: asking Lucy to sign as a witness without Mr Clarke’s consent is dishonest and could be serious misconduct. NHS standards require accurate documentation, ethical prescribing, and managing aggression through appropriate channels rather than unsafe clinical decisions.
learningPoints: Consent must be contemporaneous, informed and documented; do not rely on incomplete forms or retrospective signatures.
Do not prescribe antibiotics/opioids without clinical indication, even under pressure or threat; consider diversion risks in custodial settings.
Escalate concerns about coercion, intimidation or safety using local security and clinical governance pathways, involving seniors appropriately.
Balance confidentiality with safety: seek private discussion where possible and document rationale when security presence is necessary.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you that Mr Khan, booked for an incision and drainage, mentioned “a reaction to antibiotics” but the triage sheet only says “NKDA”. The waiting room is full and Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Mr Khan is visibly uncomfortable and impatient, insisting he needs “the same antibiotics as last time” and wants to leave quickly for work. Nadia (receptionist) says the next patient, Mrs Thompson, is already complaining about delays. Sarah looks worried and says she heard Mr Khan mention “swelling of the lips” previously. You also notice the electronic record has two different GP details and no recent discharge summary.

options.A: Ask Sarah to start preparing the I&D kit while you prescribe amoxicillin now to save time, then clarify the allergy history afterwards.
options.B: Pause the procedure, take a focused allergy history from Mr Khan (including the specific drug and reaction), update the record, and if unclear, contact his GP/pharmacy; provide analgesia and local measures while you clarify.
options.C: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot prescribe until the allergy discrepancy is resolved, offer to rebook him for another day, and move on to Mrs Thompson to reduce delays.
options.D: Call Dr Kumar’s theatre extension and, if he cannot answer, ask the on-call OMFS registrar for immediate advice on antibiotic choice given the possible allergy, documenting the discussion.
options.E: Proceed with I&D under local anaesthetic without addressing the antibiotic issue, and tell Mr Khan to see his GP later for antibiotics.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to prioritise patient safety by clarifying the allergy history before prescribing. Option B addresses Sarah’s concern, obtains informed information from Mr Khan, and corrects the documentation discrepancy (NKDA vs reported reaction). It also manages competing priorities by offering analgesia/local measures while you gather reliable details. Option D is also appropriate because you recognise limits of certainty and seek timely senior input (Dr Kumar or the on-call registrar) while documenting advice—important in an NHS urgent setting with incomplete records.

Option E is less appropriate: I&D may be clinically indicated and can reduce infection burden, but proceeding without resolving the allergy documentation risks fragmented care and misses an opportunity to ensure safe follow-up; you should still document and plan antibiotics appropriately. Option C is poor because deferring care to protect clinic flow neglects Mr Khan’s acute need and may escalate complaints; delays should be managed transparently with Nadia rather than compromising care. Option A is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin before clarifying a possible lip-swelling reaction risks anaphylaxis and breaches safe prescribing and professional standards, regardless of time pressure.
learningPoints: Take and document a specific drug allergy history (drug, reaction, timing, severity) before prescribing.
Patient safety and safe prescribing override clinic flow pressures.
Escalate appropriately when information is uncertain and senior support is limited.
Address team concerns (e.g., from the dental nurse) promptly and professionally.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Hassan, who has moderate learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Hassan. Emily (dental nurse) notes Mrs Hassan has become quieter since arriving. The plan from Dr O’Connor (consultant) is to take impressions for a denture, but Mrs Hassan keeps looking to her brother before answering and nods even when you ask open questions. Mr Hassan insists you “just get it done today” and says she “doesn’t understand all the details,” asking you to speak only to him. There is no advocate present; the pre-appointment letter offered one, but the notes show no confirmation. The clinic is running late and Olivia (receptionist) tells you transport is waiting to take Mrs Hassan home in 20 minutes. Mrs Hassan suddenly says softly, “I don’t want him in here,” then goes silent when her brother looks at her.

options.A: Ask Olivia to delay transport and arrange a quiet room so you can speak with Mrs Hassan alone, with Emily present as chaperone, to explore her wishes and assess capacity for today’s procedure.
options.B: Proceed with impressions as planned because Dr O’Connor has already outlined the treatment plan and Mr Hassan is her next of kin.
options.C: Explain to Mr Hassan that you must obtain Mrs Hassan’s consent directly where possible, and set boundaries that you will speak to her first, using simple language and checking understanding.
options.D: Ask Emily to keep Mr Hassan occupied at reception while you quickly take impressions to avoid missing transport.
options.E: Document Mrs Hassan’s statement, discuss concerns with the safeguarding lead and Dr O’Connor the same day, and consider whether further appointments should include an advocate or alternative arrangements.

explanation:
Options A, C and E best balance consent, autonomy, and safeguarding. A creates a safe environment to hear Mrs Hassan’s wishes, assesses capacity for the specific decision today, and uses Emily appropriately as a chaperone while managing the transport pressure via Olivia. C sets clear professional boundaries with Mr Hassan and reinforces that consent must be obtained from Mrs Hassan if she has capacity; using simple language and checking understanding supports valid consent. E ensures contemporaneous documentation and appropriate escalation to Dr O’Connor and the safeguarding lead given the concerning dynamic (Mrs Hassan’s request for him to leave and her change in behaviour).

B and H are unsafe: next of kin cannot consent for an adult with capacity, and nodding is not adequate evidence of understanding. D is deceptive and risks coercion by removing Mr Hassan without transparency. F is unnecessarily confrontational and could increase risk to Mrs Hassan; safeguarding escalation should be proportionate and planned. G fails patient-centred care and provides inadequate communication and documentation. Overall, patient safety, dignity, and lawful consent take priority over clinic lateness.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s understanding and freedom from coercion.
Capacity is decision-specific and should be assessed and documented when in doubt.
Safeguarding concerns should be escalated promptly and proportionately via local pathways.
Use chaperones/advocates and adjust communication to support patient autonomy.

scenario: You are on an OMFS ward reviewing Daniel, a 10-year-old admitted overnight with facial swelling. Rachel (ward nurse) says Daniel’s dad, Mr Clarke, is angry because he thinks antibiotics were delayed. The electronic chart shows IV antibiotics were prescribed by Dr Grant (registrar) but the administration record has a gap overnight. Rachel says there was a cannula issue and staffing shortage, and she feels blamed. Dr Grant is in clinic and has messaged asking you to “sort it out and reassure the family.” Daniel is stable but anxious and keeps asking if he will need an operation. Mr Clarke is filming on his phone and says he will post online if “no one takes responsibility.” Angela (ward sister) is also asking you to complete discharge paperwork quickly because beds are needed.

options.A: Tell Mr Clarke to stop filming immediately and leave the bay, then continue your ward round to avoid escalating the situation.
options.B: Check Daniel’s current observations and pain, review the drug chart and administration record with Rachel, and clarify what happened with the cannula issue before speaking to Mr Clarke.
options.C: Apologise to Mr Clarke for the delay and promise that Rachel will be formally investigated, to show that you are taking it seriously.
options.D: Escalate the possible missed antibiotic doses to Angela and Dr Grant, document objectively, and follow local incident reporting (e.g., DATIX) if criteria are met.
options.E: Complete the discharge paperwork first to free the bed, then deal with the complaint and medication gap afterwards.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it prioritises Daniel’s immediate safety and establishes facts. Reviewing observations and the prescription/administration record with Rachel addresses the clinical risk of missed antibiotics and supports teamwork by not assuming blame. Option D follows NHS governance: escalating to Angela and Dr Grant, documenting objectively, and using incident reporting where appropriate protects patients and staff and ensures learning.

Option A is less appropriate: while filming can raise confidentiality concerns, an abrupt confrontation may inflame Mr Clarke’s anger. A calmer approach would be to explain privacy expectations and offer a private space after you have assessed Daniel and gathered information. Option E is poorer because bed pressures should not override investigating a potential medication omission; discharge decisions depend on accurate treatment history. Option C is least appropriate: promising an investigation into Rachel before facts are known is unfair, undermines team relationships, and breaches professionalism. You can acknowledge Mr Clarke’s distress and apologise for the experience without attributing blame, while explaining next steps and ensuring Daniel’s care plan is safe.
learningPoints: Establish clinical facts and assess the patient before responding to complaints or conflict.
Escalate and document medication omissions through local governance and incident pathways.
Communicate with relatives empathetically without assigning blame prematurely.
Support colleagues and maintain professional teamwork under pressure.

scenario: In a restorative clinic, Dr Chen (consultant) has stepped out to an urgent MDT call. You are running behind and Lucy (dental nurse) tells you the next patient, Mrs Singh, has arrived early for a complex endodontic re-treatment on a lower molar. The notes show a previous perforation risk and that Dr Chen planned to use a microscope and consider referral if canals cannot be negotiated. Mrs Singh is anxious and says she took unpaid leave and “cannot come back.” She asks you to “just do what you can today” and mentions she is on warfarin, but the INR result is not in the record. Mark (receptionist) says there is a walk-in emergency patient waiting with severe pain. Lucy quietly says she has not set up the microscope and is unsure where the perforation repair kit is kept.

options.A: Start access and attempt canal negotiation without the microscope to show progress, and ask Lucy to locate the perforation kit while you work.
options.B: Explain to Mrs Singh that you need Dr Chen’s input and the INR result before proceeding, offer interim pain management, and rebook or convert to a stabilisation appointment if appropriate.
options.C: Ask Mark to contact the anticoagulation clinic/GP urgently for the latest INR and document the source and time of the result.
options.D: Tell Mrs Singh you will proceed only if she signs a note accepting the risks because she cannot return another day.
options.E: Ask Lucy to bring the emergency patient into another surgery so you can finish Mrs Singh’s treatment without interruption.

explanation:
B, C and F best demonstrate safe practice and recognising limits. B manages Mrs Singh’s expectations while prioritising safety: complex re-treatment with perforation risk and planned microscope use should not be started without appropriate equipment and supervision, especially when the INR is missing. Offering interim measures and reframing the appointment as stabilisation respects her circumstances without compromising standards. C is appropriate because anticoagulation status must be verified from a reliable source and documented; delegating Mark to obtain the INR helps manage time pressure safely. F is also appropriate escalation: if Dr Chen is unavailable, seeking advice from Dr Lewis with clear clinical context supports safe decision-making and shared responsibility.

A and G are unsafe: beginning without the microscope and without confirming INR increases risk and deviates from the planned approach. D is unethical—patients cannot waive clinicians’ duty of care by signing a note. E is inappropriate because it prioritises one patient over an emergency without triage and may misuse staff/resources. H lacks transparency and undermines trust. Overall, patient safety and working within competence outweigh productivity pressures.
learningPoints: Do not start complex procedures without planned equipment, information, and appropriate supervision.
Verify and document anticoagulation status from a reliable source before invasive care.
Escalate to seniors when supervision is unavailable and risks are significant.
Manage patient expectations honestly while offering safe interim care.

scenario: During a prison dental service session, you notice Dr Banerjee (consultant) has asked you to supervise Tyler, a foundation dentist on placement, while you see urgent patients. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you Tyler has been “cutting corners” and today photographed a prisoner’s mouth on his personal phone to “show his portfolio tutor,” despite the prison officer, Mr Evans, saying phones are not allowed in clinical areas. The patient, Mr Petrov, seemed confused but did not object at the time. Tyler now jokes in the staff room that “at least it wasn’t their face,” and asks you not to “make a fuss” because he’s applying for DCT. Meanwhile, Mark (receptionist) says two prisoners have arrived late and security will only hold them for 15 minutes. Dr Banerjee is in a meeting with prison management about staffing.

options.A: Tell Tyler to delete the photo immediately, explain why it breaches confidentiality and prison policy, and ensure Mr Petrov is informed and an incident is reported according to local information governance procedures.
options.B: Ignore it for now because the clinic is busy, and remind Tyler at the end of the day to use the clinic camera next time.
options.C: Ask Chloe to keep quiet to avoid damaging Tyler’s career prospects, and focus on seeing the late arrivals within the 15-minute window.
options.D: Escalate the issue to Dr Banerjee or the prison healthcare manager as soon as practicable, documenting what Chloe reported and what Tyler admitted, while ensuring patient flow is safely managed.
options.E: Confront Tyler loudly in front of other staff to demonstrate zero tolerance and deter others from similar behaviour.

explanation:
A is most appropriate because it addresses the immediate confidentiality and security breach: taking clinical images on a personal phone in a prison setting risks serious information governance and safety consequences. Involving Tyler directly, ensuring deletion, informing Mr Petrov appropriately, and initiating incident reporting aligns with NHS confidentiality standards and local prison policies. D is next best: you must escalate to Dr Banerjee or the healthcare manager and document objectively, especially given Chloe’s concern and Tyler’s admission. This supports a fair process and organisational learning while maintaining safe patient flow.

B is less appropriate because delaying action tolerates an ongoing breach and misses timely reporting requirements; workload pressure is not a justification. E is poorer: public confrontation may be unprofessional, escalate conflict, and does not ensure proper governance steps. C is least appropriate because it prioritises Tyler’s career over patient confidentiality and undermines a culture of safety; it also places Chloe in an unethical position. You can be supportive to Tyler while still addressing misconduct through appropriate channels.
learningPoints: Confidentiality and information governance breaches require prompt action and appropriate reporting.
Work pressures do not justify unsafe or unprofessional behaviour.
Raise concerns objectively and escalate via local policies, especially in high-risk settings.
Support junior colleagues through feedback while maintaining professional standards.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you Mr Khan has arrived late for an extraction and says he is “allergic to local anaesthetic” after a bad experience years ago. The triage note from Nadia (receptionist) lists “penicillin allergy” but nothing about LA. Mr Khan is anxious and wants the tooth out today because he starts a new job tomorrow. The waiting room is full and Dr Kumar (registrar) is covering two clinics and is currently in theatre, not easily reachable. Sarah is worried because Mr Khan also mentions he “fainted” during an injection previously. You have the consent form ready, but Mr Khan is pushing you to “just do it quickly” and is irritated by questions.

options.A: Tell Mr Khan you will proceed with the extraction using a different local anaesthetic cartridge and ask Sarah to prepare the syringe while you work out the details later, to avoid further delay.
options.B: Pause the procedure and take a focused history with Mr Khan about the previous reaction (symptoms, timing, treatment), clarify the documented penicillin allergy, and record this clearly before deciding on anaesthesia.
options.C: Ask Sarah to contact Dr Kumar via switchboard for urgent advice and, if unavailable, arrange review by the on-call anaesthetics/sedation team before any injection is given.
options.D: Explain to Mr Khan that because the allergy history is unclear you cannot safely extract today; offer analgesia advice and rebook him for another day without further discussion to keep the clinic moving.
options.E: Ask Nadia to print Mr Khan’s previous dental records and proceed with the extraction once they arrive, without taking further history to avoid upsetting him.

explanation:
The safest immediate step is to pause and clarify the history (B). “Allergy to local anaesthetic” is often anxiety/vasovagal syncope or adrenaline-related effects rather than true allergy, but you must not assume this. A focused history (symptoms such as rash, wheeze, facial swelling; timing; any treatment) and clear documentation are essential for informed consent and patient safety. Escalating appropriately (C) is next: Sarah’s concern should be taken seriously, and seeking senior input via switchboard is proportionate given Dr Kumar’s absence and the potential risk. 

If the history remains unclear or suggests anaphylaxis, deferring extraction (D) may be necessary, but it should be done with explanation and safety-netting rather than abruptly. Waiting for old records (E) can help but should not replace direct history-taking and may still be incomplete; it risks unnecessary delay without addressing the immediate safety question. Proceeding quickly with a different cartridge (A) is least appropriate: it disregards a potential allergy, undermines consent, and places the patient and team at avoidable risk under time pressure.
learningPoints: Take team members’ safety concerns seriously and investigate before proceeding.
Valid consent requires understanding and documentation of material risks, including allergy history.
Escalate to senior support when risk is uncertain and beyond your competence to manage alone.
Time pressure and patient demands do not justify compromising patient safety.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has moderate learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Clarke. Emily (dental nurse) notes Mrs Thompson keeps looking to her brother before answering and becomes quiet when he speaks for her. Mrs Thompson has symptomatic caries and needs a treatment plan; she nods to most questions but seems unsure when you discuss extractions versus restorations. Mr Clarke insists on “getting everything out today” and becomes irritated when you ask Mrs Thompson directly. The clinic is running late and there is no pre-booked advocate. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in another building and has asked you to “keep the list moving.” You are concerned about capacity, potential coercion, and the need to relieve pain promptly. You also want to maintain a working relationship with Mr Clarke, who says he is her “carer” and threatens to complain if you “waste time.”

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to step out briefly so you can speak with Mrs Thompson alone, explaining this is routine to support her own decision-making.
options.B: Proceed with extractions as requested by Mr Clarke because he is her carer and the patient has nodded throughout the discussion.
options.C: Use simple language and visual aids with Mrs Thompson to assess understanding, and document a structured capacity assessment specific to the decision being made.
options.D: Tell Mr Clarke that his behaviour is unacceptable and that you will cancel the appointment immediately to prevent further conflict.
options.E: Ask Emily to contact the safeguarding lead for advice while you continue the appointment as normal to avoid making Mr Clarke suspicious.

explanation:
The priority is to support Mrs Thompson’s autonomy and ensure any treatment is lawful and ethical. Asking Mr Clarke to step out (A) is appropriate and common practice to reduce undue influence and allow Mrs Thompson space to express her wishes. You should then communicate in an accessible way and carry out a decision-specific capacity assessment (C), documenting her understanding, retention, weighing of information, and ability to communicate a choice. 

If she lacks capacity for irreversible treatment such as extractions, you must follow best-interests processes (F), involving Dr O’Connor for senior oversight and considering least restrictive options and pain control. This balances patient-centred care with clinical urgency.

Proceeding based on Mr Clarke’s demands (B) risks unlawful treatment and breaches consent standards. Cancelling immediately (D) may escalate conflict and leaves pain unmanaged. Contacting safeguarding covertly while “continuing as normal” (E) is risky; safeguarding advice may be needed, but transparency and immediate consent/capacity issues must be addressed first. Discussing the family with other patients (G) breaches confidentiality. Routine antibiotics (H) is not justified and delays necessary care without addressing consent/capacity.
learningPoints: Capacity is decision-specific and must be assessed and documented for significant treatment decisions.
Support patient autonomy by reducing coercion and using accessible communication aids.
Irreversible treatment without valid consent requires best-interests decision-making and senior input.
Maintain confidentiality and avoid inappropriate information gathering from third parties.

scenario: You are on an OMFS ward reviewing post-op patients. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you she is uncomfortable because Dr Grant (specialty dentist) took a photo of Daniel, a 10-year-old with facial swelling, on his personal phone “to show the consultant later.” Daniel’s mother, Mrs Khan, is visibly upset and says she did not understand why a photo was needed and wants it deleted. Lucy (dental nurse) says the photo might help track swelling and that the team is very busy. Dr Chen (consultant) is in clinic and you are due in theatre in 15 minutes. Mrs Khan is asking to speak to “someone in charge” and threatens to complain to PALS.

options.A: Advise Mrs Khan that staff often take photos for clinical reasons and ask her to wait until Dr Chen finishes clinic, as you need to get to theatre.
options.B: Ask Dr Grant to come to the bedside immediately, explain the concern to him, and ensure the photo is transferred to an approved clinical system or deleted in line with local policy, documenting the discussion and outcome.
options.C: Tell Rachel to ignore it because it is Dr Grant’s responsibility, and focus on your theatre duties to avoid getting involved in conflict.
options.D: Apologise to Mrs Khan for the distress, explain the hospital’s policy on clinical images and consent, and offer to escalate to the nurse-in-charge or Dr Chen promptly.
options.E: Ask Lucy to take a new photo on her phone with Mrs Khan’s consent so you have a proper record, then delete Dr Grant’s photo later when convenient.

explanation:
This scenario centres on confidentiality and appropriate clinical photography. The most appropriate action is to address the issue promptly and directly with the person involved (B). Asking Dr Grant to attend, ensuring images are handled according to local policy (approved device/system, explicit consent, secure storage), and documenting what happened protects Daniel and the team and demonstrates professional integrity.

You should also manage the family’s distress (D): apologise for the upset (without pre-judging intent), explain why images may be used, and escalate appropriately to the nurse-in-charge or Dr Chen so Mrs Khan feels heard and there is senior oversight.

Option A is less appropriate because it prioritises your timetable over resolving an immediate confidentiality concern and may inflame the complaint. Option C is unprofessional; ignoring concerns raised by Rachel undermines a safety culture. Option E is least appropriate: taking another photo on a personal phone repeats the same governance breach and does not resolve the original issue in a timely, transparent way.

NHS standards require respecting patient confidentiality, obtaining informed consent for images, and using secure systems; concerns should be acted on even under time pressure.
learningPoints: Clinical images require informed consent and secure, approved storage—personal phones are usually inappropriate.
Respond promptly to concerns raised by colleagues to support an open, safe culture.
Manage complaints early with apology for distress, clear explanation, and appropriate escalation.
Document actions taken and outcomes when governance issues arise.

scenario: In a radiology department, you have brought Mrs Singh from restorative clinic for a CBCT requested for implant planning. Elena (radiographer) refuses to proceed because the referral form is missing a documented justification and does not state whether Mrs Singh is pregnant. Mrs Singh is frustrated; she has taken time off work and says Dr Ahmed (consultant) promised the scan today. Mark (receptionist) says the next available slot is in six weeks if you rebook. You phone Dr Ahmed’s secretary but she says he is in an MDT and cannot be interrupted. Meanwhile, Laura (hygienist) messages you that your next patient is waiting and is already anxious. Elena is polite but firm, and you feel caught between departments.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed “just this once” and offer to email the missing justification afterwards so Mrs Singh does not lose the slot.
options.B: Check Mrs Singh’s pregnancy status sensitively in a private space, update the referral with required information, and document the clinical justification based on the restorative plan you have been involved in.
options.C: Escalate to the radiology superintendent or duty radiologist for an immediate decision, explaining the clinical urgency and the missing referral details.
options.D: Tell Mrs Singh the radiographer is being obstructive and advise her to complain to PALS to help secure an earlier appointment.
options.E: Rebook the scan for six weeks and ask Mark to add a note that the delay is radiology’s fault, then return to clinic to keep your list moving.

explanation:
Radiation governance requires clear justification and appropriate pregnancy checks before exposure. Completing the missing information properly (B) is the best first step: speak with Mrs Singh privately about pregnancy status, add relevant clinical details you know from the restorative plan, and document justification. This supports Elena’s legal responsibilities under IR(ME)R and maintains professional respect between departments.

If uncertainty remains or time is critical, escalating appropriately (C) to a radiology senior is reasonable; it avoids conflict and ensures a defensible decision. If you cannot legitimately justify the scan or are not the correct referrer/operator under local policy, you should involve the appropriate senior (F) such as Dr Ahmed, and explain to Mrs Singh what will happen next while considering interim steps.

Pressuring Elena to “just do it” (A) undermines safety and puts staff at risk. Blaming radiology or encouraging complaints (D, E) damages relationships and does not solve the governance issue. Using WhatsApp to apply pressure (G) is unprofessional and risks confidentiality. Taking an alternative radiograph without discussion (H) breaches consent and may be clinically inappropriate.

This scenario tests calm cross-department communication, patient-centred explanation, and adherence to radiation safety standards despite service pressures.
learningPoints: IR(ME)R-style governance requires documented justification and pregnancy checks before imaging.
Maintain respectful interdepartmental communication; do not pressure colleagues to bypass safety processes.
Escalate appropriately when decisions sit outside your authority or competence.
Keep patients informed about delays and next steps to preserve trust.

scenario: You are the DCT on trauma/on-call overnight. Mr Petrov attends with a deep lower lip laceration after a fall, and Angela (ED nurse) says he has been waiting two hours and is becoming agitated. Chloe (dental nurse) has set up a suture kit and says the ED SHO is asking whether you can “just close it” so the department can meet targets. The notes are incomplete, and Mr Petrov smells of alcohol; he keeps interrupting and says he will leave if it takes any longer. You have limited experience suturing complex facial lacerations and are unsure about involvement of the vermilion border. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is off-site but available by phone; Dr Lewis (registrar) is on another call dealing with a fractured mandible.

options.A: Attempt the closure yourself immediately to reduce waiting time, and ask Chloe to document that the patient was intoxicated and demanded fast treatment.
options.B: Assess Mr Petrov’s injury carefully, including whether the vermilion border is involved, check tetanus status and contamination, and document findings before deciding on repair.
options.C: Call Dr Banerjee for advice and, if needed, request Dr Lewis to review when free, explaining to Angela and the ED SHO that complex facial repair may require senior input.
options.D: Ask Angela to discharge Mr Petrov with advice to return in the morning when the day team is present, as he has been drinking and is difficult to manage.
options.E: Explain to Mr Petrov the options and risks, assess his capacity to consent given intoxication, and if capacity is in doubt consider involving ED for observation/analgesia until he can engage safely.

explanation:
The most appropriate first step is a structured assessment and documentation (B): clarify wound depth, contamination, involvement of the vermilion border, neurovascular status, and tetanus needs. This protects patient safety and informs whether you can manage or must refer.

Given Mr Petrov’s alcohol intoxication and agitation, consent is a key ethical tension. You should explain options and assess capacity (E). If he cannot understand/retain/weigh information, proceeding with non-urgent repair may be inappropriate; involving ED for observation, analgesia, or sedation pathways may be needed to keep him safe.

Escalation (C) is appropriate: acknowledging limits of competence and seeking senior input from Dr Banerjee and/or Dr Lewis aligns with NHS professionalism, even under target pressure from the ED SHO. 

Deferring until morning by discharging (D) is risky: it may lead to infection, poor cosmetic outcome, and loss to follow-up, and it does not address capacity or safeguarding of an intoxicated patient. Attempting immediate closure primarily to meet targets (A) is least appropriate; it ignores your limited experience, may cause misalignment of the vermilion border, and uses documentation defensively rather than as an accurate clinical record.

This scenario tests prioritising safety and lawful consent over service pressure and hierarchy.
learningPoints: Recognise and act on limits of competence—complex facial lacerations may need senior review.
Assess and document wounds systematically before treatment decisions.
Capacity and valid consent must be considered when intoxication affects understanding.
Service pressures and targets do not justify unsafe or poorly supervised care.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Khan arrives with severe lower molar pain and facial swelling. The waiting room is full and reception (Nadia) is asking you to see the next patient quickly. Your dental nurse, Sarah, quietly points out that Mr Khan’s triage sheet lists a penicillin allergy, but the referral letter from his GDP suggests he “responded well to amoxicillin previously”. Mr Khan is anxious and insists he needs “the same antibiotics today” because he has a job interview tomorrow. You have not yet reviewed his medical history on the electronic record, and your supervisor, Dr Kumar, is tied up with a trauma patient and not immediately available. Sarah looks uncomfortable and says she has seen allergy errors before.

options.A: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan to a later slot so you can keep the clinic on time, and tell Sarah you will clarify the allergy at the end of the session.
options.B: Tell Mr Khan you will not prescribe anything until you have checked his electronic medical record, then take a focused allergy history with Sarah present and document it before deciding on antibiotics.
options.C: Prescribe amoxicillin as per the GDP letter to avoid delay, and advise Mr Khan to stop if he develops a rash or breathing difficulty.
options.D: Ask Sarah to call Dr Kumar for a quick decision while you start the next patient, then return to Mr Khan once you have an answer.
options.E: Explain to Mr Khan that allergy information is inconsistent, ask his consent to contact his GDP/pharmacy, and if urgent treatment is needed consider a non-penicillin option or urgent drainage while arranging senior review.

explanation: The safest immediate priority is to clarify the penicillin allergy before prescribing. Option B is most appropriate because it uses available information (electronic record and a focused allergy history) and documents clearly, addressing Sarah’s concern and protecting Mr Khan from potentially serious harm. Option E is also strong: it manages expectations, seeks corroboration (GDP/pharmacy), and keeps treatment patient-centred by considering alternative antibiotics or operative management, while planning senior input. Option D is less appropriate because it risks delaying assessment of a potentially worsening infection and fragments care; however, involving Dr Kumar is sensible if done without abandoning Mr Khan. Option A is poor because it prioritises clinic flow over safety and ignores an immediate red flag raised by Sarah; rebooking a patient with swelling could be unsafe. Option C is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin despite a documented allergy is unsafe and breaches NHS standards on medicines safety and professional integrity. It also undermines teamworking by dismissing Sarah’s concern and exposes the patient to avoidable risk.
learningPoints: Treat conflicting allergy information as a patient-safety risk and clarify before prescribing.
Document allergy histories and prescribing decisions contemporaneously and clearly.
Balance patient expectations with safe, evidence-based management and shared decision-making.
Escalate appropriately to seniors without delaying urgent assessment of infection.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has moderate learning disability and attends with her support worker, Chloe. Mrs Thompson needs multiple restorations and has been placed on a sedation waiting list. Today she arrives distressed and says she wants “everything done today” but becomes confused when you explain options. Chloe insists she has authority to consent and pushes you to proceed quickly because transport is booked. Your consultant, Dr Banerjee, is running late and the clinic coordinator (Olivia) says there are two patients waiting and only one sedation slot left this month. During the discussion, Mrs Thompson quietly tells you she does not want Chloe in the room, but Chloe becomes defensive and says Mrs Thompson “always says that when anxious”.

options.A: Ask Chloe to step outside briefly, check Mrs Thompson’s wishes and understanding in a calm environment, and document her preference about who is present.
options.B: Proceed with treatment under Chloe’s consent to avoid losing the transport and sedation slot, as delaying will disadvantage Mrs Thompson.
options.C: Contact Dr Banerjee for guidance on capacity and consent, and if unavailable seek advice from the safeguarding/mental capacity lead before any irreversible treatment.
options.D: Tell Olivia to double-book the next sedation slot so you can proceed today and keep the waiting list moving.
options.E: Offer Mrs Thompson only non-invasive care today (e.g., preventive advice) and defer all operative decisions until Dr Banerjee is present, without exploring her capacity now.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions focus on ensuring valid consent and respecting Mrs Thompson’s autonomy while managing risk. A is appropriate because Mrs Thompson has expressed a clear wish for Chloe to leave; creating a private space supports confidentiality and reduces coercion, and documenting this is essential. C is appropriate because the situation is complex: potential capacity concerns, pressure to proceed, and disagreement with a supporter. Escalating to Dr Banerjee or an appropriate lead aligns with recognising limits of competence and using MDT support. F is also appropriate because it sets out the correct legal/ethical framework: consent from the patient where capacity exists, and a structured capacity assessment and best-interests process (with advocacy) if it does not.

B, D, and H prioritise service pressures and interpersonal ease over lawful consent and patient-centred care, risking treatment without valid authority. G is unsafe because Chloe has a conflict of interest and may inadvertently influence Mrs Thompson; interpretation should be impartial. E is weaker because while deferring irreversible treatment may be sensible, it fails to address Mrs Thompson’s immediate wishes, capacity assessment, and confidentiality request, and does not actively manage the ethical tensions in the moment.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires capacity, voluntariness, and patient understanding; service pressures do not override this.
Respect patient confidentiality and preferences about who is present during discussions.
Escalate complex capacity/consent cases to seniors and appropriate leads; use advocacy where needed.
Support workers may assist but should not coerce or replace the patient’s decision-making.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover as a DCT. Nurse Angela asks you to review Mr Ibrahim, post-op day 1 after incision and drainage for a submandibular abscess. He is tachycardic and says his throat feels tighter. The ward is short-staffed and Angela is frustrated because she has already bleeped the registrar, Dr Lewis, twice with no reply. Your dental nurse, Emily, is with you and notes the oxygen saturation has dropped from 98% to 93% on room air. At the same time, the ward clerk (Mark) tells you radiology are chasing a consent form for another patient’s CT, and he needs you to sign it “now” to avoid cancellation. Mr Ibrahim’s wife is anxious and asks whether he is “going to choke”.

options.A: Go to Mr Ibrahim immediately, ask Angela to start observations and oxygen, and request an urgent review from Dr Lewis/anaesthetics while you assess airway and sepsis indicators.
options.B: Sign the CT consent form for the other patient first to prevent cancellation, then assess Mr Ibrahim once the paperwork is completed.
options.C: Ask Emily to stay with Mr Ibrahim’s wife to reassure her while you look for Dr Lewis on the ward to discuss the deterioration face-to-face.
options.D: Document Mr Ibrahim’s observations and your assessment, and use the hospital escalation policy (e.g., critical care outreach/MET call) if Dr Lewis remains unavailable.
options.E: Tell Angela that 93% saturation is acceptable post-operatively and advise her to recheck in an hour because you are not the registrar.

explanation:
Mr Ibrahim may be deteriorating with potential airway compromise and sepsis; patient safety overrides administrative pressures. A is most appropriate: you respond immediately, initiate basic supportive measures with Angela, and escalate urgently to Dr Lewis and, if needed, anaesthetics. This shows leadership, teamwork, and clinical prioritisation. D is next because clear documentation and using formal escalation pathways protects the patient when the registrar is unresponsive; NHS systems exist for exactly this scenario. C is reasonable but less urgent than A/D: supporting the wife is important, but searching for Dr Lewis in person could delay escalation; however, delegating reassurance to Emily is sensible if it does not compromise care.

B is poor because signing a CT consent form for another patient is not an emergency and may be inappropriate if you are not the treating clinician; it distracts from a potentially life-threatening situation. E is least appropriate: it dismisses a concerning trend (falling saturations, tachycardia, throat tightness) and abdicates responsibility. As a DCT you must recognise deterioration, act within competence, and escalate promptly rather than delay or normalise risk.
learningPoints: Prioritise suspected airway compromise and sepsis over non-urgent administrative tasks.
Use structured escalation pathways when senior clinicians are unavailable.
Delegate appropriately to support relatives while maintaining focus on urgent care.
Document assessments, actions, and escalation clearly in the clinical record.

scenario: In a restorative clinic, you are assisting Dr Chen with a complex endodontic case for Mrs Singh. The dental nurse, Lucy, shows you a photo on her phone of Mrs Singh’s radiograph and says she posted it in a private messaging group with friends from dental school to ask for “tips”. The image includes Mrs Singh’s name and hospital number. Lucy looks worried and says she deleted it, but one friend has already replied. Mrs Singh is in the waiting area and has been asking about how her information is stored. Dr Chen is running behind and tells you to “sort it quietly” because a complaint would be a headache. The receptionist, Olivia, mentions Mrs Singh’s husband has called twice asking for details of today’s treatment.

options.A: Tell Lucy to immediately inform Dr Chen and the information governance lead, preserve evidence of what was shared, and complete a datix/incident report according to local policy.
options.B: Advise Lucy to say nothing because the group was private, and reassure her that deleting the message means there is no breach.
options.C: Speak with Mrs Singh to acknowledge that a potential confidentiality incident has occurred, explain the next steps, and offer to involve the patient advice and liaison service if she wishes.
options.D: Provide Mrs Singh’s husband with a brief update because he is her next of kin and is worried, to reduce the risk of complaint.
options.E: Ask Lucy to forward the image to you so you can assess the severity before deciding whether to escalate.

explanation:
A is essential: sharing an identifiable radiograph with name and hospital number is a confidentiality breach. NHS professionalism requires prompt escalation through information governance processes and incident reporting (e.g., Datix), and preserving evidence supports an accurate investigation. C is appropriate because patients have a right to openness when their data may have been compromised; explaining next steps and offering support aligns with the duty of candour and maintains trust with Mrs Singh. F is also appropriate as an immediate harm-reduction step and supportive remediation for Lucy, including requesting deletion and reinforcing social media/confidentiality standards.

B and G are inappropriate because they minimise and conceal the incident, risking further harm and compounding misconduct. D is unsafe: “next of kin” does not automatically grant consent to disclose; you should only share information with explicit patient permission. E is poor because it repeats the breach by further sharing the image.
