Option E is inappropriate because delaying decisions until ward round risks deterioration and undermines timely care. Option A is least appropriate: instructing dispensing without resolving allergy uncertainty and clinical deterioration risks harm and fails medicines governance standards.
learningPoints: Prioritise deteriorating patients and initiate sepsis pathways promptly.
Clarify allergies and ambiguous prescriptions before antibiotics are dispensed.
Escalate to seniors when urgency or uncertainty exceeds your competence.
Triage competing ward tasks while ensuring safe delegation.

scenario:
You are a DCT in a restorative clinic. Dr Chen (consultant) is running late, and Dr Grant (registrar) asks you to start reviewing Mrs Hassan, who needs consent for a complex crown lengthening and crown plan. Lucy (dental nurse) quietly tells you she is worried because she overheard Dr Grant joking in the staff room about “posting the before-and-after” of Mrs Hassan’s smile on his private Instagram, saying he would “hide the name anyway.”

In the surgery, Mrs Hassan is anxious and asks whether photos will be taken and who will see them. Dr Grant pops his head in and says, in front of Mrs Hassan, “We always take photos—standard. Don’t worry, it’s just for teaching.” Lucy looks uncomfortable.

options.A: Reassure Mrs Hassan that no photos will be taken at all, to reduce her anxiety and avoid conflict with Dr Grant.
options.B: Clarify with Mrs Hassan what clinical photographs are for, explain choices (care record vs teaching), and obtain explicit consent for each use; document her decision in the notes.
options.C: After the appointment, raise Lucy’s concern with Dr Grant privately, focusing on professional standards and social media guidance, and ask him to confirm he will not use patient images on personal accounts.
options.D: Challenge Dr Grant immediately in front of Mrs Hassan, stating that his comment was inappropriate and that he is breaching confidentiality.
options.E: Ask Lucy to submit an anonymous incident report without telling anyone, so it cannot be traced back to the team.

explanation:
Option B is essential for patient-centred care and informed consent. Mrs Hassan has asked directly about photographs; you must explain the purpose, storage, and who can access images, and obtain explicit consent for clinical record use and any teaching/publication separately. Documenting her decision supports confidentiality and governance.

Option C appropriately addresses Lucy’s concern through a private, professional conversation with Dr Grant, avoiding undermining him in front of the patient while still challenging potentially risky behaviour. This aligns with NHS professionalism expectations and social media standards.

Option F is appropriate if concerns persist: escalating to Dr Chen or governance ensures patient safety and organisational accountability, with factual documentation.

Option A is misleading and may create later conflict if photos are clinically indicated. Option D may be justified if there is immediate risk, but here it risks an unhelpful confrontation in front of Mrs Hassan. Option E encourages covert action without first attempting local resolution. Option G ignores a direct consent issue. Option H is unprofessional and stigmatising.
learningPoints: Obtain explicit, documented consent for clinical images and separate consent for teaching/publication.
Address professionalism concerns with colleagues promptly and appropriately, usually in private first.
Escalate to seniors/governance if concerns are not resolved or patient confidentiality is at risk.
Avoid misleading reassurance or stigmatising notes about patients.

scenario:
You are the DCT on-call in trauma and emergency dental clinic on a Friday evening. A 7-year-old, Sophie, arrives after a fall with a suspected avulsed upper incisor. Chloe (dental nurse) says the parent, Mrs Khan, is panicking and has the tooth wrapped in tissue; she is demanding immediate reimplantation. In the next bay, Mr Clarke is waiting with facial swelling and fever, and reception (Olivia) warns that the waiting room is full and tempers are rising.

You have limited paediatric trauma experience and the paediatric dental registrar is off-site. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in theatre and may not answer quickly. Mrs Khan is filming on her phone, saying she wants “proof” you acted fast. Chloe looks stressed and tells you the resus trolley has just been checked but the paediatric sedation kit is missing.

options.A: Ask Mrs Khan to stop filming immediately and threaten to refuse treatment if she continues, to regain control of the situation.
options.B: Triage both patients: assess Sophie first for time-critical avulsion management and head injury red flags, while asking Chloe to alert the on-call paediatric dental registrar/OMFS registrar and locate the missing kit via switchboard.
options.C: Attempt reimplantation immediately without senior input to meet the time window, even if you are unsure, because delay is worse than imperfect technique.
options.D: Explain calmly to Mrs Khan that filming in clinical areas can compromise privacy; ask her to stop, but continue care regardless, and document the discussion and any ongoing filming.
options.E: Ask Olivia to call security to remove Mrs Khan from the department because she is distressed and disruptive.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate: it demonstrates structured triage under pressure, recognising Sophie’s time-critical avulsion while also assessing for head injury red flags. It also addresses limits of competence by asking Chloe to escalate to the appropriate on-call registrar and to resolve the missing kit through switchboard—supporting safe systems.

Option D is next: you should manage filming professionally by explaining confidentiality and privacy expectations, requesting that Mrs Khan stop, and documenting the interaction. Continuing necessary care avoids escalating conflict while maintaining professional boundaries.

Option A is less appropriate because threatening to refuse treatment risks worsening an already distressed situation and could delay urgent care; it may be necessary only if behaviour prevents safe care. Option F is inappropriate as a blanket rule: prioritisation should be based on clinical urgency (Mr Clarke’s fever and swelling could indicate sepsis/airway risk). Option C is least appropriate: attempting reimplantation while unsure and without appropriate support may compromise Sophie’s outcome and patient safety; seeking timely senior guidance and following protocols is safer than acting beyond competence.
learningPoints: Use structured triage to manage multiple urgent patients, prioritising by clinical risk not age alone.
Recognise limits of competence and escalate early for paediatric trauma and missing equipment issues.
Manage filming professionally by addressing confidentiality and documenting discussions without delaying essential care.
Avoid acting beyond competence under time pressure; patient safety and protocol-led care come first.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mrs Thompson (68) is booked for extraction of LL6 for acute pain, but her electronic record shows “warfarin—unknown indication” and no INR documented. Mrs Thompson is frustrated after waiting 90 minutes and says she “stopped the tablets yesterday” because her neighbour advised it. Dr Kumar (registrar) is tied up with a facial swelling case and asks you to “just get it done” because the waiting room is full. Nadia (receptionist) says two more urgent walk-ins have arrived. Mrs Thompson’s daughter is on speakerphone insisting the tooth must come out today and becomes angry when you mention postponing. You feel pressure to keep the clinic moving, but you are uncertain about anticoagulation status, consent validity under distress, and whether stopping warfarin is safe.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using local haemostatic measures, documenting that Mrs Thompson reports stopping warfarin yesterday and that Dr Kumar wanted the clinic to keep moving.
options.B: Explain to Mrs Thompson and her daughter that you cannot safely extract without an up-to-date INR/anticoagulation plan, offer analgesia and temporisation, and ask Sarah to help you contact the on-call medical team/anticoagulation service while you update Dr Kumar.
options.C: Ask Nadia to rebook Mrs Thompson into the next available slot and move on to the next patient immediately, leaving Sarah to deal with Mrs Thompson’s complaints at reception.
options.D: Ask Dr Kumar to come and authorise the extraction decision, but continue setting up for extraction in the meantime to save time.
options.E: Tell Mrs Thompson that her daughter’s behaviour is unacceptable and end the appointment, advising her to attend her GP instead.

explanation: The most appropriate action is B: Mrs Thompson’s anticoagulation status is unclear, there is no INR, and she has independently stopped warfarin. Proceeding would risk significant bleeding and also fails to address the medical risk of stopping anticoagulation. You should prioritise patient safety, provide pain control/temporisation, and escalate appropriately (e.g., anticoagulation/medical team) while keeping Dr Kumar informed. D is next best because involving Dr Kumar (as the registrar) is appropriate given the risk and your limits of competence; however, setting up to extract before a safe plan is agreed risks creating momentum bias and pressuring the patient. A is unsafe: relying on patient-reported cessation and documenting pressure from Dr Kumar does not mitigate clinical risk and could breach professional integrity. C is poor teamwork and patient-centred care: it offloads conflict to Sarah and ignores the duty to explain and manage pain safely. E is least appropriate: although boundaries matter, terminating care punitively escalates the situation and fails to ensure safe interim management or appropriate signposting within the service.
learningPoints: Do not undertake extractions on anticoagulated patients without an appropriate, documented plan (e.g., INR where relevant).
Patient safety overrides time pressure and throughput targets in urgent care settings.
Escalate to senior support when risk is high or information is missing; avoid ‘momentum’ towards unsafe treatment.
Manage conflict professionally while maintaining patient-centred communication and interim pain control.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Tyler (9) with his mother Mrs Khan for multiple carious teeth and repeated pain episodes. Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you she has noticed bruising on Tyler’s upper arms on two previous visits, and today Tyler flinches when Mrs Khan raises her voice. Mrs Khan is demanding antibiotics “to stop social services getting involved” and refuses radiographs, saying they are “dangerous”. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in an MDT meeting off-site and not immediately available. The clinic is running 40 minutes late, and Olivia (receptionist) says another child with autism is becoming distressed in the waiting area. Tyler answers questions minimally and keeps looking at his mother before speaking. You are balancing urgent dental pain management, valid consent for radiographs, confidentiality, and a possible safeguarding concern.

options.A: Prescribe antibiotics to de-escalate the situation and book Tyler for radiographs at the next visit when Mrs Khan is calmer.
options.B: Ask Emily to stay with Tyler while you step out to call the safeguarding lead for immediate advice, then return to explain next steps to Mrs Khan in a calm, factual way.
options.C: Proceed with radiographs under implied consent because Tyler is in pain and it will speed up treatment planning.
options.D: Arrange a same-day discussion with Dr O’Connor by phone, documenting Emily’s observations and your concerns, and agree whether to make a safeguarding referral.
options.E: Tell Olivia to ask the next patient to wait outside so you can keep Tyler’s appointment running without interruption.

explanation: B, D and F best balance immediate care with safeguarding duties. B is appropriate because you act promptly on Emily’s repeated observations, seek advice from the safeguarding lead, and ensure Tyler is supported while you step out—this is proportionate and maintains confidentiality. D complements this by escalating to Dr O’Connor for senior oversight and ensuring robust documentation and a clear plan about referral thresholds; safeguarding decisions should be timely, not delayed by clinic pressures. F addresses patient-centred care: Tyler’s pain can be managed with local measures and analgesia advice, and you should explain that antibiotics are not a substitute for operative care and are only indicated when clinically warranted. A is inappropriate antibiotic prescribing and risks collusion with Mrs Khan’s stated motive. C breaches consent standards and undermines trust; refusal of radiographs requires explanation and alternatives, not override. E prioritises flow over safety and does not address the core concerns. G is unprofessional and likely to escalate risk. H is unsafe because doing nothing despite a reasonable concern fails the duty to act and could leave Tyler at ongoing risk.
learningPoints: Safeguarding concerns should be acted on promptly using local pathways and senior advice.
Valid consent requires understanding; refusal of radiographs should be managed through explanation and alternatives, not coercion.
Antibiotic stewardship is part of patient safety; prescribe only when clinically indicated.
Document contemporaneously and share concerns appropriately within the team and safeguarding framework.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover as the DCT. Mr Petrov (54) is post-op day 1 after incision and drainage of a submandibular abscess. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to rewrite his discharge medications because the electronic prescribing system is slow and the FY1 is in theatre. The plan in yesterday’s notes by Dr Chen (consultant) includes IV antibiotics to continue for 48 hours, but the drug chart shows oral antibiotics started overnight. Mr Petrov is keen to leave to care for his wife and is getting agitated, saying he will self-discharge. Angela (ward nurse) says the bed is needed for an incoming trauma admission. At the same time, Dr Grant (registrar) calls asking you to review a new referral in A&E.

options.A: Agree to rewrite the discharge prescription immediately based on the drug chart to speed up discharge, and ask Rachel to file the notes later.
options.B: Explain to Rachel and Angela that you need to clarify the antibiotic plan with Dr Grant or Dr Chen before any discharge decision, then review the notes and speak to Mr Petrov about the reasons for ongoing IV treatment.
options.C: Tell Mr Petrov that if he self-discharges you will document it and that it is his responsibility, then move on to the A&E referral.
options.D: Ask Rachel to bleep Dr Grant for clarification while you assess Mr Petrov clinically (airway, swelling, observations) and reconcile the discrepancy between the notes and the chart.
options.E: Delay all action on the ward issue and go directly to A&E because the registrar requested it first.

explanation: D is most appropriate because it combines immediate patient safety (clinical assessment of Mr Petrov post-op) with practical teamwork: Rachel can bleep Dr Grant while you reconcile conflicting documentation. This reduces delay without making unsafe assumptions. B is also appropriate: it sets clear boundaries with Rachel and Angela, emphasises the need to clarify the plan (notes vs chart), and communicates transparently with Mr Petrov to reduce agitation and support informed decisions. C is less appropriate: while documenting potential self-discharge is necessary, the tone is punitive and it fails to explore capacity, risks, and mitigation. A is unsafe prescribing practice—using the chart alone when the consultant plan differs risks premature discharge and undermines professional integrity. E is least appropriate because it ignores an immediate safety issue on the ward; prioritisation should be based on risk, and a post-op deep neck infection patient with conflicting antibiotic plans requires urgent clarification before leaving the ward.
learningPoints: Reconcile discrepancies between notes and drug charts before prescribing or discharge.
Use delegation appropriately (e.g., asking Rachel to bleep Dr Grant) while you assess the patient.
Communicate reasons for delays to reduce frustration and support informed decisions.
Prioritise tasks by clinical risk, not by who asked first.

scenario: You are in a restorative clinic at a dental hospital. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is running late, and you are asked to start an assessment of Mr Clarke (37) who has severe tooth wear and is very self-conscious. Lucy (dental nurse) shows you a message from Maria (hygienist) saying she overheard Dr Lewis (registrar) joking in the staff room about Mr Clarke’s “terrible diet” and that “he’ll never change”. Mr Clarke is now in the chair and says he heard “someone laughing about me” and asks directly if staff talk about patients. He appears embarrassed and says he may leave. At the same time, Olivia (receptionist) tells you the next patient has arrived early and is complaining about delays.

options.A: Reassure Mr Clarke that staff do not discuss patients, and change the subject to keep the appointment on track.
options.B: Acknowledge Mr Clarke’s concern, apologise for how it has made him feel, explain that respectful behaviour is expected, and ask if he would like a short break or to continue with you and Lucy present.
options.C: Immediately confront Dr Lewis in front of the team and insist he apologise to Mr Clarke before any further care continues.
options.D: After the appointment, document what Lucy and Maria reported and raise the concern with Dr Banerjee or the department’s educational supervisor, focusing on behaviour standards rather than gossip.
options.E: Tell Olivia to move the next patient to another clinician so you can spend time rebuilding rapport with Mr Clarke.

explanation: B is appropriate because it is patient-centred and honest: Mr Clarke has raised a trust issue. You should acknowledge his feelings, apologise for the experience (without making unverifiable claims), and offer him control over how to proceed. D is appropriate because concerns about Dr Lewis’s behaviour should be documented and escalated via appropriate channels (Dr Banerjee/educational supervisor), focusing on maintaining professional standards and preventing recurrence. G is also appropriate: a timely, private conversation with Dr Lewis can address behaviour, promote reflection, and reduce future harm, provided it does not disrupt immediate patient care. A is inappropriate because it offers false reassurance and risks further loss of trust if later contradicted. C escalates conflict and may breach fairness and confidentiality. E may be helpful operationally but is not one of the most appropriate actions compared with directly addressing the professionalism issue and the patient’s concern. F is dismissive and undermines rapport. H is poor: it discourages speaking up and damages a safety culture.
learningPoints: Patient trust is protected by honest, respectful communication when concerns are raised.
Address unprofessional behaviour through appropriate escalation and documentation pathways.
Where possible, give feedback to colleagues privately and constructively.
A positive speaking-up culture supports patient-centred care and team professionalism.

scenario: You are the DCT on a sedation clinic list. Mrs Hassan (45) is booked for IV sedation for surgical extraction of an impacted UR8. Dr Fernandes (specialty dentist) is delayed in traffic and will arrive in 30 minutes. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you Mrs Hassan is very anxious and is threatening to leave because she waited months for this appointment. The consent form is partially completed, and the pre-sedation assessment notes mention obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) but no recent sleep study details. Mrs Hassan says she “forgets the machine sometimes” and asks if you can “just put the cannula in and start” to avoid further waiting. Mark (radiographer) phones to say the OPG on file is low quality and asks whether to proceed with a repeat image today. Meanwhile, reception reports two patients have already DNA’d this morning, and there is pressure to avoid losing another slot.

options.A: Start cannulation and begin titrating midazolam so the procedure can start as soon as Dr Fernandes arrives.
options.B: Explain to Mrs Hassan that sedation cannot start without the sedationist present and a complete assessment/consent, offer to re-check observations and update the assessment, and ask Chloe to keep Mrs Hassan informed about Dr Fernandes’ arrival time.
options.C: Agree to proceed without repeat radiography to avoid delay, relying on the existing OPG and clinical judgement.
options.D: Ask Mark to repeat the OPG only after confirming justification and pregnancy status, and document the reason for repeat imaging given the poor quality of the existing film.
options.E: Tell reception to record Mrs Hassan as a DNA if she leaves and move on to the next patient without further discussion.

explanation: B is most appropriate. With possible OSA, incomplete documentation, and the sedation lead (Dr Fernandes) absent, starting sedation would exceed your competence and breach sedation governance. You should explain clearly to Mrs Hassan why you cannot begin sedation yet, continue the pre-sedation checks, and use Chloe to support communication and anxiety management. D is next best: Mark’s concern about OPG quality affects surgical planning and patient safety. A repeat radiograph is acceptable only if justified, with pregnancy status checked and documentation completed, aligning with radiation protection principles. C is less appropriate: proceeding with a low-quality OPG increases risk (e.g., root proximity, sinus involvement) and undermines informed consent, but it is still safer than starting sedation unsupervised. A is unsafe and potentially serious misconduct: cannulation may be reasonable, but titrating midazolam without the appropriate clinician present and without complete assessment/consent is unacceptable. E is least appropriate because it is punitive, fails to manage anxiety or safety, and misses an opportunity to retain the patient through supportive communication and safe planning.
learningPoints: Do not commence IV sedation without appropriate supervision, complete assessment, and valid consent.
Recognise and act within limits of competence, especially with higher-risk comorbidities such as OSA.
Radiographs should be justified and documented; poor-quality images may require repeat imaging for safety.
Manage waiting-time pressure through communication and support rather than unsafe shortcuts.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Khan is booked for an urgent extraction. Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you she thinks he mentioned a previous reaction to “penicillin” at reception, but the medical history form is incomplete and the triage note just says “NKDA”. Mr Khan is in pain and irritable, and Nadia (receptionist) says there are six waiting patients and Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and not reachable. Mr Khan insists he needs the tooth out today and asks for “strong antibiotics as well” because his friend had them. You are also running late for a sedation assessment you promised to help with. Sarah looks anxious and says she doesn’t want to set up until the allergy is clarified.

options.A: Proceed with local anaesthetic extraction but avoid prescribing any antibiotics, documenting that the form stated NKDA.
options.B: Pause the appointment to take a focused allergy history from Mr Khan, ask Sarah to confirm what she heard, and update the records before deciding on treatment.
options.C: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan for another day when Dr Kumar is available, explaining you cannot treat him without senior input.
options.D: Call the on-call consultant Dr Ahmed for advice about managing uncertain penicillin allergy and whether treatment should proceed today.
options.E: Ask Sarah to set up while you quickly start the sedation assessment, then return to clarify the allergy when you are ready to inject.

explanation:
The most appropriate first step is to clarify the immediate safety risk. Option B prioritises patient safety by taking a focused allergy history from Mr Khan, corroborating Sarah’s concern, and correcting the documentation before administering local anaesthetic or prescribing. Incomplete or conflicting records (“NKDA” vs possible penicillin reaction) require verification; this also supports informed consent and good record-keeping.

Option D is next best: if uncertainty remains (e.g., unclear reaction details, language barriers, complex history) escalating to Dr Ahmed is appropriate given Dr Kumar is unavailable. Seeking senior support is consistent with recognising limits of competence under pressure.

Option C may be necessary if safe care cannot be delivered today, but rebooking is disruptive and should follow reasonable attempts to clarify and/or obtain advice.

Option A is unsafe and professionally weak: proceeding while relying on an incomplete form ignores a raised concern and risks harm; it also fails to address the patient’s antibiotic request appropriately. Option E is least appropriate: asking Sarah to set up while you divert to another task undermines teamwork, increases error risk, and pressures the nurse to act despite her expressed concern.
learningPoints: Conflicting or incomplete allergy documentation must be clarified before treatment or prescribing.
Patient safety and accurate records outweigh time pressure and clinic flow.
Escalate appropriately when senior support is needed and supervisors are unavailable.
Support team members who raise safety concerns; do not bypass them to save time.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Daniel, who insists on speaking for her. Mrs Thompson has early dementia and limited English; today the interpreter service has failed to connect. Lucy (dental nurse) notices Daniel answers every question and becomes defensive when you try to address Mrs Thompson directly. The referral from Dr O’Connor (consultant) requests assessment for extractions under sedation due to repeated infections, but Mrs Thompson quietly says she is “scared” and does not want “sleep medicine”. Daniel pushes for sedation today, saying he has taken time off work and threatens to complain to Mr Lewis (practice manager) if you “waste their appointment”. Meanwhile, Maria (hygienist) is waiting to hand over another patient who is becoming distressed in the waiting area.

options.A: Ask Daniel to step out briefly so you can assess Mrs Thompson’s understanding and wishes in private, with Lucy present as a chaperone.
options.B: Proceed to book Mrs Thompson for sedation as per Dr O’Connor’s referral to avoid delay and potential infections.
options.C: Document concerns about capacity and communication barriers, and arrange a rebook with a professional interpreter and, if needed, a best-interests discussion involving Dr O’Connor.
options.D: Tell Daniel you will only speak to him because he is her next of kin and it will keep the clinic running on time.
options.E: Ask Maria to take over the consent discussion so you can keep to time and manage the distressed patient in the waiting area.

explanation:
A, C and F best protect Mrs Thompson’s autonomy and safety. Option A addresses the interpersonal dynamic: Daniel’s dominance may be unintentionally coercive. Speaking to Mrs Thompson privately (with Lucy as chaperone) helps assess her wishes and capacity, and reduces undue influence. 

Option C is appropriate because the interpreter failure and potential cognitive impairment mean valid consent is unlikely today. Documenting concerns and arranging a rebook with a professional interpreter supports NHS standards on informed consent, capacity assessment and clear records. Involving Dr O’Connor for a best-interests framework may be needed if capacity is lacking.

Option F sets boundaries with Daniel and explains why care cannot proceed without adequate communication, while still offering interim management and safety-netting to address infection risk.

Options B and H are unsafe/invalid: proceeding to sedation or having Daniel sign undermines consent and could constitute assault. Option D is incorrect: next of kin has no automatic decision-making authority. Option E inappropriately delegates consent to Maria and fragments care. Option G may help manage conflict, but it should not replace direct clinical steps to assess capacity and communication; it is secondary to A/C/F.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires patient understanding; interpreter failure may necessitate rebooking.
Assess capacity and guard against undue influence from relatives.
Next of kin cannot consent for an adult with capacity; best-interests processes apply if capacity is lacking.
Maintain patient-centred care while offering interim management and safety-netting.

scenario: On an OMFS ward, you review Mr Petrov post-operatively after incision and drainage. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you she overheard Dr Grant (specialty dentist) joking in the staff room about Mr Petrov’s “terrible breath” and showing a photo of the swelling on his personal phone to two medical students. Mr Petrov is awake, anxious and asks you whether staff have been “talking about him” because he heard laughter earlier. Dr Grant is senior to you and is known to be defensive. At the same time, Angela (ward nurse) is asking you to rewrite a drug chart because the antibiotic prescription is unclear, and the ward round with Dr Chen (consultant) starts in 10 minutes.

options.A: Reassure Mr Petrov that staff conversations are private, avoid discussing the allegation, and focus only on his clinical questions.
options.B: Prioritise clarifying the antibiotic prescription with Angela to ensure safe administration, then speak to Dr Grant privately about the phone photo and comments.
options.C: Confront Dr Grant in the staff room immediately in front of the students and nurses, instructing him to delete the photo.
options.D: Escalate the concern directly to Dr Chen before taking any other action, as Dr Grant is senior.
options.E: Document Rachel’s report factually, advise Rachel on raising the concern through appropriate channels, and consider informing the ward manager or governance lead if confidentiality breach is likely.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it manages competing priorities: Angela’s query about the unclear antibiotic prescription is an immediate patient-safety issue, and resolving it prevents harm. Once urgent prescribing is addressed, speaking to Dr Grant privately is proportionate, maintains professionalism, and gives him an opportunity to reflect and take corrective action (e.g., deleting images, stopping inappropriate sharing).

Option E is also appropriate: documenting Rachel’s report factually and supporting use of appropriate reporting pathways aligns with NHS duty of candour, information governance and raising concerns. If a confidentiality breach is suspected, escalation to the ward manager/governance may be necessary.

Option D may be needed if Dr Grant does not respond appropriately or if the breach appears serious/ongoing, but escalating immediately without attempting local resolution can be premature.

Option A is weak: Mr Petrov’s anxiety should be addressed honestly and sensitively; dismissing his concern risks undermining trust. You should not confirm unverified allegations, but you can acknowledge his feelings and explain how confidentiality is protected.

Option C is least appropriate: public confrontation risks escalation, humiliates Dr Grant, may not stop the behaviour effectively, and could worsen team dynamics while still not addressing governance properly.
learningPoints: Address immediate clinical safety issues first (e.g., unclear prescriptions) while not ignoring professionalism concerns.
Maintain confidentiality and follow information governance rules regarding patient images.
Raise concerns proportionately: start with a private discussion when appropriate, then escalate if needed.
Support colleagues who report concerns and document facts objectively.

scenario: In the radiology department, you accompany Mrs Khan from restorative clinic for a CBCT requested for implant planning. Elena (radiographer) refuses to proceed because the referral form from Dr Banerjee (consultant) lacks justification and does not record pregnancy status. Mrs Khan is frustrated; she took unpaid leave and says Dr Banerjee promised it would be done today. Mark (radiology receptionist) says the next slot is in three weeks and the clinic is already overbooked. You notice the restorative notes include a brief mention of “possible retained root close to IAN” but no explicit CBCT justification. Dr Banerjee is in an MDT meeting and not answering calls. Elena is polite but firm, saying IR(ME)R requires adequate information and she will not be pressured. You also have a patient waiting back in clinic with a loose temporary crown.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed based on the restorative notes and promise to email the justification later once Dr Banerjee replies.
options.B: Speak with Mrs Khan to explain why Elena cannot legally proceed without adequate justification, apologise for the inconvenience, and discuss options for rebooking or alternative imaging.
options.C: Contact the on-call registrar Dr Lewis to obtain immediate clinical justification and pregnancy status, and update the referral so radiology can decide appropriately.
options.D: Ask Mark to “squeeze” Mrs Khan into a walk-in slot because she has taken time off and is becoming angry.
options.E: Return Mrs Khan to restorative clinic without explanation and tell her to complain to Dr Banerjee if she is unhappy.

explanation:
B, C and F best balance patient-centred communication with legal and safety requirements. Option B manages Mrs Khan’s frustration by explaining IR(ME)R constraints clearly, apologising, and offering practical next steps. This supports trust and reduces conflict between departments.

Option C is appropriate escalation: with Dr Banerjee unavailable, contacting Dr Lewis to provide justification and confirm the clinical question allows the referral to be completed properly. Radiology can then make an informed decision about whether CBCT is indicated.

Option F addresses a specific missing safety element—pregnancy status—by checking directly with Mrs Khan and documenting it. However, pregnancy status alone is not sufficient; justification still must come from an entitled referrer, hence pairing with C.

Options A and D inappropriately pressure radiology to breach IR(ME)R and undermine Elena’s professional responsibilities. Option E is dismissive and likely to escalate a complaint. Option G is unprofessional and coercive. Option H may be clinically inappropriate and does not answer the implant planning/IAN proximity question; it also bypasses the agreed pathway and may duplicate radiation exposure.
learningPoints: IR(ME)R requires adequate justification and safety information before imaging proceeds.
Manage patient expectations with clear explanations and apologies without blaming other teams.
Use appropriate escalation routes when the original referrer is unavailable.
Avoid pressuring colleagues to bypass legal or governance requirements.

scenario: You are the DCT covering trauma/on-call overnight. A 9-year-old, Tyler, arrives with his mother after a fall. He has an avulsed upper incisor wrapped in tissue and is crying. Chloe (dental nurse) is new to on-call and looks overwhelmed. The ED junior doctor has already given Tyler ibuprofen and asks you to “just splint it quickly” because the department is full. You have limited experience with avulsion management in children and are unsure about replantation timing and tetanus considerations. Tyler’s mother is anxious and keeps asking if the tooth can be saved; she also says Tyler has a “heart condition” but cannot recall details, and the ED notes are incomplete. Dr Fernandes (registrar) is off-site but contactable; Dr Ahmed (consultant) is on-call second.

options.A: Attempt immediate replantation and splinting yourself to avoid delay, then call Dr Fernandes afterwards if there are complications.
options.B: Ask Chloe to prepare the trauma kit while you obtain a focused medical history from Tyler’s mother and check ED records for the cardiac condition and tetanus status.
options.C: Call Dr Fernandes for urgent guidance and, if needed, attendance, explaining your limited experience and the uncertainty about Tyler’s medical history.
options.D: Tell the ED junior doctor you cannot help and ask them to refer Tyler to a dentist tomorrow because you are not confident.
options.E: Explain to Tyler’s mother that you need to confirm the cardiac history and discuss risks/benefits before proceeding, and document the consent discussion clearly.

explanation:
Option C is most appropriate: avulsion management is time-critical, but the combination of your limited experience, Tyler’s age, and an unclear “heart condition” makes early senior input essential. Contacting Dr Fernandes demonstrates insight into limits of competence and supports safe decision-making under pressure.

Option B is next: delegating practical preparation to Chloe while you rapidly gather key information (cardiac history, medications, tetanus, time out of mouth, storage medium) is efficient and supports clinical safety. It also helps Chloe feel supported.

Option E is important for informed consent: Tyler’s mother needs a clear explanation of risks, benefits, and uncertainties, especially with incomplete medical history. Documenting the discussion protects the patient and team.

Option A is risky: acting independently to “avoid delay” could cause harm if, for example, prophylaxis or medical considerations are missed. Speed does not justify unsafe practice.

Option D is least appropriate: refusing to help and delaying to “tomorrow” ignores the emergency nature of avulsion and fails your on-call responsibility. If you cannot manage alone, you must escalate rather than defer care.
learningPoints: Recognise and act on limits of competence early, especially in time-critical emergencies.
Gather key medical history rapidly and verify incomplete records before invasive treatment.
Use the team effectively: delegate preparation tasks while you assess and consent.
Time pressure from other departments should not override safe, supervised care.

scenario: You are a DCT in an NHS oral surgery clinic. Dr Chen (consultant) is running two rooms to reduce the waiting list and is briefly called to theatre. Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you that Mr Ibrahim, booked for an extraction, mentioned a “bad reaction to antibiotics” years ago but the referral letter is vague and the medical history form is incomplete. Mr Ibrahim is anxious and says he needs the tooth out today because he has a job interview tomorrow. In the next room, Dr Kumar (registrar) asks you to start the local anaesthetic for another patient to keep the clinic moving. Receptionist Nadia warns that three patients are already complaining about delays. You can’t access the GP summary because the IT system is intermittently down.

options.A: Proceed with Mr Ibrahim’s extraction as planned, document “no known drug allergies” based on his incomplete form, and reassure Sarah that delays will upset Dr Chen.
options.B: Ask Sarah to stay with Mr Ibrahim while you clarify the nature of his past reaction, check previous hospital notes on the electronic record, and if still unclear, postpone extraction and arrange urgent GP confirmation or allergy documentation.
options.C: Start local anaesthetic for Dr Kumar’s patient immediately to demonstrate efficiency, then return to Mr Ibrahim and deal with the allergy issue afterwards.
options.D: Call Dr Chen in theatre to request an immediate decision, explaining you cannot progress without clear allergy information, and ask Dr Kumar to cover the other room meanwhile.
options.E: Tell Mr Ibrahim that if he insists on treatment today you will proceed, but he must accept the risk; ask Nadia to obtain a GP phone number while you prepare instruments.

explanation:
Option B best prioritises patient safety and informed decision-making. Sarah has raised a credible concern about a possible drug allergy, and proceeding without clarifying the reaction could expose Mr Ibrahim to avoidable harm (e.g., inappropriate antibiotics or analgesics), as well as undermine documentation integrity. Exploring the history, checking available notes, and arranging confirmation if uncertainty remains reflects working within limits and using available systems safely.

Option D is appropriate next: escalating to Dr Chen for senior input is justified given the risk and the competing pressure from Dr Kumar and clinic delays. It also demonstrates openness and teamwork rather than silently taking on risk.

Option C is less appropriate: helping Dr Kumar may reduce delays, but it risks splitting attention and leaving Mr Ibrahim’s safety issue unresolved; however, it is not inherently unsafe if you ensure Mr Ibrahim is supervised by Sarah and you return promptly.

Option E is poor because it frames consent as accepting clinician risk; patients cannot be asked to “waive” professional duties. It also risks coercion under time pressure.

Option A is least appropriate: it ignores Sarah’s concern, falsifies or assumes allergy status, and prioritises throughput over safety and professional standards expected in NHS practice.
learningPoints: Act on concerns raised by dental nurses and other team members; they are part of the safety net.
Do not proceed with treatment when key medical history information is unclear and could affect safety.
Escalate appropriately to seniors when risk is significant or beyond your competence/authority.
Consent does not remove the clinician’s duty to provide safe care and accurate records.

scenario: You are in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Mr Clarke, who speaks for her throughout. Laura (hygienist) has already attempted debridement but stopped because Mrs Thompson repeatedly pulled away and looked tearful. Mrs Thompson has moderate dementia and is booked for urgent extractions under local anaesthetic next week. Today she has a painful ulcer and asks, “Can you just give me antibiotics?” Mr Clarke becomes irritated, saying the team is “wasting time” and insists you do whatever is quickest because he has to return to work. Receptionist Olivia tells you the clinic is overbooked due to staff sickness. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is off-site and only reachable by phone.

options.A: Ask to speak with Mrs Thompson alone for a few minutes (with a chaperone) to assess her understanding and wishes, then invite Mr Clarke back to discuss the plan.
options.B: Prescribe antibiotics immediately to satisfy Mrs Thompson and Mr Clarke, as this is the fastest way to reduce pain and avoid escalation.
options.C: Explain to Mr Clarke that you must assess capacity and obtain valid consent; if Mrs Thompson lacks capacity, discuss best-interests decision-making and involve the wider team as needed.
options.D: Ask Laura to continue debridement despite Mrs Thompson’s distress so the appointment is not ‘wasted’.
options.E: Offer appropriate analgesia advice and topical measures for the ulcer, and arrange a prompt review if symptoms worsen, rather than defaulting to antibiotics without indication.

explanation:
A, C and E best balance patient-centred care, consent, and clinical appropriateness. Speaking with Mrs Thompson alone (A), with a chaperone, helps you assess her understanding and whether she is being unduly influenced by Mr Clarke. This supports valid consent and respects her autonomy as far as possible. Clearly explaining the need to assess capacity and, if required, follow best-interests processes (C) aligns with NHS standards and protects Mrs Thompson from decisions being driven by convenience.

Providing symptom-appropriate management for the ulcer and pain (E) is clinically responsible and avoids unnecessary antibiotics, which should not be prescribed to manage distress or time pressure.

F is reasonable but not essential as a “top three” if you can competently assess capacity and document; however, phoning Dr O’Connor may still be appropriate depending on local policy. B is inappropriate because it prioritises speed and appeasement over stewardship and safety. D disregards Mrs Thompson’s distress and risks harm. G may be needed later but prematurely ends care and could worsen pain. H escalates conflict and may compromise access to care; boundaries should be set calmly while keeping the patient’s interests central.
learningPoints: Assess capacity and support decision-making; if capacity is lacking, follow best-interests processes.
Consider whether relatives are dominating decisions and create space to hear the patient’s voice.
Avoid prescribing antibiotics without indication, even under pressure or to satisfy demands.
Manage distress with empathy and adapt the plan rather than forcing treatment.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, post-op day 1 after incision and drainage for a facial swelling. While you are examining him, you notice Dr Grant (specialty dentist) taking a selfie video in the corridor with Mr Petrov visible in the background, joking about “another big abscess” and the busy night. Mr Petrov looks uncomfortable and asks you quietly if staff are allowed to film on the ward. Rachel looks annoyed but says, “Don’t start drama—Dr Grant is stressed.” At the same time, Dr Banerjee (consultant) phones asking you to quickly update the electronic notes before the ward round, and you have two bleeps waiting from A&E.

options.A: Ask Dr Grant to stop recording immediately, remind them that patients must not be identifiable, and request they delete the video; then document and escalate via the appropriate route if needed.
options.B: Ignore the filming as it is not your responsibility, focus on Mr Petrov’s clinical review, and leave any professionalism issues to management.
options.C: Tell Rachel that she must report Dr Grant because nurses are responsible for ward behaviour, and you will not get involved.
options.D: After finishing Mr Petrov’s review, speak privately to Dr Grant about the impact on patient trust and confidentiality, and encourage them to seek support for stress; then inform your supervisor or duty consultant if the video involved identifiable patient information.
options.E: Confront Dr Grant loudly in the corridor so everyone understands filming is unacceptable, even if it embarrasses them.

explanation:
A is most appropriate because it acts immediately to reduce ongoing risk to confidentiality and patient trust. Mr Petrov is potentially identifiable in the background; NHS professional standards require prompt action to prevent or limit breaches. Asking Dr Grant to stop and delete the recording is proportionate, and escalation/documentation may be necessary depending on what was captured.

D is also appropriate and should follow: addressing the issue privately supports a constructive, non-punitive approach while still prioritising patient safety and professionalism. Linking the behaviour to stress and signposting support maintains team functioning, but it does not excuse the breach; informing a supervisor if identifiable information was recorded is consistent with duty of candour and governance.

E is less appropriate: while the message is correct, a public confrontation risks escalating conflict and undermining working relationships, especially on a busy ward.

B is inappropriate because it normalises a potential confidentiality breach and fails to respond to Mr Petrov’s expressed concern. C is least appropriate: it inappropriately shifts responsibility to Rachel and avoids your own professional duty to act when you witness unprofessional behaviour.
learningPoints: Act promptly to prevent or limit confidentiality breaches, including inappropriate filming.
Address concerns in a proportionate way: immediate risk reduction, then private feedback and escalation if needed.
Maintain patient trust by responding to patient concerns about staff behaviour.
You share responsibility for professionalism; do not deflect concerns onto other staff groups.

scenario: You are a DCT in the radiology department supporting an emergency dental clinic. Elena (radiographer) refuses to take an OPG for Mrs Khan, who has trismus and suspected mandibular fracture after a fall, because the electronic request lists “routine dental pain” and lacks pregnancy status. Mrs Khan is distressed and says she just wants to go home. Emily (dental nurse) tells you the referring clinician, Dr Lewis (registrar), is in resus with another patient and cannot amend the request soon. Mark (reception admin) says the radiology list is overrunning and the manager is monitoring breaches. You are concerned about delay to diagnosis versus following imaging governance, and you also need to manage Mrs Khan’s anxiety and maintain a good working relationship with Elena, who feels dental staff “always send incomplete forms.”

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed with the OPG now because the fracture risk outweighs paperwork, and you will “sort the request later.”
options.B: Speak with Mrs Khan to confirm pregnancy status and document it; then contact Dr Lewis by phone/bleep to clarify indication and request an amended urgent imaging request.
options.C: Offer Mrs Khan a brief explanation of why radiology needs specific details, provide reassurance, and check pain control while you coordinate the request update.
options.D: Ask Mark to book Mrs Khan into a routine slot later in the week to avoid breaches on today’s list.
options.E: Escalate to the radiology duty superintendent or on-call radiologist to agree an appropriate pathway (e.g., alternative imaging or authorisation) given the urgent suspected fracture and incomplete request.

explanation:
B, C and E are the most appropriate because they address governance, patient experience, and urgency in parallel. Confirming and documenting pregnancy status and clarifying the clinical indication with Dr Lewis (B) helps make the request safe and compliant without unnecessary delay. Communicating with Mrs Khan (C) reduces distress, supports informed cooperation, and maintains trust while processes are being completed.

Escalating to the radiology duty lead/on-call radiologist (E) is appropriate in a time-critical suspected fracture where the referrer is unavailable and the request is incomplete. This respects Elena’s professional accountability while enabling a senior decision on authorisation or alternative imaging.

A is unsafe and undermines imaging governance; “sorting later” can lead to untraceable exposure and poor documentation. D and G inappropriately delay or deny urgent assessment. F damages interprofessional relationships and is unlikely to resolve the issue. H is dishonest and risks serious professional consequences; you must not submit requests under another clinician’s name.
learningPoints: Balance urgency with imaging governance: ensure adequate clinical details and pregnancy status where relevant.
Use calm, respectful interprofessional communication to resolve cross-department conflict.
Escalate to appropriate senior radiology decision-makers when urgent imaging is blocked by process issues.
Never falsify documentation or submit requests under someone else’s name.

scenario: You are on-call in the hospital emergency dental clinic late evening. Mr Khan presents with swelling, fever and difficulty swallowing. Lucy (dental nurse) notes his oxygen saturation is 93% and says his voice sounds “muffled.” Mr Khan insists he cannot stay because he must pick up his child, and he is angry that he has waited two hours. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is in theatre with a trauma case and has asked you to “keep things moving” and only call if absolutely necessary. The A&E registrar, Dr Fernandes, is busy and has previously criticised dental for “over-referring.” You suspect a deep neck space infection and are concerned about airway risk, but you have limited experience assessing airway compromise.

options.A: Explain to Mr Khan that his symptoms could be life-threatening and request immediate assessment in A&E/resus; call Dr Fernandes to hand over using clear observations from Lucy and your exam.
options.B: Prescribe oral antibiotics and analgesia, advise Mr Khan to return if worse, and document that he declined admission.
options.C: Call Dr Ahmed in theatre to update them on the observations and your concern about airway risk, and ask for explicit direction while you arrange urgent A&E review.
options.D: Attempt incision and drainage in the emergency dental clinic to reduce swelling quickly so Mr Khan can leave.
options.E: Ask Lucy to contact the site manager or emergency team for urgent support if Mr Khan deteriorates while you keep him in the clinic area and monitor observations.

explanation:
A is most appropriate because suspected deep neck space infection with dysphagia, muffled voice and low saturations is an airway-risk scenario requiring urgent medical assessment. A structured handover to Dr Fernandes using Lucy’s observations and your findings supports safe escalation and reduces the chance of interdepartment friction.

C is next: despite Dr Ahmed’s request to minimise calls, this situation meets the threshold for immediate senior escalation. Seeking explicit guidance demonstrates insight into your limits and protects the patient and you professionally.

E is appropriate as a supportive action: keeping Mr Khan under observation with Lucy and involving the site/emergency response pathway if he deteriorates manages immediate risk while transfer is arranged.

B is inappropriate: oral antibiotics and safety-netting are insufficient given red flags; documenting refusal does not remove your duty to act when capacity and risk are in question. D is least appropriate and potentially dangerous; attempting incision and drainage in a setting not equipped for airway compromise exceeds typical DCT competence and could worsen swelling or precipitate airway obstruction.
learningPoints: Recognise red flags for airway compromise and escalate urgently to appropriate emergency pathways.
Hierarchy and service pressure must not delay escalation when patient safety is at risk.
Work within limits of competence; high-risk procedures require senior support and appropriate setting.
Use structured handover and objective observations to support cross-department communication.

scenario: You are a DCT in an NHS oral surgery clinic. Dr Kumar (registrar) is running late after being called to theatre, and the waiting room is full. Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you that Mr Khan, booked for an extraction of 36, looks unwell and mentioned “a new blood thinner” at reception. Nadia (receptionist) adds that his referral letter is missing from the notes, and the electronic record shows “AF” but no medication list. Mr Khan is frustrated about delays and says he has taken “apixaban” this morning and wants the tooth out today because he is travelling. Dr Kumar texts asking you to “crack on” and he will review later. You are confident with routine extractions but have limited experience managing DOACs and there is no recent INR or GP letter.

options.A: Tell Nadia to rebook Mr Khan and ask Sarah to give him a standard post-op leaflet while you continue with the next patient to reduce delays.
options.B: Explain to Mr Khan that you need to clarify his apixaban regimen and BP before extracting; ask Sarah to seat him in a side room, take a focused history, and phone his GP/pharmacy for an urgent medication confirmation.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction using local measures (suturing and haemostatic dressing) because Mr Khan insists, and document that he accepted the bleeding risk.
options.D: Call Dr Kumar to attend immediately and, while waiting, ask Nadia to locate the missing referral and check if Mr Khan has had recent medical updates recorded by the hospital.
options.E: Ask Mr Khan to stop apixaban for 48 hours and return tomorrow morning for extraction, advising him this is standard for all blood thinners.

explanation: The priority is patient safety despite time pressure and Mr Khan’s frustration. Option B is most appropriate: you acknowledge his concerns, move him to a calmer space with Sarah, take a targeted history (dose, last intake, renal issues, indication), and urgently verify medications via GP/pharmacy. His markedly raised BP adds peri‑operative risk and needs assessment before any invasive procedure. Option D is next best: escalating to Dr Kumar is appropriate given your limited experience with DOAC management and the missing referral; asking Nadia to retrieve documentation supports safe decision‑making without abandoning the patient. Option A is less appropriate because immediately rebooking without assessment may miss deterioration and does not address the BP or anticoagulant uncertainty; however, it reduces conflict and could be part of a plan once safety checks are done. Option E is unsafe: advising a blanket 48‑hour cessation is outside competence, risks thromboembolism, and should only occur with appropriate guidance and senior/medical input. Option C is least appropriate: proceeding under pressure with incomplete information and uncontrolled BP breaches clinical safety and consent standards; documenting “accepted risk” does not make an unsafe plan acceptable. NHS professionalism requires recognising limits and seeking supervision appropriately.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides clinic flow and patient pressure when key medical information is missing.
Recognise limits of competence and escalate to a supervisor when anticoagulant management is uncertain.
Valid consent cannot be obtained when risks are not properly assessed or explained using accurate information.
Address anxiety and conflict with calm communication while maintaining safe clinical boundaries.

scenario: You are the DCT on-call in the hospital emergency dental clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with severe pain from a lower molar. She is accompanied by her partner, Mr Clarke, who answers most questions and insists on staying in the room. Emily (dental nurse) notices bruising on Mrs Thompson’s forearm when taking her blood pressure and later whispers that Mrs Thompson avoided eye contact and flinched when Mr Clarke raised his voice at reception. Mrs Thompson asks for “strong antibiotics” because she has an important work meeting tomorrow and says she cannot stay for treatment. Your notes show she attended twice in the last month for similar pain and did not attend follow-up. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. The clinic is busy with several children waiting, and Olivia (receptionist) is asking how long you will keep the room.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to step out briefly, explaining you need to confirm medical history and consent in private, and check with Mrs Thompson if she feels safe to talk.
options.B: Prescribe antibiotics immediately to de-escalate the situation and ask them to leave so you can keep the clinic moving.
options.C: With Emily present, use a sensitive, non-leading question about bruising and safety at home, and offer information about support services regardless of disclosure.
options.D: Tell Mr Clarke you suspect abuse and that you will be contacting the police unless he leaves the building.
options.E: Document the bruising and behaviour, and inform the safeguarding lead/on-call manager for advice on next steps given Dr O’Connor is unavailable.

explanation:
The three best actions balance patient-centred care, safeguarding, and clinic pressures. A is appropriate because valid consent and a safe history require speaking to Mrs Thompson privately; framing it as routine medical/consent checking is professional and reduces confrontation. C is also appropriate: with Emily present you can ask sensitive, non-leading questions and offer support information; this aligns with safeguarding principles without forcing disclosure. E is appropriate because you should document objective findings (bruising, interactions) and seek guidance from the safeguarding lead/on-call manager when Dr O’Connor is unavailable; this supports a proportionate, defensible plan.

B is weak: antibiotics to “de-escalate” may be clinically inappropriate and reinforces unsafe practice; it also misses safeguarding. D and F are disproportionate and may escalate risk; removal or police involvement without immediate threat should be guided by local policy and senior support. G is unethical—care should not be conditional on disclosure. H undermines consent and misses an opportunity to assess safety. Overall, the NHS expects clinicians to prioritise safety, maintain confidentiality, and use safeguarding pathways while managing pressure and difficult interpersonal dynamics.
learningPoints: Consent and history-taking should occur in a setting where the patient can speak freely.
Safeguarding concerns can be explored sensitively without requiring disclosure.
Document objective observations and escalate via local safeguarding pathways when seniors are unavailable.
Avoid inappropriate prescribing as a means of conflict management.

scenario: You are a DCT in a restorative clinic seeing Mrs Singh for a crown preparation review. Dr Chen (consultant) has asked you to finalise the plan and then present it. Maria (hygienist) has just seen Mrs Singh and reports persistent bleeding on probing and poor plaque control; she is concerned a crown will fail without stabilising periodontal health first. Mrs Singh is upset because she has already taken time off work twice and says the tooth looks ‘embarrassing’ and she wants it finished today. Lucy (dental nurse) tells you the clinic is running 40 minutes late and Dr Chen is in a meeting for the next 20 minutes. Mrs Singh’s previous notes are brief and do not clearly record whether periodontal risks were discussed. Maria feels you are being pushed to prioritise aesthetics over health and says, in front of Mrs Singh, “This is a bad idea.

options.A: Ask Maria to step outside with you, acknowledge her concern, and agree a consistent message before returning to explain to Mrs Singh why stabilisation is needed and what can be done today.
options.B: Tell Mrs Singh you will proceed with the crown today to meet her expectations, and ask Maria to book hygiene later.
options.C: Ask Lucy to fetch Dr Chen immediately from the meeting because Maria has disagreed with the plan in front of the patient.
options.D: Apologise to Mrs Singh for the disagreement, explain the periodontal findings, and offer a same-day interim aesthetic solution (e.g., smoothing/temporary) while arranging periodontal therapy before definitive crown.
options.E: Tell Maria in front of Mrs Singh that she should not undermine dentists, and instruct her to continue with the next patient.

explanation:
D is most appropriate: it is patient-centred and clinically safe, addressing Mrs Singh’s aesthetic concern with an interim option while prioritising periodontal stabilisation before a definitive crown. It also helps repair trust after the visible team disagreement. A is next: stepping aside with Maria supports teamwork, de-escalates the interpersonal dynamic, and ensures consistent communication to the patient. C can be appropriate but is lower: involving Dr Chen may be needed if there is uncertainty or conflict, but interrupting a meeting should be proportionate; first attempt to manage the situation and present a clear plan.

B is unsafe because proceeding with a crown despite active periodontal issues risks failure and does not meet standards for evidence-based care; it also suggests care is driven by convenience. E is least appropriate: reprimanding Maria in front of Mrs Singh is unprofessional, damages team functioning, and increases patient anxiety. You should maintain respect across roles and focus on shared decision-making with Mrs Singh, documenting that periodontal risks and options were discussed. NHS practice expects collaborative working and clear, honest communication even under time pressure.
learningPoints: Prioritise long-term oral health and clinical safety over immediate cosmetic demands.
Manage disagreements away from the patient and present a unified, respectful team message.
Offer interim solutions when definitive treatment is not currently appropriate.
Escalate to seniors proportionately when conflict or uncertainty persists.

scenario: You are a DCT in community dental services on a care home visit. Mrs Hassan has moderate dementia and needs an urgent denture adjustment because she is not eating well. Her daughter, Mrs Khan, is present and appears exhausted and irritable. Rebecca (dental therapist) has already assessed and says Mrs Hassan is cooperative at times but becomes distressed if rushed. Mark (care home manager) tells you the home is short-staffed and asks you to “keep it quick” because staff are needed for medication rounds. During the visit, Mrs Khan starts filming on her phone, saying she wants evidence because “the NHS always messes up,” and she asks you to state your full name and GMC number on camera. Mrs Hassan becomes agitated when she sees the phone and tries to push it away. Chloe (dental nurse) looks uncomfortable and whispers that the care assistant is also watching through the open door.

options.A: Ask Mrs Khan to stop filming, explain that recording can affect Mrs Hassan’s comfort and confidentiality, and offer to discuss concerns after the adjustment.
options.B: Allow filming to continue because it may protect you from complaints, and proceed quickly while Chloe holds Mrs Hassan’s hands to stop her pushing the phone.
options.C: Ask Mark to close the door and request privacy, ensuring only essential people remain, and check Mrs Hassan’s assent before continuing the adjustment.
options.D: Provide your full personal details on camera to reassure Mrs Khan and avoid conflict, then continue treatment.
options.E: Pause the procedure, acknowledge Mrs Khan’s anxiety, and offer to involve the service’s complaints/PALS route while you prioritise Mrs Hassan’s best interests and capacity/assent.

explanation:
A, C and E best protect Mrs Hassan’s dignity and support lawful, ethical care. A is appropriate because filming is increasing Mrs Hassan’s distress and raises confidentiality concerns (including other residents/staff). You can set boundaries respectfully and offer a separate conversation. C is appropriate: closing the door and limiting observers reduces distress and protects privacy; checking Mrs Hassan’s assent (and capacity considerations) keeps the focus on her best interests rather than her daughter’s demands. E is appropriate because it acknowledges Mrs Khan’s concerns without capitulating; signposting complaints/PALS provides a constructive route while you maintain professionalism and continue only if it is in Mrs Hassan’s best interests.

B is unsafe and could be construed as restraint; it disregards consent and could escalate distress. D is unnecessary and risks sharing personal data; you can provide professional identification appropriately but not under coercion or in a way that compromises safety. F is disproportionate and risks abandonment. G may be inappropriate delegation if the situation is contentious and consent/privacy issues remain unresolved. H prioritises organisational pressure over patient-centred care. NHS standards emphasise confidentiality, dignity, and proportionate boundary-setting during challenging interactions.
learningPoints: Protect patient dignity and confidentiality, especially in shared environments like care homes.
Recording should not compromise consent, comfort, or privacy; set respectful boundaries.
Focus on the patient’s capacity, assent, and best interests when relatives are distressed or demanding.
Use formal routes (complaints/PALS) to address concerns without escalating conflict.

scenario: You are a DCT in the radiology department during a busy trauma/on-call afternoon. Dr Grant (specialty dentist) asked you to request an OPG for Mr Petrov after a facial injury. Elena (radiographer) reviews your request and says the clinical details are insufficient and the justification does not meet IR(ME)R requirements; she refuses to perform the exposure until it is clarified. Mr Petrov is in pain and angry about waiting, and Rachel (ED nurse) is pushing for rapid imaging so he can be discharged. You feel pressured because Dr Grant is in clinic and previously told you “radiology always delays things.” When you phone Dr Grant, he sounds irritated and says to “just write suspected fracture” and resubmit. Elena quietly adds that she has seen repeated poor requests from your team and is considering escalating to the radiation protection supervisor.

options.A: Amend the request with accurate, specific clinical findings from your assessment (e.g., occlusal change, trismus, step deformity) and discuss with Elena what information is needed to justify the OPG.
options.B: Resubmit the request exactly as Dr Grant suggested, using ‘suspected fracture’ without further detail so imaging can proceed quickly.
options.C: Ask Rachel to give Mr Petrov additional analgesia and explain the delay, while you obtain Dr Grant’s direct clinical rationale or ask him to review Mr Petrov briefly.
options.D: Challenge Elena in front of Rachel and Mr Petrov, stating that radiographers should not refuse requests made by dentists.
options.E: After the immediate issue, email Dr Grant and copy the department lead to report Elena for obstructing patient care.

explanation:
A is most appropriate: under IR(ME)R, imaging must be justified with adequate clinical information. Providing specific findings and collaborating with Elena supports patient safety and professional relationships. C is next: it addresses Mr Petrov’s pain and Rachel’s discharge pressure while ensuring the request is properly supported; asking Dr Grant for rationale or review is appropriate escalation given hierarchy pressure and your limits.

B is less appropriate: while it may speed up flow, it risks inaccurate justification and undermines governance; you should not add vague or potentially misleading details purely to satisfy a process. E is also inappropriate: escalating a complaint against Elena is disproportionate and punitive when she is acting within her professional responsibilities; concerns about team patterns should be handled constructively (e.g., joint learning) rather than blame. D is least appropriate: публичly challenging Elena breaches professionalism, escalates conflict in front of Mr Petrov and Rachel, and may worsen delays. NHS standards emphasise respectful interprofessional working, accurate documentation, and responding to feedback by improving practice rather than bypassing safety checks.
learningPoints: Radiographic exposures require proper justification and accurate clinical details; speed is not a substitute for governance.
Respond to interprofessional challenge collaboratively and respectfully, not defensively.
Manage patient discomfort and system pressure while maintaining safe processes.
Escalate concerns constructively and proportionately, focusing on learning and quality improvement.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you that Mr Khan, booked for an extraction, mentioned a “bad reaction” to local anaesthetic years ago. The referral letter is brief and the medical history on the system is incomplete because the clinic has just switched software. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in another room dealing with a facial swelling and has asked you to “keep things moving” as there are six waiting patients. Mr Khan is anxious and says he cannot stay long because he needs to collect his child from school. Nadia (receptionist) is pressuring for an estimate of delays because the waiting room is getting restless. You are confident with routine extractions but unsure how to interpret the past reaction, and there is no clear documentation of allergy vs vasovagal episode.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using a reduced dose of local anaesthetic, monitor Mr Khan closely, and document that he reported a past reaction but details were unclear.
options.B: Pause the procedure, take a focused history from Mr Khan about the previous reaction (symptoms, timing, treatment), check any GP summary on the spine/record, and update the medical history before deciding on anaesthetic.
options.C: Ask Sarah to phone Mr Khan’s GP for details while you start preparing and consent him for extraction to avoid delaying the clinic.
options.D: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot safely proceed today without clarifying the reaction, offer analgesia and a rebooked appointment, and inform Nadia to reallocate the slot.
options.E: Call Dr Kumar into the room immediately to take over the case, stating you are not comfortable giving any local anaesthetic without senior input.

explanation:
The most appropriate first step is to clarify the risk before administering local anaesthetic. Option B prioritises patient safety by taking a focused history from Mr Khan, checking available records, and correcting the incomplete medical history—consistent with NHS expectations around safe prescribing/administration and accurate documentation. Given the uncertainty (allergy vs vasovagal), involving senior support is appropriate; option E is next best because it recognises limits of competence and seeks timely supervision from Dr Kumar, especially in an emergency clinic where escalation is available. If uncertainty remains after reasonable checks, option D is safer than proceeding: it balances Mr Khan’s time pressure with the duty to avoid preventable harm and communicates appropriately with Nadia to manage flow.

Option C is weaker because consenting and preparing while a GP call is pending risks normalising the plan to proceed and may undermine informed consent if the anaesthetic choice changes. Option A is least appropriate: reducing the dose does not mitigate a true allergy and could create a false sense of safety; proceeding without clarification exposes Mr Khan to avoidable risk and falls short of professional standards. Overall, safety and informed consent outweigh throughput pressures.
learningPoints: Clarify reported drug reactions before administering local anaesthetic; dose reduction is not a safety strategy for true allergy.
Accurate, contemporaneous medical history and documentation are essential during system transitions.
Recognise limits of competence and escalate early when uncertainty affects patient safety.
Manage clinic pressures transparently with the team while prioritising safe care.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Daniel, who acts as her informal carer. Mrs Thompson has mild learning disability and becomes visibly distressed when Daniel answers questions for her. Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you she has seen Daniel read Mrs Thompson’s letters and speak over her at previous appointments. The plan today is to take impressions for a denture, but Mrs Thompson says she does not want “any more moulds” and asks to leave. Daniel insists you continue, saying he has taken time off work and she “always changes her mind.” Dr Chen (consultant) is running late and has asked you to start patients to reduce backlog. The interpreter service is not booked, and you are unsure about Mrs Thompson’s capacity for this decision in the moment.

options.A: Ask Daniel to step into the waiting area for a few minutes so you can speak with Mrs Thompson alone, with Emily present, and explore her wishes and understanding.
options.B: Proceed with impressions because denture provision is in Mrs Thompson’s best interests and Daniel is her carer who knows her preferences.
options.C: Offer Mrs Thompson a short break, acknowledge her distress, and check whether she feels pressured or unsafe at home before continuing with any treatment.
options.D: Document that Mrs Thompson refused impressions and discharge her from the service to avoid repeated missed care and wasted appointments.
options.E: Contact the safeguarding lead for advice the same day if concerns about coercion persist, and inform Dr Chen of the potential safeguarding issue.

explanation:
Options A, C and E best balance autonomy, safety and professional duties. A is appropriate because speaking to Mrs Thompson without Daniel present helps assess her wishes and reduces potential coercion, while keeping Emily as a chaperone supports safety and transparency. C is also appropriate: acknowledging distress, offering a pause, and exploring whether Mrs Thompson feels pressured supports patient-centred care and allows an initial capacity check (understanding, retaining, weighing, communicating) without escalating conflict. If concerns about coercion or neglect remain, E is necessary: seeking advice from the safeguarding lead and informing Dr Chen ensures concerns are acted on promptly and escalated appropriately within NHS safeguarding pathways.

B and F are inappropriate because Daniel cannot consent on behalf of an adult unless he has legal authority (e.g., LPA) and even then consent must reflect the patient’s best interests; proceeding without valid consent breaches professional standards. D is punitive and risks abandoning care. G is confrontational and may worsen dynamics; boundaries should be set calmly but not as a condition of care. H is unsafe and potentially assault: restraining a distressed patient to complete impressions is unacceptable. Overall, clinic efficiency and Dr Chen’s backlog request cannot override valid consent and safeguarding responsibilities.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s own decision-making unless legal authority and best-interests processes apply.
Separate the patient from a potentially coercive relative/carer to assess wishes and capacity.
Safeguarding concerns should be documented, escalated, and discussed with appropriate leads promptly.
Clinic time pressure must not drive coercive or restrictive practices.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, admitted overnight with a mandibular fracture. While checking the notes, you notice Dr Grant (registrar) has written that Mr Petrov “declined smoking cessation advice,” but Mr Petrov tells you no one discussed smoking with him and he wants help because he is worried about healing. At the same time, Lucy (dental nurse) shows you a message in the staff group chat where Dr Grant jokes about Mr Petrov being “a nightmare” and includes a photo of the whiteboard with his surname and bed number visible. Rachel is concerned the family are in the bay and might overhear staff comments. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in theatre and has asked for a concise ward update in 30 minutes.
