options.A: Tell Lucy to delete the message and remind the group chat that patient details should not be shared; then move on to the next task to avoid conflict with Dr Grant.
options.B: Speak to Dr Grant privately, explain the confidentiality risk and inaccurate documentation, ask him to correct the notes and remove the photo, and offer to support him to raise concerns appropriately if he is stressed.
options.C: Report Dr Grant immediately to the consultant and clinical director for misconduct without speaking to him first.
options.D: Apologise to Mr Petrov on behalf of the team, provide smoking cessation advice and referral, and document your discussion accurately in the notes.
options.E: Ask Rachel to confront Dr Grant in the bay because ward nurses should address unprofessional behaviour on the ward.

explanation:
Option D is most appropriate because it directly addresses Mr Petrov’s care need and corrects the clinical record: providing smoking cessation advice/referral supports healing and documenting accurately meets NHS standards for record-keeping and patient-centred care. Option B is next best: the group chat photo with identifiable information is a clear confidentiality breach, and the smoking-cessation entry appears inaccurate. A private, timely conversation with Dr Grant is proportionate, maintains professionalism, and gives him the opportunity to rectify the notes and remove the image while also acknowledging potential stress.

Option A is weaker: asking Lucy to delete the message and issuing a general reminder may reduce immediate risk but does not address the inaccurate documentation or ensure Dr Grant understands the seriousness. Option C may be necessary if Dr Grant refuses to act or there is repeated behaviour, but immediate escalation without attempting local resolution can be disproportionate and may undermine team functioning. Option E is least appropriate: involving Rachel to confront Dr Grant publicly risks conflict in front of patients/family and is unfair; concerns should be handled through appropriate professional channels. Overall, protect confidentiality, correct records, and address behaviour constructively and promptly.
learningPoints: Confidentiality breaches (including photos/identifiers in chats) must be addressed promptly and proportionately.
Accurate documentation is a patient-safety issue and should be corrected when errors are identified.
Raise concerns about colleagues’ behaviour in an appropriate, usually private, manner first unless immediate risk demands escalation.
Provide patient-centred interventions even when team dynamics are challenging.

scenario: You are a DCT in a sedation clinic. Mrs Hassan is scheduled for IV sedation for multiple extractions. On arrival she is tearful and says she had a panic attack on the bus and hasn’t eaten since yesterday. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you the recovery area is short-staffed because a colleague called in sick. Mark (radiographer) phones to say the pre-op OPG is delayed due to a machine fault and may not be available for an hour. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is running two rooms and asks you to “start the list” to avoid cancelling patients. Mrs Hassan’s escort, Mr Clarke, is frustrated about time off work and asks if you can “just give her something to calm her down” and proceed.

options.A: Explain to Mrs Hassan and Mr Clarke that you will not proceed with IV sedation until you have confirmed appropriate pre-op imaging and assessed her readiness; offer to rebook or convert to a non-sedation plan if suitable.
options.B: Ask Chloe to bring Mrs Hassan straight into the chair and cannulate immediately to keep the list moving, then decide later whether to proceed.
options.C: Call Dr Banerjee to review Mrs Hassan promptly, outlining the fasting/anxiety concerns, staffing shortage in recovery, and delayed OPG, and agree a safe plan for the session.
options.D: Ask Mark to prioritise Mrs Hassan’s OPG ahead of other patients because sedation lists are more important than routine imaging.
options.E: Provide a small dose of sedative medication before full assessment to help Mrs Hassan calm down so you can obtain consent more easily.

explanation:
C, A and F are the most appropriate actions because they prioritise safety while managing pressure. C appropriately escalates to Dr Banerjee with a clear, structured summary: fasting/anxiety, recovery staffing shortage (a key safety control), and delayed imaging. Shared decision-making with the consultant supports safe service delivery and recognises limits of competence. A is also appropriate: it sets clear safety boundaries with Mrs Hassan and Mr Clarke, explaining why sedation cannot proceed without adequate assessment and imaging, and offers alternatives (rebook, non-sedation plan) to remain patient-centred. F supports coping with pressure and compassionate care by de-escalating anxiety in a quiet space and reassessing consent; anxiety can impair understanding and voluntariness.

B and E are unsafe: cannulation or giving sedatives before full assessment and valid consent is poor practice and increases risk. D is unprofessional; you can request an estimated time but cannot demand priority by status. G escalates conflict unnecessarily; boundaries should be calm and proportionate. H is unsafe because relying on outdated imaging may miss current pathology and breaches clinical governance. Overall, service pressures must not override sedation safety criteria and informed consent.
learningPoints: Sedation requires robust pre-assessment, valid consent, and adequate recovery staffing; do not proceed under unsafe conditions.
Escalate early with a structured summary when multiple safety factors align under time pressure.
Support anxious patients with de-escalation strategies and reassess capacity/consent.
Manage relatives’ frustration professionally while maintaining safety boundaries.

scenario: During an oral medicine clinic, you see Mrs Singh for recurrent oral ulceration. Maria (hygienist) has already taken a history and notes that Mrs Singh has started methotrexate prescribed by rheumatology. Mrs Singh now shows you a new prescription from her GP for co-trimoxazole for a urinary infection and asks if it is “fine to take with everything.” She is worried because she felt unwell after her last methotrexate dose. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is in an MDT meeting and has asked you to keep the clinic running and only interrupt for urgent issues. Olivia (receptionist) says two patients have arrived early and are complaining about waiting times. You are aware of a potentially serious interaction between methotrexate and co-trimoxazole but you are not the prescriber of either medication.

options.A: Advise Mrs Singh to stop methotrexate immediately and throw away the co-trimoxazole, then write this in the notes so she has clear instructions.
options.B: Phone the on-call pharmacist (Mr Evans) for urgent medicines advice about methotrexate and co-trimoxazole, and document the advice and any safety-netting given to Mrs Singh.
options.C: Tell Mrs Singh to continue both medications as prescribed and to contact her GP if she develops a rash or fever.
options.D: Ask Maria to explain to Mrs Singh that drug interactions are not the dental team’s responsibility and to speak to rheumatology instead.
options.E: Interrupt Dr Ahmed briefly with a concise summary of the interaction concern and your plan to seek pharmacy/GP input, so there is senior oversight for urgent risk.

explanation:
B is most appropriate because it addresses an urgent patient-safety risk within your role: contacting Mr Evans (pharmacist) for immediate advice is a reliable route in hospital settings, and documenting the discussion plus safety-netting supports clinical governance. Given the potential severity of methotrexate–co-trimoxazole interaction, E is also appropriate: interrupting Dr Ahmed briefly with a clear, concise summary ensures senior awareness and shared responsibility, aligning with escalation expectations when significant harm is possible.

C is less appropriate because it downplays risk and gives vague advice; waiting for symptoms could allow serious toxicity to develop. A is inappropriate: instructing Mrs Singh to stop or discard medicines without confirming with pharmacy/medical prescribers exceeds competence and could cause harm (e.g., rheumatology flare or undertreated infection). D is least appropriate: deflecting responsibility undermines patient-centred care. While you are not the prescriber, you do have a duty to act on safety concerns, coordinate with relevant professionals, and ensure Mrs Singh receives clear next steps (e.g., urgent GP/rheumatology review, bloods if advised). Managing Olivia’s waiting-time pressure should not override immediate medication safety.
learningPoints: Act on potential medicines interactions even if you are not the prescriber; escalate and coordinate appropriately.
Use pharmacy and senior clinical support for urgent medicines safety advice and document clearly.
Do not give definitive stop/start instructions outside competence without appropriate confirmation.
Patient safety takes priority over clinic flow pressures; communicate delays appropriately.

scenario: You are a DCT in an NHS oral surgery urgent clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you Mr Khan has been squeezed in for an extraction but his medical history on the electronic record is blank because the system is running slowly. Mr Khan says he is “on blood thinners for my heart” but cannot recall the name. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in theatre and has asked you to “just get the simple ones done” to keep the waiting room moving; three patients are already complaining at reception to Nadia about delays. Mr Khan is in pain and becomes frustrated, saying he will leave if you “mess him about”. Sarah quietly adds she remembers seeing a recent discharge letter mentioning a stent, but she can’t find it.

options.A: Ask Nadia to phone Mr Khan’s GP immediately for a current medication list while you start local anaesthetic to save time, and proceed if no answer within 10 minutes.
options.B: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot safely extract without confirming his anticoagulant/antiplatelet regimen; offer analgesia advice and rebook, and document that he declined to wait.
options.C: Ask Sarah to bring Mr Khan into a side room, take a focused history (indication for “blood thinners”, last dose, stent date), check summary care record/EDL again, and if still unclear, contact the on-call medical team/pharmacy before deciding.
options.D: Proceed with the extraction as planned because it is urgent pain relief, and ask Mr Khan to bring his medication list next time for your notes.
options.E: Call Dr Kumar in theatre to update him and request a brief pause in the list to review Mr Khan’s anticoagulation status with you before any procedure starts.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to prioritise patient safety by clarifying Mr Khan’s anticoagulant/antiplatelet status before any invasive procedure. Option C is best because it uses the team (Sarah) to create privacy, obtains a targeted history, re-checks available records, and escalates to appropriate sources (medical team/pharmacy) when documentation is unreliable—consistent with recognising limits of competence and safe prescribing/bleeding risk management. Option E is next: Dr Kumar has supervisory responsibility and has applied time pressure; updating him and seeking a decision supports safe delegation and shared accountability.

Option B may be necessary if confirmation cannot be obtained; it respects informed consent and avoids unsafe treatment, but should follow reasonable attempts to verify medications and should include offering interim pain management and clear safety-netting. Option A is weaker: starting local anaesthetic before confirming bleeding risk and medical details risks proceeding down an unsafe path and may be perceived as coercive under pressure. Option D is least appropriate because it disregards the uncertainty around “blood thinners,” exposing Mr Khan to avoidable harm and failing NHS standards for safe care and documentation.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides time pressure and throughput targets.
Uncertain anticoagulant/antiplatelet history requires verification before invasive procedures.
Escalate to supervisors when documentation is incomplete or systems fail.
Use the team to obtain accurate histories and maintain patient dignity.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson for multiple extractions under IV sedation next week. Today is the pre-assessment. Emily (dental nurse) notes Mrs Thompson looks confused and keeps asking the same questions. Mrs Thompson’s daughter, Olivia, insists on staying in the room and answers most questions, saying her mother “doesn’t need all the details” because she gets anxious. Mrs Thompson nods but then asks you privately, “Will I be asleep? I don’t want anyone doing things if I don’t understand.” Dr O’Connor (consultant) is running late and has asked you to complete pre-assessments quickly to avoid cancelling the sedation list. The interpreter service is unavailable today, but Mrs Thompson’s first language is English; the issue is fluctuating understanding.

options.A: Ask Olivia to step outside for part of the assessment so you can speak to Mrs Thompson alone, explaining this is routine to check understanding and consent.
options.B: Proceed with consent today using Olivia as the main communicator because she knows her mother best and the sedation list depends on timely paperwork.
options.C: Document concerns about fluctuating capacity and inform Dr O’Connor the consent may need to be deferred or repeated on the day with a capacity assessment.
options.D: Reassure Mrs Thompson that sedation means she won’t remember much, and focus on completing the checklist rather than detailed discussion to reduce anxiety.
options.E: Ask Emily to obtain previous clinic letters and contact Mrs Thompson’s GP for information about any cognitive diagnosis or medications affecting capacity.

explanation:
Valid consent requires that Mrs Thompson can understand, retain, weigh information and communicate a decision. The scenario suggests fluctuating understanding and possible undue influence from Olivia. Option A is appropriate because speaking with Mrs Thompson alone helps assess voluntariness and understanding while maintaining respectful family involvement. Option C is also appropriate: documenting concerns and escalating to Dr O’Connor ensures senior oversight, protects patient safety, and aligns with NHS standards on consent and accountability. Option G supports patient-centred care by adapting the process to Mrs Thompson’s needs (simple explanations, staged discussions) while planning timely senior review rather than forcing completion under pressure.

Option E may be helpful but is not a substitute for direct assessment of capacity and could delay without addressing immediate consent concerns. Options B and D are unsafe and unethical: relying on Olivia or minimising discussion risks invalid consent and breaches professional integrity. Option F misunderstands best-interests decision-making; family should usually be involved and conflict should be managed, not bypassed. Option H is disproportionate and fails to provide reasonable adjustments for a vulnerable patient.
learningPoints: Consent must be informed, voluntary and given by a patient with capacity for that decision.
Family input can support care but must not replace the patient’s own voice.
Escalate and document when capacity is uncertain, especially before sedation.
Adapt communication and appointments to support understanding under service pressure.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, admitted with facial cellulitis. While checking his notes, you notice a progress entry from yesterday by Dr Grant (registrar) stating “patient non-compliant, likely drug-seeking,” but there is no supporting detail. Mr Petrov appears distressed and asks why staff “treat me like a criminal.” He says he has a history of opioid dependence but is trying to stay stable; he is requesting analgesia and is worried about withdrawal. Angela (another nurse) tells you quietly that Dr Grant was irritated after a busy on-call and “wrote it in frustration.” Meanwhile, Dr Chen (consultant) is in clinic and expects a clear plan before the afternoon ward round.

options.A: Ignore the comment to avoid conflict with Dr Grant and focus only on prescribing analgesia for cellulitis pain.
options.B: Discuss Mr Petrov’s pain and substance history with Rachel and Angela, agree an objective analgesia plan (including non-opioid options and escalation criteria), and document your assessment using neutral language.
options.C: Confront Dr Grant immediately in the corridor and tell him his note is unacceptable, copying in Dr Chen by email.
options.D: Add an addendum in the notes stating that the “drug-seeking” comment was inappropriate and likely written in frustration, without speaking to Dr Grant first.
options.E: Speak to Dr Grant privately when feasible to share the impact of the wording, ask for clarification or amendment if needed, and escalate to Dr Chen if there is a pattern or patient safety concern.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it addresses Mr Petrov’s immediate needs with a patient-centred, clinically safe plan and documents an objective assessment. Using neutral language reduces stigma and supports continuity of care, while working with Rachel and Angela strengthens teamwork. Option E is next: raising concerns with Dr Grant privately is professional, allows learning and correction, and maintains a just culture. Escalation to Dr Chen is appropriate if the issue persists or affects safety.

Option D is less appropriate because publicly attributing motive (“written in frustration”) without discussion risks unfairly criticising a colleague in the record and may inflame conflict; however, correcting the record’s tone may sometimes be necessary. Option A is weak: leaving stigmatising documentation unchallenged can harm patient trust and future care. Option C is least appropriate: confronting Dr Grant in a corridor and immediately copying in Dr Chen is likely to be perceived as punitive, may damage working relationships, and does not prioritise Mr Petrov’s care.

Overall, NHS professionalism requires respectful challenge, accurate records, and compassionate care for patients with substance use histories.
learningPoints: Use objective, non-stigmatising documentation to support safe, compassionate care.
Address immediate patient needs while managing team dynamics.
Challenge concerns with colleagues privately first and escalate proportionately.
Good records should describe behaviour and clinical findings, not labels or assumptions.

scenario: In the hospital radiology department, you attend with Mrs Singh for an urgent OPG requested by the emergency dental clinic to assess a suspected mandibular fracture after a fall. Elena (radiographer) refuses to proceed because the request form lists Mrs Singh as pregnant, but Mrs Singh insists she is not and becomes upset, saying the receptionist Mark “clicked the wrong box” when booking. Lucy (dental nurse) is worried because the ED clinic is holding a slot for Mrs Singh to be reviewed by Dr Ahmed (consultant) within an hour, and delaying the imaging may mean she waits overnight. Elena is also concerned about audit compliance and says she cannot amend the request without confirmation. Mrs Singh’s partner is angry and starts raising his voice at staff.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed with the OPG immediately because the clinical need is urgent, and you will correct the pregnancy status later in the notes.
options.B: Step aside with Mrs Singh to clarify her pregnancy status sensitively (LMP, possibility of pregnancy) and explain why radiology must check, while Lucy supports the partner in the waiting area.
options.C: Contact Mark or the ED clinic to confirm and correct the referral details on the system, and request Elena’s guidance on the department’s process for urgent amendments.
options.D: Ask Mrs Singh’s partner to leave the department immediately and warn security you will call them if he speaks again.
options.E: Offer Mrs Singh a pregnancy test on the spot in radiology and insist she completes it before any further discussion.

explanation:
Option B is appropriate because it addresses patient-centred communication and de-escalation: clarifying pregnancy status with Mrs Singh respectfully supports informed decision-making and radiation safety, while using Lucy to manage the partner reduces immediate conflict. Option C is also appropriate: the likely system error from Mark must be corrected through the proper channel, and involving Elena in the process shows interdepartmental respect and improves efficiency. Option F is appropriate escalation when urgent imaging is needed but governance prevents action; senior radiology input can authorise an approach (e.g., amended request, alternative imaging) with clear documentation.

Option A is unsafe and breaches radiation protection processes. Option D may be necessary if there is immediate threat, but jumping to exclusion/security without attempting de-escalation risks worsening the situation. Option E is not proportionate; pregnancy testing requires consent and may not be available or appropriate. Option G undermines colleagues and damages trust. Option H abandons the situation and delays care, failing teamwork and patient safety.

NHS standards support timely diagnosis, accurate records, respectful cross-team communication, and proportionate escalation.
learningPoints: Radiation safety checks must be completed and documented, even under time pressure.
Use calm, sensitive questioning and team roles to de-escalate conflict.
Correct referral errors through agreed pathways and involve relevant departments.
Escalate to senior decision-makers when governance blocks urgent care.

scenario: You are a DCT in a prison dental service. Mr Ibrahim attends with severe toothache and asks for extraction today. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you the prison officer is insisting on staying in the surgery “for security,” and the officer has been making jokes about Mr Ibrahim’s “bad choices.” Mr Ibrahim becomes guarded and quietly says he doesn’t want the officer to hear his medical history because it includes HIV and past self-harm. The medical summary provided by prison healthcare is incomplete, and the officer is pressuring you to “just get on with it” because staffing is short and escorts are limited. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is off-site but available by phone.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction with the officer present to avoid escalating security issues, and take the medical history in a raised voice so everyone can hear clearly.
options.B: Explain to the officer that you need privacy for confidential history-taking, propose the officer stands just outside with the door ajar or within sightline per local policy, and ask Chloe to confirm the agreed security protocol.
options.C: Telephone Dr Banerjee for advice and ask him to speak directly to the officer to reinforce your request for privacy.
options.D: Ask Mr Ibrahim to write down his medical history on paper so you can avoid speaking about it, then file it in the clinical notes.
options.E: If privacy cannot be achieved and the medical history remains incomplete, provide interim pain management advice, arrange urgent follow-up when appropriate escorting can be provided, and document the reasons and discussions.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it balances confidentiality with prison security by seeking a practical adjustment consistent with local policy: the officer can maintain safety without overhearing sensitive information. Involving Chloe to confirm protocol supports teamwork and ensures you are not making ad hoc security decisions. Option E is next: if you cannot obtain a confidential, adequate medical history and the summary is incomplete, proceeding with extraction could be unsafe. Providing interim pain measures, arranging timely review, and documenting the constraints prioritises patient safety and professional integrity.

Option C can be appropriate after you have attempted to resolve the issue yourself; senior support from Dr Banerjee may help manage hierarchy and ensure policy-compliant practice, but it should not be the first step for a routine confidentiality request. Option D is weaker: written disclosure may help, but it still risks confidentiality breaches in a prison environment and does not replace a proper, clarified history with opportunity for questions. Option A is least appropriate: it disregards confidentiality and dignity, reinforces the officer’s unprofessional behaviour, and risks undermining Mr Ibrahim’s trust and consent.

NHS standards require respecting confidentiality, obtaining informed consent, and not allowing external pressure to compromise safe care.
learningPoints: Confidentiality applies in secure settings; seek policy-based solutions to maintain privacy.
Do not proceed with invasive treatment without an adequate medical history and valid consent.
Use the team and proportionate escalation to manage security and professionalism issues.
Document constraints, decisions and safety-netting when care is delayed.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Khan is booked for an extraction and tells you he is “allergic to penicillin” but can’t recall the reaction. Sarah (dental nurse) shows you the triage sheet that lists “no known drug allergies”. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is running late and asks you to “just crack on” because the waiting room is full. Mr Khan looks anxious and says he needs to leave within 30 minutes for work. You notice that last month he attended OMFS and was prescribed amoxicillin, but the discharge summary is scanned poorly and hard to read. Sarah quietly says she’s uncomfortable preparing local anaesthetic until the allergy status is clear. Meanwhile, Nadia (receptionist) says Mrs Thompson has arrived with facial swelling and is demanding to be seen immediately.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using standard local anaesthetic and defer clarifying the allergy until afterwards, as Mr Khan is time-limited and Dr Kumar asked you to continue.
options.B: Pause treatment, take a focused allergy history from Mr Khan (including reaction, timing, and any subsequent penicillin exposure), and ask Sarah to help contact OMFS records or the GP to verify the recent amoxicillin prescription before proceeding.
options.C: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan for another day when Dr Kumar is available, and move straight to Mrs Thompson because she is causing disruption in reception.
options.D: Ask Sarah to prepare the local anaesthetic while you briefly step out to find Dr Kumar for a quick verbal plan, leaving Mr Khan in the chair.
options.E: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot proceed safely until the allergy discrepancy is resolved, offer analgesia/advice if needed, and escalate Mrs Thompson’s swelling for urgent clinical triage while you seek senior input if verification cannot be obtained quickly.

explanation: The most appropriate approach prioritises safety while managing competing urgent demand. Option E is best: you are transparent with Mr Khan about the discrepancy, avoid proceeding with incomplete information, and simultaneously ensure Mrs Thompson’s facial swelling is triaged promptly (potential airway/sepsis risk). It also appropriately escalates if rapid verification is not possible. Option B is also strong because it uses a structured allergy history and attempts to verify the record, responding to Sarah’s concern and supporting safe consent and prescribing decisions; however, it does not explicitly address the immediate competing priority of Mrs Thompson’s swelling. Option D is less appropriate: seeking Dr Kumar’s guidance is sensible, but leaving Mr Khan unattended and asking Sarah to prepare LA before resolving the concern risks pressuring the nurse and undermining teamworking. Option C addresses flow but is disproportionate and may delay necessary care for Mr Khan without attempting resolution; it also rewards disruptive behaviour rather than using clinical triage. Option A is least appropriate: proceeding despite an unresolved allergy discrepancy and a staff member’s safety concern breaches patient safety standards and professional integrity, and could lead to harm and complaints.
learningPoints: Take and document a structured drug allergy history and reconcile discrepancies in records.
Patient safety and team concerns should override time pressure and senior impatience.
Urgent presentations (e.g., swelling) should be prioritised by clinical triage, not by who is most demanding.
Be transparent with patients about delays when safety information is missing and escalate appropriately.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, Mrs Hassan attends with her adult son, who answers most questions for her. Emily (dental nurse) mentions Mrs Hassan seems to understand some English but keeps glancing at her son before speaking. Mrs Hassan needs multiple restorations and asks for “something to calm me,” saying her son can sign whatever is needed. Dr Chen (consultant) is in another building and only available by phone. The appointment is already running 20 minutes late and Olivia (receptionist) says transport will leave in 15 minutes. During examination, Mrs Hassan quietly tells you she doesn’t want her son to know she takes medication for depression. Her son becomes irritated and says, “Just do it quickly—she’s fine.

options.A: Ask the son to step into the waiting area for a few minutes so you can assess Mrs Hassan’s understanding and wishes in private, using Emily to support communication and checking if an interpreter is needed.
options.B: Proceed with treatment and accept the son’s signature on the consent form because Mrs Hassan appears agreeable and transport time is short.
options.C: Phone Dr Chen for advice on consent and confidentiality, and document the discussion and agreed plan in the notes before proceeding or rearranging.
options.D: Tell the son that because he is being difficult you will refuse to treat Mrs Hassan today and discharge her back to the referrer.
options.E: Ask Olivia to delay the transport without explanation so you can complete the restorations today.

explanation:
A, C and G best uphold NHS standards on consent, confidentiality and patient-centred care. A is essential because Mrs Hassan may be experiencing undue influence; assessing her understanding and wishes privately supports valid consent and respects her autonomy, while Emily can help ensure communication is appropriate and an interpreter considered. C is appropriate because Dr Chen is available by phone; seeking senior advice in a complex consent/confidentiality situation demonstrates recognising limits and creates a defensible, well-documented plan. G balances pressures ethically: it offers a safe, proportionate plan within time constraints and arranges proper support for anxiety management rather than rushing into sedation or extensive treatment without robust consent.

B and H are unsafe: a relative cannot sign consent for a competent adult, and starting preparation before consent is confirmed risks coercion. F is incorrect: confidentiality should be maintained unless there is a lawful basis to disclose; being a carer does not automatically entitle the son to medication details. E inappropriately manipulates transport arrangements and ignores the need to communicate honestly. D is punitive and not patient-centred; difficult behaviour should be managed professionally without disadvantaging Mrs Hassan.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s own understanding and voluntariness; relatives cannot consent for competent adults.
Confidentiality should be maintained unless there is a clear legal/clinical justification to disclose.
In complex consent situations, seek senior input and document reasoning and discussions.
Use proportionate, safe interim plans when service pressures threaten consent quality.

scenario: On an OMFS ward, you are reviewing Mr Petrov post-operatively. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you she is worried because Dr Grant (registrar) asked her to administer an extra dose of IV opioid “off the record” to keep the patient calm before a consultant ward round. Mr Petrov is drowsy, with a respiratory rate of 10, and his observation chart shows borderline oxygen saturations overnight. Dr Grant is in a hurry and says, “Don’t make a fuss—write it up later.” Mr Petrov’s daughter calls your phone repeatedly, demanding details of his surgery and pain medication changes, saying Dr Grant promised to update her. At the same time, Dr O’Connor (consultant) is due on the ward in 10 minutes and the team is behind schedule.

options.A: Ask Rachel to give the extra opioid dose now as Dr Grant requested, then write the prescription retrospectively to avoid delaying the ward round.
options.B: Tell Rachel not to administer any unprescribed medication, assess Mr Petrov’s sedation and observations urgently, and escalate to Dr O’Connor or the on-call anaesthetics team if respiratory depression is suspected.
options.C: Call Mr Petrov’s daughter back immediately and provide full details of the opioid plan to reduce complaints, since she is next of kin.
options.D: Challenge Dr Grant calmly that retrospective prescribing and ‘off the record’ doses are unsafe, and document your concerns and actions in the notes and incident reporting system if needed.
options.E: Ignore Rachel’s concern because Dr Grant is the registrar, and focus on preparing the patient for the consultant ward round.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because Mr Petrov shows possible opioid-related respiratory depression (drowsiness, RR 10, borderline saturations). Preventing administration of unprescribed medication and urgently assessing/escalating protects patient safety and aligns with medicines governance. Option D is also highly appropriate: you should address Dr Grant’s request professionally, make clear that retrospective prescribing is unacceptable, and document actions; if the behaviour persists or poses risk, incident reporting may be necessary to protect patients and staff such as Rachel.

Option C is less appropriate: while communication with relatives matters, confidentiality and consent apply. Unless Mr Petrov has agreed to share details (and he is currently drowsy), you should provide only appropriate updates via agreed channels, not “full details,” and prioritise immediate clinical risk first. Option E is poor because it dismisses a nurse’s safety concern and reinforces unsafe hierarchy. Option A is least appropriate: instructing Rachel to administer an unprescribed opioid and then prescribing retrospectively is unsafe, breaches professional standards, and exposes Mr Petrov to harm and staff to disciplinary consequences.
learningPoints: Never administer or prescribe medication ‘off the record’; retrospective prescribing is unsafe and unprofessional.
Escalate promptly when observations suggest clinical deterioration or opioid toxicity.
Respond to concerns from any team member; hierarchy does not override safety.
Maintain confidentiality when speaking with relatives and check patient consent/capacity.

scenario: In the radiology department, you accompany Tyler (10) from the paediatric dental GA list for a pre-operative OPG. His mother is upset because the GA list is tomorrow and says no one explained why an OPG is needed. Mark (radiographer) refuses to take the image because the request form lacks a clear clinical justification and Tyler is anxious and wriggling. Lucy (dental nurse) whispers that the consultant, Dr Banerjee, told the team to “just get it done” and is in theatre prep. The waiting area is crowded and Elena (radiographer) says they can fit Tyler in only if it’s quick. Tyler starts crying and says he won’t stand still; his mother asks you to “hold him down if you have to.

options.A: Add a clear written justification to the request (e.g., assessment of unerupted teeth/extent of pathology) after checking the notes, and discuss with Mark whether a different view or timing is more appropriate given Tyler’s distress.
options.B: Ask Lucy to physically restrain Tyler so Mark can take the OPG quickly, because the GA list depends on it.
options.C: Telephone Dr Banerjee to confirm the indication and agree a plan, and document the advice given before proceeding or deferring imaging.
options.D: Tell Tyler’s mother that refusal will lead to cancellation of the GA and that she must make him cooperate.
options.E: Proceed with the OPG without further discussion because Mark is being obstructive and the radiation dose is low.

explanation:
A, C and F best balance governance with patient-centred care. A addresses Mark’s legitimate concern: radiographs require a documented clinical justification under IR(ME)R principles, and checking notes ensures the request is appropriate; it also explores alternatives if Tyler cannot tolerate an OPG. C appropriately involves Dr Banerjee, who set the pathway and can confirm indication or accept deferral; documenting advice supports accountability and teamwork across departments. F is crucial: Tyler’s distress and his mother’s request to restrain him raise ethical concerns. Explaining the purpose and process, seeking parental consent and Tyler’s assent, and being prepared to stop promotes trust and reduces trauma.

B and D are inappropriate and risk harm and complaints; coercion or restraint for convenience is rarely justified and must follow strict policy. E undermines radiology governance and damages interdepartmental working. G is deceptive and risks theatre delays if the GA team is not updated. H abdicates responsibility and breaks communication, potentially jeopardising tomorrow’s surgery.
learningPoints: Radiographs require clear clinical justification and appropriate documentation (IR(ME)R principles).
Manage child anxiety with explanation and assent; avoid coercion and follow restraint policies.
Work collaboratively with radiology and escalate to the responsible consultant when pathways are at risk.
Keep relevant teams informed promptly when delays may affect theatre/GA lists.

scenario: During an evening on-call trauma shift, you are the DCT covering OMFS. Mr Clarke attends A&E with a lip laceration and a suspected mandibular fracture after an assault. Angela (A&E nurse) says he is intoxicated and wants to leave. Dr Lewis (registrar) is scrubbed in theatre and asks you over the phone to “suture the lip and organise the CT” because the department is overwhelmed. Chloe (dental nurse) prepares the suture kit but tells you she has never assisted with facial trauma before. Mr Clarke becomes verbally aggressive when you mention imaging, and Mark (A&E receptionist) says police are waiting to take a statement. You find the previous notes are incomplete and there is no documented tetanus status.

options.A: Attempt the lip repair immediately without further assessment because Dr Lewis instructed you and A&E is busy, and document details later when you have time.
options.B: Assess Mr Clarke’s capacity given intoxication, address immediate risks (bleeding, airway, pain), and ask Angela to support de-escalation and security if needed before any procedure.
options.C: Tell Dr Lewis you are not confident with facial trauma suturing and request he reviews when free; in the meantime, provide temporising measures and arrange CT as appropriate.
options.D: Ask Chloe to proceed with setting up and assisting as best she can, and continue with suturing to avoid delays, even if she is inexperienced.
options.E: Prioritise contacting the GP for tetanus history before doing anything else, as documentation is missing.

explanation:
B is most appropriate because safety and consent come first. Mr Clarke’s intoxication and aggression mean you must assess capacity for consent, manage immediate clinical risks, and ensure staff safety; involving Angela and, if required, security is appropriate. C follows closely: recognising limits of competence is essential in trauma. Informing Dr Lewis that you need senior review for facial suturing is responsible, while still progressing care with temporising measures and arranging CT based on clinical need.

E is reasonable but not urgent above immediate assessment; tetanus status matters, but you can often manage this through hospital protocols and vaccination once stabilised rather than delaying essential assessment. D is less appropriate: Chloe has stated she is inexperienced, and pressuring her to assist beyond competence risks errors and undermines teamwork; you should allocate tasks safely and provide guidance. A is least appropriate: performing a facial repair without proper assessment, consent/capacity consideration, documentation, and adequate support risks poor outcomes and medico-legal issues, especially in trauma with suspected fracture.
learningPoints: Assess capacity and obtain valid consent, especially where intoxication may impair decision-making.
Recognise and declare limits of competence; escalate appropriately while providing safe temporising care.
Maintain staff safety and use de-escalation/support when patients are aggressive.
Address documentation gaps (e.g., tetanus status) without delaying urgent clinical assessment.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you Mr Khan has arrived with severe facial swelling and trismus. The triage note says “penicillin allergy,” but the previous discharge letter in the record lists “no known drug allergies.” Mr Khan is in pain and insists he “just needs antibiotics” because he has an important job interview tomorrow. Nadia (receptionist) warns the waiting room is full and two patients are already complaining about delays. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and not immediately available. Sarah looks worried and says Mr Khan’s voice sounds “muffled.” You are concerned about airway risk and the conflicting allergy documentation. Mr Khan becomes frustrated when you ask more questions and says he will leave if you “keep wasting time.

options.A: Ask Nadia to book Mr Khan into the next available routine slot later today, and provide self-care advice so you can keep the clinic running on time.
options.B: Take Mr Khan to a clinical room immediately with Sarah, perform an ABCDE-style assessment focusing on airway and red flags, and document the allergy history directly with him before deciding on any prescription.
options.C: Prescribe amoxicillin based on the discharge letter stating no allergies, and ask Sarah to monitor Mr Khan in the waiting area while you see the next patient.
options.D: Tell Mr Khan you cannot help without Dr Kumar, and ask him to wait until the registrar returns from theatre even if the swelling worsens.
options.E: Call the on-call OMFS team/registrar via switchboard for urgent advice and potential escalation while continuing your assessment and arranging observations, explaining to Nadia that a high-risk patient must be prioritised.

explanation: The priority is patient safety. Mr Khan’s severe swelling, trismus and “muffled” voice are potential airway red flags; delaying for clinic flow or routine booking is unsafe. Option B is most appropriate because you immediately move him to a clinical area, assess systematically (ABCDE), clarify allergy history directly with the patient, and document contemporaneously—addressing both clinical deterioration risk and the conflicting record. Option E follows closely: early escalation to the on-call OMFS/registrar via switchboard is justified when airway compromise is possible and Dr Kumar is unavailable; it also shows appropriate recognition of limits of competence while maintaining momentum with assessment and observations. Option D is less appropriate because it passively waits for Dr Kumar rather than escalating; however, it at least avoids unsafe prescribing. Option A inappropriately prioritises waiting-room pressure over a potentially life-threatening presentation. Option C is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin with unresolved allergy discrepancy risks harm, and leaving Mr Khan in the waiting area with possible airway compromise breaches standards on safe prescribing, monitoring, and urgent care escalation.
learningPoints: Potential airway compromise requires immediate assessment and escalation, regardless of clinic pressure.
Conflicting allergy documentation must be clarified directly with the patient and recorded clearly.
Recognise limits of competence and seek senior/OMFS support early for high-risk infections.
Clinic flow considerations must not override urgent patient safety priorities.

scenario: In a community special care dentistry clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has moderate learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Clarke. Maria (dental therapist) has completed an oral hygiene review and notes Mrs Thompson keeps looking to her brother before answering. Mrs Thompson needs an extraction for a painful lower molar; she nods when you mention “taking the tooth out,” but cannot explain risks back to you. Mr Clarke answers most questions and says, “Just do it—she hates dentists and won’t come back.” Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you Mrs Thompson seemed more engaged when Mr Clarke stepped out to take a phone call. The clinic is running late and Dr Chen (consultant) is in another surgery. Mrs Thompson becomes visibly anxious when you mention local anaesthetic and starts to cry, while Mr Clarke becomes irritated and says he will complain if you “mess him around.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to step into the waiting area briefly so you can speak to Mrs Thompson alone, using simple language and visual aids to assess her understanding and preferences.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction because Mrs Thompson nodded and Mr Clarke appears to be acting in her best interests, documenting that the family consented.
options.C: Offer immediate temporary measures for pain (e.g., dressing/analgesia advice) and arrange a prompt review with Dr Chen to support a best-interests decision if capacity is lacking.
options.D: Ask Maria to obtain written consent from Mr Clarke while you prepare the instruments, to avoid further delay to the clinic.
options.E: Contact the learning disability liaison/advocate service (if available) and explore whether Mrs Thompson has any existing care plan, power of attorney, or communication passport before proceeding.

explanation: Valid consent requires that Mrs Thompson understands, retains, weighs and communicates a decision. Her nodding, inability to explain risks, and reliance on Mr Clarke suggest capacity may be in question and that her autonomy could be overshadowed. Option A is appropriate: speaking to Mrs Thompson alone (with support from Emily) helps reduce undue influence and allows a tailored capacity assessment using accessible communication. Option E is also appropriate because involving a learning disability liaison/advocate and checking for a communication passport or legal authority supports person-centred care and lawful decision-making; Mr Clarke cannot automatically consent unless he has relevant legal power. Option C is appropriate to balance pain relief with safety: temporary measures and prompt senior input from Dr Chen support best-interests processes if capacity is lacking, avoiding rushed irreversible treatment. Options B, D and H undermine consent and are unsafe/illegal: “family consent” and minimising risk discussion breach professional standards. Option F may escalate conflict and could distress Mrs Thompson; it may be needed only if there is immediate risk. Option G abandons care and fails to make reasonable adjustments for disability.
learningPoints: Assess capacity and consent using accessible communication and without undue influence where possible.
Relatives cannot consent for an adult unless they have appropriate legal authority; follow best-interests processes if capacity is lacking.
Make reasonable adjustments and use advocacy/liaison services to support patient autonomy.
Balance timely pain relief with lawful, patient-centred decision-making and appropriate senior support.

scenario: You are on an OMFS ward covering evening reviews. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to check Mr Petrov, who had an incision and drainage earlier and is due for discharge tomorrow. While reviewing the notes, you notice Dr Lewis (registrar) has documented a postoperative review that you know did not happen—Dr Lewis was in clinic at that time and told you he “would sign it later.” Mr Petrov’s partner, Mrs Hassan, is at the bedside and asks whether he has been reviewed by “the surgeon,” saying she is worried because he looks more swollen. Rachel is also concerned that the observation chart shows a rising temperature, but the drug chart includes antibiotics that may not match the microbiology advice. The ward is short-staffed, and Dr Ahmed (consultant) is not on site.

options.A: Tell Mrs Hassan that Dr Lewis reviewed Mr Petrov and everything is fine, to reassure her and avoid causing conflict about the notes.
options.B: Assess Mr Petrov yourself, check observations, wounds and airway risk, and clarify the antibiotic plan with Rachel and the drug chart, documenting your findings and actions clearly.
options.C: Immediately email the clinical director about Dr Lewis falsifying notes, copying in the whole team, before you assess Mr Petrov.
options.D: Contact Dr Lewis directly to clarify the documentation and the antibiotic plan, and if the entry is inaccurate ask him to correct it and inform the consultant/on-call senior as per local policy.
options.E: Avoid documenting anything further until the morning ward round so you do not contradict Dr Lewis’s entry.

explanation: Option B is most appropriate because patient safety is immediate: Mr Petrov may be deteriorating (rising temperature, swelling) and antibiotics may be incorrect. A timely clinical review, checking observations and documenting accurately supports safe care and continuity. Option D is next: addressing the documentation concern promptly and proportionately with Dr Lewis allows correction of the record and clarification of the plan; if concerns persist, escalation via the on-call senior/consultant pathway aligns with professional integrity and duty of candour while being fair to a colleague. Option C may ultimately be necessary if there is deliberate falsification or repeated behaviour, but emailing the clinical director immediately—before ensuring Mr Petrov is safe and without following local escalation routes—can be disproportionate and risks undermining teamwork. Option E is inappropriate because withholding documentation compromises patient care and governance. Option A is least appropriate: misleading Mrs Hassan breaches honesty and candour, and could delay recognition of deterioration. You can communicate truthfully (e.g., “I’m reviewing him now and will update you”) without speculating or blaming Dr Lewis at the bedside.
learningPoints: Prioritise immediate clinical safety and accurate contemporaneous documentation.
Address concerns about colleagues’ records proportionately and follow local escalation/governance pathways.
Be honest with patients/relatives; reassure without misleading or blaming colleagues.
Unsafe or inaccurate documentation is a patient safety and professionalism issue that must be managed.

scenario: You are in the radiology department with Lucy (dental nurse) for a CBCT request for a trauma patient, Daniel (10), referred from the emergency department. Mark (radiographer) says the request form is incomplete and refuses to scan because the justification section is vague and the referrer signature is missing. Daniel’s mother, Mrs Khan, is upset and says no one is explaining what is happening; she is also worried about radiation exposure and asks whether the scan is “really necessary.” Dr O’Connor (consultant) is expecting the imaging before the afternoon trauma clinic, but you cannot reach him immediately as he is in an MDT meeting. Meanwhile, the ED team is asking for Daniel back to free up a cubicle. Lucy tells you Daniel is becoming distressed and may not cooperate much longer.

options.A: Ask Mark to proceed with the CBCT as a favour because the consultant needs it urgently, and promise you will fix the paperwork afterwards.
options.B: Complete the request form properly with clear clinical justification, confirm the referrer details according to local policy, and if needed contact the on-call senior to authorise the request before re-presenting it to Mark.
options.C: Explain to Mrs Khan, in age-appropriate terms for Daniel, why CBCT is being considered, discuss radiation and alternatives, and check her understanding and questions before proceeding.
options.D: Tell the ED team you will keep Daniel in radiology until the scan is done, regardless of their capacity issues, because imaging is more important.
options.E: Ask Lucy to take Daniel and Mrs Khan back to ED to wait, and abandon the scan request because radiology are being obstructive.

explanation: Option B is appropriate because Mark’s concern is legitimate: ionising radiation requires clear justification and correct authorisation. Completing the form with specific trauma indications and confirming referrer details (and contacting an on-call senior if required) supports governance and avoids pressuring radiology to breach IR(ME)R processes. Option C is also appropriate: Mrs Khan’s anxiety about radiation and Daniel’s distress require clear, compassionate explanation and checking understanding; this supports informed consent/assent and patient-centred care while helping cooperation. Option F is appropriate if, after you have met documentation and justification requirements, there is still an impasse—escalating professionally to the superintendent/duty manager resolves system barriers without conflict. Option A undermines safety and professional relationships. Option D disregards ED pressures and may not be necessary; you should coordinate rather than dictate. Option E abandons clinically indicated imaging. Option G is unsafe and outside scope/authorisation. Option H misunderstands consent: parental signature cannot override justification requirements and would be coercive.
learningPoints: Ionising radiation requests must be properly justified and authorised; do not pressure staff to bypass governance.
Explain imaging benefits/risks and check understanding to support informed consent and reduce distress.
Use professional escalation routes for interdepartmental disagreements.
Coordinate with other departments to balance patient flow without compromising safety.

scenario: You are a DCT2 on a paediatric GA list in oral surgery theatre. Chloe (theatre nurse) tells you the list is overbooked after a cancellation was filled at short notice. Sophie (6) is first on the list; her father, Mr Ibrahim, is anxious and says Sophie has had a cough for two days but “seems fine today.” The pre-op assessment form is incomplete because the paper notes from the pre-assessment clinic are missing. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is scrubbed in the adjacent theatre and asks you to “get things moving” because the anaesthetist is waiting. The anaesthetist, Angela, asks whether Sophie has any recent URTI symptoms and whether consent has been rechecked. Mr Ibrahim becomes defensive and says if you cancel, he will “go to PALS” because he took time off work. Chloe quietly tells you she overheard Mr Ibrahim mention Sophie used an inhaler last night.

options.A: Tell Angela there are no concerns and proceed, as delaying will disrupt the list and Dr Banerjee wants to start on time.
options.B: Pause the process, speak with Mr Ibrahim to clarify Sophie’s symptoms (including inhaler use), check available electronic records, and update Angela and Dr Banerjee with the new information before a decision is made.
options.C: Ask Chloe to locate the missing pre-assessment notes while you continue preparing Sophie for theatre to avoid wasting time.
options.D: Explain to Mr Ibrahim that safety checks are mandatory, and that the anaesthetist and consultant will decide whether to proceed; offer to involve the senior nurse and provide a clear plan if surgery is postponed.
options.E: Proceed with induction but plan to “mention the cough” to Dr Banerjee afterwards, once Sophie is asleep.

explanation: Potential URTI symptoms and recent inhaler use in a 6-year-old raise anaesthetic risk; incomplete pre-assessment documentation adds uncertainty. Option B is most appropriate: you pause and actively clarify the history with Mr Ibrahim, check electronic records, and promptly inform Angela (anaesthetist) and Dr Banerjee so the team can make a safe, informed decision. This demonstrates coping with pressure without cutting corners. Option D is also appropriate: it manages Mr Ibrahim’s frustration professionally, sets expectations, and keeps decision-making with the appropriate seniors while maintaining patient-centred communication. Option C is helpful but secondary—locating missing notes supports safety, yet it must not replace immediate history-taking and escalation. Option E is unsafe: proceeding to induction before sharing relevant information undermines team situational awareness and could expose Sophie to avoidable harm. Option A is least appropriate: it is dishonest and prioritises list efficiency over patient safety, breaching professional integrity and theatre safety culture. The correct approach balances flow with robust pre-op checks and open communication.
learningPoints: Theatre time pressure must not override pre-operative safety checks and honest communication.
Escalate promptly to anaesthetist/consultant when new risk information emerges.
Manage distressed relatives with clear explanations and a plan, while maintaining professionalism.
Missing documentation should trigger additional verification, not assumptions or shortcuts.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you that Mr Khan is booked for an urgent extraction and has written “penicillin allergy” on his medical history, but the triage sheet from reception (Nadia) lists “no known drug allergies”. Mr Khan is in pain and repeatedly asks to “just take the tooth out quickly” because he needs to pick up his child. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is tied up with a facial swelling case and is not immediately available. The waiting room is full and Nadia is pressing you to keep to time because several patients have already complained. Mr Khan becomes irritated when you ask more questions and says he “might have had a rash years ago” but can’t remember. You are considering local anaesthetic and possible post‑op antibiotics depending on findings.

options.A: Ask Nadia to print the last GP summary on the system and, while it loads, take a focused allergy history from Mr Khan (what happened, when, what drug), documenting it clearly before deciding on antibiotics.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction under local anaesthetic now, avoid prescribing antibiotics, and plan to clarify the allergy later because the immediate treatment is not affected.
options.C: Explain to Mr Khan that the discrepancy must be clarified for safety, ask Sarah to re-check the medical history form with him, and if uncertainty remains, call Dr Kumar for a brief decision on antibiotic choice and documentation before starting.
options.D: Tell Sarah to update the record to “penicillin allergy” based on the form, proceed, and prescribe metronidazole post‑op if needed to avoid penicillin risk.
options.E: Ask Mr Khan to wait while you phone his GP practice to confirm the allergy status, even if this causes significant delay to the clinic.

explanation: The safest and most appropriate approach is to resolve the discrepancy enough to make safe prescribing and documentation decisions before treatment. Option C prioritises patient safety while acknowledging time pressure: you explain the need to clarify, use Sarah to re-check the history, and escalate briefly to Dr Kumar if uncertainty persists—appropriate given limits of competence and the potential consequences of an incorrect allergy label. Option A is also strong: obtaining a structured allergy history and checking available records supports informed decision-making and good documentation, though it may not address supervision as directly if the uncertainty remains. Option D is weaker because instructing Sarah to label a penicillin allergy without confirmation risks inaccurate records and future harm; choosing metronidazole “to be safe” can be inappropriate if not clinically indicated. Option B underestimates risk: even if extraction can proceed, intra‑operative decisions and post‑op prescribing may be affected, and failing to document properly is unsafe. Option E is least appropriate: phoning the GP during an emergency clinic is unlikely to be timely and may unnecessarily delay urgent care; using local records and senior input is usually more proportionate. This aligns with NHS expectations on patient safety, accurate records, and seeking supervision when needed.
learningPoints: Resolve discrepancies in allergy status before prescribing and document the history clearly.
Patient safety and accurate records outweigh pressure to maintain clinic flow.
Use the team effectively (e.g., dental nurse, reception) to verify information.
Recognise limits of competence and seek timely senior input when uncertainty could affect safety.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has mild learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Clarke. Emily (dental nurse) notes that Mrs Thompson keeps looking to her brother before answering. She needs a lower denture reline, but she also has an ulcer on the lateral tongue that you are concerned could be sinister. Mr Clarke repeatedly answers for her and says, “She doesn’t want any hospital referrals—just fix the denture today.” Mrs Thompson quietly says, “I’m scared,” but does not elaborate. The clinic is running late and the next patient is a wheelchair user with booked transport. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in another building and can be contacted by phone. When you suggest examining the ulcer more thoroughly, Mr Clarke becomes defensive and asks why you are “making a fuss”, insisting you do not mention anything to their GP. Emily looks uncomfortable and later tells you she feels Mrs Thompson is being “spoken over”.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to step out briefly so you can speak to Mrs Thompson alone, then assess her understanding and wishes about examination and referral, documenting capacity and consent carefully.
options.B: Proceed with the denture reline only, avoid discussing the ulcer further today, and arrange a routine review in six weeks to keep the clinic moving.
options.C: Explain to Mr Clarke that you cannot agree to keep clinically relevant findings from the GP, and that referral may be needed; invite him to share his concerns while keeping the focus on Mrs Thompson’s choices.
options.D: Call Dr Banerjee immediately and hand over the situation, asking them to speak directly to Mr Clarke to resolve the conflict while you move to the next patient.
options.E: Use Emily as a chaperone and complete a full oral examination with Mrs Thompson’s permission, offering a same-day urgent referral pathway if red flags are present.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions focus on Mrs Thompson’s autonomy, safe assessment, and appropriate escalation. A is key: speaking to Mrs Thompson alone (with Emily aware) helps reduce undue influence, allows you to assess capacity and obtain valid consent, and addresses the interpersonal dynamic where Mr Clarke is dominating. E is also appropriate: with consent, completing a full oral examination and acting on red flags supports patient safety; offering an urgent referral pathway is proportionate given concern for possible malignancy. G recognises the ethical tension between respecting family involvement and potential coercion; Emily’s observation that Mrs Thompson is being “spoken over” warrants considering safeguarding and following local policy, including advice from the safeguarding lead.

C is reasonable but not in the top three because it centres on managing Mr Clarke rather than first establishing Mrs Thompson’s wishes in private; it may follow after A. D risks inappropriate delegation and delay—Dr Banerjee can advise, but you should not simply pass the conflict to a consultant to keep to time. B is unsafe given a potentially sinister ulcer. F is incorrect: do not assume lack of capacity; capacity is decision-specific and must be assessed. H is unacceptable: omitting clinical findings breaches professional integrity and record-keeping standards and could harm Mrs Thompson.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s own understanding and freedom from undue influence.
Potentially malignant lesions require timely assessment and appropriate referral.
Capacity is decision-specific; do not assume incapacity based on disability.
Recognise and act on possible safeguarding indicators, following local policy and seeking advice.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover as a DCT. Dr Lewis (registrar) asks you to prescribe analgesia for Mr Petrov post‑op, but the drug chart already has an opioid prescription signed earlier. When you check, the dose seems unusually high for Mr Petrov’s weight and renal function. Rachel (ward nurse) says Mr Petrov is drowsy and his respiratory rate has dropped to 10, but she is reluctant to “bother the registrar again” because Dr Lewis was irritated during the morning ward round. Dr Chen (consultant) is in theatre. At the same time, Angela (ward nurse) asks you to quickly rewrite a missed discharge summary for another patient because transport is waiting. Dr Lewis messages you: “Just re-chart the same dose so we don’t get delayed.

options.A: Refuse to re-prescribe the opioid dose, ask Rachel for current observations, and urgently review Mr Petrov, escalating immediately to Dr Lewis and the nurse-in-charge/medical emergency team if opioid toxicity is suspected.
options.B: Re-chart the opioid at the same dose as requested to maintain continuity, then review Mr Petrov when you have finished the discharge summary.
options.C: Ask Rachel to continue monitoring and reduce the dose herself if she is worried, as nursing staff can titrate opioids on the ward.
options.D: Phone Dr Chen in theatre to inform them of the concern and ask for instruction, as they are ultimately responsible for the patient’s post-op plan.
options.E: Complete the discharge summary first to reduce ward pressure, then return to review Mr Petrov and discuss the chart later with Dr Lewis.

explanation:
Option A is most appropriate because it prioritises immediate patient safety and appropriate escalation. Mr Petrov is drowsy with a low respiratory rate and a potentially excessive opioid dose; as a prescriber you must not re-chart a dose you believe is unsafe. Reviewing the patient, obtaining observations from Rachel, and escalating to Dr Lewis and senior nursing/medical emergency support aligns with NHS patient safety duties and speaking up despite hierarchy pressure.

Option D is next best: if Dr Lewis is not responsive or the risk is high, contacting Dr Chen in theatre (or the on-call consultant pathway) is appropriate escalation. It shows recognising limits of competence and ensuring senior oversight, though it should not delay urgent action on the ward.

Option E is less appropriate but still above the unsafe choices: discharge paperwork matters, but it is secondary to a deteriorating patient; however, once immediate safety is addressed, returning to documentation tasks is reasonable.

Option B is unsafe and unprofessional: re-charting an apparently excessive opioid dose to avoid delay breaches prescribing standards and could cause harm. Option C is least appropriate because it inappropriately delegates prescribing decisions to Rachel; nurses cannot independently alter prescribed opioid doses without an authorised prescription, and it avoids your responsibility to act on a safety concern.
learningPoints: Do not prescribe or re-chart medication you believe is unsafe; patient safety overrides hierarchy pressure.
Respond promptly to signs of clinical deterioration and escalate appropriately.
Use clear escalation routes (registrar/consultant, nurse-in-charge, emergency team) when needed.
Documentation and flow pressures are secondary to immediate clinical risk.

scenario: In the radiology department, you bring Mrs Singh from the restorative clinic for a CBCT requested for implant planning. Elena (radiographer) reviews the request and says the referral form is incomplete: it lacks justification, pregnancy status, and the exact region of interest. She is busy and says she will have to cancel the scan because “we can’t accept this”. Mrs Singh is anxious and says she took time off work and cannot come back for weeks. Mark (receptionist) from radiology tells you the next slot is in 20 minutes and the list is overbooked. Your supervising consultant, Dr O’Connor, is in clinic with a difficult case and has asked not to be interrupted unless urgent. You wrote the request based on Dr O’Connor’s plan, but you are not fully confident about radiation regulations and the precise field needed. Elena’s tone becomes sharp and Mrs Singh starts to cry, saying she feels “passed around”.

options.A: Apologise to Elena for the incomplete form, ask what minimum information is required to proceed today, and update the request immediately with a clear clinical question and region of interest.
options.B: Tell Elena that Dr O’Connor requested the scan and insist she proceeds because the patient has already attended and the slot will be wasted.
options.C: Explain to Mrs Singh why justification and safety checks are required, offer a short break, and keep her informed of the plan and likely timings.
options.D: Ask Mark to rebook Mrs Singh for the next available slot without discussion, as the scan cannot proceed today.
options.E: Phone Dr O’Connor briefly with a focused question about the exact region/clinical question, then complete the request to meet IR(ME)R requirements.

explanation:
A, C and E best balance patient-centred care, interdepartmental teamwork, and legal imaging requirements. A is appropriate because you acknowledge the problem, work collaboratively with Elena, and correct the request with specific justification and region of interest—key under IR(ME)R principles and good professional relationships. C addresses Mrs Singh’s distress and maintains trust: explaining safety checks and keeping her updated reduces anxiety and helps manage expectations while you resolve the issue. E is also appropriate because you recognise your limits and obtain the necessary clinical detail from Dr O’Connor with a brief, focused call; this supports accurate justification without unnecessary delay.

B is inappropriate: pressuring Elena to scan without proper documentation undermines radiology governance and damages teamwork. D is premature and not patient-centred; rebooking may be necessary, but only after attempting proportionate fixes. F is unsafe: increasing field of view increases radiation exposure and is not justified “medico-legally”. H is unprofessional: the referrer is responsible for providing adequate clinical information; shifting responsibility to Elena is unfair and may breach governance. G can be appropriate if disagreement persists, but it is not required if you can rectify the request promptly with Elena and Dr O’Connor.
learningPoints: Imaging must be justified and properly documented; do not bypass governance to save time.
Maintain professional, collaborative communication with radiology colleagues under pressure.
Explain delays and safety requirements to patients to reduce distress and preserve trust.
Recognise limits of competence and seek focused senior input to complete referrals safely.

scenario: You are assisting in a paediatric GA list in oral surgery theatre. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you the list is running 40 minutes late because a child needed extra time in recovery. The next patient, Sophie (6), is due for multiple extractions. Sophie’s mother, Mrs Hassan, is upset and says she hasn’t been told what’s happening; she is worried Sophie will miss school and asks whether “we can just do fewer teeth today”. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is scrubbed and preparing for the next case. Dr Grant (registrar) asks you to obtain final consent and confirm the planned teeth with the chart. When you check the notes, the treatment plan lists different teeth from the theatre list, and one tooth is marked as “review” in clinic. Mrs Hassan becomes angry and says, “No one explains anything—are you even sure you’ve got the right child?” Chloe quietly mentions that Sophie’s father is on the phone demanding updates.

options.A: Pause the process, reconcile the discrepancy by checking Sophie’s clinic notes and radiographs with Dr Grant, and only proceed once the correct teeth are confirmed and documented.
options.B: Ask Chloe to reassure Mrs Hassan while you quickly sign the consent form based on the theatre list so the case can start on time.
options.C: Explain to Mrs Hassan that there is a discrepancy in the plan that must be clarified for Sophie’s safety, give a realistic time update, and involve Dr Grant in the discussion before consent is finalised.
options.D: Proceed with extracting only the teeth that are clearly symptomatic today, and leave any uncertain teeth to avoid delay and disagreement.
options.E: Ask Mrs Hassan to step out so you can call Sophie’s father and confirm what was agreed in clinic, then update the team.

explanation:
A is most appropriate because it directly addresses the highest-risk issue: a discrepancy between the clinic plan and theatre list. Correct-site surgery and valid consent require you to reconcile documentation (notes, radiographs) with Dr Grant and ensure the final plan is clearly recorded before proceeding. C is also highly appropriate: Mrs Hassan’s distress and loss of trust must be managed with honest communication. Explaining that safety checks are necessary, providing a realistic update, and involving Dr Grant supports patient-centred care and shared decision-making.

D is less appropriate but preferable to unsafe shortcuts: limiting extractions to clearly agreed/symptomatic teeth may reduce harm if uncertainty persists, but it still risks deviating from the planned GA episode and should only follow senior agreement and proper consent.

B is inappropriate because it prioritises time over safety and consent; signing based on an unverified theatre list undermines governance and exposes Sophie to wrong-tooth extraction. E is least appropriate: contacting Sophie’s father without clear consent raises confidentiality issues and does not resolve the documentary discrepancy; updates should be managed through appropriate channels and with parental authority clarified. Overall, NHS standards require robust identity checks, accurate documentation, and speaking up when there is a risk of harm, even under theatre time pressure.
learningPoints: Discrepancies in treatment plans must be resolved before GA to prevent wrong-site surgery.
Valid consent requires an agreed, clearly documented plan and time for questions.
Communicate transparently with distressed relatives while maintaining safety processes.
Do not let time pressure in theatre override governance and patient safety.

scenario:
You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan has been booked for an incision and drainage of a swelling, but his triage sheet lists “penicillin allergy – rash as child” while the electronic record shows “no known drug allergies.” Mr Khan is in pain and repeatedly asks for “the strongest antibiotics” so he can go back to work. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is running two rooms and tells you to “just start, we’re already 40 minutes behind.” At the same time, Nadia (receptionist) says a child with facial swelling has arrived as a walk-in and is getting upset in the waiting area.

You need to balance patient safety, documentation uncertainty, and service pressure. Mr Khan is frustrated and starts raising his voice at Sarah, who looks shaken. You have not yet examined him, and the notes from his GDP are missing.

options.A: Ask Sarah to bring Mr Khan straight into the surgery, start local anaesthetic, and plan to avoid prescribing penicillin “just in case,” without delaying for further checks.
options.B: Pause the procedure, apologise to Mr Khan for the delay, clarify his allergy history directly, and ask Sarah to check the GP summary/previous discharge letters while you examine the swelling and assess need for antibiotics.
options.C: Tell Nadia you cannot see the walk-in child because Mr Khan is booked, and ask her to rebook the child for another day to protect the clinic flow.
options.D: Call Dr Kumar into the room immediately to take over Mr Khan’s care so you can go and assess the walk-in child with facial swelling.
options.E: Proceed with incision and drainage now and plan to document the allergy discrepancy afterwards to avoid further delay to other patients.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it prioritises patient safety and informed prescribing: the discrepancy between the triage sheet and electronic record must be clarified before any antibiotic decisions. Speaking directly with Mr Khan, while Sarah checks available sources (GP summary/discharge letters), uses the team effectively and addresses his distress, reducing the risk of an avoidable adverse drug reaction. Examining the swelling first also ensures antibiotics are only used when clinically indicated.

Option A is a reasonable interim approach because it avoids penicillin and brings Mr Khan into care, but starting local anaesthetic before resolving the documentation issue and without a clinical assessment risks rushing and does not address the interpersonal tension with Sarah.

Option D may be appropriate if the walk-in child appears unwell, but escalating immediately to Dr Kumar without first triaging urgency can be inefficient; however, it shows awareness of competing priorities.

Option E is unsafe: proceeding and “documenting later” undermines record accuracy and governance.

Option C is least appropriate because turning away a child with facial swelling without clinical triage risks harm and conflicts with patient-centred care and safeguarding responsibilities.
learningPoints: Resolve allergy and medication discrepancies before prescribing or administering drugs.
Patient safety and clinical assessment come before clinic speed targets.
Use the team to gather information while you communicate with an anxious or angry patient.
Walk-in facial swellings require prompt triage; do not dismiss without assessment.

scenario:
In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has moderate learning disability and attends with her support worker, Olivia. Mrs Thompson needs extraction of a painful lower molar. She appears anxious but engages with you when you speak slowly.
