Olivia answers most questions for her and says, “Just get it out today—she won’t cope if we come back.” The appointment is running late and Chloe (dental nurse) quietly tells you the next patient is a wheelchair user with hospital transport booked and cannot wait long.

During consent, Mrs Thompson asks you, “Will it hurt? I don’t want injections,” and looks to Olivia for reassurance. Dr O’Connor (consultant in special care) is in another building and only reachable by phone. Olivia becomes irritated and says she has other clients to see and cannot stay for long.

options.A: Offer Mrs Thompson a short break, then re-explain the extraction and local anaesthetic using simple language and visual aids, checking her understanding with Chloe present.
options.B: Ask Olivia to step outside briefly so you can assess Mrs Thompson’s understanding and wishes without undue influence, while reassuring Olivia you will update her.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction because Olivia is the regular support worker and is requesting it, documenting that Olivia consented on her behalf.
options.D: Phone Dr O’Connor for advice on capacity and consent, and if you cannot establish valid consent today, plan a follow-up with a best-interests process if needed.
options.E: Tell Chloe to start preparing instruments immediately so the procedure can begin as soon as Mrs Thompson stops asking questions.

explanation:
A is appropriate because it supports valid consent by adapting communication to Mrs Thompson’s needs, using simple explanations and checking understanding. Involving Chloe appropriately provides support and a clear record of the discussion.

B is also appropriate: Olivia may unintentionally exert pressure. Seeing Mrs Thompson briefly alone helps you assess capacity and ensure the decision is hers, while maintaining professionalism by explaining to Olivia why this is necessary.

D is appropriate because Dr O’Connor can advise on capacity assessment and, if consent cannot be established, on best-interests pathways. This protects Mrs Thompson and you, even under time pressure.

F is helpful clinically, but it comes after you have established capacity/consent; it is less immediately critical than D in this scenario. C and G are inappropriate: a support worker cannot consent unless legally authorised, and relying on their signature breaches ethical and legal standards. E prioritises speed over patient-centred care and may increase anxiety. H is punitive and risks discrimination; you should not discharge simply to manage clinic flow.

Overall, the best actions uphold NHS standards on consent, capacity, and respectful multidisciplinary working despite Olivia’s frustration and clinic pressures.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s understanding and voluntary decision; supporters cannot consent unless legally authorised.
Assess capacity and manage potential undue influence sensitively and professionally.
Escalate to senior support when consent/capacity is unclear, even under time pressure.
Reasonable adjustments and alternative treatment plans support patient-centred care in special care dentistry.

scenario:
You are on-call in OMFS trauma. A junior registrar, Dr Grant, asks you to clerk Mr Petrov, who has a mandibular fracture and is requesting strong analgesia. While you are documenting, you notice Dr Grant has already prescribed co-codamol and morphine on the electronic chart, but the allergy section is blank and the weight is estimated. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you Mr Petrov mentioned he “reacted badly” to codeine previously but can’t remember details. Mr Petrov’s partner is on speakerphone, repeatedly demanding you “give him something that knocks him out.”

Angela (ward nurse) whispers that Dr Grant has been “snappy all night” and earlier asked her to administer meds before the chart was fully completed. Dr Grant is now in theatre and not answering his bleep. The ward is busy, and you are aware delays will increase Mr Petrov’s distress and complaints.

options.A: Ask Rachel to withhold the co-codamol and morphine until the allergy history is clarified and the prescription is reviewed, and document the concern in the notes.
options.B: Administer the prescribed medication as charted to prevent Mr Petrov escalating, and plan to complete missing details later when Dr Grant returns.
options.C: Attempt to contact Dr Grant again and, if unsuccessful, escalate to the on-call consultant Dr Ahmed for urgent review of the prescription and analgesia plan.
options.D: Tell Mr Petrov’s partner on speakerphone that she is being unreasonable and end the call to reduce pressure on the team.
options.E: Take a focused allergy and adverse reaction history from Mr Petrov, update the electronic record with what you can verify, and discuss safer analgesia options with Rachel while awaiting senior input.

explanation:
E is most appropriate because it directly addresses the immediate safety risk: Mr Petrov has reported a prior problem with codeine, and the allergy section is incomplete. Taking a focused history, updating the record, and working with Rachel on safer options demonstrates patient-centred care, accurate documentation, and teamwork.

C is next because Dr Grant is unavailable and there are concerns about prescribing practice. Escalating to Dr Ahmed for urgent review is proportionate and protects patient safety, aligning with recognising limits of competence and appropriate supervision.

A is reasonable as an interim safety measure; withholding potentially harmful medication is appropriate, but it should be combined with active steps to provide alternative analgesia and escalation, not just documentation.

D is less appropriate: the partner’s behaviour is challenging, but abruptly ending the call may inflame conflict. A calmer boundary-setting explanation would be better.

B is least appropriate because administering medications with incomplete allergy information and estimated weight risks harm and undermines medicines governance. “Fixing it later” is not acceptable in an acute setting.

The approach should also note Angela’s concern about Dr Grant’s behaviour, which may need follow-up through appropriate channels after the immediate risk is managed.
learningPoints: Medicines safety requires accurate allergy documentation and appropriate verification before administration.
Escalate when the prescriber is unavailable and there is a patient safety concern.
Manage distressed relatives with clear, respectful boundaries rather than confrontation.
Address professionalism concerns about colleagues through appropriate escalation and documentation.

scenario:
In a radiology department supporting an oral surgery clinic, you request an OPG for Mrs Hassan, who is booked for extraction of multiple teeth under sedation later today. Elena (radiographer) refuses, saying the referral form is missing justification and pregnancy status, and there is no documented consent for imaging. You are under pressure because Dr Chen (consultant) expects the imaging before the sedation list starts, and Lucy (dental nurse) has already prepared the sedation paperwork.

Mrs Hassan is in the waiting area with her adult son, who is translating. She looks embarrassed and says quietly she does not want her son to know about a possible pregnancy. The son becomes impatient and tells Elena, “Just do the X-ray—we’ve been waiting ages.” Elena remains firm and says she will escalate to her manager if you insist.

options.A: Ask Mrs Hassan (with her son translating) directly about pregnancy status and proceed based on her answer to avoid delaying Dr Chen’s list.
options.B: Request a professional interpreter or arrange to speak to Mrs Hassan privately, explain why pregnancy status matters for radiation safety, and obtain her consent before resubmitting a corrected referral.
options.C: Tell Elena that Dr Chen requested the OPG and that radiology should not delay clinical care, then ask her to proceed while you amend the form afterwards.
options.D: Escalate immediately to Elena’s manager, stating radiology is obstructing urgent patient care.
options.E: Work with Lucy to quickly complete the justification and consent documentation, and include clear clinical details for Elena, while keeping Mrs Hassan informed about the delay.

explanation:
B is appropriate because it protects Mrs Hassan’s confidentiality and supports valid consent. Using a professional interpreter or speaking privately avoids relying on her son for sensitive information and allows you to explain radiation risks and pregnancy checks clearly, meeting IR(ME)R principles and patient-centred care.

E is appropriate because it addresses Elena’s legitimate governance concerns by completing justification and consent documentation properly. Collaborating with Lucy and keeping Mrs Hassan informed maintains teamwork and reduces conflict.

D can be appropriate if, after you have attempted to correct the referral and complete safety checks, there is still an unreasonable barrier to timely care. Escalation should be proportionate and focused on resolving the issue, not blame.

A is inappropriate because it breaches confidentiality and may coerce disclosure in front of her son. C undermines radiology safety processes and damages interdepartmental relationships. F is confrontational and risks escalating the situation. G and H compromise patient care and planning for sedation without first exhausting reasonable steps to obtain safe imaging.

Overall, the best actions balance timely service delivery with radiation safety, consent, and respectful cross-department communication.
learningPoints: Do not use family members as interpreters for sensitive information; protect confidentiality.
Radiography requires documented justification, consent, and pregnancy checks under IR(ME)R principles.
Work collaboratively with other departments to resolve governance issues rather than applying hierarchy pressure.
Escalate appropriately if barriers persist after reasonable corrective steps.

scenario:
You are a DCT on a paediatric GA list in oral surgery theatre. Daniel (age 7) is next for multiple extractions. Emily (theatre nurse) tells you the consent form is signed by Daniel’s father, Mr Clarke, but the procedure listed is “fillings and extraction,” which doesn’t match the clinic letter stating “extractions only.” Dr Banerjee (consultant) is scrubbed and asks you to “sort it quickly” because the list is already running late and the anaesthetist is waiting.

In the anaesthetic room, Mr Clarke looks exhausted and says he signed whatever was put in front of him because Daniel was crying. Daniel is now calm but asks, “Are you taking my teeth out today?” Mr Clarke becomes defensive when you mention the mismatch and says, “Don’t start delaying things—he’s been fasting since 6am.” Emily quietly adds that the wrong template has been used twice this month but nothing has changed.

options.A: Proceed with the extractions as planned because the clinic letter supports it, and correct the consent form after the case to avoid delaying the list.
options.B: Pause and clarify with Mr Clarke and Daniel what procedures are planned, then ask Dr Banerjee to briefly re-confirm and amend the consent form before induction.
options.C: Ask Emily to change the consent form wording herself while you focus on keeping the list moving, since she has seen the template issue before.
options.D: Escalate immediately to the theatre manager mid-list to report the template problem, even if it causes a delay to Daniel’s case.
options.E: After Daniel’s case, document the near-miss and raise the recurring template issue through the appropriate governance route (e.g., incident report) with Emily’s input.

explanation:
B is most appropriate because valid consent must match the intended procedure and be confirmed before induction. Clarifying with Mr Clarke and Daniel supports patient-centred care and avoids proceeding on an incorrect form. Involving Dr Banerjee to re-confirm and amend consent is appropriate given senior responsibility and the theatre context.

E is next because Emily has identified a recurring system failure. Documenting the near-miss and reporting via governance processes (e.g., incident reporting) helps prevent repetition and supports a safety culture without disrupting immediate care.

D may be justified if there is an immediate risk that cannot be controlled, but escalating mid-list is likely disproportionate once the consent issue is being corrected; it could unnecessarily delay care.

C is inappropriate because consent is not a nursing task to “fix” independently; the clinician responsible must ensure accuracy and discussion with the parent/child.

A is least appropriate: proceeding with a mismatched consent form undermines legal and ethical standards and exposes Daniel, Mr Clarke, and the team to harm and complaint risk. Time pressure and fasting do not override consent requirements.

This approach balances theatre efficiency with safety, professionalism, and learning from system errors.
learningPoints: Consent must accurately reflect the planned procedure and be confirmed before anaesthesia.
Time pressure and list delays do not justify bypassing consent safeguards.
Report and address recurring system failures through appropriate governance routes.
Use senior support in theatre when consent or documentation is unclear.

scenario: You are the DCT in an NHS oral surgery clinic. Dr Kumar (registrar) is running late after being called to theatre, and the waiting room is full. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan’s notes show a “penicillin allergy?” scribbled in an old referral letter, but today’s clerking sheet lists “NKDA”. Mr Khan is anxious and says he “gets a rash sometimes” but can’t recall the antibiotic. Dr Kumar has asked you to “crack on” with an extraction and to prescribe antibiotics “if it looks infected” because he may not return for 30 minutes. Meanwhile, Nadia (receptionist) says Mrs Thompson is threatening to complain about delays, and a medical student is observing.

options.A: Ask Sarah to phone Mr Khan’s GP for clarification of the allergy while you bring Mr Khan into a side room to take a focused allergy history and explain the delay.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction as planned, avoid prescribing antibiotics, and document that the patient reported only “a rash sometimes”.
options.C: Call Dr Kumar to update him about the conflicting allergy documentation and ask whether he can review Mr Khan before any prescribing or treatment starts.
options.D: Tell Nadia to rebook Mr Khan to another day because the clinic is running late and you cannot confirm the allergy today.
options.E: Prescribe amoxicillin after the extraction “just in case”, as Dr Kumar suggested, to reduce the risk of post‑op infection and speed up turnover.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety in the face of conflicting documentation and pressure to maintain clinic flow. Option A is most appropriate: involving Sarah to seek clarification while you take a structured allergy history respects Mr Khan’s anxiety and supports informed decision-making. It also uses the team effectively without abandoning the patient.

Option C is next best because the discrepancy (“penicillin allergy?” vs “NKDA”) and the request to prescribe require senior input; escalating to Dr Kumar is appropriate given limits of competence and the risk of harm. Option B may be acceptable only if, after careful history, you judge treatment safe and no antibiotics are indicated; however, it is weaker because it risks proceeding without resolving uncertainty or senior oversight.

Option D is overly disruptive and does not address the immediate safety issue; rebooking may be necessary if safe care cannot be delivered, but it should not be the first response simply due to delays or complaint pressure from Mrs Thompson. Option E is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin “just in case” ignores the possible penicillin allergy, undermines antimicrobial stewardship, and breaches professional standards by prescribing for convenience rather than clinical indication. Documenting clearly and prioritising safety over throughput aligns with NHS expectations.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy information must be clarified before prescribing or treatment that could cause harm.
Escalate to a senior when uncertainty and risk exceed your competence, even under time pressure.
Antibiotics should be prescribed only when clinically indicated and with a reliable allergy history.
Use the wider team to gather information while maintaining patient-centred communication.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Hassan, who has severe dental anxiety and mild learning disability. Emily (dental nurse) tells you the appointment was booked for a simple restoration, but the referral letter from Dr Chen (consultant) suggests considering extraction under sedation if cooperation is limited. Mrs Hassan arrives with her brother, Mr Hassan, who answers most questions and insists you “just take it out today” because she has been “crying all week”. Mrs Hassan avoids eye contact and nods, but when you ask directly about extraction she becomes tearful and says quietly, “I don’t want needles.” There is no interpreter booked, and you are unsure how much she understands. Laura (hygienist) is waiting to use the surgery next, and reception (Olivia) says there are no spare slots for rebooking this month.

options.A: Ask Mr Hassan to step into the waiting area for a few minutes so you can assess Mrs Hassan’s capacity and preferences privately, with Emily present.
options.B: Proceed with extraction under local anaesthetic today because the brother is requesting it and the referral suggested extraction might be needed.
options.C: Offer to place a temporary dressing and provide clear, simple explanations using visual aids, then arrange a follow‑up with sedation assessment if needed.
options.D: Ask Olivia to find any cancellation slot and prioritise Mrs Hassan on the waiting list due to pain, documenting the rationale.
options.E: Tell Mrs Hassan that if she refuses needles she cannot be helped, and discharge her back to the GDP.

explanation:
The key issues are valid consent, autonomy, and safeguarding against undue influence, alongside pain management and service pressures. A is highly appropriate: asking Mr Hassan to step out allows you to explore Mrs Hassan’s wishes and assess capacity without coercion, while keeping Emily present for support and chaperoning. F is also essential; using teach-back checks understanding in an accessible way and strengthens documentation, which is critical when communication is challenging.

C is appropriate because it offers immediate, proportionate pain relief (e.g., temporary dressing) while avoiding irreversible treatment without robust consent. It also creates a pathway to sedation assessment consistent with Dr Chen’s referral.

D is helpful but secondary; prioritising on the waiting list supports access but does not replace the need to address consent and current symptoms. B and H are inappropriate because family preference cannot substitute for the patient’s informed consent, especially when she appears distressed and possibly not fully understanding. E is unprofessional and fails patient-centred care. G is unsafe and unrealistic: handing over to Laura to “keep time” undermines continuity and does not resolve the consent dilemma. NHS standards require patient understanding and freedom from pressure, even when clinics are busy.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s own understanding and voluntary agreement, not family pressure.
Use accessible communication methods (teach-back, visual aids) and document consent discussions clearly.
Provide interim symptom control when definitive treatment cannot be ethically or safely delivered today.
Service pressures should not drive irreversible decisions in vulnerable patients.

scenario: On an OMFS ward during an on-call shift, you review Mr Petrov post-operatively after drainage of a facial abscess. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you Mr Petrov is angry because he believes he was “left fasting for no reason” and wants to see “the boss”. Dr Grant (registrar) is in ED dealing with trauma. While you are documenting, you overhear Chloe (dental nurse) showing a colleague a photo on her phone of Mr Petrov’s swollen face taken in theatre, joking that it looks “like a cartoon”. Mr Petrov’s partner, Mrs Petrov, is at the bedside and looks upset, asking if staff are “laughing at him”. Chloe notices you and says, “It’s fine—it’s not on social media.” At the same time, Rachel asks you to prescribe analgesia urgently because Mr Petrov is in pain, and the ward round is due in 10 minutes.

options.A: Ask Chloe to delete the photo immediately, explain why it breaches confidentiality, and inform Dr Grant and the ward sister about the incident.
options.B: Ignore the phone issue for now and focus on prescribing analgesia to avoid delaying pain relief.
options.C: Reassure Mrs Petrov that staff are not laughing and suggest she raises concerns later via PALS if she remains unhappy.
options.D: Speak to Chloe privately away from the bedside, ask her to stop sharing the image, and then return to apologise to Mr and Mrs Petrov and address their concerns.
options.E: Confront Chloe in front of Mr and Mrs Petrov to show you take confidentiality seriously and deter future behaviour.

explanation:
This scenario involves an immediate professionalism breach alongside urgent clinical needs. D is most appropriate: speaking to Chloe privately reduces escalation and preserves dignity, while stopping the behaviour promptly. Returning to apologise to Mr and Mrs Petrov addresses trust and acknowledges their distress, which is crucial given the interpersonal tension at the bedside.

A is also appropriate and should follow: the photo should be deleted and the incident escalated to Dr Grant and the ward sister for governance and learning, consistent with NHS confidentiality standards. However, doing A without first managing the bedside dynamic may worsen distress.

B is next: Mr Petrov’s pain relief is a patient-safety priority, but it should not be used as a reason to ignore the confidentiality issue; both can be managed sequentially and efficiently.

C is weaker because it deflects responsibility and may appear dismissive; while PALS is an option, you should address concerns directly and transparently. E is least appropriate: confronting Chloe publicly risks humiliating her, escalating conflict, and further undermining Mr Petrov’s confidence in the team. Professional integrity requires prompt action, proportionate escalation, and maintaining respectful team communication while safeguarding patient confidentiality.
learningPoints: Patient images are confidential clinical data and must not be shared informally, even if not posted online.
Address unprofessional behaviour promptly but proportionately, using private discussion where possible.
Maintain patient trust through honest, empathetic communication and appropriate apology when standards fall short.
Balance urgent clinical care with governance responsibilities; both are part of patient safety.

scenario: In the hospital radiology department, you attend with Mr Clarke for a CBCT requested by Dr O’Connor (consultant) before complex third molar surgery. Elena (radiographer) reviews the request and says the form is incomplete: the justification is vague and the pregnancy status box is blank. Mr Clarke is frustrated; he took time off work and says he will “walk out” if delayed again. You check the electronic record and find a brief note from Dr O’Connor: “CBCT for 38—close to IAN.” No recent OPG is uploaded. Elena says radiology governance requires clear justification and prior imaging unless clinically urgent, and she feels pressured because the scanner is overbooked. You are also due back in clinic to assist Dr Lewis (specialty dentist) with an anxious patient. Mark (reception) calls to ask whether to cancel Mr Clarke’s scan slot.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed with the CBCT because Dr O’Connor requested it, and document that radiology refused initially.
options.B: Phone Dr O’Connor to clarify the clinical justification and whether an OPG exists, then relay the information to Elena to complete the request appropriately.
options.C: Offer to arrange an urgent OPG first if available today, and only proceed to CBCT if the OPG confirms proximity concerns, explaining this to Mr Clarke.
options.D: Tell Mark to cancel the scan and rebook Mr Clarke for another day to avoid delaying other patients.
options.E: Explain to Mr Clarke why radiology needs adequate justification, apologise for the inconvenience, and check what flexibility he has before he leaves.

explanation:
The most appropriate actions focus on safe, justified imaging and constructive interdepartmental communication. B is key: contacting Dr O’Connor to clarify justification and prior imaging supports IR(ME)R-style governance expectations and helps Elena proceed appropriately rather than bypassing safeguards. It also models respectful teamwork across departments.

F is also appropriate and practical: confirming and documenting pregnancy status (where relevant) addresses a clear omission on the request and reduces delay without undermining radiology standards.

E supports patient-centred care. Mr Clarke’s frustration is understandable; explaining the reason for checks and apologising can de-escalate and may prevent him leaving, while you explore his constraints.

C may be reasonable in some pathways, but arranging extra imaging without consultant agreement can add delay and may not match Dr O’Connor’s plan. D and G prioritise convenience over patient care and risk losing the slot. A and H are inappropriate because they pressure Elena to breach governance; “because the consultant asked” is not sufficient justification. Overall, you should facilitate proper documentation, escalation, and transparent communication while balancing clinic pressures.
learningPoints: Imaging requests must meet governance requirements; pressure and overbooking do not justify bypassing checks.
Effective cross-department communication can resolve delays and maintain professional relationships.
Address patient frustration with clear explanations and apology while keeping safety standards intact.
Complete missing request details (e.g., pregnancy status) promptly and document clearly.

scenario: During a busy sedation clinic, Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in the adjacent room consenting another patient. You are asked to see Sophie, a 10‑year‑old scheduled for inhalation sedation for multiple extractions. Lucy (dental nurse) quietly tells you Sophie’s mother, Mrs Singh, has just mentioned Sophie had wheeze overnight and used her salbutamol inhaler this morning. The pre-assessment form from last month states “asthma well controlled”. Mrs Singh is anxious and says, “We can’t come back—she’s missed too much school.” Sophie looks pale and says she feels “tight” in her chest but wants the teeth out. The list is running late, and Maria (therapist) is waiting for the sedation room afterwards. You are confident with routine inhalation sedation but have limited experience managing acute asthma symptoms in the sedation setting.

options.A: Proceed with inhalation sedation as planned, as Sophie has used her inhaler and the extractions are urgent.
options.B: Ask Lucy to obtain Sophie’s baseline observations and peak flow (if available), and immediately update Dr Banerjee for a senior review before any sedation starts.
options.C: Explain to Mrs Singh and Sophie that safety comes first, and that you may need to postpone today depending on Dr Banerjee’s assessment, acknowledging the inconvenience.
options.D: Offer to do the extractions under local anaesthetic without sedation to avoid postponement.
options.E: Document the new respiratory history, inform Maria about a likely delay, and ask reception to identify a contingency slot if the case is deferred.

explanation:
B is most appropriate: new symptoms (overnight wheeze, salbutamol use, chest tightness) change Sophie’s risk profile. Getting observations and escalating promptly to Dr Banerjee recognises your limits of competence and prioritises safety before sedation. C follows because clear, empathetic communication with Mrs Singh and Sophie supports informed expectations and reduces conflict; it also reinforces that the plan depends on clinical assessment, not convenience.

E is a sensible practical step once safety is being addressed: documenting the change, warning Maria of delay, and exploring contingency planning helps manage service pressures transparently without compromising care.

D is less appropriate. Switching to local anaesthetic to “avoid postponement” may still be unsafe if Sophie is unwell and anxious; it also risks coercion and does not address respiratory risk. A is least appropriate because proceeding despite active symptoms could precipitate deterioration during sedation, breaching clinical safety standards. NHS practice expects dynamic reassessment on the day, appropriate senior involvement, and willingness to defer when risk is increased. Managing the list is important, but not at the expense of a child’s safety.
learningPoints: Day-of-treatment changes in medical status require reassessment and may necessitate postponement.
Escalate to a senior and recognise limits of competence, especially in paediatric sedation.
Communicate delays and safety decisions empathetically to families to maintain trust.
Document new information and manage clinic flow transparently without compromising safety.

scenario: You are a DCT in an oral surgery clinic. Mr Khan is booked for an extraction of LR6. The referral letter is brief and the electronic record lists “penicillin allergy—rash as child,” but the pre-assessment form completed today states “no allergies.” Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you she overheard Mr Khan telling reception that he “swells up with antibiotics.” Dr Kumar (registrar) is scrubbed in next door and has asked you to “crack on” because the clinic is already running 40 minutes late and Mrs Thompson is waiting in pain. Mr Khan appears anxious and says he wants to “get it done quickly” and doesn’t want “lots of questions.” You are considering local anaesthetic and whether antibiotic cover is needed due to a history of infective endocarditis mentioned in an old discharge summary that is not clearly linked to today’s referral.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction under local anaesthetic without further delay, and document that the patient denied allergies on today’s form to keep the clinic moving for Mrs Thompson.
options.B: Pause the procedure, ask Mr Khan directly about any previous reactions and the endocarditis history, and ask Sarah to retrieve the old discharge summary while you update Dr Kumar about the discrepancy and potential need for advice.
options.C: Ask Sarah to call Dr Kumar to come immediately and refuse to see any further patients until he reviews Mr Khan’s allergy status and cardiac history in person.
options.D: Ask Nadia (receptionist) to print the referral and ask Mr Khan to sign a statement confirming he has no allergies, then proceed as planned.
options.E: Prescribe amoxicillin prophylaxis “to be safe” based on the old discharge summary, then carry out the extraction quickly to minimise further delays.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to pause and clarify the conflicting safety-critical information. Option B directly addresses Sarah’s concern, checks Mr Khan’s account of reactions, and seeks corroborating evidence (old discharge summary) while keeping Dr Kumar informed. This aligns with NHS expectations on patient safety, accurate records, and responding to team members who raise concerns.

Option C is cautious and recognises limits of competence, but it is less proportionate than B because it unnecessarily halts care for others and demands immediate in-person review when a focused history and record check can be started promptly.

Option D attempts to obtain a signature, but it treats consent/confirmation as a legal shield rather than ensuring understanding and safety; it also ignores the discrepancy and the nurse’s report.

Option A is unsafe: proceeding despite contradictory allergy information and a possible endocarditis history risks harm and undermines team trust, even under time pressure and with other patients waiting.

Option E is least appropriate: prescribing antibiotics “to be safe” without clear indication or senior agreement is poor antimicrobial stewardship and potentially dangerous if the allergy is genuine. It also bypasses informed consent and proper assessment.
learningPoints: Treat conflicting allergy histories as a patient-safety red flag and clarify before treatment.
Respond appropriately to concerns raised by dental nurses and other team members.
Recognise limits of competence and involve seniors proportionately, especially when cardiac history affects management.
Avoid defensive practice and unnecessary antibiotics; follow antimicrobial stewardship principles.

scenario: You are on an emergency dental clinic rota in a hospital setting. Mrs Hassan attends with severe pain from a lower molar. She demands antibiotics and says she will complain if she doesn’t get them today. Emily (dental nurse) notes Mrs Hassan is 28 weeks pregnant and visibly distressed. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in theatre and not immediately available. Mark (receptionist) tells you there are four patients waiting, including Mr Clarke who has facial swelling. Mrs Hassan refuses an intraoral radiograph, saying she has seen social media posts about “radiation harming babies,” but she also insists on immediate extraction “if that’s the only way.” Emily privately says she is worried Mrs Hassan doesn’t understand the risks of extraction in pregnancy without imaging and may be reacting to anxiety rather than informed decision-making.

options.A: Explain to Mrs Hassan why antibiotics are not routinely indicated for dental pain without signs of spreading infection, and offer definitive treatment options while exploring her concerns about radiographs in pregnancy.
options.B: Provide antibiotics to de-escalate the situation and book Mrs Hassan for an extraction another day when radiographs can be taken.
options.C: Offer to use a lead apron and thyroid collar, explain the very low dose of an intraoral radiograph, and check understanding; if she still refuses, document the discussion and reassess whether any treatment can be safely provided without imaging.
options.D: Ask Mark to move Mrs Hassan to the top of the list because she is threatening a complaint, even though Mr Clarke has facial swelling.
options.E: Ask Emily to speak to Mrs Hassan alone to “calm her down” while you start seeing the next patient, then return when Mrs Hassan has agreed to a radiograph.

explanation:
Options A, C and F best balance patient-centred care with safety and stewardship. A addresses Mrs Hassan’s expectations and complaint threat by giving a clear rationale about antibiotics, while still offering definitive care. C supports informed consent: it tackles misinformation about radiographs in pregnancy, offers reasonable mitigation (apron/thyroid collar), and plans for documented shared decision-making if she continues to refuse. F ensures you triage properly in a busy emergency clinic—checking for red flags and prioritising safety, while offering analgesia and safety-netting if immediate definitive care is not possible.

B is inappropriate because prescribing antibiotics to placate a complaint is poor antimicrobial stewardship and may delay definitive treatment. D is unfair and unsafe: Mr Clarke’s facial swelling may indicate spreading infection and should not be deprioritised. E inappropriately delegates complex consent discussions to Emily and risks delay without addressing the clinical decision. G is unsafe: extraction without imaging may increase risk (e.g., root morphology, proximity to vital structures) and undermines informed consent. H is usually unnecessary; dental radiographs are generally safe in pregnancy and you should not create avoidable barriers to care, though you could seek senior advice if uncertainty remains.
learningPoints: Antibiotics should not be used to manage expectations or complaint threats; follow antimicrobial stewardship.
Valid consent requires addressing misinformation and checking patient understanding, especially in pregnancy.
Prioritise patients using clinical urgency (e.g., swelling/red flags) rather than who is most demanding.
Document discussions and provide clear safety-netting when definitive care is deferred.

scenario: You are a DCT in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Singh, who has mild learning disability, attends with her support worker for multiple restorations. Laura (hygienist) tells you she has noticed Dr Grant (specialty dentist) taking photos of Mrs Singh’s teeth on his personal phone “for teaching,” and she is unsure whether consent was obtained. Mrs Singh seems keen to please staff and nods along, but when you ask about the photos she looks confused and asks, “Will it go on Facebook?” Dr Grant later says you are overreacting and that “everyone does it” because the clinic camera is broken. Chloe (dental nurse) is anxious because she assisted and didn’t challenge it at the time. The clinic is running late and the support worker says they must leave in 20 minutes for transport.

options.A: Speak to Mrs Singh and her support worker to clarify what she understood about the photos, apologise for any confusion, and explain how images should be stored and used; then pause further photography until proper consent and secure systems are in place.
options.B: Confront Dr Grant in front of the team and tell him to delete the photos immediately, making it clear his behaviour is unacceptable.
options.C: Ask Chloe to write a statement about what happened and submit it to the service manager today, without discussing it with Dr Grant to avoid conflict.
options.D: Continue the appointment to finish the restorations on time, and plan to raise the phone photography issue at the next team meeting when the clinic is less busy.
options.E: Escalate the concern promptly via the clinic’s governance route (e.g., to the service manager/IG lead), and ask Dr Grant to move the images to an approved secure system or delete them if that is not possible, documenting actions taken.

explanation:
Option A is most appropriate because it is immediately patient-centred and safeguards Mrs Singh, who may have limited capacity to understand how images are used. Clarifying understanding with the support worker present, apologising, and stopping further photography until consent and secure storage are ensured reflects NHS standards on confidentiality and informed consent.

Option E is also highly appropriate: potential information governance breaches require timely escalation through formal routes. Asking Dr Grant to transfer images to an approved system or delete them if transfer is not possible addresses risk containment and creates an audit trail.

Option D is less appropriate: completing urgent care and avoiding disruption is understandable given time pressure and transport constraints, but delaying action risks ongoing confidentiality breaches. It might be acceptable only after immediate risk is contained.

Option B is unhelpful due to its confrontational public approach; while deletion may be necessary, challenging Dr Grant in front of others can escalate conflict and reduce openness to learning.

Option C is least appropriate: instructing Chloe to produce a statement immediately without first addressing patient welfare and risk containment can feel punitive, may distress Chloe, and bypasses proportionate, transparent discussion and governance processes.
learningPoints: Patients with additional needs may require extra support to give valid consent; check understanding and avoid acquiescence.
Clinical images must be captured and stored using approved secure systems; personal phones pose IG risks.
Concerns about colleagues should be escalated promptly and professionally through governance routes.
Address immediate risk first, then document and manage team dynamics constructively.

scenario:
You are covering OMFS on-call overnight. Daniel, a 9-year-old, arrives in ED after a fall with a suspected mandibular fracture. Rachel (ED nurse) says Daniel has not eaten since lunchtime and is very upset. His father is angry and repeatedly interrupts, insisting Daniel must be taken to theatre immediately. Dr Chen (consultant) is at home and asks you to assess and call back with a clear plan. Elena (radiographer) says CT slots are limited and she can only fit Daniel in if the request is completed correctly within 15 minutes. Meanwhile, Angela (ward nurse) calls to ask whether you can review Mr Petrov on the ward who has worsening post-op bleeding from an earlier extraction.

You feel confident assessing trauma but have limited experience in paediatric consent and managing aggressive relatives.

options.A: Ask Rachel to arrange a quiet room and offer Daniel age-appropriate reassurance; explain to his father the need for imaging and senior input, and set boundaries about respectful communication.
options.B: Tell Elena to proceed with the CT without a formal request to save time, and complete the paperwork afterwards once the situation is calmer.
options.C: Phone Dr Chen immediately to ask him to come in, explaining you are not comfortable managing the father’s aggression and paediatric consent.
options.D: Triage urgency by first assessing Mr Petrov’s bleeding with Angela while asking Rachel to keep Daniel nil by mouth and monitor observations, then return to Daniel for imaging arrangements.
options.E: Complete the CT request promptly with clear clinical details and liaise directly with Elena about timing, while delegating Rachel to perform baseline observations and analgesia for Daniel.

explanation:
A, E and H are the strongest actions because they combine effective communication, safety and appropriate use of the wider team. A supports Daniel’s welfare and uses de-escalation with clear boundaries for his father, which is essential in ED and maintains a therapeutic relationship. E addresses the system pressure: CT access depends on a correct request, so completing it promptly and coordinating with Elena is both safe and efficient, while delegating observations/analgesia to Rachel is appropriate teamwork.

H recognises limits of competence around paediatric consent and potential safeguarding issues. Involving the paediatric registrar can support communication and ensure Daniel’s best interests, while you keep Dr Chen informed with a structured plan.

B is unsafe and breaches process; imaging without a valid request risks errors and governance issues. C may be premature—seeking senior advice is appropriate, but asking Dr Chen to come in solely due to a difficult relative does not use available supports first. D may miss time-critical CT access and delays Daniel’s pathway; Mr Petrov needs review, but you should coordinate rather than abandon the CT window. F and G are disproportionate and likely to escalate conflict; security involvement is a last resort after attempts to de-escalate and if there is immediate risk.
learningPoints: Use de-escalation and set boundaries with aggressive relatives while keeping the child’s welfare central.
Coordinate across departments; correct documentation enables safe, timely imaging.
Recognise limits of competence in paediatric consent and involve appropriate teams (paediatrics/seniors).
Prioritise competing urgencies using clinical risk and time-critical system constraints.

scenario: You are a DCT in a sedation clinic providing inhalation sedation for anxious adults. Mrs Thompson is booked for extraction of UL5 under nitrous oxide. On arrival she admits she took diazepam from a friend “to take the edge off,” because she was terrified. Lucy (dental nurse) is worried because the pre-op instructions said no sedatives and she has already set up the sedation machine. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is running a GA list and has asked not to be interrupted unless urgent. Maria (therapist) is waiting to use the surgery next and is frustrated about delays. Mrs Thompson insists she feels fine and begs you not to cancel because she has taken time off work and arranged childcare. You are concerned about capacity, safety of combined sedatives, and whether proceeding would breach clinic policy.

options.A: Proceed with inhalation sedation as planned because diazepam is commonly used for anxiety, and monitor Mrs Thompson more closely to compensate.
options.B: Explain to Mrs Thompson that taking unprescribed diazepam changes the risk profile and you cannot proceed safely today; offer alternative pain control and rebook with clear written instructions, documenting the discussion.
options.C: Ask Lucy to keep Mrs Thompson in the waiting area while you start Maria’s patient to avoid losing the surgery slot, then return later to decide about sedation.
options.D: Contact Dr Ahmed (or the on-call sedation lead) for advice, summarise the situation and clinic policy, and agree whether cancellation and rescheduling is required, documenting the senior input.
options.E: Ask Mrs Thompson to sign a form stating she took diazepam against advice and accepts the risks, then proceed with treatment to avoid a complaint.

explanation:
B is most appropriate: Mrs Thompson’s disclosure of unprescribed diazepam creates a significant safety concern with inhalation sedation, and proceeding could breach policy and compromise capacity and consent. A clear explanation, alternative pain management, and rebooking protects her and maintains trust. D is next best because it recognises limits of competence and supports defensible decision-making; involving Dr Ahmed (or the sedation lead) proportionately ensures alignment with local governance.

C is less appropriate: managing clinic flow matters, but leaving Mrs Thompson waiting without a clear plan may increase distress and is unfair; if used, it should only be a brief holding action while you seek senior advice.

E is poor practice: a signature does not replace safe care or valid consent, and it shifts responsibility inappropriately onto the patient. A is least appropriate because it normalises unsafe practice—combining unknown-dose benzodiazepines with sedation increases risk, and “monitoring more closely” cannot mitigate an avoidable hazard.

Overall, patient safety and adherence to sedation standards override time pressure and potential complaints, while good communication with Lucy and Maria helps maintain team functioning.
learningPoints: Do not proceed with sedation when pre-assessment conditions are breached; reassess risk and follow local policy.
Recognise limits of competence and seek senior/lead clinician input for sedation safety decisions.
A patient’s signature does not legitimise unsafe practice; consent must be informed and care must be appropriate.
Manage service pressures transparently while prioritising patient safety and clear communication with the team.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan has been booked for an urgent extraction of LR6 but the triage note is unclear about anticoagulants. Mr Khan is visibly anxious and says he “takes a blood thinner” but cannot name it; he is also fasting because he thought he was having sedation. The waiting room is full and Nadia (receptionist) tells you two patients have already complained about delays. Dr Lewis (registrar) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Mr Khan insists the tooth is “ruining his life” and asks you to “just get it out today,” but he also refuses to let Sarah call his GP because he is worried about “privacy.” You have a partially completed medical history form with no signature, and the previous clinician has not documented vital signs.

options.A: Offer Mr Khan antibiotics and strong analgesia and rebook the extraction for another day without clarifying the anticoagulant, explaining the clinic is too busy to sort it out now.
options.B: Ask Sarah to obtain baseline observations and, with Mr Khan’s consent, call his community pharmacy to confirm the anticoagulant and last dose before deciding on extraction today.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction using local measures for haemostasis, documenting that Mr Khan reported a ‘blood thinner’ but was unsure which one.
options.D: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot proceed safely without an accurate medical history; offer urgent analgesia, attempt to contact Dr Lewis, and if still unavailable arrange senior review or reschedule with clear instructions.
options.E: Ask Nadia to tell Mr Khan that policy requires GP contact and that if he refuses, he will be removed from today’s list.

explanation: The priority is patient safety and valid consent. Mr Khan’s anticoagulant status is unknown, the medical history is incomplete and unsigned, and there are no documented observations. Proceeding with extraction (C) risks significant bleeding and undermines safe practice, especially without knowing the drug, dose, INR/renal function context, or last dose. The most appropriate step is to gather essential information efficiently: asking Sarah to take observations and, with Mr Khan’s consent, contacting his community pharmacy (B) is practical and proportionate when the GP is hard to reach. In parallel, setting boundaries that you cannot treat safely without accurate information and seeking senior input (D) respects limits of competence and maintains professionalism while offering interim pain control. Offering antibiotics purely due to pressure (A) is less appropriate; it may be clinically unnecessary and contributes to antimicrobial stewardship issues, but it is safer than undertaking an extraction blindly. Using Nadia to issue a punitive ultimatum (E) escalates conflict and may breach patient-centred care; you should explain confidentiality and why information is needed rather than threaten removal. Overall, safety overrides time pressure and complaints about delays.
learningPoints: Do not undertake invasive treatment without a reliable medical history, observations and documented consent.
Use proportionate ways to verify medicines (e.g., pharmacy) when records are unclear, with patient consent.
Patient safety and antimicrobial stewardship outweigh pressure to ‘do something’ quickly.
Escalate appropriately and recognise limits of competence when senior support is not immediately available.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson for multiple extractions under local anaesthetic. Emily (dental nurse) tells you Mrs Thompson’s daughter has phoned twice demanding ‘everything out today’ because transport is arranged. Mrs Thompson arrives with her neighbour, Mr Clarke, who insists on staying in the surgery and answers questions for her. Mrs Thompson has mild learning disability and appears uncertain; she nods along but avoids eye contact. The referral letter from Dr Banerjee (consultant) suggests staged extractions and notes previous distress. There is no easy-read consent form available and the booked interpreter has not arrived, although Mrs Thompson speaks some English. Mark (receptionist) says the clinic is overbooked and there is no spare slot this month. Mrs Thompson asks quietly, “Do I have to do what my daughter says?” while Mr Clarke becomes irritated and says you are “wasting time.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to step out briefly so you can speak with Mrs Thompson alone, explain confidentiality, and check her understanding and wishes without external pressure.
options.B: Proceed with all planned extractions today because transport and clinic capacity are limited, documenting that her daughter requested it.
options.C: Use a teach-back approach with simple language and diagrams, and if she cannot demonstrate understanding, defer irreversible treatment and arrange a capacity assessment/senior review.
options.D: Ask Mark to call Mrs Thompson’s daughter on speakerphone so she can reassure her mother and speed up consent.
options.E: Offer to complete one extraction today as a compromise, even if Mrs Thompson appears unsure, to avoid losing the slot.

explanation: Valid consent requires that Mrs Thompson understands, retains and weighs information and communicates a decision free from coercion. The scenario contains clear external pressure from her daughter and Mr Clarke, plus cues that Mrs Thompson is unsure (“Do I have to do what my daughter says?”). Asking Mr Clarke to step out (A) is appropriate to reduce undue influence, protect confidentiality, and allow a private assessment of her wishes. Using a structured, accessible consent process such as teach-back with simple explanations (C) directly addresses her communication needs; if she cannot demonstrate understanding, deferring irreversible extractions and arranging a formal capacity assessment/senior review is safer and aligns with NHS consent standards. Contacting Dr Banerjee’s team (F) supports continuity and the previously documented staged approach, helping avoid retraumatisation and ensuring reasonable adjustments (e.g., easy-read materials). Proceeding due to transport/overbooking (B, E, H) prioritises service pressures over autonomy and risks treating without valid consent. Speakerphoning her daughter (D) increases coercion. Immediate security removal (G) may be disproportionate; you should first set boundaries calmly and only escalate if safety is threatened. Overall, patient autonomy and safeguarding against coercion outweigh clinic flow pressures.
learningPoints: Consent must be informed, voluntary and based on patient understanding; nodding alone is not sufficient.
Create space to assess the patient’s wishes without family/third-party pressure.
Use reasonable adjustments (simple language, teach-back, easy-read materials) and defer irreversible treatment if capacity/understanding is unclear.
Seek senior input and follow documented care plans to support continuity and reduce distress.

scenario: During an OMFS ward round, you notice Dr Kumar (registrar) is rushing because the team is short-staffed. Rachel (ward nurse) quietly tells you that Dr Kumar has been writing discharge prescriptions for co-amoxiclav for several patients “to be safe,” including Mr Petrov who has a documented penicillin allergy on the electronic record. When you check, the allergy banner is present, but Dr Kumar has already signed the electronic prescription and says, “It’s fine, he’s had it before—don’t undermine me in front of the nurses.” Mr Petrov is due to be discharged within the hour and his family are waiting. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is in clinic and difficult to interrupt. Rachel looks worried and asks what to do because pharmacy has queried the prescription. Dr Kumar becomes defensive and tells you to “just tell pharmacy to dispense it” so the bed can be freed.

options.A: Tell Rachel to hold the discharge and contact pharmacy to stop dispensing until the allergy is clarified, and inform Dr Kumar you are escalating due to immediate patient safety risk.
options.B: Email Dr Ahmed later with a summary of the issue so it can be discussed at the next governance meeting, but allow the discharge to proceed now.
options.C: Ask Mr Petrov directly about his penicillin reaction and, if uncertain or severe, request Dr Kumar amends the prescription; if he refuses, escalate to the on-call consultant/pharmacy policy route.
options.D: Tell pharmacy to dispense the co-amoxiclav because the registrar has signed it and discharge delays will worsen bed pressures.
options.E: Challenge Dr Kumar loudly in the bay so that everyone understands the seriousness of prescribing despite an allergy banner.

explanation: This is an immediate clinical safety and professionalism issue. A documented penicillin allergy with a co‑amoxiclav prescription creates a foreseeable risk of harm; bed pressures and hierarchy do not justify proceeding. The best approach is to verify the allergy history with Mr Petrov and seek prompt correction (C), while being prepared to escalate if Dr Kumar refuses. This respects teamwork while prioritising safety and aligns with prescribing standards and antimicrobial stewardship. Closely following is taking immediate action to prevent dispensing and discharge until clarified (A); it is appropriate to involve Rachel and pharmacy and to be explicit with Dr Kumar that escalation is due to patient risk. Merely emailing Dr Ahmed later (B) is too slow for the immediate risk, but it is still more appropriate than unsafe dispensing because it supports longer-term learning and governance once the acute issue is managed. Publicly challenging Dr Kumar in the bay (E) may damage working relationships and undermine him unnecessarily; concerns should be raised calmly and, where possible, privately—though safety may require firmness. Telling pharmacy to dispense because a registrar signed it (D) is least appropriate and breaches duty of care and professional integrity. Patient safety must come first, with clear documentation and appropriate escalation routes.
learningPoints: Immediate medication safety concerns (e.g., allergy conflicts) require prompt action, even under discharge pressure.
Use a stepwise approach: verify facts, request correction, and escalate if needed via senior/pharmacy policies.
Maintain professionalism by addressing colleague concerns respectfully and, where possible, privately.
Antimicrobial stewardship and safe prescribing are shared responsibilities across the MDT.

scenario: In the radiology department, Elena (radiographer) refuses to take a CBCT you requested for Amira, a 9-year-old referred from the paediatric GA list, because the request form is missing justification and the referral letter is not attached. Chloe (dental nurse) says the theatre list starts in 90 minutes and Dr O’Connor (consultant) expects the scan before finalising the surgical plan. Amira is in the waiting area with her father, who is frustrated and says, “We’ve taken a day off—just do the scan.” Elena becomes curt and says she is “not risking an IR(ME)R breach.” You realise the electronic record has two Amiras with similar details, and the uploaded referral might be in the other chart. Meanwhile, Mark (receptionist) calls to say another child has arrived early and is distressed.

options.A: Apologise to Elena, pause the scan process, and verify Amira’s identifiers and correct record before resubmitting a fully justified request.
options.B: Ask Chloe to tell Dr O’Connor that radiology is being difficult and that the list will have to start late.
options.C: Work with Elena to locate the correct referral/previous imaging in the system, and if needed contact the paediatric GA coordinator to confirm the clinical question and urgency.
options.D: Ask Amira’s father to show photos of any letters on his phone so you can use them as justification for the CBCT.
options.E: Insist Elena proceeds because Dr O’Connor requested it and theatre time is expensive.

explanation: Under IR(ME)R, imaging must be justified, correctly attributed to the right patient, and appropriately authorised. The presence of two similar records creates a serious identification risk, so stopping to verify identifiers and the correct chart (A) is essential and protects Amira from wrong-patient exposure. Collaborating with Elena to locate the correct referral and clarify the clinical question (C) demonstrates teamwork and respects radiology governance rather than framing radiology as obstructive. If justification cannot be completed safely within the time constraints, promptly updating Dr O’Connor and discussing alternatives (G) manages theatre pressures transparently and supports shared decision-making about risk and delay. Options that pressure Elena to proceed (E) or complain immediately (F) undermine a safety culture and are disproportionate. Using the father’s phone photos as justification (D) is unreliable and risks confidentiality and documentation standards. Blaming radiology to the consultant (B) damages interdepartmental relationships and avoids your responsibility to submit a compliant request. Rebooking without timely communication (H) is disrespectful and likely to escalate complaints. The best actions prioritise patient identification, legal governance, and clear communication with both radiology and the surgical team.
learningPoints: Radiation exposure must be justified and correctly linked to the right patient; time pressure does not override IR(ME)R governance.
Work collaboratively with radiology staff to resolve documentation and identification issues.
Escalate and communicate early with the surgical team when imaging cannot be obtained safely in time.
Be transparent with families while maintaining confidentiality and accurate records.

scenario: You are on trauma/on-call overnight. A&E calls about Mr Ibrahim who has a displaced mandibular angle fracture and significant trismus. Angela (A&E nurse) says he is in severe pain and is demanding to leave unless “someone fixes it now.” Lucy (dental nurse) has arrived to assist you, but the on-call registrar Dr Grant is tied up in theatre with a facial laceration case and has asked you to “start the paperwork and keep him calm.” Mr Ibrahim smells of alcohol and keeps interrupting, saying he does not want “any wires” and refuses a CT because he is claustrophobic. His partner is angry and accuses the team of ignoring him. The CT scanner is running behind, and the radiographer Mark says he can squeeze him in only if you confirm the request within 10 minutes.

options.A: Give appropriate analgesia, assess and document Mr Ibrahim’s capacity given possible intoxication, and explain why CT is needed; if he refuses with capacity, document and escalate to Dr Grant for a risk-based plan.
options.B: Agree to manage the fracture yourself and attempt to place intermaxillary fixation to prevent him leaving before senior review.
options.C: Ask Lucy to stay with Mr Ibrahim and his partner to de-escalate while you contact Dr Grant and A&E to coordinate safe timing for CT and review.
options.D: Tell Mark (radiographer) to book the CT anyway and state that consent can be obtained after the scan to avoid losing the slot.
options.E: Discharge Mr Ibrahim with antibiotics and ask him to return to clinic in the morning if he is calmer.

explanation: As a DCT, you must recognise limits of competence and prioritise immediate safety. Providing analgesia, assessing capacity in the context of possible intoxication, and clearly explaining the need for CT (A) is most appropriate. If Mr Ibrahim has capacity and refuses, you must respect autonomy while documenting the discussion and escalating to Dr Grant for a senior, risk-based plan. Using Lucy to help de-escalate and maintain safety while you coordinate with Dr Grant, Angela and radiology (C) supports teamwork and reduces risk of confrontation. Discharging with antibiotics (E) is not ideal for a displaced mandibular fracture and may delay definitive care, but it is safer than attempting procedures beyond competence; it might be considered only if he has capacity, refuses imaging/treatment, and risks are explained with clear safety-netting. Booking CT without consent (D) is inappropriate and may be unlawful unless in an emergency where the patient lacks capacity and imaging is in best interests—conditions not yet established. Attempting intermaxillary fixation yourself (B) is least appropriate: it exceeds competence, risks harm, and could be perceived as coercive. Overall, your role is to stabilise, assess, document, and escalate appropriately.
learningPoints: Recognise and work within your competence; escalate early when definitive trauma management is required.
Assess and document capacity carefully, especially with intoxication or distress.
De-escalation and coordinated team communication can prevent conflict and support safe care.
Consent is required for imaging and treatment unless the patient lacks capacity and it is in their best interests.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan’s triage sheet lists “penicillin—rash as child,” but the electronic record shows “no known drug allergies.” Mr Khan is in pain with a spreading facial swelling and insists he “needs antibiotics now” because he has an important job interview tomorrow. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and has asked you to “keep things moving” as the waiting room is full and Nadia (receptionist) is dealing with several complaints about delays. Mr Khan becomes irritated when you ask more questions, and Sarah quietly says she is uncomfortable dispensing anything until the allergy is clarified. You also note the triage notes are incomplete and the last clinician’s entry is unsigned.

options.A: Ask Sarah to dispense amoxicillin as per the usual swelling protocol, documenting that the electronic record states “NKDA,” to avoid further delay for Nadia and the waiting patients.
options.B: Take a focused allergy history from Mr Khan (including reaction, timing, and any subsequent penicillin exposure), check any available GP summary/previous prescriptions, and if uncertainty remains, discuss an alternative antibiotic plan and urgent senior review with Dr Kumar before prescribing.
options.C: Tell Mr Khan that because the records conflict you will not provide any antibiotics today, and advise him to book with his GDP after the job interview.
options.D: Prescribe metronidazole immediately without further discussion because it avoids penicillin, and ask Sarah to update the allergy section later when the clinic is quieter.
options.E: Explain to Mr Khan that swelling may need operative management, perform an urgent clinical assessment (airway/red flags), and if indicated arrange same-day escalation to OMFS/on-call while documenting the allergy discrepancy and your discussion with Dr Kumar when available.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety and timely escalation of potentially serious infection while working within your competence. Option E is most appropriate because it combines immediate clinical assessment for red flags (airway compromise, systemic signs) with prompt escalation to OMFS/on-call if needed, rather than relying on antibiotics alone. It also addresses documentation by recording the discrepancy and the rationale for decisions.

Option B is next best: clarifying Mr Khan’s penicillin history and checking reliable sources supports safe prescribing and informed consent. Involving Dr Kumar if uncertainty remains is appropriate given the conflicting records and the pressure to “keep moving.”

Option D is less appropriate: choosing metronidazole purely to bypass the allergy issue risks inappropriate therapy (and may be insufficient as monotherapy depending on diagnosis) and delays proper documentation and senior input.

Option C is unsafe and not patient-centred; delaying care for a spreading swelling prioritises convenience over clinical risk.

Option A is least appropriate: asking Sarah to dispense amoxicillin despite uncertainty disregards her concern, risks harm, and undermines professional integrity. NHS standards emphasise accurate allergy documentation, safe prescribing, and escalation when risk is high despite service pressures.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy information must be resolved as far as possible before prescribing; document clearly.
Assess and escalate facial swelling/red-flag infection urgently; antibiotics are not a substitute for definitive management.
Team members’ safety concerns (e.g., Sarah’s) should be taken seriously and acted upon.
Service pressure should not override safe clinical decision-making.

scenario: You are a DCT2 in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Daniel, who insists on answering questions for her. Mrs Thompson has early dementia but usually can consent with support. Today she needs an extraction of a painful upper molar. Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you Mrs Thompson looked frightened in the waiting room and whispered, “Please don’t tell him.” There is no interpreter issue, but Daniel is controlling and becomes defensive when you ask to speak to his mother directly. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is off-site, and the clinic is running late with two domiciliary visits scheduled after lunch. Daniel demands you “just get it done” and threatens to complain to Olivia (receptionist) if you delay. You notice bruising on Mrs Thompson’s forearm; she avoids eye contact and says she “falls a lot.

options.A: Ask Daniel to step into the corridor while you speak with Mrs Thompson alone, explaining it is routine to confirm her wishes and consent; document his response and her answers.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction using Daniel’s agreement as next-of-kin, because Mrs Thompson has dementia and is in pain.
options.C: Call Olivia to witness the interaction and ask her to tell Daniel that complaints will not be accepted if he continues to be difficult.
options.D: Complete a capacity assessment focused on the extraction decision, using simple language and checking understanding/retention, and if capacity is lacking, pause and seek a best-interests discussion with Dr Banerjee or the safeguarding lead.
options.E: Ignore the bruising because it is not your role; focus on the dental problem to avoid delaying the domiciliary visits.

explanation:
A, D and F best balance patient-centred care, consent and safeguarding. Option A is appropriate because Mrs Thompson’s autonomy must be respected; speaking to her alone helps you confirm her wishes and reduces coercion. It also manages the interpersonal dynamic with Daniel in a professional, non-confrontational way.

Option D is essential: dementia does not automatically remove capacity. A decision-specific capacity assessment for the extraction supports valid consent. If capacity is lacking, a best-interests process with senior input (Dr Banerjee) or the safeguarding lead is appropriate, particularly given time pressure and potential coercion.

Option F appropriately responds to the bruising, fearfulness and “Please don’t tell him” statement. Following local safeguarding policy, documenting objective findings, and considering immediate safety are consistent with NHS duty of care.

B is unsafe and unlawful: “next-of-kin” cannot consent for an adult with capacity, and even without capacity you must use best-interests processes. E is a failure to act on safeguarding indicators. G escalates conflict, risks safety, and breaches professional conduct; safeguarding should be handled via policy. H may inappropriately defer definitive care and does not address consent/safeguarding concerns.
learningPoints: Capacity is decision-specific; dementia does not equal lack of capacity.
Obtain consent directly from the patient wherever possible and minimise coercion.
Safeguarding concerns should be acted on using local policy and documented factually.
Time pressure and complaints must not override legal/ethical duties.

scenario: You are a DCT1 on an OMFS ward during a weekend on-call. Mr Petrov is post-op day 1 after drainage of a deep neck space infection. Rachel (ward nurse) calls you because Mr Petrov is refusing IV antibiotics, saying they make him feel sick and he wants to go home. His partner is on speakerphone and is angry, insisting the hospital is “keeping him hostage.” Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in another hospital site and has asked to be contacted only for urgent issues. You review the notes and find the consent form is completed, but the last antibiotic plan is handwritten and partially illegible, and the drug chart has a missed dose overnight. The ward is short-staffed and Rachel is juggling another deteriorating patient.

options.A: Tell Rachel to administer the antibiotics covertly by adding them to fluids, because the infection is serious and Mr Petrov is being unreasonable.
options.B: Speak to Mr Petrov to explore his concerns and symptoms (e.g., nausea), explain the risks of stopping treatment and the benefits/side effects, assess capacity, and involve Rachel to agree an antiemetic and a clear plan; document the discussion and update the drug chart legibly.
options.C: Discharge Mr Petrov against medical advice immediately to reduce conflict on the ward, asking Rachel to complete the paperwork as you are busy.
options.D: Ask Rachel to call Dr O’Connor herself because it is her patient and you do not want to disturb the consultant.
options.E: Review the antibiotic regimen in the notes and electronic system, clarify the intended plan with the on-call registrar if needed, and ensure missed doses are addressed and incidentally documented, while keeping Mr Petrov informed about what you are doing.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it addresses refusal through patient-centred communication: exploring nausea, explaining material risks, checking capacity, and agreeing supportive measures with Rachel (e.g., antiemetic). It also improves safety by documenting and making the prescription legible.

Option E is also important: the illegible plan and missed dose are clinical safety issues. Clarifying the intended regimen via reliable sources and the on-call registrar (rather than guessing) reduces harm and supports the ward team.

Option D is less appropriate: while escalation may be needed, it is your responsibility as the clinician to seek appropriate senior advice; asking Rachel to do it shifts responsibility and undermines teamwork.

Option C is unsafe: discharging a post-op deep neck infection patient to reduce conflict fails duty of care and could lead to serious deterioration.

Option A is least appropriate and unethical: covert administration breaches consent, autonomy and trust, and is not justified here. NHS standards require respecting competent refusals, clear documentation, and collaborative working under pressure.
learningPoints: Respect competent refusals; use clear explanations and symptom management to support shared decisions.
Illegible plans and missed doses are safety risks—clarify and document clearly.
Do not shift clinical responsibility to nursing staff; escalate appropriately yourself.
Covert medication is exceptional and requires strict legal/ethical safeguards.

scenario: You are a DCT2 in a restorative clinic. Dr Chen (consultant) is running behind and asks you to see Mrs Hassan for a crown review. Laura (hygienist) quietly tells you she overheard Dr Grant (specialty dentist) joking in the staff room about Mrs Hassan’s “terrible breath” and showing a photo of her mouth on his phone to Chloe (dental nurse), saying “no one will recognise her.” Mrs Hassan is in the waiting area and seems anxious; her husband is with her and is already frustrated about the delay. When you enter the surgery, Chloe looks uncomfortable and says she didn’t know what to do. Dr Grant is due to treat the next patient and is known to be defensive when challenged.

options.A: Focus on Mrs Hassan’s crown review and ignore the staff-room conversation; raising it will only worsen team morale during a busy clinic.
options.B: After ensuring Mrs Hassan is clinically stable and informed about the delay, speak to Dr Grant privately, explain the concern about patient confidentiality and dignity, and ask him to stop and delete the image; document your actions and escalate to Dr Chen or the clinical lead if needed.
options.C: Ask Chloe to submit an anonymous complaint about Dr Grant so you do not get involved directly.
options.D: Immediately confront Dr Grant in front of Chloe and Laura in the corridor, telling him his behaviour is “disgusting” and that you will report him.
options.E: Check with Mrs Hassan whether she consented to any photos being taken, and if not, explain that you will look into a concern about image use and ensure it is handled according to policy without sharing unnecessary details.

explanation:
B and E are most appropriate because they protect Mrs Hassan while addressing unprofessional conduct proportionately. Option B involves a timely, private conversation with Dr Grant focused on confidentiality and dignity, requesting deletion of the image and stopping the behaviour. It also recognises escalation to Dr Chen/clinical lead if Dr Grant is defensive or the breach is serious—aligning with NHS professionalism and information governance.

Option E is appropriate because consent for clinical photography must be explicit. Checking what Mrs Hassan understands, and reassuring her that concerns will be handled via policy, supports openness without unnecessarily alarming her or breaching staff confidentiality.

Option A is the third best of the set: while ignoring the issue is not ideal, prioritising immediate patient care and avoiding immediate disruption may be reasonable only if followed by prompt action (as in B/E). 

C inappropriately offloads responsibility to Chloe. D escalates conflict and risks undermining teamwork. F risks wider confidentiality breaches and unprofessional discussion on WhatsApp. G may be disproportionate without establishing facts and local processes. H is inappropriate: it involves Laura disclosing hearsay to the patient and could distress Mrs Hassan without a clear plan.
learningPoints: Patient images require explicit consent and must be handled under information governance policies.
Challenge unprofessional behaviour promptly, privately and proportionately; escalate if needed.
Maintain patient dignity and confidentiality even in informal staff settings.
Balance immediate clinical care with the duty to act on professionalism concerns.

scenario: You are a DCT1 allocated to a sedation clinic for the first time. Mrs Singh is booked for extraction of a lower molar under IV sedation. Mark (radiographer) has just phoned to say the OPG image from last week is unavailable due to a system outage, and only a small periapical is on the record. Dr Lewis (registrar) is delayed in traffic and will arrive in 30 minutes. Lucy (dental nurse) tells you the list is already overbooked and the next patient has complex medical needs. Mrs Singh is visibly anxious and says she has arranged childcare and cannot come back. She also mentions she took “a couple of diazepam” this morning because she was so nervous, but it is not documented on the pre-assessment. The practice manager, Mr Lewis, asks if you can “start the cannulation and paperwork” to save time.

options.A: Proceed with cannulation and begin titrating sedation before Dr Lewis arrives so the extraction can start on time, documenting the diazepam use afterwards.
options.B: Confirm exactly what Mrs Singh took (dose/time), check observations, and explain that taking diazepam may change the sedation plan; pause progression and wait for Dr Lewis to review before any sedation is given.
options.C: Ask Lucy to obtain verbal consent for sedation and extraction while you prepare the surgery, as this will save time and Mrs Singh is keen to proceed.
options.D: Contact Mark to see if the OPG can be retrieved or repeated later, and consider whether the existing periapical is sufficient for the planned extraction; discuss imaging adequacy with Dr Lewis before proceeding.
