Mr Clarke is in the chair and seems anxious; he asks you directly whether students “talk about patients online”. Dr Chen (consultant) is running late and the clinic is behind. Receptionist Mark has already moved two patients to next week and is asking for an update. Sophie looks flustered and says the photo didn’t show Mr Clarke’s face and was “only for friends”.

options.A: Ask Sophie to step aside with Emily present, instruct her to delete the post immediately, and inform Dr Chen that a potential confidentiality breach has occurred so it can be managed through the appropriate educational/governance route.
options.B: Tell Mr Clarke that students sometimes share cases online but it is harmless if names aren’t used, and continue with treatment to avoid further delay.
options.C: Offer Mr Clarke an apology that his concerns have been raised, explain confidentiality expectations in the service, and offer to pause treatment or arrange another clinician if he prefers.
options.D: Ignore Emily’s comment for now, finish the crown prep quickly, and address Sophie’s behaviour at the end of the session to avoid disrupting the clinic.
options.E: Ask Mark to tell waiting patients that the clinic is delayed due to an incident, without giving details, and request a short slot to resolve the issue before continuing clinical care.

explanation:
Option A is appropriate because it acts promptly on a potential confidentiality breach, involves a witness (Emily) for clarity, and escalates to Dr Chen via the proper educational/governance pathway rather than informal handling. Immediate deletion reduces ongoing harm, though it does not remove the need for reporting. Option C addresses Mr Clarke’s anxiety honestly and patient-centredly: acknowledging his concern, explaining standards, and offering to pause or change clinician supports trust and consent without revealing unnecessary details. Option E helps manage service pressure appropriately by coordinating with Mark to create time to handle the incident while maintaining confidentiality about the specifics.

Less appropriate actions: B normalises unprofessional behaviour and undermines public trust. D delays addressing an active breach and risks further distress to Mr Clarke. F may humiliate Sophie and escalate, damaging learning and patient confidence. G dismisses Emily’s legitimate concern and harms team culture. H is disproportionate and bypasses local processes; public notices risk breaching confidentiality further. NHS professionalism requires safeguarding patient information, responding to concerns raised by staff, and using fair, supportive escalation routes for learners.
learningPoints: Patient confidentiality applies to social media even without names or faces.
Act promptly to limit harm and escalate through appropriate educational/governance channels.
Respond to patient concerns transparently and offer choice to maintain trust.
Support team members who raise concerns; avoid blame and public confrontation.

scenario: You are the DCT on an oral surgery theatre list for paediatric extractions under GA. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is scrubbed and the list is running late due to an earlier airway issue. Anaesthetist Dr Shaw says the next patient, Tyler (7), is ready. Tyler’s mother, Mrs Hassan, is tearful and says she has just realised the consent form lists “LL6” but she thinks the problem tooth is “upper left”. Theatre nurse Chloe tells you the charting in the notes is messy and the pre-op radiograph is not in the folder. Dr Lewis (registrar) whispers, “It’s always LL6—don’t hold up theatre.” Meanwhile, the recovery area is short-staffed and Chloe is urging you to keep cases moving. Mrs Hassan asks you to promise Tyler will not wake up in pain and says she will complain if anything goes wrong.

options.A: Proceed as planned; reassure Mrs Hassan that the team knows what it’s doing, and rely on Dr Lewis’s experience that it is LL6 to avoid delaying the list.
options.B: Pause the case before incision, confirm tooth identity using available documentation and, if the radiograph cannot be located quickly, request it from radiology or postpone this case until correct-site verification is possible; inform Dr O’Connor immediately.
options.C: Ask Chloe to bring Mrs Hassan out of theatre reception so you can continue, as her anxiety is increasing pressure on staff.
options.D: Complete the WHO surgical safety checklist with the team, explicitly addressing the tooth discrepancy, and ensure the consent form and site match before proceeding.
options.E: Ask Dr Shaw to start anaesthesia now to keep the list moving, and you will resolve the tooth discrepancy while Tyler is asleep.

explanation:
Option D is most appropriate: the WHO surgical safety checklist is designed to prevent wrong-site surgery, and explicitly addressing the LL6 vs “upper left” discrepancy ensures shared team understanding and valid consent. Option B is next because it prioritises patient safety and correct-site verification; if you cannot confirm the correct tooth (missing radiograph, messy charting), postponing and informing Dr O’Connor is justified despite theatre pressure. 

Option C may help reduce immediate distress and allow a calmer discussion, but it must not be used to sideline Mrs Hassan’s legitimate consent concern; it is therefore less appropriate than resolving the discrepancy. Option E is unsafe: inducing anaesthesia before resolving consent/site issues removes Tyler’s ability to be protected by last-minute checks and increases risk of proceeding incorrectly. Option A is least appropriate because it relies on hierarchy and assumptions (“always LL6”), dismisses Mrs Hassan’s concern, and risks wrong-tooth extraction—an avoidable serious incident. NHS standards require that patient safety overrides list efficiency, and that consent and correct-site surgery are robust even under time pressure and interpersonal pressure from Dr Lewis and theatre staff.
learningPoints: Use the WHO surgical safety checklist to address site/procedure discrepancies explicitly.
Do not proceed (or induce anaesthesia) when consent or correct-site verification is uncertain.
Escalate promptly to the consultant when safety concerns arise, regardless of list pressure.
Manage anxious relatives with empathy while maintaining safety-critical processes.

scenario: You are a DCT in an NHS oral surgery clinic. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is running late because theatre overran, and the waiting room is full. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan is booked for an extraction under local anaesthetic but his medical history in the electronic record shows “penicillin allergy” while the referral letter from his GDP mentions “no known drug allergies.” Mr Khan is anxious and says he “just wants it out today” because he has a flight tomorrow. He also mentions he took “some antibiotics from my cousin” last week but cannot recall the name. The receptionist, Nadia, asks if you can “just crack on” because several patients are already complaining about delays. You are unsure whether prophylactic antibiotics were intended, and Dr Ahmed is not yet available to review the case.

options.A: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot proceed until the allergy discrepancy is clarified, ask Sarah to contact his GDP for confirmation, and rebook if you cannot verify today.
options.B: Pause the extraction, take a focused allergy history directly with Mr Khan (reaction type, timing, drug name if possible), check GP summary if accessible, and ask Nadia to bleep Dr Ahmed for a quick decision before proceeding.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction under local anaesthetic as planned because antibiotics are not routinely required, and document that the allergy status is “unclear.”
options.D: Ask Sarah to search previous hospital notes and prescriptions, and if still unclear, proceed but avoid prescribing penicillin post‑op and advise Mr Khan to see his GP about allergies later.
options.E: Tell Nadia to inform the waiting room that the clinic is delayed due to a safety check, and ask her to prioritise calling in the next patient while you deal with Mr Khan in parallel.

explanation:
The most appropriate first action is B: you should stop and clarify the discrepancy by taking a focused allergy history from Mr Khan and checking reliable sources (e.g., accessible GP summary/previous records). Involving Dr Ahmed promptly is proportionate because you are uncertain about the implications and need senior oversight in a pressured clinic. E is also appropriate: asking Nadia to communicate transparently about a safety-related delay helps manage service pressure without compromising care, and allows some flow by seeing another patient if feasible.

A is safe but may be premature; immediately rebooking without attempting same-day verification could disadvantage Mr Khan and may not be necessary if clarification is achievable quickly. D is weaker because proceeding while the allergy status remains unclear normalises unsafe shortcuts; avoiding penicillin later does not address the immediate concern about undocumented antibiotic exposure and reliability of the record. C is least appropriate: proceeding and merely documenting “unclear” fails to meet NHS expectations for patient safety and informed decision-making. Documentation should reflect actions taken to resolve uncertainty, not justify bypassing it. Overall, patient safety and appropriate supervision outweigh clinic efficiency pressures and patient impatience.
learningPoints: Resolve discrepancies in allergy/medical history before treatment; do not rely on a single source.
Use proportionate senior support when uncertainty affects patient safety or consent.
Manage waiting-room pressure through honest communication without compromising safety.
Documentation should evidence safety checks and decision-making, not excuse shortcuts.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson for urgent pain. Emily (dental nurse) notes that Mrs Thompson has moderate dementia and attends with her son, Mr Clarke, who answers most questions. Mrs Thompson repeatedly says, “I don’t want any injections,” but also points to her lower molar and says it “hurts all the time.” The son insists on an extraction today and becomes frustrated when you try to speak directly to his mother. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is in another surgery and the clinic is running behind. There is no interpreter need, but Mrs Thompson’s understanding fluctuates; she sometimes agrees, then minutes later appears unsure.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to wait in reception for a few minutes so you can assess Mrs Thompson’s wishes privately, then invite him back to support her if she agrees.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction using local anaesthetic after obtaining consent from Mr Clarke as next of kin because Mrs Thompson has dementia.
options.C: Offer immediate non-invasive pain management options (analgesia advice, temporary dressing if appropriate) and arrange a short review slot with Dr Kumar to assess capacity and best interests if extraction is still requested.
options.D: Explain to Mr Clarke that his mother’s consent is not required because the tooth is the cause of pain and extraction is clinically indicated.
options.E: Use simple language and visual aids to explain options to Mrs Thompson, check understanding by asking her to repeat back what will happen, and document the capacity/consent discussion.

explanation:
C, E and A best balance patient-centred care with legal/ethical requirements. C prioritises Mrs Thompson’s pain while recognising that fluctuating understanding may affect capacity for an irreversible procedure; involving Dr Kumar for a timely capacity/best-interests discussion is appropriate and proportionate. E supports valid consent by using accessible communication, checking understanding, and documenting the assessment—key NHS expectations when capacity may vary.

A can be appropriate to reduce undue influence and hear Mrs Thompson’s preferences directly, then re-involving Mr Clarke as a supporter. This also helps manage the interpersonal tension.

B and D are incorrect: “next of kin” has no automatic legal authority to consent, and dementia does not equal lack of capacity. F is poor practice because persuasion under time pressure risks coercion and undermines consent. G may be disproportionate; you should seek senior input but not halt all services unnecessarily—arranging a short review is more realistic. H escalates conflict and risks abandoning care; boundaries can be set calmly without punitive discharge. Overall, respect for autonomy, capacity assessment, and least restrictive, reversible options come first.
learningPoints: Dementia does not automatically remove capacity; assess decision-specific capacity and document it.
Valid consent requires the patient’s understanding and freedom from undue influence.
Use least restrictive, reversible measures when consent for irreversible treatment is uncertain.
Involve seniors proportionately and manage relatives’ expectations professionally.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover as a DCT. Rachel (ward nurse) tells you that Dr Lewis (registrar) asked her to “just sign” a consent form for Mr Ibrahim’s incision and drainage because he was busy in clinic. Mr Ibrahim is in pain and wants the procedure done quickly; his sister is on the phone demanding updates and says she will “go to PALS” if there are delays. When you review the notes, the consent form has been signed by Dr Lewis but the risks section is blank, and there is no record of a discussion about alternatives. Rachel feels uncomfortable and asks what to do. Dr Lewis is now in theatre and not easily reachable. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in an MDT meeting. The ward is short-staffed, and you are also due to review another patient with rising observations.

options.A: Ask Rachel to sign as a witness to the consent discussion even though she did not hear it, to avoid delaying Mr Ibrahim’s procedure.
options.B: Speak with Mr Ibrahim yourself to confirm what he understands, explain the procedure, risks and alternatives within your competence, and document the discussion; if he still consents, arrange for an appropriate clinician to complete the consent form properly before proceeding.
options.C: Proceed with preparing Mr Ibrahim for theatre and leave the consent issue for Dr Lewis to correct later, as the procedure is urgent.
options.D: Escalate to Dr O’Connor via the MDT coordinator, stating there is a consent documentation concern that may delay an urgent procedure, and ask for immediate guidance on who should re-consent.
options.E: Tell Mr Ibrahim’s sister that you cannot discuss details without his permission and offer to update her later if he agrees; focus first on addressing the consent issue and the unwell patient.

explanation:
B is most appropriate: consent must be valid and informed, and documentation should reflect a genuine discussion. You can clarify Mr Ibrahim’s understanding and provide information within your competence, but the person undertaking (or appropriately delegated clinician) should ensure the form is completed correctly before an invasive procedure proceeds. D is next: timely escalation to Dr O’Connor (via MDT coordinator) is proportionate given Dr Lewis is unavailable and the issue could delay urgent care; this supports patient safety and governance.

E is appropriate professionalism: you must maintain confidentiality with Mr Ibrahim’s sister unless he consents to information sharing, while still acknowledging her concerns. It also helps you prioritise the competing clinical task of reviewing the deteriorating patient.

C is unsafe: urgency does not justify bypassing consent standards; proceeding with incomplete documentation risks harm and complaint escalation. A is least appropriate and asks Rachel to act dishonestly by witnessing something she did not observe, undermining professional integrity and potentially exposing staff to disciplinary action. Overall, address immediate safety, escalate appropriately, and keep communication clear and lawful.
learningPoints: Consent must be informed, documented, and completed by an appropriate clinician; urgency does not remove this duty.
Do not ask colleagues to sign or witness something they did not observe.
Escalate early when a governance issue risks delaying urgent care.
Maintain confidentiality with relatives unless the patient consents to information sharing.

scenario: In the hospital radiology department, you accompany Dr Fernandes (specialty dentist) for a CBCT request for Sophie, a 12‑year‑old referred for an impacted canine. Elena (radiographer) refuses the scan because the referral form lacks justification and the pregnancy status box is blank; she says the department has been criticised for poor IR(ME)R compliance. Dr Fernandes becomes irritated and says, loudly, that radiology is “blocking patient care,” while Sophie’s mother, Mrs Hassan, looks alarmed and asks if something is being hidden. The clinic list is tight, and you still need imaging today to plan whether Sophie can be added to next week’s theatre list. You notice that the referral also contains an incorrect DOB, raising identity concerns. Mark (radiology receptionist) is asking you to move away from the desk because other patients are waiting.

options.A: Ask Elena to make an exception because Sophie is a child and the scan is needed urgently for theatre planning.
options.B: Step aside with Dr Fernandes, lower the tone, and suggest you both apologise to Elena for the public confrontation while focusing on completing a compliant referral together.
options.C: Check Sophie’s identity with Mrs Hassan using two identifiers, correct the DOB error, and ensure the pregnancy status question is addressed appropriately for age before resubmitting.
options.D: Tell Mrs Hassan that radiology staff are being difficult and that you will complain to their manager after the scan is done.
options.E: Ask Mark to find a quiet space for a brief discussion, then clarify to Mrs Hassan that the delay is due to safety checks required for imaging, and outline the next steps.

explanation:
C, B and E are the most appropriate actions. C addresses a critical safety and governance issue: an incorrect DOB raises patient identification risk, and completing required fields (including pregnancy status where applicable) supports IR(ME)R compliance. Patient safety and correct identification must come before list pressures.

B manages the interpersonal dynamic: stepping aside with Dr Fernandes, de-escalating, and apologising for the public confrontation helps preserve working relationships and models professional behaviour. It also refocuses the team on solving the problem rather than blaming radiology.

E supports patient-centred communication: moving to a quieter space and explaining to Mrs Hassan that the delay is due to mandatory safety checks reduces anxiety and maintains trust, while respecting the busy radiology environment.

A, F and G promote unsafe workarounds and undermine radiology’s regulatory responsibilities. D damages interdepartmental relationships and increases patient distress. H risks inappropriate planning and potential harm by proceeding without the necessary imaging. Overall, collaborate, correct the referral, and communicate transparently.
learningPoints: IR(ME)R compliance and correct patient identification are essential; do not pressure staff to bypass checks.
De-escalate conflict and maintain professional respect across departments.
Explain delays to patients/parents in a calm, factual way focused on safety.
Avoid workarounds that compromise governance or clinical decision-making.

scenario: You are a DCT on evening on-call for trauma. A teenager, Tyler, attends ED with a displaced maxillary incisor after a sports injury. Angela (ED nurse) says the ED is overcrowded and asks if you can “just splint it quickly” so the cubicle can be freed. Tyler is with his father, Mr Petrov, who is angry about the wait and demands immediate treatment. You have placed simple splints before but have not managed this exact injury pattern; the radiograph is ambiguous and Tyler is struggling to cooperate due to pain. Dr Grant (OMFS registrar) is in theatre with a facial fracture and has asked you to “handle dental injuries unless it’s complicated.” You are also aware that sedation support is limited overnight.

options.A: Attempt the repositioning and splint yourself to avoid delay, and call Dr Grant only if it fails.
options.B: Provide immediate analgesia advice and local measures, explain to Mr Petrov and Tyler that you need senior input due to the injury complexity, and request Dr Grant to review when safe to do so.
options.C: Ask Angela to arrange a short pause in cubicle turnover so you can reassess Tyler calmly, obtain a clearer radiograph if needed, and contact Dr Grant with specific questions about management.
options.D: Tell Mr Petrov that if he continues to be aggressive you will discharge Tyler without treatment and document his behaviour.
options.E: Proceed with an improvised splint without repositioning, document that Tyler was uncooperative, and arrange review in the dental clinic tomorrow.

explanation:
C is most appropriate: it addresses immediate system pressure (cubicle turnover) while prioritising safe assessment—pain control, cooperation, and adequate imaging are essential before manipulating a displaced tooth. It also frames a focused escalation to Dr Grant, demonstrating insight into your limits and the need for senior support.

B is also appropriate: providing analgesia and explaining the need for senior review maintains patient-centred care and manages expectations. It avoids unsafe intervention beyond your competence while ensuring Tyler is not abandoned.

E is less appropriate: an improvised splint without proper repositioning may be clinically inadequate and documenting “uncooperative” can appear defensive if pain management and communication were insufficient; however, arranging review is better than unsafe manipulation. A is risky because it prioritises speed over safety and may cause harm if the injury is more complex than you recognise, especially with ambiguous radiographs and limited sedation support. D is least appropriate: while aggression should be addressed, threatening discharge of an injured child escalates conflict and risks neglecting duty of care; boundaries should be set calmly with support from ED staff/security if needed.
learningPoints: Recognise limits of competence and seek senior input when injury complexity or imaging is uncertain.
Prioritise analgesia, cooperation and adequate assessment before irreversible interventions.
Manage pressure from other departments without compromising clinical safety.
Address challenging behaviour professionally; do not use discharge threats as leverage.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Clarke is booked for an urgent extraction of a lower molar. Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you she overheard Mr Clarke mention “a bad reaction to antibiotics” at reception. The electronic record shows a penicillin allergy, but the referral letter from his GDP lists “NKDA”. Mr Clarke is irritated by the delay and says he has already waited two hours; he wants the tooth out now and refuses “more forms”. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is scrubbed in theatre and not immediately reachable. Meanwhile, Emily (another nurse) says the waiting room is escalating and asks you to keep to time. Mr Clarke has mild facial swelling and reports he took amoxicillin last year and “couldn’t breathe properly”, but he is vague and wants to proceed.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction as planned, document “penicillin allergy” as per the electronic record, and ask Sarah to monitor Mr Clarke closely during the procedure.
options.B: Pause the procedure and take a focused allergy history from Mr Clarke, clarifying the reaction, timing and any treatment received, and update the record before deciding on local anaesthetic and any antibiotics.
options.C: Ask Emily to call theatre and interrupt Dr Kumar mid-list to come immediately, explaining you will not treat Mr Clarke without direct senior review.
options.D: Explain to Mr Clarke that the conflicting allergy information affects safety; offer analgesia and a short rebooked slot later today once you have verified details with his GDP or GP, and ask Sarah to help contact them.
options.E: Tell Mr Clarke that refusing further questions means you cannot treat him; ask reception to discharge him from the clinic and advise him to see his GDP.

explanation: The most appropriate first step is to pause and obtain a clear, patient-specific allergy history (B). Mr Clarke reports possible anaphylaxis (“couldn’t breathe”), and conflicting documentation (electronic record vs GDP letter) makes it unsafe to proceed without clarification. This supports patient safety and valid consent: he must understand why questions are necessary, even under time pressure. If uncertainty remains, offering interim pain control and arranging verification/rebooking (D) is proportionate and maintains care while reducing risk; involving Sarah to contact the GDP/GP uses the team effectively. Escalating to Dr Kumar (C) may be needed if the history suggests high risk or if you feel out of your depth, but interrupting theatre immediately is not the first response when you can gather key information yourself. Proceeding while merely ‘monitoring’ (A) is unsafe; monitoring does not mitigate a potentially severe allergy-related risk and undermines professional standards. Discharging Mr Clarke for being frustrated (E) is least appropriate: it risks abandonment and ignores his urgent need; you should de-escalate and offer safe alternatives rather than punitive refusal.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy documentation must be resolved before treatment or prescribing.
Valid consent requires the patient to understand the reasons for safety checks, even under time pressure.
Use the dental team to obtain collateral information and manage clinic flow without compromising safety.
Escalate to seniors appropriately when risk is high or beyond your competence.

scenario: You are in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson, who has moderate learning disability, attends with her brother, Mr Clarke, for multiple restorations. Laura (dental therapist) has already spent time building rapport and notes Mrs Thompson becomes withdrawn when her brother answers for her. Today Mr Clarke insists you “just get on with it” and asks you to sedate her because he has taken time off work. Mrs Thompson nods occasionally but avoids eye contact and says quietly, “I don’t like needles.” Chloe (dental nurse) tells you the clinic is running late and the next patient is a child with autism who struggles with waiting. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in another building and available by phone.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to remain in the room because he knows Mrs Thompson best, and proceed with local anaesthetic after getting his agreement that it is necessary.
options.B: Speak directly to Mrs Thompson using simple language and visual aids, check her understanding and preferences about today’s treatment, and document your capacity assessment and consent discussion.
options.C: Ask Chloe to arrange a brief private conversation with Mrs Thompson (with an advocate or chaperone present) to explore whether she feels pressured and what support she wants during treatment.
options.D: Agree to provide sedation today to avoid distress and delay, as Mr Clarke is requesting it and the clinic is already running late.
options.E: Tell Mr Clarke firmly that he is being obstructive and ask reception to remove him from the premises immediately.

explanation: The best actions prioritise Mrs Thompson’s autonomy and safety while managing time pressure. Speaking directly to her in an accessible way and documenting capacity/consent (B) is essential: consent must be the patient’s, not her brother’s, and a capacity assessment is decision-specific. Creating space for a brief private discussion with appropriate support (C) helps explore whether Mr Clarke is unduly influencing her and allows Mrs Thompson to express preferences about needles, breaks, or coping strategies; Chloe can help organise this while maintaining dignity. Seeking senior advice from Dr Banerjee (F) is appropriate because the situation involves potential coercion, capacity uncertainty and possible need for advocacy (e.g., IMCA) before irreversible treatment. Options A and D wrongly substitute Mr Clarke’s wishes for Mrs Thompson’s consent and risk unlawful treatment. E is disproportionate and escalatory; you should manage behaviour professionally unless there is immediate risk. G compromises clinical safety and consent by changing treatment to fit time. H risks abandoning care; complexity is a reason to slow down and seek support, not to discharge without a plan.
learningPoints: Capacity is decision-specific and consent must come from the patient wherever possible.
Be alert to undue influence from relatives and create opportunities for the patient to speak privately.
Use accessible communication tools and document capacity/consent clearly.
Escalate early to seniors/advocacy pathways when best-interest decisions may be required.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover as a DCT. Dr Grant (registrar) asks you to “quickly” write up discharge medications for Mr Ibrahim after incision and drainage of a dental abscess. Mr Ibrahim is keen to leave; his partner is on the phone complaining about parking costs and says she will “make a formal complaint” if he is delayed. Angela (ward nurse) notes the patient has a red ‘allergy’ wristband but the drug chart is incomplete. The EPR shows a previous admission with “reaction to co-amoxiclav—rash”, but today’s clerking states “no known allergies”. Dr Grant is rushing to clinic and says, “Just prescribe co-amoxiclav like we always do; I’ll sign later.” You are also due in a restorative clinic in 15 minutes.

options.A: Prescribe co-amoxiclav as requested to avoid delaying discharge, and ask Angela to observe for any reaction on the ward before Mr Ibrahim leaves.
options.B: Tell Dr Grant you cannot prescribe until the allergy status is clarified; take a brief allergy history from Mr Ibrahim and reconcile it with the EPR and wristband before choosing an antibiotic.
options.C: Ask Angela to remove the allergy wristband since the latest clerking says “NKDA”, so the discharge can proceed smoothly.
options.D: Contact the on-call pharmacist (Mr Evans) to confirm appropriate antibiotic choice given the possible co-amoxiclav reaction, and document the discussion in the notes.
options.E: Refuse to do any discharge paperwork because Dr Grant is responsible, and leave immediately for your restorative clinic.

explanation: Clarifying allergy status before prescribing is the most appropriate action (B). Mr Ibrahim has conflicting records and a red wristband; prescribing co-amoxiclav without checking risks harm and breaches patient-safety standards. Taking a focused history and reconciling sources demonstrates professional accountability and supports safe discharge. Involving Mr Evans, the pharmacist (D), is a strong next step if uncertainty remains or alternative antibiotics are needed; documenting advice supports governance. Prescribing first and ‘monitoring’ (A) is unsafe—Mr Ibrahim is leaving, and observation is not a mitigation for a potentially avoidable adverse drug reaction. Refusing all discharge tasks and leaving (E) is unhelpful; while Dr Grant’s pressure is inappropriate, you still have a duty to ensure safe handover and can escalate or negotiate timing rather than abandon the process. Asking Angela to remove the wristband (C) is least appropriate: it undermines a safety control and could propagate error. Overall, you should resist hierarchy pressure politely, act within competence, and prioritise safe prescribing over clinic timetables.
learningPoints: You are responsible for prescriptions you write; do not prescribe under pressure without adequate checks.
Reconcile conflicting allergy information using patient history and records before discharge.
Use pharmacists and the wider MDT to support safe medication decisions.
Safety systems (e.g., allergy wristbands) should not be bypassed for convenience.

scenario: You are a DCT in the radiology department supporting an oral surgery clinic. Mrs Khan has attended for a CBCT requested by Dr Chen (consultant) to plan removal of an impacted canine close to the incisive canal. Elena (radiographer) refuses to proceed because the referral form lacks a recorded justification and pregnancy status; she says the department has recently been audited and “we’re not taking risks.” Mrs Khan is upset and says she has taken unpaid leave and travelled two hours; she demands the scan today. Mark (receptionist) from oral surgery calls, frustrated, saying Dr Chen is in clinic and “expects you to sort it” because the operating list is being planned this afternoon. You notice the referral includes clinical notes but the ‘IR(ME)R justification’ section is blank. There is also a language barrier; Mrs Khan’s English is limited and her husband is trying to translate.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed with the CBCT based on the clinical notes, and you will complete the justification section afterwards so Mrs Khan is not inconvenienced.
options.B: Contact Dr Chen (or his registrar Dr Lewis) to obtain and document a clear IR(ME)R justification and pregnancy status, and ensure the referral is completed before imaging.
options.C: Use the hospital telephone interpreting service to explain to Mrs Khan why the scan cannot proceed until specific safety questions are answered, and to check pregnancy status directly with her.
options.D: Ask Mrs Khan’s husband to sign a written statement confirming she is not pregnant and understands the radiation risks, then proceed with the scan.
options.E: Escalate to the radiology superintendent immediately and complain that Elena is being obstructive, asking them to overrule her decision.

explanation: IR(ME)R compliance and patient safety are non-negotiable. The most appropriate actions are to obtain proper justification and pregnancy status from the referrer and document it (B), ensuring Elena can legally undertake the exposure. Because Mrs Khan has limited English, using a professional interpreter (C) is essential for informed discussion and to ask pregnancy questions directly; relying on her husband risks inaccuracies and undermines confidentiality and consent. Coordinating with Mark to secure an alternative slot and update the oral surgery team (F) manages competing priorities—patient inconvenience and time-critical planning—while maintaining interdepartmental teamwork. Asking Elena to scan first and ‘fix paperwork later’ (A) breaches governance and puts Elena at professional risk. Having the husband sign (D) is not a valid substitute for proper clinical assessment and interpretation. Escalating by complaining about Elena (E) is adversarial; she is raising a legitimate safety concern. Sending Mrs Khan away without support (G) is poor patient-centred care. Substituting an OPG without consultant agreement (H) is unsafe and may compromise treatment planning.
learningPoints: Radiation exposures require documented IR(ME)R justification and pregnancy checks before imaging.
Use professional interpreters for consent and safety-critical questions; avoid relying on family members.
Maintain constructive interdepartmental communication to resolve governance issues without blame.
Offer practical alternatives and keep patients informed when delays occur.

scenario: You are the DCT on-call for trauma. Tyler, a 9-year-old, arrives with his mother, Mrs Singh, after a fall at school. He has a fractured upper incisor with a deep enamel–dentine fracture and is very distressed, repeatedly pulling away. Lucy (dental nurse) says the paediatric GA list is full and the ED is asking you to “do something quickly” because cubicles are needed. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in theatre and has said earlier to “use your judgement” unless it’s an emergency. Mrs Singh is anxious and insists Tyler must have the tooth “fixed today” for school photos tomorrow; she also refuses any restraint and is worried about “strong medicines”. You have limited experience managing paediatric trauma and conscious sedation.

options.A: Attempt definitive restoration immediately using local anaesthetic despite Tyler’s distress, so ED flow improves and Mrs Singh is satisfied.
options.B: Provide immediate analgesia advice and place a temporary protective dressing if Tyler will tolerate it, while explaining to Mrs Singh the limits of what can be safely done today.
options.C: Call Dr O’Connor in theatre to discuss the case and agree a plan for urgent paediatric review, including whether sedation/GA assessment is needed.
options.D: Ask Lucy to help hold Tyler’s hands firmly while you place the dressing, as this will be quicker than arranging further support.
options.E: Arrange an urgent follow-up in the paediatric dental trauma clinic and give clear written safety-netting, including signs of pulp exposure/infection and how to access out-of-hours care.

explanation: Given your limited experience with paediatric trauma management and sedation, early senior input is most appropriate (C). Dr O’Connor can help determine urgency, need for sedation/GA assessment, and ensure an appropriate pathway—this protects Tyler’s welfare and supports you working within competence. In the meantime, proportionate pain control and a temporary protective dressing if tolerated (B) addresses immediate discomfort without escalating distress, and it allows you to maintain trust with Mrs Singh through honest explanation. Arranging timely specialist follow-up with robust written safety-netting (E) ensures continuity and manages the competing pressure of ED flow versus safe care. Attempting definitive restoration immediately (A) prioritises speed and parental demand over the child’s ability to cooperate and your competence, risking trauma and compromised consent. Using staff to physically restrain Tyler (D) is least appropriate: it risks harm, escalates distress, and raises ethical and potentially safeguarding concerns; restraint should only occur within clear policy, with trained staff, and when absolutely necessary for immediate safety, not convenience.
learningPoints: Recognise limits of competence and seek senior support early in paediatric trauma cases.
Prioritise the child’s welfare and minimise distress; avoid convenience-based restraint.
Provide interim pain control and temporisation when definitive care is not safely achievable.
Safety-netting and timely follow-up are essential when treatment is deferred.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan is booked for an urgent extraction of LL6 and looks pale and sweaty in the waiting area. The referral letter is brief and the electronic record shows “on warfarin” but no recent INR. Mr Khan says his GP changed his dose last week and he “doesn’t know the number”. Dr Lewis (registrar) is tied up with a facial swelling review and asks you to “just get on with it” to keep the clinic moving. Receptionist Nadia tells you there are three more urgent patients waiting and one is already complaining about delays. Mr Khan is anxious and says he cannot return another day because he is a carer for his wife.

options.A: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan for the next available extraction slot and send him home with advice, without contacting anyone, so the clinic can catch up.
options.B: Tell Dr Lewis you are not proceeding until anticoagulation status is clarified, and ask Sarah to take observations while you contact Mr Khan’s GP/anticoagulation clinic for an urgent INR result and dosing history.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction using local measures only, documenting that Mr Khan reported being on warfarin but was unsure of INR, because delaying would inconvenience him and the waiting patients.
options.D: Ask Sarah to obtain Mr Khan’s consent for extraction while you continue seeing other patients, planning to check INR later if you have time.
options.E: Request Dr Lewis to review Mr Khan immediately and, if unavailable, arrange same-day point-of-care INR or transfer to ED/medical assessment if he appears unwell, while updating Nadia about likely delays.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety: Mr Khan appears unwell and has an unclear, recently adjusted warfarin regimen. Option E is most appropriate because it escalates promptly to Dr Lewis and, if he cannot attend, seeks same-day assessment (point-of-care INR where available) and considers ED/medical review given pallor and sweating. It also manages the operational impact by updating Nadia.

Option B is also strong: it sets a clear safety boundary with Dr Lewis, uses Sarah to take observations, and seeks objective anticoagulation information before an invasive procedure. However, it may be slower than arranging immediate senior review and urgent medical assessment if Mr Khan is systemically unwell.

Option D is less appropriate: delegating consent to Sarah is unacceptable (consent is the clinician’s responsibility) and continuing with other patients before clarifying INR risks unsafe prioritisation.

Option A is poor because it avoids responsibility and fails to assess a potentially unwell patient; rebooking without escalation could miss serious illness.

Option C is least appropriate: proceeding with extraction without INR confirmation after a dose change risks significant bleeding and breaches safe practice and professional standards under pressure from waiting times.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides time pressure and throughput targets.
Anticoagulation status must be confirmed and documented before invasive dental procedures when uncertain.
Consent must be obtained by the treating clinician; it should not be delegated to nursing staff.
Escalate early to senior clinicians and consider medical assessment when patients appear systemically unwell.

scenario: You are in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with her adult son, Daniel, who insists on staying in the room and answers questions for her. Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you she has seen Daniel become abrupt with his mother at reception. Mrs Thompson needs multiple extractions and has mild cognitive impairment; her capacity seems variable. She looks down when Daniel speaks and says she is “fine with whatever he says” but later asks you softly if she can “have a break from him”. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is off-site and only available by phone. The clinic is overbooked and the next patient requires hoist transfer, which the team is preparing for.

options.A: Ask Daniel to step outside for part of the consultation, explaining you need to speak to Mrs Thompson alone to assess her wishes and understanding, and ask Emily to stay with you as a chaperone.
options.B: Proceed with extractions as Daniel requests because he is her next of kin and the clinic schedule cannot accommodate delays.
options.C: Telephone Dr Banerjee for advice on assessing capacity and safeguarding escalation, documenting the discussion and agreed plan.
options.D: Ask Nadia (receptionist) to call security immediately to remove Daniel from the building to protect Mrs Thompson, without speaking to him first.
options.E: Offer Mrs Thompson an independent advocate/interpreter-style support (e.g., liaison with safeguarding lead/IMCA pathway if indicated) and explain her right to be seen alone and to consent or refuse.

explanation:
The scenario raises potential coercion and adult safeguarding concerns alongside variable capacity. The three most appropriate actions are A, C and F.

A is essential: seeing Mrs Thompson alone (with Emily as chaperone) supports confidentiality, reduces undue influence, and allows a proper assessment of her wishes and understanding before consent. C is appropriate because Dr Banerjee is the available senior; discussing capacity assessment and safeguarding pathways ensures you work within your competence and follow local policy. F is crucial: accurate contemporaneous documentation of Emily’s observations, Mrs Thompson’s statements, and your assessment supports patient safety and any safeguarding referral.

E is also helpful, but as a “best3” it is slightly secondary to immediate steps to assess capacity and document/escalate; it may follow once concerns are clarified. B is inappropriate: next of kin cannot consent for an adult with capacity, and time pressure does not justify bypassing valid consent. D is disproportionate without immediate threat. G risks escalating conflict and could compromise Mrs Thompson’s safety. H delays action and may leave Mrs Thompson at risk, breaching safeguarding responsibilities.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s own understanding and freedom from coercion.
Adults with possible impaired capacity should be assessed and supported to decide; involve seniors when unsure.
Safeguarding concerns should be documented and managed via local procedures without unnecessary escalation.
Create opportunities for patients to speak privately, using a chaperone where appropriate.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, day 2 post-op after drainage of a deep neck space infection. He is requesting discharge and is frustrated about being nil by mouth for a planned repeat CT. The notes are incomplete, and the drug chart shows IV antibiotics but the last dose is unsigned. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and has asked to be bleeped only for “urgent issues”. Angela (ward sister) says bed pressures are severe and management wants discharges by noon. Mr Petrov’s wife phones repeatedly, demanding updates and accusing staff of “keeping him in for money”.

options.A: Tell Angela you will discharge Mr Petrov immediately to help bed pressures, and ask Rachel to arrange oral antibiotics with pharmacy later.
options.B: Review Mr Petrov’s observations and airway status, clarify the antibiotic administration with Rachel, and contact Dr Kumar with a concise SBAR update about the unsigned dose and discharge request.
options.C: Provide Mr Petrov’s wife with full clinical details over the phone to reduce complaints and stop repeated calls.
options.D: Ask Rachel to sign the missed antibiotic dose retrospectively so the chart looks complete, then proceed with discharge planning.
options.E: Speak with Mr Petrov directly about the reasons for ongoing inpatient care and CT, check his understanding and concerns, and agree a plan for updates to his wife with his consent.

explanation:
B is most appropriate because it prioritises clinical safety: you assess Mr Petrov’s current status, address the unsigned IV antibiotic dose with Rachel, and escalate to Dr Kumar using a structured SBAR despite theatre pressures. Deep neck infections can deteriorate, so discharge decisions require senior oversight and clear antimicrobial plans.

E is also highly appropriate: it manages the interpersonal tension by acknowledging Mr Petrov’s frustration, explaining the rationale for nil by mouth and repeat CT, and agreeing how and what can be shared with his wife based on his consent—supporting patient-centred care and confidentiality.

A is less appropriate: bed pressures are real, but discharging without confirming stability, imaging plan and antibiotics risks harm. C is poor because sharing full details with his wife without Mr Petrov’s consent breaches confidentiality; you can offer general reassurance and request he speak to her or consent to updates. D is least appropriate: asking Rachel to sign retrospectively is dishonest, undermines documentation integrity, and could mask a medication error; instead, the dose must be clarified and recorded accurately per medicines management standards.
learningPoints: Escalate potential post-op deterioration and antimicrobial issues early, using structured communication.
Bed pressures do not override safe discharge planning and senior review requirements.
Confidentiality applies to family members; agree information sharing with the patient.
Documentation must be accurate; never falsify or retrospectively “tidy” records.

scenario: You are a DCT in a restorative clinic. Laura (hygienist) tells you she overheard Dr Chen (consultant) joking with a dental student about a patient’s “terrible breath” in the corridor. The patient, Mrs Hassan, is in the waiting area and looks upset; she later asks you if staff were “talking about her”. Chloe (dental nurse) says the student looked uncomfortable but didn’t say anything. Dr Chen is usually supportive but is under pressure because the clinic is running 40 minutes late and radiology has rejected two requests this morning.

options.A: Acknowledge Mrs Hassan’s concern, apologise for how it may have sounded, and reassure her that her care will be respectful; offer to discuss any worries privately.
options.B: Confront Dr Chen loudly in the corridor so the team sees you take professionalism seriously.
options.C: Speak to Chloe and Laura to gather exactly what was said and where, then raise the concern with Dr Chen privately and respectfully, focusing on impact on Mrs Hassan and the student.
options.D: Submit an immediate formal complaint about Dr Chen to HR without speaking to him first, because consultants should know better.
options.E: Advise the dental student to ignore it because challenging a consultant could harm their career.

explanation:
A is appropriate because it addresses Mrs Hassan’s immediate distress, maintains her trust, and offers a private space to talk—supporting patient-centred care and respect. You should not confirm unverified details, but you can apologise for the experience and reassure her about professionalism.

C is appropriate because it checks the facts with Laura and Chloe and then raises the issue with Dr Chen privately, focusing on behaviour and impact rather than blame. This aligns with NHS expectations for speaking up and maintaining constructive working relationships.

F is appropriate because the student’s discomfort matters; encouraging reflection and signposting to their educational supervisor supports a learning culture and helps them raise concerns safely.

B risks escalating conflict and undermining team cohesion. D is disproportionate as a first step when the concern may be addressed locally, unless there is repeated or severe misconduct. E normalises unprofessional behaviour and discourages speaking up. G is dishonest and could worsen Mrs Hassan’s trust if she later learns more. H delays action and leaves the patient and student unsupported, and the behaviour unaddressed.
learningPoints: Address patient distress promptly and respectfully to maintain trust.
Raise concerns about colleague behaviour proportionately, privately and with a focus on impact.
Support students and junior staff to speak up through appropriate channels.
Avoid denial, public confrontation or disproportionate escalation unless risk or severity demands it.

scenario: You are the DCT covering an evening trauma/on-call shift. Mark (radiographer) phones to say the CT request for Mr Clarke, who fell and has facial swelling, was rejected because the clinical details are “too vague” and the protocol requested is incorrect. Mr Clarke is in pain and repeatedly asks for strong analgesia; he admits he drank alcohol earlier. Lucy (dental nurse) tells you the ED cubicles are full and an angry relative is demanding you “do something now”. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in a remote MDT meeting and has asked not to be disturbed unless there is airway compromise. You have limited experience with midface fractures and are unsure whether Mr Clarke needs urgent imaging or can be managed conservatively.

options.A: Rewrite the CT request with more specific clinical findings after examining Mr Clarke, then discuss the correct protocol directly with Mark to avoid further rejection.
options.B: Prescribe a strong opioid immediately because Mr Clarke is in pain, without checking observations or alcohol intake, to reduce complaints and calm the relative.
options.C: Bleep Dr O’Connor with a focused summary of your exam findings, concerns, and the radiology rejection, asking for guidance on imaging urgency and analgesia plan.
options.D: Tell Mark the radiology department is being obstructive and insist he accepts the request as originally written because the patient is waiting.
options.E: Assess Mr Clarke urgently for red flags (airway, vision changes, occlusion, neurological status), document clearly, and if any are present escalate to ED senior/anaesthetics while arranging appropriate imaging.

explanation:
E is most appropriate: with facial trauma you must first assess for immediate threats (airway compromise, visual symptoms, neurological deficit, malocclusion) and document findings. If red flags are present, escalation to ED senior/anaesthetics is essential and aligns with patient safety priorities.

C is next: you are unsure about midface fracture management and imaging urgency, so a focused bleep to Dr O’Connor is justified despite his request, because the situation may affect safe decision-making. It also addresses the rejected CT and analgesia planning within your limits of competence.

A is appropriate once you have examined Mr Clarke: improving clinical details and discussing protocol with Mark supports effective cross-department communication and avoids delays. However, it should follow initial safety assessment and senior input if needed.

D is unprofessional and unlikely to help; it strains interdepartment relationships and does not address the inadequate request details. B is least appropriate: prescribing opioids without assessment (including observations and alcohol use) risks harm and breaches safe prescribing standards, especially under pressure from an angry relative.
learningPoints: In trauma, prioritise assessment for red flags and escalation pathways over administrative tasks.
Recognise limits of competence and seek senior guidance when unsure about imaging and management.
Communicate effectively with radiology by providing clear clinical details and correct protocols.
Do not let complaints pressure drive unsafe prescribing decisions.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan is booked for an extraction but his electronic record shows a penicillin allergy and a recent discharge summary mentioning “possible warfarin use”. Mr Khan is in pain and says he “just wants the tooth out today” because he has work later. The waiting room is full and Nadia (receptionist) is asking you to keep to time because two patients have already complained. Dr Lewis (registrar) is in theatre and not immediately reachable. Mr Khan becomes irritated when Sarah asks more questions and says he stopped “a blood thinner” last week but can’t remember the name; he has no yellow book and his GP is closed. You can see an incomplete referral letter with no INR.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction using local anaesthetic, ask Sarah to apply local measures for haemostasis, and document that Mr Khan reports stopping his “blood thinner” last week.
options.B: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan into the next available slot and discharge him with advice to see his GDP, as you cannot confirm his medication history today.
options.C: Explain to Mr Khan that you cannot safely extract without clarifying anticoagulant status; offer appropriate analgesia, attempt urgent medication verification (e.g., call out-of-hours GP/111 or check Summary Care Record if available), and arrange review once confirmed.
options.D: Ask Sarah to contact the on-call registrar switchboard for Dr Lewis while you take a focused history, check the referral, and assess whether temporising treatment (e.g., dressing) could reduce pain today.
options.E: Tell Mr Khan that his attitude is delaying care for everyone, and ask Nadia to move him to the end of the list until he cooperates with questions.

explanation:
The safest immediate priority is to clarify anticoagulant/antiplatelet risk before an extraction. Option D is most appropriate because it addresses time pressure while acting on Sarah’s concern: you gather key information, review documentation, and escalate to Dr Lewis via switchboard rather than waiting passively. Option C follows closely: it clearly explains the safety rationale to Mr Khan, offers analgesia, and uses appropriate systems (e.g., Summary Care Record or urgent verification routes) to confirm medication—supporting informed decision-making and patient-centred care.

Option B is less appropriate because simply rebooking and deflecting to the GDP risks ongoing pain and may be unsafe if the patient is lost to follow-up; however, postponing definitive treatment can be justified if safety cannot be assured. Option A is inappropriate: proceeding based on an unreliable history and incomplete INR exposes Mr Khan to avoidable bleeding risk and undermines clinical safety standards. Option E is least appropriate because it is punitive, escalates conflict, and compromises professionalism; it may deter disclosure and damages rapport, contrary to NHS expectations of respectful communication even under pressure.
learningPoints: Act on team members’ safety concerns and escalate appropriately when supervision is not immediately available.
Do not proceed with invasive treatment when anticoagulant status is unclear; use available systems to verify medicines.
Patient-centred explanations can maintain rapport while prioritising safety over time pressure.
Document decision-making and safety-netting when treatment is deferred or temporised.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson for multiple extractions under IV sedation next week. Emily (dental nurse) tells you Mrs Thompson’s adult son has been calling daily, insisting on details and saying his mother “doesn’t understand anything”. Today Mrs Thompson arrives with her son, who answers most questions. Mrs Thompson is quiet, avoids eye contact, and nods when he speaks. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in a meeting and you are asked to complete consent paperwork now to avoid delaying the list. When you ask about medical history, the son mentions she “sometimes forgets tablets” and asks you to “just sedate her and get it done”. Mrs Thompson briefly says she is “not sure” about sedation but her son interrupts, saying she agreed at home. The clinic is running late and Laura (therapist) is waiting to use the room for another patient.

options.A: Ask the son to step out briefly so you can speak to Mrs Thompson alone, assess her understanding and wishes about sedation, and document the discussion.
options.B: Proceed with completing the consent form based on the son’s assurances to keep the sedation list on track, and ask Emily to witness the signature.
options.C: Explain to the son that you cannot discuss clinical details without Mrs Thompson’s permission, and check with Mrs Thompson whether she consents to him being involved in the conversation.
options.D: Book a longer follow-up appointment and postpone all consent until Dr Banerjee is available, without discussing sedation further today.
options.E: Ask Laura to swap rooms so you can take extra time, then continue with the son present to avoid upsetting him.

explanation:
Valid consent requires that Mrs Thompson understands, retains, and can weigh information, and that her decision is voluntary. The son’s dominance creates a real risk of undue influence. Option A is appropriate because speaking to Mrs Thompson alone helps establish her wishes and capacity, and supports voluntariness; documenting this protects both patient and team. Option C reinforces confidentiality and patient autonomy by checking whether Mrs Thompson wants her son involved before sharing details.

Option F is also appropriate: if Mrs Thompson remains unsure, you should pause and involve appropriate senior support (Dr Banerjee) and consider additional safeguards such as an advocate. This prioritises patient safety and legal/ethical standards over clinic flow.

Options B and H are unsafe: completing consent based on a relative’s assurances, or deferring meaningful consent to the day, risks invalid consent and undermines professional integrity. Option D may be unnecessarily delaying because you can still explore wishes and confidentiality today. Option E prioritises the son’s comfort over Mrs Thompson’s autonomy. Option G is disproportionate unless there is immediate threat; it escalates conflict and may deter engagement.
learningPoints: Consent must be informed, voluntary and given by the patient with capacity; relatives cannot consent on an adult’s behalf.
Manage dominating relatives by offering private discussion and confirming the patient’s preference for involvement.
If capacity or undue influence is uncertain, pause and escalate for senior/advocacy support rather than rushing due to service pressures.
Confidentiality applies even when relatives are assertive; obtain explicit permission before sharing information.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, admitted with facial cellulitis. He is due for a CT scan, but Elena (radiographer) has called to say the request lacks a clear clinical question and the latest eGFR is missing, so radiology may cancel. Mr Petrov is anxious and repeatedly asks when he can eat; he is currently nil by mouth in case theatre is needed. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in clinic and has asked you to “sort the scan” quickly. When you check the notes, the last bloods were taken but not resulted in the system, and the CT request was submitted by a different team overnight with minimal detail. Rachel is frustrated because she has other acutely unwell patients and feels radiology is being “difficult”.

options.A: Call Elena in radiology, provide a clear clinical question (extent/source of infection and airway risk), confirm whether a point-of-care creatinine or recent eGFR can be used, and update Dr Kumar with the plan.
options.B: Ask Rachel to chase the lab results while you write a new CT request with a detailed indication and documented renal function plan, then inform Mr Petrov about the reason for delay and NBM status.
options.C: Tell Elena that the registrar requested the scan and insist it proceeds immediately, as delaying could harm Mr Petrov.
options.D: Cancel the CT request and instead book an outpatient ultrasound to avoid the renal function issue and reduce pressure on radiology.
options.E: Avoid updating Mr Petrov until the scan time is confirmed, to prevent further anxiety and repeated questions.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it coordinates tasks with Rachel, improves the quality of the imaging request, and keeps Mr Petrov informed—supporting teamwork and patient-centred care. It also addresses the system issue (missing results) rather than blaming radiology. Option A is also strong: direct communication with Elena to clarify the clinical question and renal function requirements is efficient and reduces cancellation risk; updating Dr Kumar maintains supervision and shared situational awareness.

Option C is less appropriate: using hierarchy to pressure radiology undermines interdepartmental collaboration and does not solve the safety requirement for renal function documentation. Option D is inappropriate because it changes imaging modality without clinical justification and may delay diagnosis of deep space infection. Option E is least appropriate: withholding updates increases Mr Petrov’s anxiety and erodes trust; patients should receive honest explanations and reassurance, especially when NBM and potential theatre are involved.

Overall, NHS professional standards emphasise respectful cross-team communication, clear documentation, and keeping patients informed while prioritising safety.
learningPoints: Effective interdepartmental communication requires clear clinical questions and complete safety information (e.g., renal function).
Delegate and coordinate tasks with ward staff to manage workload while maintaining accountability.
Do not use hierarchy to bypass safety checks; collaborate to resolve requirements.
Keep anxious patients informed with honest, timely updates and reasons for delays.

scenario: During a restorative clinic in a hospital setting, you are supervising Daniel (final-year dental student) who is assisting with Mrs Singh’s crown preparation review. Dr Chen (consultant) steps out to take an urgent call, asking you to “keep things moving”. Daniel quietly shows you a photo on his phone of Mrs Singh’s pre-op smile that he took “for my portfolio” without mentioning consent. Mrs Singh is in the chair and appears uneasy; she asks Daniel why he is using his phone. Lucy (dental nurse) looks concerned and says hospital policy is strict about images. Daniel insists it’s harmless and that he will delete it later. The clinic is running behind, and Olivia (receptionist) messages that the next patient has arrived early and is already complaining.

options.A: Ask Daniel to stop using his phone immediately, apologise to Mrs Singh, and explain that clinical photographs require explicit consent and approved devices; then inform Dr Chen and follow local incident/reporting policy.
options.B: Tell Daniel to delete the photo in front of you and move on without involving Dr Chen, to avoid disrupting the clinic and damaging Daniel’s confidence.
options.C: Ask Lucy to continue with the appointment while you take Daniel outside to explain confidentiality expectations and check whether any images have been shared or backed up.
options.D: Reassure Mrs Singh that students often take photos and that it is part of training, and continue the review to avoid further delay.
options.E: Offer Mrs Singh the option to pause treatment and speak to Dr Chen, and document her preference and any concerns raised.

explanation:
This scenario involves a potential confidentiality breach and loss of patient trust. Option A is appropriate because it stops the behaviour, addresses Mrs Singh directly with an apology and clear explanation, and escalates to Dr Chen while following local policy (often including incident reporting). This aligns with NHS expectations on information governance and professional integrity.

Option C is also appropriate: taking Daniel aside protects patient dignity, allows you to assess the scope of the breach (sharing/cloud backup), and provides immediate education while Lucy maintains safe chairside support. Option E supports patient-centred care by offering Mrs Singh control over whether to continue and ensuring her concerns are documented.

Option B is insufficient: deletion alone may not remove cloud backups and fails to address governance or learning appropriately. Option D normalises inappropriate behaviour and undermines consent. Option F is disproportionate and may not be lawful or effective; focus should be on governance processes. Option G prioritises paperwork over the patient in the chair; reporting is important but should be balanced with immediate patient care and support. Option H compounds the breach by further transferring the image.
learningPoints: Clinical images require explicit informed consent and must follow local information governance policies and approved devices.
Respond immediately to potential confidentiality breaches: stop the action, be honest with the patient, and escalate appropriately.
Support learners with clear feedback while maintaining patient dignity and trust.
Document discussions and follow incident reporting pathways without abandoning patient care.

scenario: You are the DCT covering a sedation clinic list. Mrs Hassan is booked for a difficult lower molar extraction under IV sedation. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you the referring GDP noted “previous panic attacks” and that Mrs Hassan is asking whether you can “just increase the sedation” if she panics. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is delayed in traffic and the list is already tight. Mrs Hassan becomes tearful in the waiting area and says she has not eaten since yesterday because she is scared of vomiting; she also admits she took an over-the-counter herbal sleep aid last night. Mark (receptionist) says the next patient has arrived and the driver for Mrs Hassan can only wait an hour. Chloe looks worried and asks whether you are comfortable cannulating and starting sedation without the consultant present because “we’ll lose the slot otherwise”.

options.A: Start cannulation and begin titrating sedation yourself to keep the list on time, and ask Chloe to call Dr O’Connor once you have started.
options.B: Explain to Mrs Hassan that sedation levels cannot be guaranteed to remove all anxiety, check fasting status and medication use, and wait for Dr O’Connor before starting sedation given the complexity and your current limits.
options.C: Offer Mrs Hassan the choice to proceed with local anaesthetic only today to avoid delays, and document that sedation was declined due to list pressures.
options.D: Ask Chloe to contact Dr O’Connor and the anaesthetic/sedation lead for advice, while you reassess suitability today (fasting, herbal sedative, panic history) and consider postponing safely if criteria are not met.
options.E: Tell Mark to inform the next patient there will be a delay due to a safety issue, and ask reception to help rebook if postponement is needed.

explanation:
Option D is most appropriate: it recognises your limits and the heightened risk factors (panic history, prolonged fasting, herbal sedative use, and high expectations about “increasing sedation”). Escalating to Dr O’Connor and the sedation lead ensures appropriate supervision and governance, while you reassess suitability against sedation protocols. Option B is also appropriate because it manages expectations, supports informed consent, and prioritises safety by waiting for the consultant for a complex case.

Option E appropriately manages system pressure by communicating delays transparently and arranging rebooking support, rather than allowing time pressure to drive unsafe practice.

Option C is less appropriate: offering local anaesthetic only may be clinically reasonable, but framing it to avoid delays risks coercion and undermines genuine choice; it may also be unrealistic for a highly anxious patient. Option A is least appropriate: initiating IV sedation beyond your competence and without the responsible consultant present breaches safe practice standards and exposes Mrs Hassan to avoidable harm.

Overall, patient safety and working within competence override list efficiency, and clear communication with Chloe, Mark and Mrs Hassan helps maintain trust.
learningPoints: Do not start sedation beyond your competence or without appropriate supervision; escalate early when risk factors are present.
Informed consent includes managing expectations—sedation cannot be promised to eliminate all anxiety.
Assess suitability against sedation protocols (fasting, co-ingestants, mental health history) and postpone if criteria are not met.
Communicate delays and rebooking needs transparently to balance safety with service pressures.

scenario: You are the DCT in an NHS oral surgery clinic. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is delayed in theatre, and Dr Kumar (registrar) is covering two clinics. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan’s notes show a “penicillin allergy” sticker, but today’s referral letter from his GDP requests co‑amoxiclav after a recent flare-up. Mr Khan is in pain and says he “might have had a rash years ago” but isn’t sure. Receptionist Nadia tells you three patients are already waiting and one has complained about delays. Dr Kumar asks you to “just prescribe something sensible” so he can keep the list moving. Mr Khan also insists he needs antibiotics today because he has a flight tomorrow, and he refuses another radiograph because he “had one last week somewhere else,” but no image is on the system.

options.A: Tell Mr Khan you will prescribe co‑amoxiclav as per the GDP letter, document “uncertain allergy,” and ask Sarah to book him for a review next week.
options.B: Explain to Mr Khan that you need to clarify the allergy and assess whether antibiotics are appropriate; perform a focused assessment, check SCR/previous records for allergy details, and if needed discuss an alternative antibiotic plan with Dr Kumar before prescribing.
options.C: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan to another day when Dr Ahmed is present, as you cannot safely manage without consultant input.
options.D: Prescribe metronidazole immediately to avoid penicillin risk, advise Mr Khan to see his GDP after his flight, and move to the next patient to reduce waiting times.
options.E: Proceed to drain the swelling under local anaesthetic without radiographs because Mr Khan is refusing imaging, and avoid prescribing antibiotics altogether.

explanation:
Option B is most appropriate because it prioritises patient safety and antimicrobial stewardship while working within supervision. Mr Khan’s reported penicillin allergy is unclear, and prescribing co‑amoxiclav purely from the GDP letter would be unsafe. Checking available records (e.g., Summary Care Record/allergy documentation) and doing a focused clinical assessment supports an evidence‑based decision about whether antibiotics are indicated at all. Discussing with Dr Kumar acknowledges limits of competence and uses available senior support despite time pressure.

Option E can be appropriate after assessment if there is a fluctuant swelling requiring drainage; however, proceeding without imaging carries risk if diagnosis is uncertain, so it sits below B. Option C is safer than inappropriate prescribing, but rebooking a patient in pain may be avoidable if you can assess and seek registrar advice.

Option D is weaker: prescribing metronidazole “to be safe” is not justified without diagnosis and ignores stewardship, and it defers care for convenience. Option A is least appropriate: prescribing co‑amoxiclav with an unresolved allergy history risks serious harm and undermines professional integrity, even if clinic flow improves.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides time pressure and clinic flow pressures.
Clarify and document drug allergy history before prescribing; use available records where possible.
Antibiotics should be prescribed only when clinically indicated and with appropriate agent selection.
Seek senior input when uncertainty exists, especially when pressured by hierarchy.

scenario: You are working in an NHS community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Thompson attends with her son Daniel (aged 9) for extraction of a carious primary molar under inhalation sedation. Dr Chen (consultant) is running late from a safeguarding meeting. Emily (dental nurse) tells you Daniel has been tearful in the waiting room and keeps asking if “Mum will be cross.” Mrs Thompson is visibly frustrated and says she needs to leave in 20 minutes for work. She asks you to “just get it done quickly” and requests that you do not mention Daniel’s previous missed appointments because “it makes us look bad.” When you try to confirm consent, Mrs Thompson answers for Daniel and dismisses his questions. Daniel whispers to Emily that he sometimes looks after his younger sister at night because “Mum sleeps a lot.” You are concerned about capacity to assent, anxiety management, and a potential safeguarding issue, but you also have a full list and no interpreter is needed.

options.A: Pause the procedure and ask Emily to bring Daniel to a quiet space; speak to Daniel in an age‑appropriate way to check understanding, anxiety, and willingness, while keeping Mrs Thompson informed about delays.
options.B: Proceed with sedation and extraction to meet Mrs Thompson’s time constraint, as delaying will increase Daniel’s anxiety and disrupt the clinic.
options.C: Tell Mrs Thompson you cannot continue unless she leaves the room, and threaten to cancel the appointment if she refuses.
options.D: Document Daniel’s comment to Emily verbatim, and contact the on‑call safeguarding lead for advice while Dr Chen is delayed.
options.E: Avoid raising safeguarding concerns today because it may escalate conflict; instead, book Daniel for a review and mention it to Dr Chen next week.

explanation:
A, D and F best balance patient‑centred care, safeguarding duties and clinical safety. Option A supports valid consent/assent by engaging Daniel directly in an age‑appropriate way and helps manage anxiety ethically rather than rushing due to Mrs Thompson’s pressure. It also uses Emily appropriately and maintains transparency with the parent.

Option F reinforces professional standards: parental consent does not replace the child’s right to be involved. Setting boundaries around time pressure protects Daniel’s welfare and supports a defensible decision if treatment must be deferred.

Option D is appropriate because Daniel’s disclosure about caring for a sibling at night and fear of Mum being “cross,” alongside missed appointments, raises potential safeguarding concerns. Recording verbatim and seeking advice from the safeguarding lead is proportionate while Dr Chen is unavailable.

B and H prioritise throughput over consent and wellbeing. C is unnecessarily confrontational and may escalate, though you may need to manage the environment. E delays action despite a live concern. G is unfair to other patients and risks compromising care quality across the clinic.
learningPoints: Valid consent in paediatrics includes engaging the child and seeking assent where appropriate.
Time pressure from relatives should not compromise consent, anxiety management or safeguarding responsibilities.
Record safeguarding disclosures accurately and seek timely advice from safeguarding leads.
Set professional boundaries while communicating clearly with families.

scenario: You are the DCT on OMFS ward cover. Nurse Angela asks you to review Mrs Hassan, admitted overnight with facial cellulitis. The ward is busy and Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in clinic. Dr Grant (registrar) messages that he is tied up in theatre and says, “Just update the family and write the plan in the notes.” When you arrive, Mrs Hassan’s husband is filming on his phone and demands to know why antibiotics were “delayed,” saying he will post the video online. Mrs Hassan looks embarrassed and says little. Angela quietly tells you the IV cannula tissued earlier and there was a delay getting a new one. The notes are incomplete, and you are unsure exactly when antibiotics were first given. Meanwhile, Sarah (ward clerk) asks you to sign discharge paperwork for another patient to free a bed.

options.A: Ask Mr Hassan to stop filming and explain you cannot discuss clinical details while recording; offer to speak in a private area and involve the nurse in the discussion if Mrs Hassan agrees.
options.B: Give Mr Hassan a detailed timeline of events from memory to calm him down, even though the notes are incomplete, and ask him not to post it online.
options.C: Focus on writing “antibiotics delayed due to cannula issue” in the notes immediately, then leave the family to avoid confrontation.
options.D: Check Mrs Hassan’s current observations and IV access with Angela, confirm antibiotic administration times from drug chart/electronic records, then document accurately before updating the family.
