Balance urgent treatment needs with safeguarding responsibilities and confidentiality.

scenario: You are a DCT in a restorative clinic. Dr Chen (consultant) has asked you to review Mr Petrov, who is booked for root canal treatment on a symptomatic premolar. Emily (dental nurse) tells you the referral letter mentions a history of bisphosphonate use, but the scanned copy is partly illegible. Mr Petrov is frustrated because he has taken time off work, and he says, “My last dentist never asked about all these medications.” Dr Fernandes (registrar) is running late and messages that he can only briefly check your plan between patients. Meanwhile, Olivia (receptionist) informs you that the next patient has arrived early and is complaining about delays.

options.A: Explain to Mr Petrov that incomplete medical information could affect safety, obtain a detailed medication history (including bisphosphonate type and timing), and if unclear, defer irreversible treatment until verified.
options.B: Start the root canal treatment as planned because bisphosphonates mainly affect extractions, and document that the referral letter was illegible.
options.C: Ask Emily to call Mr Petrov’s GDP immediately for clarification while you continue with rubber dam placement and access to avoid losing time.
options.D: Send Mr Petrov back to reception to rebook without discussion, telling Olivia to “fit him in another day” because the referral is inadequate.
options.E: Briefly update Dr Fernandes on the uncertainty, agree a safe interim plan (e.g., pulpotomy/temporary dressing if indicated), and inform Mr Petrov of the rationale and next steps.

explanation:
Option A is most appropriate because it addresses the safety issue raised by Emily and the illegible referral by taking a thorough medication history directly from Mr Petrov. Even if bisphosphonates are most relevant to surgical procedures, incomplete medical information can affect broader planning and consent; deferring irreversible steps until verified is proportionate.

Option E follows closely: it uses available senior support from Dr Fernandes to agree a safe interim approach and communicates transparently with Mr Petrov, maintaining trust while managing time pressure from Olivia and the waiting room.

Option C is less appropriate because it starts operative steps before confirming key information; however, asking Emily to contact the GDP is reasonable if you avoid irreversible treatment until clarified.

Option D is poor: sending Mr Petrov away without explanation is not patient-centred and risks complaint escalation.

Option B is the least appropriate because it dismisses uncertainty and relies on an assumption; documenting illegibility does not mitigate the risk of proceeding without adequate history or informed consent.
learningPoints: Take concerns from nursing staff seriously and address documentation gaps proactively.
Do not start irreversible treatment when key medical history is uncertain.
Use senior input efficiently and communicate transparently with patients under time pressure.
Manage clinic flow without compromising patient-centred care.

scenario: You are a DCT in a community dental service special care clinic. Mrs Hassan attends with her adult son for a denture adjustment. Maria (dental therapist) mentions that last week she saw a photo on Chloe’s (dental nurse) public social media showing a patient in the clinic chair with the caption “Another nightmare appointment.” The patient’s face is partially visible and the clinic logo is in the background. Chloe is working with you today and seems stressed; she tells you she posted it “ages ago” and that it was “just a joke” because the clinic has been short-staffed. Mrs Hassan becomes upset when she overhears Chloe discussing the post with Maria in the corridor and asks you, “Are you talking about me online?” Dr Banerjee (consultant) is off-site, and the clinic manager, Mr Lewis, is in a meeting.

options.A: Reassure Mrs Hassan, apologise for her distress, and explain you will look into her concern without discussing staff details in front of her.
options.B: Tell Mrs Hassan that staff are allowed to post about work as long as names aren’t used, and proceed with the denture adjustment quickly.
options.C: Ask Chloe to delete the post immediately, check whether any patient-identifiable information is present, and advise her to stop discussing it in public areas.
options.D: Escalate the incident to Mr Lewis (clinic manager) or the appropriate lead as soon as possible, documenting what Maria reported and what you observed/heard.
options.E: Publicly challenge Chloe in the waiting room to demonstrate to patients that the team takes confidentiality seriously.

explanation:
A, C and D are the most appropriate actions. A addresses Mrs Hassan’s immediate distress in a patient-centred way, offering an apology for how the situation has made her feel and reassuring her that the concern will be handled, while maintaining appropriate confidentiality about staff matters.

C is appropriate immediate risk management: asking Chloe to delete the post, checking for identifiable details (face visible, clinic logo), and stopping corridor discussions reduces further breaches. It also supports Chloe by giving clear boundaries without humiliating her.

D ensures the issue is handled through proper governance. Escalating to Mr Lewis or the relevant information governance lead and documenting Maria’s report and your observations aligns with NHS standards on confidentiality, professionalism and duty of candour.

B and F minimise the seriousness and risk repeating harm. E is inappropriate because public confrontation is unprofessional and may escalate distress and damage team dynamics. G risks further confidentiality breaches by circulating the image. H may be appropriate if Mrs Hassan wishes, but pushing her to complain is not supportive and bypasses internal safeguarding of confidentiality concerns.
learningPoints: Maintain patient confidentiality and professional boundaries on social media at all times.
Address immediate risk (remove content, stop public discussion) and then escalate via governance pathways.
Respond to patient concerns with empathy, apology for distress, and clear next steps.
Manage colleague performance issues fairly without public shaming.

scenario: You are the DCT covering trauma/on-call overnight. Tyler, a 9-year-old, arrives with his father after a fall. Lucy (dental nurse) notes Tyler is bleeding from a lip laceration and has a displaced upper incisor; he is crying and repeatedly pushes your hands away. The ED doctor, Dr Grant (registrar in OMFS), is tied up with a mandibular fracture and asks you to “just splint it quickly” because the department is overcrowded. Mark (radiographer) says the OPG machine is down and only a lateral oblique is available, which may not answer your question about root fracture. Tyler’s father is angry about waiting and demands sedation “like last time,” but you have limited experience with paediatric dental trauma and no sedation-trained staff on shift.

options.A: Explain to Tyler’s father that you need to prioritise Tyler’s comfort and safety, provide appropriate analgesia/local measures, and request Dr Grant to review before any splinting given your limited paediatric trauma experience.
options.B: Attempt to splint the incisor immediately without imaging to satisfy Dr Grant’s request and reduce crowding in ED.
options.C: Ask Mark to proceed with the best available imaging, document limitations, and use results alongside clinical findings to decide whether urgent intervention is required.
options.D: Agree to provide sedation because Tyler’s father insists it worked previously, and ask Lucy to “help monitor” even though she is not sedation-trained.
options.E: If Tyler cannot cooperate and urgent intervention is needed, arrange transfer/escalation to a setting with appropriate paediatric support (e.g., on-call consultant/paediatric GA pathway), while keeping Tyler comfortable and updating the father.

explanation:
Option A is most appropriate because it recognises your limits of competence and prioritises Tyler’s safety. Under pressure from Dr Grant and Tyler’s father, you should focus on comfort (bleeding control, analgesia) and involve senior review before undertaking a procedure you are not confident to perform.

Option C is next: working with Mark to obtain the best available imaging, while documenting its limitations, supports safe decision-making. You should not delay essential care solely due to imperfect imaging, but you must acknowledge uncertainty.

Option E is appropriate escalation when cooperation is poor and resources are limited. Arranging transfer or senior-led management ensures Tyler receives definitive care in an appropriate environment, while you maintain pain control and communication.

Option B is unsafe: splinting without adequate assessment/imaging and beyond your competence risks harm and poor outcomes, even if it improves ED flow.

Option D is the least appropriate. Providing sedation without trained staff, facilities or governance is a serious patient safety breach and places Tyler at significant risk; parental demand does not justify unsafe practice.
learningPoints: Recognise and act on limits of competence, especially in paediatric trauma and procedural care.
Patient safety outweighs hierarchy pressure and department crowding.
Use available investigations appropriately and document limitations and uncertainty.
Escalate to senior/appropriate setting when resources or cooperation are inadequate; do not provide sedation without trained support.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah, the dental nurse, flags that Mr Khan’s triage sheet lists a “penicillin allergy,” but the electronic record shows “NKDA.” Mr Khan is in severe pain with facial swelling and is demanding antibiotics because he has a flight tonight. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is scrubbed in theatre and has asked you to “crack on” with simple cases to keep the waiting room moving. Receptionist Nadia says two more walk-in emergencies have arrived and the clinic is already running 45 minutes late. Mr Khan becomes angry when you ask about the allergy, saying “it was years ago, just give me something strong.” You are considering prescribing, but you are unsure whether the swelling is odontogenic cellulitis requiring escalation. Sarah looks uncomfortable and quietly says she has seen a previous near-miss with allergy documentation in this clinic.

options.A: Prescribe amoxicillin now to meet Mr Khan’s request and document “allergy unclear” in the notes, then ask Dr Kumar to review later.
options.B: Pause prescribing and take a focused allergy history from Mr Khan (reaction, timing, treatment), checking GP summary if available, while Sarah retrieves any previous clinic letters to clarify the discrepancy.
options.C: Ask Nadia to move Mr Khan to the top of the queue so you can treat him quickly, and reassure him you will prescribe something once you have time to look at the allergy record.
options.D: Phone Dr Kumar in theatre to briefly outline the conflicting allergy information and the facial swelling, and ask whether he wants you to start antibiotics or arrange same-day OMFS assessment.
options.E: Tell Sarah that documentation errors happen, proceed with your best judgement, and ask her not to worry because the clinic is already delayed.

explanation: The safest priority is to clarify the penicillin allergy discrepancy before prescribing. Option B is most appropriate: taking a structured allergy history from Mr Khan and checking corroborating sources (GP summary/previous letters) addresses a high-risk safety issue and responds to Sarah’s concern, aligning with NHS expectations on medicines safety and accurate records. Option D is next: Mr Khan has facial swelling and time pressure, and you are unsure about severity; contacting Dr Kumar for senior input is appropriate escalation and recognises limits of competence, especially with potential cellulitis needing OMFS review. Option C is less appropriate: moving him up may help flow, but it risks implying antibiotics are inevitable and doesn’t address safety first; however, it can be part of managing an escalating situation if paired with proper assessment. Option E undermines an open safety culture and dismisses Sarah’s near-miss experience, damaging teamwork and psychological safety. Option A is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin with unclear allergy information exposes Mr Khan to potential anaphylaxis and is not defensible; documenting “unclear” does not mitigate the risk or meet professional standards for safe prescribing and informed decision-making under pressure.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy documentation must be resolved before prescribing; take a structured allergy history and seek corroboration.
Escalate to a senior when infection severity is uncertain or outside your competence; patient safety overrides time pressure.
Support an open, blame-free safety culture by taking team concerns seriously.
Managing clinic flow is important, but it must not create unsafe shortcuts or implied promises to patients.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson for multiple extractions under local anaesthetic. She has moderate learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Clarke, who is insistent that you “just get them out today” because he has taken time off work. Maria (dental therapist) tells you quietly that Mrs Thompson seemed anxious in the waiting area and kept asking, “Will it hurt?” The referral letter is brief and doesn’t mention capacity assessment. Dr Chen (consultant in special care dentistry) is off-site at an outreach session but is contactable by phone. The clinic is running late and receptionist Olivia has already rebooked two patients due to staff sickness. When you begin explaining the procedure, Mr Clarke repeatedly answers for his sister and becomes irritated when you direct questions to her. Mrs Thompson avoids eye contact and nods to everything.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to wait in reception for part of the discussion so you can assess Mrs Thompson’s understanding and wishes without pressure, while ensuring she feels supported.
options.B: Proceed with extractions because Mrs Thompson is nodding and Mr Clarke is clearly acting in her best interests, documenting that her brother consented.
options.C: Use simple language and visual aids to explain the options (including staged treatment and analgesia), and ask Mrs Thompson to repeat back what will happen to check understanding.
options.D: Tell Olivia to book a double appointment next week and end today’s visit immediately to avoid any consent risk, without further discussion.
options.E: Call Dr Chen to discuss the consent concerns and whether a formal capacity assessment or best-interests process is needed before proceeding today.

explanation: Valid consent requires that Mrs Thompson understands, retains, weighs information and communicates a decision; nodding while her brother answers raises concern about understanding and undue influence. Option A is appropriate because separating Mr Clarke for part of the conversation reduces coercion risk and allows you to hear Mrs Thompson’s wishes, while still being sensitive to support needs. Option C directly supports patient-centred communication: using accessible explanations and a teach-back approach helps confirm understanding and respects autonomy. Option E is also appropriate: Dr Chen is the responsible senior and can advise whether a formal capacity assessment or best-interests decision-making is required before extractions proceed, ensuring decisions are defensible and aligned with NHS and professional standards. In contrast, options B and H are unsafe and unethical: relatives cannot consent on behalf of an adult with capacity, and “next of kin” has no legal authority; documenting this would be misleading. Option D is overly risk-averse and may leave Mrs Thompson in pain without exploring whether consent can be achieved today. Option G promotes inappropriate antibiotic prescribing and does not address the consent issue. Option F can be helpful for support and witnessing, but it is secondary to ensuring Mrs Thompson’s autonomous decision-making and senior input where needed.
learningPoints: Consent must be obtained from the patient whenever possible; relatives cannot consent for an adult with capacity.
Use accessible communication and teach-back to confirm understanding in patients with additional needs.
Address potential coercion by creating space for the patient to speak privately.
Escalate to senior clinicians when capacity or best-interests processes may be required.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to review Mr Petrov, admitted overnight with a mandibular fracture. During your review, Mr Petrov shows you a photo on his phone of a junior doctor, Dr Grant, who took a selfie with him in the resus bay and posted it on a public social media story with the caption “another Friday night face smash.” Mr Petrov is embarrassed and says his friends have already messaged him about it. He asks you to “make them take it down” but also says he doesn’t want “a big investigation.” Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in clinic and has a full list. The ward is short-staffed and Rachel is worried Mr Petrov will refuse surgery if he feels disrespected. Meanwhile, Chloe (dental nurse) tells you the theatre coordinator is asking whether Mr Petrov is fit to go to theatre this afternoon.

options.A: Advise Mr Petrov to message Dr Grant directly and ask him to delete the post, and take no further action unless the patient complains formally.
options.B: Ask Mr Petrov for permission to view the post, explain confidentiality expectations, and reassure him you will act; then inform the on-call registrar Dr Lewis and follow local incident reporting guidance.
options.C: Confront Dr Grant on the ward immediately in front of staff and demand he apologises to Mr Petrov and deletes the post straight away.
options.D: Document Mr Petrov’s concerns in the medical notes, check his immediate wellbeing and willingness to proceed with care, and offer support on how to raise a complaint if he wishes.
options.E: Ignore the issue for now and focus on getting Mr Petrov to theatre on time, as the post is not directly related to clinical care.

explanation: Option B is most appropriate because it is patient-centred and follows professional governance: with Mr Petrov’s consent you verify the concern, explain why the post breaches confidentiality and dignity, and escalate to Dr Lewis while using local reporting processes. This supports NHS expectations on information governance and maintains trust. Option D is also appropriate: documenting the concern, assessing Mr Petrov’s ongoing engagement with treatment, and offering routes for support/complaint respects his autonomy and helps Rachel’s concern about refusal of surgery. Option A is less appropriate: encouraging direct resolution can be part of a proportionate response, but doing nothing else risks minimising a serious professionalism breach and fails to protect other patients. Option C is poorly handled: although deletion is urgent, confronting Dr Grant publicly may escalate conflict, compromise professionalism, and does not ensure appropriate reporting or learning. Option E is least appropriate: ignoring a confidentiality breach prioritises throughput over patient dignity and safety culture; it may worsen Mr Petrov’s distress and undermine consent for theatre. A measured approach that protects the patient and triggers appropriate governance is required even under service pressure.
learningPoints: Social media posts involving patients can breach confidentiality and dignity even without clinical details.
Seek the patient’s consent to review/share information and document concerns appropriately.
Use local escalation and incident reporting to support governance and learning.
Address professionalism concerns promptly but avoid confrontational, public exchanges.

scenario: In the radiology department, you have brought Mrs Hassan from restorative clinic for a CBCT requested to assess an impacted canine. The referral form completed earlier by Dr Banerjee (consultant) is missing justification details and doesn’t state whether pregnancy status was checked. Elena (radiographer) refuses to proceed, saying recent audits have highlighted poor dental referrals and she could be personally accountable. Mrs Hassan is frustrated; she has taken unpaid leave and says she will “complain to PALS” if she has to rebook. Your nurse Emily whispers that Dr Banerjee is in a difficult MDT meeting and “hates being interrupted.” Meanwhile, Dr Fernandes (registrar) texts you asking where you are because the clinic is overbooked and another patient is waiting for you to start impressions. You believe the scan is clinically important, but you also recognise IR(ME)R requirements and the need for respectful cross-department working.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed because Dr Banerjee is the referrer and you will add the missing justification later in the notes.
options.B: Apologise to Elena and Mrs Hassan, then contact Dr Banerjee or Dr Fernandes to provide the required clinical justification and pregnancy status promptly, updating the referral before any exposure.
options.C: Tell Mrs Hassan that radiology are being obstructive and advise her to complain, so the department will be pressured to scan today.
options.D: Offer Mrs Hassan a clear explanation of why the scan cannot proceed without proper information, discuss interim options (rebooked slot, alternative imaging if appropriate), and confirm how you will coordinate with the restorative team to minimise delay.
options.E: Ask Emily to verbally confirm pregnancy status with Mrs Hassan and then instruct Elena to scan, as that should satisfy requirements.

explanation: IR(ME)R and local radiology governance require adequate justification and relevant safety checks (including pregnancy status where applicable) before exposure. Option B is most appropriate because it respects Elena’s professional accountability and resolves the deficiency by obtaining the missing information from Dr Banerjee or Dr Fernandes and updating the referral properly before scanning. Option D is also appropriate: it manages Mrs Hassan’s frustration with a transparent explanation, supports informed engagement, and reduces the risk of complaint escalation by showing a plan to minimise disruption. Option F can be appropriate as a third action if timely resolution is blocked: escalating to the radiology superintendent is reasonable to coordinate a safe, proportionate pathway, but it should not be used to pressure staff into bypassing safeguards. Options A, E, and H attempt to circumvent formal processes and place Elena at risk; verbal checks or informal photos do not replace proper referral documentation. Option C undermines interdepartmental relationships and is unprofessional. Option G damages teamwork and clinic planning by failing to communicate the delay to Dr Fernandes and the restorative team. The best approach is collaborative, documented, and safety-led while actively managing patient expectations.
learningPoints: IR(ME)R compliance requires documented justification and relevant safety checks before imaging.
Respect cross-department roles; radiographers can appropriately refuse incomplete referrals.
Communicate transparently with patients to manage frustration and reduce complaint escalation.
Escalate through appropriate channels to coordinate safe solutions, not to bypass safeguards.

scenario: You are the DCT on a paediatric GA list. Sophie (7) is scheduled for multiple extractions due to caries. Her mother, Mrs Khan, is tearful and says she only agreed because Sophie is in constant pain, but she now wants “just the bad tooth out” and asks whether you can do it under local anaesthetic instead. The consent form was signed at pre-assessment, but today Sophie is distressed and refusing to open her mouth. Lucy (theatre dental nurse) tells you the list is tight because one case overran, and the anaesthetist is asking whether to proceed or cancel to avoid wasting theatre time. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is between theatres and has asked you to start preparing the notes and liaise with the family. You have limited experience discussing GA consent changes on the day. There is tension between avoiding unnecessary GA, respecting parental wishes, and the practical reality of theatre scheduling.

options.A: Explain to Mrs Khan that consent was already signed at pre-assessment, so the plan cannot be changed today, and proceed with GA to avoid wasting theatre time.
options.B: Ask Dr Ahmed to speak with Mrs Khan urgently about her change in decision, outlining options including proceeding, postponing for reassessment, or alternative pain management, and document the discussion clearly.
options.C: Work with Lucy to give Mrs Khan a brief, calm explanation of the planned extractions and why multiple teeth are involved, checking her understanding and whether she still agrees before any anaesthetic is started.
options.D: Suggest to the anaesthetist that you will continue preparing the patient for GA while Mrs Khan decides, because starting the process may reassure her and keep the list moving.
options.E: Tell Mrs Khan that if she cancels today, Sophie will go back to the end of the waiting list, and she should think about the consequences.

explanation: Consent must be ongoing and can be withdrawn at any time; a signed form alone does not make consent valid on the day. Option B is most appropriate because Mrs Khan is reconsidering a significant intervention (GA and multiple extractions) and you have limited experience managing on-the-day changes. Involving Dr Ahmed promptly ensures senior support, appropriate risk explanation, and defensible documentation. Option C is also appropriate: with Lucy present, you can provide a calm, accessible recap and check understanding, which may resolve anxiety and supports patient-centred care before any anaesthetic. Option D is less appropriate: preparing while a decision is unresolved risks perceived coercion and undermines voluntary consent, though keeping the anaesthetist informed and maintaining readiness can be practical if clearly paused before induction. Option E is inappropriate pressure; using waiting-list consequences to influence a decision breaches ethical standards and may invalidate consent. Option A is least appropriate: it prioritises theatre efficiency over autonomy and could lead to proceeding without valid consent, exposing Sophie and the team to significant ethical and governance risk. Balancing list pressures is important, but consent and patient welfare come first.
learningPoints: Consent is a process and can be withdrawn; a signed form does not guarantee valid consent on the day.
Escalate to a senior when a parent changes their decision about GA or when you are outside your experience.
Provide clear, calm explanations and check understanding, especially when families are distressed.
Avoid coercive language linked to waiting lists or service pressures, as it can invalidate consent.

scenario: You are the DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Khan attends with facial swelling and trismus. Sarah (dental nurse) shows you the triage sheet: it lists a penicillin allergy, but the previous ED letter scanned into the record states “no known drug allergies”. Mr Khan is in pain and repeatedly asks for “strong antibiotics now” because he needs to return to work. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and has asked you to keep the clinic moving. Nadia (receptionist) warns that two patients are already complaining about waiting times. Mr Khan’s English is reasonable but he becomes defensive when you ask about past reactions, saying “it’s all on the computer.” You are considering prescribing amoxicillin and arranging urgent drainage, but you are not fully confident the allergy status is correct and you cannot immediately access the GP summary.

options.A: Ask Sarah to call the GP practice for an allergy confirmation while you take a focused allergy history from Mr Khan and check the ED letter details; prescribe a non‑penicillin antibiotic only if the history remains unclear.
options.B: Pause prescribing, explain to Mr Khan why clarification is needed, and phone Dr Kumar in theatre for advice on safe interim management and whether he can review Mr Khan urgently.
options.C: Prescribe amoxicillin based on the ED letter stating no allergies, document the discrepancy, and ask Nadia to book Mr Khan into the next available slot for drainage.
options.D: Ask Nadia to reprint the triage sheet and proceed with amoxicillin if the reprint still shows “penicillin allergy,” as this indicates the system is correct.
options.E: Tell Mr Khan you cannot help until his GP confirms the allergy, ask him to return another day, and move on to the next waiting patient.

explanation:
The safest approach is to treat the allergy discrepancy as a potential patient safety risk. Option A is most appropriate because you actively clarify with a focused history (e.g., rash vs anaphylaxis, timing, previous antibiotics) and involve Sarah in obtaining corroboration, while avoiding penicillin exposure if uncertainty persists. This aligns with NHS expectations of safe prescribing and accurate documentation.

Option B is also appropriate: escalating to Dr Kumar recognises limits of competence and ensures senior input when prescribing risk is high, while still progressing care. It may delay the clinic, but patient safety outweighs flow.

Option C is less appropriate because relying on a scanned ED letter over current triage data risks harm if Mr Khan truly is allergic. Documenting the discrepancy is good, but it does not mitigate the immediate prescribing risk.

Option D is weaker: reprinting does not resolve the underlying uncertainty and could create false reassurance. 

Option E is least appropriate because it inappropriately defers urgent care for a potentially serious infection, prioritising waiting times over safety, and risks deterioration and loss of trust. You should provide timely assessment, analgesia advice, and a safe plan even under pressure.
learningPoints: Treat allergy discrepancies as a patient safety issue and clarify with history and corroboration.
Recognise limits of competence and seek senior support for high‑risk prescribing decisions.
Time pressure and complaints do not justify unsafe shortcuts in prescribing.
Document discrepancies clearly and update records to prevent future harm.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, Mrs Thompson attends with severe dental anxiety and asks for a tooth extraction under IV sedation. Emily (dental nurse) tells you Mrs Thompson arrived without an escort and admits she took diazepam at home “to cope”. Dr Chen (consultant) is running late and the session is overbooked due to a staff sickness. Olivia (receptionist) says the transport service cannot bring Mrs Thompson back if the appointment is cancelled, and Mrs Thompson becomes tearful, saying she has waited months and will “never come back” if you refuse. You have not met her before and her medical history includes obstructive sleep apnoea and obesity; the sedation assessment form is incomplete and there is no recent GP letter.

options.A: Explain to Mrs Thompson that sedation cannot proceed safely today due to lack of escort, self‑medication, and incomplete assessment; offer local anaesthetic options and agree a plan for rescheduling with the correct pre‑sedation requirements.
options.B: Ask Emily to record the diazepam dose and time, then proceed with IV sedation at a reduced dose to avoid wasting the slot, ensuring Mrs Thompson signs the consent form.
options.C: Contact Dr Chen to discuss the risks (OSA, obesity, self‑medication) and ask whether he can review Mrs Thompson before any decision; document the discussion and outcome.
options.D: Ask Olivia to arrange an urgent taxi home and obtain a written statement from Mrs Thompson accepting responsibility for travelling alone after sedation.
options.E: Offer to complete the sedation assessment now, including airway and baseline observations, and request a same‑day GP summary via email before proceeding.

explanation:
The key issues are sedation safety, valid consent, and patient-centred alternatives. A is appropriate because it sets clear boundaries: no IV sedation without an escort and with unplanned benzodiazepine use, especially given Mrs Thompson’s OSA and obesity. It also maintains rapport by offering options and a rescheduled plan.

C is appropriate as it recognises limits of competence and seeks senior input from Dr Chen for a higher-risk sedation case. Documenting the discussion supports professional accountability.

F is appropriate because it offers timely care within a safer modality (local anaesthetic) with supportive measures, provided Mrs Thompson understands and agrees. This respects autonomy while prioritising safety.

B and D are unsafe: reducing dose or obtaining a “responsibility” statement does not mitigate sedation risks or NHS duty of care. E may be helpful, but same-day GP information is unlikely and does not solve the escort/self-medication issues. G delays decision-making and risks poor communication. H is disproportionate and not patient-centred; anxiety should trigger support, not dismissal.
learningPoints: IV sedation requires robust assessment, appropriate escort arrangements, and avoidance of unplanned sedative self‑medication.
High‑risk comorbidities (e.g., OSA, obesity) warrant senior review and cautious planning.
Offer safe alternatives and maintain a therapeutic relationship even when refusing a requested option.
Consent is valid only when the patient understands options and is not impaired by sedatives.

scenario: During an oral surgery clinic, you notice Dr Grant (specialty dentist) dictating a letter about Mr Petrov’s biopsy results in the open waiting area, within earshot of other patients. Lucy (dental nurse) looks uncomfortable and quietly tells you she thinks someone nearby is filming on a phone. Mr Petrov is sitting close by with his adult daughter and appears anxious; he has not yet been reviewed today. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in another room and the clinic is running 40 minutes late. When you approach, Dr Grant says, “It’s fine—everyone does this when we’re behind,” and asks you to “just get the next patient in” so he can catch up.

options.A: Quietly ask Dr Grant to stop dictating in the waiting area and move to a private space; offer to bring Mr Petrov into a room to reduce the risk of disclosure and distress.
options.B: Ignore the situation to avoid conflict, and focus on keeping the clinic moving by calling the next patient in as Dr Grant requested.
options.C: Inform Dr O’Connor as soon as possible that confidential information is being discussed in the waiting area, and document your concern according to local incident reporting guidance if needed.
options.D: Ask Lucy to tell the person filming to delete any recording immediately and threaten to call security if they refuse.
options.E: Approach Mr Petrov and his daughter to apologise for the delay and check whether they have heard any sensitive information, then proceed with the clinic as normal.

explanation:
Confidentiality is a core NHS professional standard and should not be compromised by time pressure. A is most appropriate: it addresses the immediate risk by stopping the disclosure and relocating to a private area, while also supporting Mr Petrov by bringing him into a room.

C is next: informing Dr O’Connor ensures senior oversight and reinforces a culture of safety and professionalism. If there has been a breach or near miss, documenting and reporting via local processes is appropriate.

E is helpful once the immediate risk is controlled: acknowledging delay and checking Mr Petrov’s concerns is patient-centred and may identify whether a breach occurred, but it should not replace stopping the disclosure.

D is less appropriate because it escalates confrontation and you cannot compel deletion; involving security may be necessary only if there is ongoing risk, but it should be handled calmly and via appropriate channels.

B is least appropriate: ignoring a potential breach prioritises clinic flow over confidentiality and undermines professional integrity and team culture.
learningPoints: Confidential discussions and dictation should occur in private settings, regardless of time pressure.
Challenge unprofessional behaviour respectfully and escalate to seniors when needed.
Support patients who may be distressed by perceived breaches and address concerns promptly.
Use local incident reporting processes for breaches or near misses to improve systems.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Rachel (ward nurse) asks you to rewrite a discharge prescription for Mr Clarke, who had a mandibular fracture fixation. The electronic discharge summary is incomplete and lists “anticoagulant: yes” but does not specify which. Mr Clarke is keen to leave and is arguing on the phone with his partner about childcare. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is in an MDT meeting and has said not to be interrupted unless urgent. Mr Evans (pharmacist) calls to query a proposed ibuprofen prescription because the chart shows a history of peptic ulcer disease, but the admission clerking notes are missing from the scanned record. Rachel says the bed is needed for an emergency admission and asks you to “just sign it so we can get him out.

options.A: Ask Rachel to delay discharge briefly while you speak directly with Mr Clarke to confirm his anticoagulant name/dose, last intake, and ulcer history; cross-check with the medication administration record and GP summary if available.
options.B: Tell Mr Evans you will proceed with ibuprofen because the patient needs analgesia, and ask him to dispense it to avoid delaying discharge.
options.C: Escalate to Dr Banerjee (or the on-call registrar if available) explaining the specific safety uncertainties (anticoagulant, ulcer history) and request a quick decision on analgesia and VTE/bleeding risk before signing.
options.D: Remove ibuprofen from the prescription and provide paracetamol only, without further checks, so Mr Clarke can leave immediately.
options.E: Ask Mr Evans to hold dispensing until you clarify the history; agree a safer interim analgesia plan and document the rationale in the discharge summary.

explanation:
A is appropriate because it tackles the key uncertainties using reliable sources: speaking with Mr Clarke, checking the medication administration record, and seeking GP information. This supports safe prescribing and informed discharge.

C is appropriate because the risk profile is potentially high (unknown anticoagulant plus possible peptic ulcer disease). Escalating with clear, specific questions respects Dr Banerjee’s request while recognising that unsafe discharge is urgent. If Dr Banerjee is unavailable, contacting the on-call registrar is appropriate.

E is appropriate teamwork: collaborating with Mr Evans to prevent dispensing until clarified, agreeing safer analgesia, and documenting the rationale reduces harm and supports continuity.

B and G are unsafe because they prioritise speed over safe prescribing and shift responsibility to the patient/GP. D may be overly restrictive without assessment and could leave Mr Clarke undertreated, but it is safer than NSAID exposure; however, it fails to resolve anticoagulant issues. F risks confidentiality and accuracy; family members may be unreliable and consent is unclear. H is inappropriate because discharge documentation and medication accuracy are essential before the patient leaves.
learningPoints: Safe discharge requires accurate medicines reconciliation and clear documentation.
Pressure to free beds should not override prescribing safety or risk assessment.
Escalate early with specific clinical questions when information is incomplete.
Work collaboratively with pharmacists and nursing staff to agree safe interim plans.

scenario: During a prison dental service session, you see Mr Ibrahim for ongoing pain from a heavily carious molar. He asks for “something strong” and insists on antibiotics, saying he cannot sleep. Sanjay (dental therapist) mentions Mr Ibrahim has repeatedly requested opioids from healthcare staff. Chloe (dental nurse) quietly tells you she overheard an officer say Mr Ibrahim is “trying to get meds to trade.” The prison officer remains in the surgery doorway and Mr Ibrahim becomes hostile when you ask the officer to step back, saying you are “taking his side.” Dr Ahmed (consultant) is off-site and only reachable by email today. You feel uneasy: Mr Ibrahim may be manipulating the situation, but he is also in genuine pain and you must maintain confidentiality, safety, and professional boundaries in a constrained environment.

options.A: Explain to Mr Ibrahim that antibiotics are not indicated without signs of spreading infection, offer appropriate dental treatment options, and agree a safe analgesia plan within prison policy; document your clinical findings and rationale clearly.
options.B: Prescribe antibiotics to reduce confrontation and ask the officer to escort Mr Ibrahim out quickly so the session can continue safely.
options.C: Ask the officer to remain present throughout because of Mr Ibrahim’s hostility, and discuss the suspected medication trading openly in front of the officer to set boundaries.
options.D: Arrange definitive dental management (e.g., extirpation or extraction if appropriate) as soon as feasible, and liaise with prison healthcare for monitored analgesia rather than issuing stronger medication yourself.
options.E: Email Dr Ahmed for advice and delay any decision until you receive a response, offering no treatment today to avoid risk.

explanation:
A is most appropriate: it applies evidence-based prescribing and ethical practice—antibiotics should not be used to manage pain alone. Clear explanation to Mr Ibrahim, documentation, and an appropriate analgesia plan support patient-centred care while maintaining professional boundaries.

D is also appropriate because definitive dental treatment addresses the cause of pain and reduces repeat medication requests. Liaising with prison healthcare for monitored analgesia is a proportionate response to concerns about diversion, without making unsubstantiated accusations.

B is less appropriate: prescribing antibiotics to avoid confrontation compromises antimicrobial stewardship and patient safety. It may temporarily defuse tension but reinforces inappropriate expectations.

C is weaker because discussing suspected trading in front of the officer breaches confidentiality and risks escalating hostility. Officer presence may be necessary for safety, but information-sharing should be minimal and justified.

E is least appropriate: withholding care until an email reply neglects pain management and could worsen the condition. You can manage within your competence using guidelines and local policy, escalating through appropriate urgent channels if required.

Overall, balance safety, confidentiality, and appropriate prescribing, recognising the constraints of the prison setting.
learningPoints: Do not prescribe antibiotics for dental pain without clinical indication; explain decisions clearly.
Address the underlying cause with definitive treatment where possible to reduce repeat presentations.
In custodial settings, share information with officers only when necessary for safety and proportionate to the risk.
Document clinical findings and prescribing rationale carefully, especially when diversion is a concern.

scenario: You are a DCT in an oral surgery clinic. Mr Khan is booked for an extraction of LR6. Sarah (dental nurse) quietly tells you the medical history sheet says “penicillin allergy—rash,” but the electronic record shows “no known drug allergies.” Mr Khan is anxious and says he “always gets antibiotics after extractions” and wants to leave quickly because his taxi is waiting. Dr Kumar (specialty dentist) is running late and asks you to “crack on” so the list doesn’t overrun. When you ask Mr Khan about allergies, he shrugs and says his GP “once mentioned something” but he’s not sure. The referral letter is missing from the notes, and the previous clinic entry is brief. You are considering whether antibiotics are indicated and whether local anaesthetic containing adrenaline is appropriate given he also mentions occasional palpitations.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction under local anaesthetic and issue amoxicillin afterwards because Mr Khan expects it, documenting that the allergy status was unclear.
options.B: Pause the procedure, ask Sarah to phone Mr Khan’s GP for clarification on the penicillin allergy and cardiac history, and update Dr Kumar and Nadia that the appointment may need to be rebooked if information cannot be confirmed safely.
options.C: Ask Mr Khan to sign consent immediately, reassure him that antibiotics are routine, and avoid raising the allergy discrepancy to prevent increasing his anxiety.
options.D: Explain to Mr Khan that you need to clarify his allergy and palpitations before proceeding; ask Dr Kumar to review the case urgently while you obtain a focused history and check the electronic record for prior prescriptions.
options.E: Cancel the extraction outright and tell Mr Khan to rebook through his GDP without offering interim pain control advice or escalation options.

explanation:
Patient safety and valid consent come before list pressure. The key issue is the discrepancy in allergy status and the possibility of inappropriate prescribing. Option D is most appropriate: you explain the need to clarify risks to Mr Khan, take a focused history (including what reaction occurred, when, and any prior antibiotics), and involve Dr Kumar promptly because you are being pressured to proceed. This supports safe decision-making and respects Mr Khan’s autonomy by ensuring he understands why delays may be necessary. Option B is also appropriate, especially if uncertainty persists; contacting the GP (or checking summary care/previous prescriptions) and warning Nadia about delays is reasonable, though phoning the GP may not be immediately feasible and should not be the only step.

Option A is unsafe: prescribing amoxicillin with unclear penicillin allergy is high risk, and antibiotics are not routine after uncomplicated extractions. Option C undermines informed consent and is dishonest, breaching professional integrity. Option E is disproportionate and not patient-centred; if postponing is necessary, you should provide analgesia advice, safety-netting, and arrange an appropriate review pathway rather than simply sending Mr Khan away.
learningPoints: Resolve discrepancies in allergy documentation before prescribing or administering treatment.
Valid consent requires patient understanding; do not minimise or misrepresent risks to maintain clinic flow.
Involve senior supervision when uncertainty or pressure could compromise safety.
Antibiotics should be prescribed on clinical indication, not patient expectation.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover. Mrs Thompson, 78, is admitted with facial swelling and trismus from an odontogenic infection. She has mild cognitive impairment but usually lives independently. Her daughter, Olivia, is at the bedside and insists you “do whatever you need” but asks you not to mention the possibility of surgery because it will “panic her.” The consultant, Dr O’Connor, is in theatre; the registrar, Dr Lewis, is in clinic and intermittently reachable. Ward nurse Angela says Mrs Thompson has repeatedly tried to remove her cannula and is refusing IV antibiotics, saying she wants to go home. The notes include a DNACPR form completed last year, but the capacity assessment section is blank. You need to decide whether Mrs Thompson has capacity to refuse treatment, whether to involve the daughter in discussions, and how to manage immediate infection risks while respecting autonomy and confidentiality.

options.A: Assess Mrs Thompson’s capacity for the specific decision about IV antibiotics and CT, documenting your assessment and involving Angela to support communication and orientation.
options.B: Accept Olivia’s request and avoid discussing surgery or risks with Mrs Thompson, focusing only on getting her to agree to the CT quickly.
options.C: Ask Angela to apply physical restraint to keep the cannula in place and proceed with IV antibiotics while you organise the CT.
options.D: Contact Dr Lewis to update on the refusal and DNACPR documentation gap, and request senior input on best-interests planning if capacity is lacking.
options.E: Share full clinical details with Olivia outside the bay without checking Mrs Thompson’s wishes, because Olivia is next of kin.

explanation:
The priority is immediate safety while respecting autonomy, consent and confidentiality. Option A is essential: capacity is decision-specific and time-specific, and the notes are incomplete. A documented assessment (under the Mental Capacity Act principles) supports safe consent for CT and treatment decisions. Option F is also key: Mrs Thompson may be distressed, in pain or delirious; a clear, calm explanation with reasonable adjustments can improve understanding and reduce refusal, aligning with patient-centred care. Option D is appropriate because the situation is high risk (potential airway/rapid spread infection) and ethically complex; early escalation to Dr Lewis for senior input on best-interests decisions is prudent if capacity is lacking.

Options B and E are inappropriate: colluding with Olivia to withhold information undermines informed consent, and “next of kin” does not automatically grant decision-making authority or access to all details without considering Mrs Thompson’s wishes and capacity. Option C risks unlawful restraint; any restriction must be proportionate, necessary and in the patient’s best interests, and should follow local policy. Option G may cause harmful delay. Option H is unsafe given the severity of infection and trismus without a robust plan.
learningPoints: Capacity assessments are decision-specific and must be documented when refusal or consent is in doubt.
Involve seniors early for high-risk infections and potential best-interests decisions.
Family can support care, but confidentiality and informed consent remain central unless capacity is lacking and disclosure is justified.
Use de-escalation and reasonable adjustments before considering restrictive interventions.

scenario: You are in a community dental service special care clinic. Sophie, a 9-year-old, attends with her father for multiple extractions under inhalation sedation planned today. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you Sophie has been coughing in the waiting room and looks flushed. The father insists they have waited months and demands you proceed. The sedation dentist, Dr Chen, is between rooms and asks you to “get them set up” to keep the session moving. Receptionist Mark says another child is already late for the next slot. When you speak to Sophie, she says she feels “wheezy” and admits she used her blue inhaler this morning. The pre-assessment form states asthma is “well controlled,” but there is no recent peak flow recorded. The father becomes irritated and raises his voice at Chloe, saying staff are “making excuses.

options.A: Proceed with sedation as planned because the father is consenting and the clinic schedule cannot accommodate cancellations.
options.B: Ask Dr Chen to review Sophie immediately, obtain a focused respiratory history and observations, and explain to the father that proceeding may be unsafe if Sophie is unwell; offer a rebook and interim advice.
options.C: Tell Mark to book Sophie into the next slot anyway and move her ahead of the late child to avoid confrontation with the father.
options.D: Ask Chloe to continue setting up sedation equipment while you try to calm the father in the corridor, without informing Dr Chen until later.
options.E: Pause the process, move the conversation to a private space, set boundaries about speaking respectfully to Chloe, and ensure Sophie is assessed for fitness for sedation before any consent is finalised.

explanation:
Sophie’s welfare and clinical safety override throughput. She is symptomatic (cough, wheeze, recent reliever use), and the documentation lacks objective measures. Option E is most appropriate: you pause, protect staff by setting clear behavioural boundaries with the father, and ensure Sophie is assessed before proceeding—also supporting a calm environment for a child. Option B is next: involving Dr Chen promptly is essential because sedation fitness and any decision to defer should be made with the sedation clinician; a focused history/observations and clear explanation with a rebook plan is patient-centred.

Option D is less appropriate because it continues preparation without senior awareness and risks momentum toward an unsafe procedure; it also leaves Chloe exposed to ongoing hostility. Option C is unfair to other patients and does not address safety; altering the list to appease an angry parent undermines service integrity. Option A is least appropriate: parental pressure and waiting times do not justify proceeding with sedation when there are red flags about acute respiratory illness and potential increased risk of adverse events.
learningPoints: Child patient safety and fitness for sedation must be confirmed before proceeding, especially with new respiratory symptoms.
Escalate promptly to the sedation clinician/senior when there are concerns about suitability or consent.
Manage aggressive behaviour by setting boundaries and moving discussions to a private, calm space.
Service pressures should not drive unsafe clinical decisions or unfair prioritisation.

scenario: You are covering the emergency dental clinic in a hospital. Mr Petrov attends with severe pain and swelling. English is his second language, and he is using his phone to translate. Emily (dental nurse) notices that a junior colleague, Dr Grant, has already assessed him and typed a note stating “patient consented for I&D,” but Mr Petrov looks confused and keeps asking, “Antibiotic only?” Dr Grant tells you quietly that he is behind and asks you to “just do the incision” because the swelling is worsening. At the same time, radiographer Elena calls to say the OPG request has been rejected because the indication is unclear, and she needs you to clarify urgently. Receptionist Nadia says Mr Petrov is threatening to complain if he is not treated immediately.

options.A: Ask Mr Petrov, using a professional interpreter service if available (or telephone interpreting), to confirm understanding of the proposed I&D, risks, alternatives and aftercare before any procedure, and update the consent/documentation accordingly.
options.B: Proceed with I&D based on Dr Grant’s note to avoid delay, as swelling is worsening and the patient is demanding treatment.
options.C: Speak to Dr Grant privately, explain your concern about the consent note and language barrier, and agree a plan for who will re-consent and document clearly before treatment continues.
options.D: Tell Elena to accept the OPG request as an emergency without further details, because the patient is in pain.
options.E: Ask Emily to witness a brief verbal consent while you prepare instruments, without checking Mr Petrov’s understanding beyond a yes/no response.

explanation:
The central risks are invalid consent due to a language barrier and poor-quality documentation, compounded by time pressure and a colleague urging you to proceed. Option A is crucial: Mr Petrov’s repeated request for “antibiotic only” indicates he may not understand I&D. Using appropriate interpreting support and re-checking understanding protects patient autonomy and reduces the risk of harm and complaints. Option C addresses professionalism and teamwork: you challenge Dr Grant’s request constructively, agree who will re-consent, and ensure the record accurately reflects the discussion—this supports a just culture while prioritising safety. Option F is also appropriate: radiology rejection is a system barrier; clarifying the indication with Elena and documenting it enables timely imaging without bypassing governance.

Option B is unsafe because a typed note is not a substitute for valid consent, especially when the patient appears confused. Option D is inappropriate; pressuring radiology to “accept” without indication undermines interdepartmental working. Option E reduces consent to a tick-box and does not ensure understanding. Option G abdicates your responsibility to communicate with the patient. Option H is dishonest and risks falsifying records.
learningPoints: Consent must be informed and understood; language barriers require appropriate interpreting support.
Challenge colleague pressure professionally and ensure documentation reflects what actually occurred.
Work collaboratively with other departments by providing clear clinical indications rather than bypassing processes.
Accurate, contemporaneous records protect patients and staff.

scenario: You are a DCT in a restorative clinic. Mrs Hassan attends for a difficult root canal treatment on UL6. Laura (hygienist) has just handed over that Mrs Hassan is very anxious and previously complained about “not being listened to.” During the appointment, you struggle to achieve adequate isolation and take longer than planned. Lucy (dental nurse) looks concerned and whispers that the rubber dam clamp keeps slipping. Dr Banerjee (consultant) briefly comes in, observes for two minutes, and says in front of Mrs Hassan, “This is basic—why haven’t you prepared properly?” Mrs Hassan becomes tearful and says she wants to stop. The clinic coordinator, Olivia, messages that the next patient is already waiting and the session is overbooked. You feel embarrassed and defensive but must maintain professionalism, protect patient confidence, and decide how to proceed clinically within your competence.

options.A: Acknowledge Mrs Hassan’s distress, offer to pause, and ask Dr Banerjee for a brief private discussion to agree a safe plan (continue with supervision, temporise, or rebook), then explain the plan to Mrs Hassan in a calm, supportive way.
options.B: Continue the root canal quickly to avoid further criticism and delay, accepting the slipping clamp as manageable and planning to “fix it later.”
options.C: Tell Mrs Hassan that Dr Banerjee’s comment was unfair and apologise for him, then ask her to submit a formal complaint against him.
options.D: Ask Lucy to keep trying different clamps while you step out to message Olivia that you will overrun, without speaking further to Mrs Hassan until you return.
options.E: After the appointment, reflect on Dr Banerjee’s feedback, arrange targeted skills support (e.g., supervised sessions/rubber dam training), and document the learning needs in your portfolio.

explanation:
Option A is most appropriate because it centres Mrs Hassan’s wellbeing and safety. She is tearful and wants to stop; you should pause, acknowledge her anxiety, and ensure any decision to continue is safe and within your competence. A brief private discussion with Dr Banerjee helps manage the interpersonal dynamic and avoids undermining confidence in front of the patient, while still obtaining necessary supervision. Option E is also appropriate: even if feedback was poorly delivered, you have a duty to respond constructively, identify skill gaps (e.g., rubber dam placement), and seek training—supporting patient safety long term.

Option D is less appropriate because leaving without addressing Mrs Hassan’s distress risks abandonment and worsens trust, though informing Olivia about overruns is reasonable once the patient is settled. Option C is unprofessional: it triangulates the patient into a staff conflict and escalates unnecessarily; concerns about Dr Banerjee’s behaviour should be raised through appropriate channels later. Option B is least appropriate: rushing a technically challenging endodontic procedure with inadequate isolation increases risk of iatrogenic harm and compromises quality, driven by embarrassment and time pressure rather than clinical judgement.
learningPoints: Pause and re-establish patient comfort and consent when distress arises during treatment.
Seek senior support and manage feedback professionally, even when delivered inappropriately.
Do not rush complex procedures due to time pressure; prioritise safety and quality.
Use feedback to plan targeted development and document learning appropriately.

scenario: You are a DCT1 in a busy oral surgery clinic. Dr Ahmed (consultant) is in theatre and the registrar, Dr Kumar, is covering two clinics. Sarah (dental nurse) flags that Mr Khan is booked for an extraction and the electronic record shows a penicillin allergy, but the referral letter from GDP says “no known drug allergies”. Mr Khan looks anxious and says he “might have reacted years ago” but isn’t sure. The clinic is already running 40 minutes late and Nadia (receptionist) warns two patients are complaining at the desk. Dr Kumar messages that he can only come in 25 minutes. Mr Khan asks if you can “just get it done today” because he has taken unpaid leave. You are also aware the planned prescription after extraction is amoxicillin if there is infection risk.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction as planned and document that Mr Khan is “unsure” about allergy, then avoid prescribing antibiotics unless infection develops.
options.B: Pause the procedure, take a focused allergy history with Mr Khan, check previous hospital/GP records if available, and ask Sarah to help contact the GP while you update Dr Kumar about the uncertainty.
options.C: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan to a later date because the clinic is running late and you cannot confirm the allergy today.
options.D: Start the extraction now but ask Sarah to prepare emergency drugs in case of an allergic reaction, and plan to discuss antibiotics with Dr Kumar afterwards.
options.E: Tell Mr Khan you will only treat him today if he agrees to take amoxicillin afterwards, as this is the standard post‑extraction plan.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to stop and clarify the allergy status before proceeding (B). Sarah has raised a credible safety concern, and Mr Khan’s uncertainty means consent is not fully informed unless risks and alternatives are explored. Taking a focused allergy history, attempting to verify records, and updating Dr Kumar demonstrates safe practice, teamwork, and recognising limits under pressure.

If, after assessment, there is no immediate need for antibiotic prescribing, proceeding with the extraction while clearly documenting the uncertainty and using a non‑penicillin plan if needed (A) can be acceptable, but only after adequate risk assessment and discussion with the patient.

Rebooking (C) may be necessary if safety cannot be assured, but it should not be driven primarily by clinic delay; it also risks worsening pain and inconvenience.

Starting treatment while merely “preparing for a reaction” (D) is unsafe and normalises avoidable risk; emergency preparedness does not replace proper assessment.

Option E is inappropriate and coercive: it pressures Mr Khan into a specific medication and is not patient‑centred. Antibiotics should be prescribed only when clinically indicated and with allergy‑appropriate choices, not as a condition of treatment.
learningPoints: Act on concerns raised by nursing staff and treat them as safety-critical.
Valid consent requires exploring uncertainty and explaining risks, including drug allergy implications.
Time pressure and patient inconvenience do not justify proceeding with avoidable safety risks.
Antibiotic prescribing must be clinically indicated and tailored to allergy status.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has moderate learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Clarke. Dr Chen (consultant) has asked you to assess for extractions under sedation because Mrs Thompson has recurrent infections. Emily (dental nurse) tells you quietly that Mr Clarke answered all questions in the waiting room and became irritated when Mrs Thompson tried to speak. During the appointment, Mrs Thompson avoids eye contact and says “I don’t want teeth out” but then looks at her brother, who insists she “doesn’t understand” and demands you sign the consent form now because transport is booked. The sedation list is nearly full and Olivia (receptionist) says there is a long waiting list.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to step out briefly and speak to Mrs Thompson alone, using simple language and checking her understanding and wishes about extractions and sedation.
options.B: Proceed with consent based on Mr Clarke’s agreement because he is her next of kin and seems to know her well.
options.C: Explain to Mr Clarke that you cannot take consent today without assessing capacity, and offer to arrange a further appointment with an advocate or learning disability liaison if needed.
options.D: Ask Emily to witness Mr Clarke signing the consent form to protect the team from complaints and keep the sedation list on track.
options.E: Book Mrs Thompson onto the sedation list immediately to avoid delays, and plan to confirm consent on the day of treatment.

explanation:
The best actions prioritise lawful, patient-centred consent and safeguarding against undue influence. Speaking to Mrs Thompson alone (A) is crucial to assess her ability to communicate a choice and to reduce the interpersonal pressure from Mr Clarke. This supports autonomy and helps determine whether she can consent herself.

You should clearly explain to Mr Clarke that consent cannot be rushed or delegated without a proper capacity assessment (C). “Next of kin” does not automatically confer decision-making authority. Offering involvement of an advocate or liaison is appropriate and supports reasonable adjustments.

Accurate documentation and escalation (F) are important because Emily has raised concerns and you have observed potential coercion. Discussing with Dr Chen ensures senior input and a defensible plan, particularly given the clinical urgency of recurrent infections.

Options B and D are unsafe and unethical: they bypass capacity assessment and risk invalid consent. Option E is risky because booking sedation before consent is confirmed may pressure the patient and waste limited resources. Option G disrupts the service without communication, undermining teamwork. Option H is disproportionate and could be discriminatory; carers may be appropriate attendees if managed safely.
learningPoints: Capacity and consent must be assessed for the specific decision; “next of kin” is not automatic consent authority.
Consider undue influence and create opportunities for patients to speak privately.
Make reasonable adjustments (simple language, advocates, liaison services) to support understanding.
Document concerns and involve seniors early when consent validity is uncertain.

scenario: You are on OMFS ward cover as a DCT2. Rachel (ward nurse) calls you to review Mr Petrov, who had a mandibular fracture repair yesterday. He is complaining of increasing pain and says his face feels “tight”. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is in clinic, and Dr Grant (registrar) is in ED assessing a trauma patient. Rachel is frustrated because the analgesia chart is unclear and the overnight note says “continue as per protocol” without doses. Meanwhile, Angela (another nurse) tells you the patient’s partner is filming staff on a phone and demanding to speak to “the surgeon now”. You also notice Mr Petrov’s observations are borderline: HR 110, temp 37.9, and he is struggling to open his mouth. The ward is short-staffed and Rachel asks you to rewrite the drug chart quickly so she can give something immediately.

options.A: Assess Mr Petrov immediately at the bedside, including airway, swelling, wound and observations, and ask Rachel to repeat observations while you contact Dr Grant to update and request urgent review if needed.
options.B: Rewrite the analgesia chart from memory of the usual protocol so Rachel can administer medication quickly, then review Mr Petrov afterwards.
options.C: Ask Angela to request the partner stops filming and offer a private update once Mr Petrov has been assessed, explaining confidentiality and staff safety expectations.
options.D: Tell Rachel you cannot help until Dr O’Connor returns from clinic, as only consultants should adjust post‑operative analgesia.
options.E: Document the current situation, your assessment findings, and any medication changes clearly in the notes and on the chart, including rationale and escalation steps.

explanation:
The priority is patient safety: Mr Petrov may be developing a complication (e.g., haematoma, infection, airway risk) given tachycardia, fever and trismus. Immediate bedside assessment and repeating observations, with early escalation to Dr Grant, is most appropriate (A). This balances urgent clinical review with recognising limits and ensuring senior support.

Clear documentation is essential when charts are unclear and the ward is under pressure (E). Recording findings, decisions, doses and escalation protects the patient and supports the nursing team.

Managing the interpersonal conflict is also important: Angela’s report of filming raises confidentiality and staff wellbeing issues. Asking the partner to stop and offering a structured update after assessment (C) is proportionate and maintains professionalism.

Rewriting analgesia “from memory” (B) risks prescribing errors, especially post-operatively and with an unclear chart. Analgesia can be provided safely, but only after checking records, allergies, renal function and existing prescriptions.

Deferring all action until Dr O’Connor returns (D) is inappropriate; as ward cover you must act within competence, assess, and escalate. Waiting could allow deterioration and undermines teamwork with Rachel.
learningPoints: Prioritise assessment and escalation when post-operative patients show signs of deterioration.
Prescribing must be accurate and based on records, not memory or pressure from workload.
Good documentation and clear drug charts support safe multidisciplinary care.
Manage challenging relatives professionally while protecting confidentiality and staff safety.

scenario: In the radiology department, you have brought Mrs Hassan from restorative clinic for a CBCT requested by Dr Banerjee (consultant) to plan an implant in a complex case. Elena (radiographer) reviews the request and says the justification is vague and the referral form lacks pregnancy status and recent radiographs. Mrs Hassan is frustrated because she took time off work and says Dr Banerjee promised “everything would be done today”. Elena is polite but firm that she cannot proceed without adequate justification and documentation. You notice the clinic note mentions a periapical radiograph done six weeks ago at the GDP but it is not uploaded. Mark (radiographer) adds that the department has had a recent audit and is under scrutiny.

options.A: Call Dr Banerjee to clarify the clinical question, confirm why CBCT is needed beyond existing imaging, and update the request with a clear justification for Elena.
options.B: Ask Mrs Hassan to insist to Elena that she was promised the scan today, as patient pressure may persuade the department to proceed.
options.C: Check with restorative clinic/admin to obtain the GDP periapical radiograph or report, and upload/attach it to support justification and avoid unnecessary exposure.
options.D: Tell Elena you will file an incident report against radiology for delaying patient care, so she should proceed to avoid escalation.
options.E: Explain to Mrs Hassan why radiology must follow safety and justification rules, apologise for the inconvenience, and offer a realistic plan (e.g., reschedule promptly once information is complete).

explanation:
The most appropriate actions maintain patient safety and professional relationships while addressing expectations. Clarifying the clinical question and strengthening justification with Dr Banerjee (A) aligns with IR(ME)R principles: exposures must be justified and properly authorised. It also demonstrates respectful cross-department communication.

Obtaining the recent periapical radiograph/report (C) may show that CBCT is unnecessary or help define the field of view, reducing radiation dose and avoiding duplication. This is a practical step that addresses Elena’s legitimate concerns and the department’s audit pressures.

Explaining the situation to Mrs Hassan and setting a realistic plan (E) supports patient-centred care and maintains trust. An apology acknowledges inconvenience without blaming radiology.

Options B, F and D inappropriately use pressure or hierarchy to bypass safety processes and damage interprofessional working. Option G is unsafe and dishonest: pregnancy status must be asked sensitively and documented accurately as part of consent and radiation safety. Option H fails to communicate and risks complaint escalation; you should coordinate rather than abandon the issue.
learningPoints: Radiological exposures must be justified with clear clinical questions and adequate documentation.
Avoid using hierarchy or patient pressure to circumvent safety standards.
Communicate transparently with patients when processes prevent same-day care.
Seek existing imaging to avoid unnecessary radiation and duplication.

scenario: You are the DCT1 on-call in the hospital emergency dental clinic on a Friday evening. Lucy (dental nurse) tells you the waiting room is full and one patient, Mrs Khan, is crying with severe toothache. At the same time, Mark (receptionist) hands you a handwritten note from a GDP about 7-year-old Tyler who arrived with his aunt; the note says “possible non-accidental injury?” Tyler has a bruised cheek and is quiet. The aunt insists he “fell off a scooter” and becomes defensive when Lucy asks basic questions. Dr Fernandes (registrar) is in theatre and has limited phone signal. You also have Mr Ibrahim in the chair who has started to feel faint after local anaesthetic, and the clinic computer system has just gone down so you cannot access Tyler’s previous attendances.

options.A: Stabilise Mr Ibrahim first, ensuring he is safe (positioning, monitoring, reassurance), and ask Lucy to keep Tyler and his aunt in a private area while you contact the on-call safeguarding lead for advice.
options.B: Ask Mark to tell the aunt you will not see Tyler tonight because the system is down, and advise her to return to the GDP on Monday.
options.C: See Mrs Khan next because she is the loudest and most distressed, and ask Lucy to “keep an eye” on Tyler in the waiting room until you are free.
options.D: Call Dr Fernandes and, if you cannot reach them, follow the local safeguarding pathway yourself, documenting concerns and contacting the hospital safeguarding team/children’s social care as indicated.
options.E: Confront the aunt directly in the waiting room, stating you suspect abuse and will call the police immediately unless she explains the bruising properly.

explanation:
Immediate clinical safety comes first: Mr Ibrahim is symptomatic after local anaesthetic and could deteriorate. Managing him promptly while delegating appropriately to Lucy to keep Tyler in a private, supervised space (A) balances acute care with safeguarding risk. Privacy reduces escalation and supports a calm environment.

Safeguarding cannot wait for perfect information or senior availability. If Dr Fernandes is unreachable, you should follow the established safeguarding pathway, document clearly, and contact the safeguarding team for advice (D). The GDP note and the aunt’s defensiveness are concerning; the computer outage does not remove your duty to act.

Seeing Mrs Khan next (C) may be reasonable once immediate risks are controlled, but leaving Tyler unsupervised in the waiting room is not ideal; it is ranked lower because it risks missing safeguarding opportunities.

Turning Tyler away due to system issues (B) is inappropriate and may place him at further risk. You can still assess, record manually and escalate.

Confronting the aunt publicly and threatening police (E) is the least appropriate. It may increase risk to the child, breach confidentiality, and undermine a proportionate safeguarding approach. Safeguarding should be handled calmly, privately, and via agreed processes unless there is immediate danger.
learningPoints: Prioritise immediate clinical stability while delegating tasks to maintain overall safety.
Safeguarding duties persist despite workload, IT failures, or senior unavailability.
Use private, calm communication and follow local safeguarding pathways; document contemporaneously.
Avoid confrontational accusations that may escalate risk or compromise the child’s safety.

scenario: You are the DCT in an overbooked hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Khan (52) attends with facial swelling and trismus. The triage note from reception (Nadia) says “penicillin allergy,” but the hospital record lists “no known drug allergies.” Your dental nurse Sarah quietly tells you she overheard Mr Khan telling Nadia he “got a rash once” but wasn’t sure which antibiotic. Dr Kumar (registrar) is in theatre and has asked you to “start antibiotics and keep the list moving.” Mr Khan is frustrated about waiting and asks for “the strongest antibiotics” so he can go back to work. There are two patients still in pain waiting, and radiology is about to close.

options.A: Prescribe amoxicillin immediately because the hospital record states no allergies, and ask Sarah to update the allergy status later to avoid delaying the clinic.
options.B: Ask Mr Khan targeted questions about the previous reaction (timing, symptoms, drug name if known) and check with Nadia what was documented, before deciding on a safe antibiotic.
options.C: Tell Mr Khan you cannot prescribe anything until Dr Kumar returns from theatre, and ask him to wait even if radiology closes.
options.D: Prescribe co‑amoxiclav “to be safe” and document that the allergy history is unclear, then move on to the next patient.
options.E: Ask Sarah to call Dr Kumar in theatre and request he authorises an antibiotic choice without you taking a further history, as he is the senior.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to clarify the allergy history directly with Mr Khan and corroborate what Nadia recorded (B). In NHS practice, medication safety and accurate documentation are fundamental; a vague “rash once” could represent a true allergy or a non‑allergic side effect, and the antibiotic choice depends on the nature and severity of the reaction. Next, involving the senior appropriately (E) is reasonable because Dr Kumar has requested rapid prescribing and the history is conflicting; however, you should not delegate clinical decision-making without providing a clear history—hence E is second, not first.

Telling Mr Khan you will wait for Dr Kumar without progressing assessment (C) protects safety but is inefficient and risks delaying necessary imaging and treatment; you can gather information and plan while awaiting advice.
