Prescribing co‑amoxiclav with an unclear allergy history (D) is unsafe; if the reaction was to penicillin, co‑amoxiclav is contraindicated and could cause harm. Prescribing amoxicillin based solely on the electronic record (A) is least appropriate because it ignores a credible concern raised by Sarah and the triage note, and prioritises throughput over patient safety and informed prescribing.
learningPoints: Conflicting allergy information must be resolved with a focused history and corroboration before prescribing.
Patient safety overrides time pressure and clinic flow in urgent care settings.
Escalate appropriately when uncertainty remains, but do not bypass your own assessment responsibilities.
Accurate, timely documentation and respectful team communication (including reception and nursing staff) reduce risk.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson (34) who has learning disability and attends with her support worker, Laura. Mrs Thompson needs an extraction under local anaesthetic. She appears calm but keeps looking to Laura before answering. Halfway through the consent discussion, Laura says, “Just get it done—she always agrees,” and tries to answer questions for her. Your dental nurse Emily looks uncomfortable and later tells you she thinks Mrs Thompson didn’t understand the risks. The clinic is running late, and the next patient is a child with acute pain. Dr O’Connor (consultant) is off-site and only reachable by phone. Mrs Thompson then quietly asks you, “Can I say no?” Laura becomes defensive and says she has another appointment and cannot stay.

options.A: Pause the procedure and speak directly to Mrs Thompson using simple language and visual aids, checking understanding and voluntariness without Laura answering for her.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction because Laura is the support worker and says Mrs Thompson usually agrees, documenting ‘consent obtained with support.’
options.C: Ask Emily to escort Laura to the waiting area briefly so you can assess Mrs Thompson’s capacity and wishes in private, explaining the reason respectfully.
options.D: Phone Dr O’Connor immediately and ask him to decide whether Mrs Thompson has capacity, then follow his instruction without further discussion.
options.E: Offer to rebook the extraction for another day when Laura can stay longer, even if Mrs Thompson says she wants it done today.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions prioritise valid consent and patient autonomy while managing the interpersonal tension with Laura. First, you should address Mrs Thompson directly and check understanding using accessible communication (A). Her question “Can I say no?” is a clear cue to explore voluntariness and ensure she understands options, risks and alternatives.

Creating space to assess capacity and wishes without the support worker dominating (C) is appropriate, provided it is explained respectfully and Mrs Thompson is comfortable. This helps you confirm that Laura is not exerting undue influence and supports confidentiality.

Setting clear, calm boundaries with Laura (F) maintains professionalism and a workable relationship while reinforcing that consent must come from Mrs Thompson if she has capacity.

Proceeding based on Laura’s assertion (B) undermines informed consent and could constitute assault. Phoning Dr O’Connor to “decide capacity” (D) is inappropriate because capacity assessment is decision-specific and usually done by the treating clinician; you can seek advice but not outsource it. Automatically rebooking (E) may disregard Mrs Thompson’s wishes. Ignoring Emily (G) is unsafe and dismisses team concerns. Using Mark as a ‘witness’ to justify proceeding (H) is defensive and does not fix consent validity.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires the patient’s understanding and voluntariness; supporters cannot consent on their behalf if they have capacity.
Assess capacity and undue influence sensitively, and consider private discussion when appropriate.
Set professional boundaries with carers/support workers while maintaining respectful communication.
Team members’ concerns (e.g., Emily’s) should be taken seriously to protect patient safety.

scenario: On an OMFS ward, you are covering the weekend. Mr Petrov (67) is post-op and asks you to explain why his surgery was delayed. While you are speaking, Nurse Angela tells you quietly that Dr Grant (registrar) has been writing notes late and backdating times “to keep the audit happy.” Angela is worried because a medication change for Mr Petrov was recorded as happening before it was actually prescribed. You also overhear Dr Grant joking with Nurse Rachel that “no one checks the times anyway.” The ward is busy; a new trauma admission is expected, and you have not met Dr Grant before. Angela asks you not to mention she raised it because she fears retaliation.

options.A: Speak to Dr Grant privately, describe the specific concern about Mr Petrov’s medication timing, and ask him to correct the record and clarify what happened.
options.B: Ignore the issue because it is a registrar’s responsibility, and focus on the incoming trauma admission to avoid conflict.
options.C: Document your own contemporaneous note about what you have observed/been told regarding the medication timing for Mr Petrov, ensuring your entry is accurate and dated correctly.
options.D: Report Dr Grant immediately to the consultant on call without speaking to him first, naming Angela as the source so the issue is taken seriously.
options.E: Ask Angela to submit a Datix herself and reassure her you will stay out of it to protect your working relationship with Dr Grant.

explanation:
The most appropriate immediate step is to ensure the patient record is safe and accurate from your perspective (C). Making a clear, contemporaneous entry about what you know regarding Mr Petrov’s medication timing supports continuity of care and reduces risk, especially on a busy weekend.

Next, addressing the concern with Dr Grant privately (A) is proportionate and aligns with NHS professionalism: raise concerns early, focus on patient safety, and give the colleague an opportunity to correct errors. It also helps distinguish poor practice from a misunderstanding.

Escalation to the consultant on call (D) may be necessary if Dr Grant refuses to correct records, if there is ongoing risk, or if the behaviour appears deliberate. However, immediately reporting without first seeking clarification can be premature; additionally, naming Angela against her wishes is not justified unless required for safety.

Asking Angela to file a Datix while you ‘stay out of it’ (E) is inappropriate; raising concerns is a shared professional duty and you should support her, not offload responsibility. Ignoring the issue (B) is least appropriate because inaccurate documentation can directly compromise patient care and breaches professional integrity.
learningPoints: Accurate, contemporaneous documentation is essential for patient safety and legal defensibility.
Concerns about colleagues should be raised promptly, proportionately, and with a focus on patient safety.
Escalate appropriately if risks persist, while being mindful of protecting staff who raise concerns.
Do not ignore or delegate away professional responsibilities because of hierarchy or workload.

scenario: In the radiology department, you bring Mrs Hassan (45) from the restorative clinic for a CBCT requested for implant planning. The referral form completed by Dr Chen (consultant) is missing justification details and does not mention that Mrs Hassan is anxious and has previously refused radiographs. Radiographer Elena says she cannot proceed without adequate IR(ME)R justification and asks you to get it corrected. Mrs Hassan becomes visibly upset, saying she has taken time off work and “won’t come back again.” Your dental nurse Chloe is trying to calm her but is also worried you will miss the last scan slot. Dr Chen is in an MDT meeting and not answering. Receptionist Olivia from radiology is pressuring Elena to “just do it” because the list is behind.

options.A: Explain to Mrs Hassan why Elena needs the referral justification and apologise for the delay, offering to wait with her while you seek the missing information.
options.B: Ask Olivia to overbook Mrs Hassan into another slot today and tell Elena you will send the justification later so the scan can go ahead now.
options.C: Call Dr Chen’s secretary to locate him during the MDT or identify an alternative referrer (e.g., Dr Lewis) who can provide IR(ME)R justification promptly.
options.D: Ask Elena to proceed with the CBCT because the consultant requested it, and document that radiology refused initially due to admin issues.
options.E: Offer Mrs Hassan the option to return to clinic to discuss alternative imaging or treatment planning today if the CBCT cannot be justified in time.

explanation:
The best actions prioritise legal/regulatory compliance and patient-centred communication. Explaining the situation to Mrs Hassan and apologising, while keeping her informed and supported (A), helps manage her anxiety and maintains trust. It also reduces conflict between departments by showing transparency.

Obtaining appropriate justification from the referrer is essential under IR(ME)R; contacting Dr Chen’s secretary to reach him during the MDT or identifying another authorised referrer such as Dr Lewis (C) is practical and respects governance. In parallel, escalating to the radiology duty lead (G) is appropriate when time-critical access is at risk and there is pressure from Olivia to breach process; the duty lead can advise on compliant pathways.

Options that bypass justification are unsafe. Proceeding and “sending justification later” (B) or asking Elena to scan because “the consultant requested it” (D) undermines IR(ME)R and places Elena at professional risk. Consent alone (F) cannot replace justification. Offering alternatives (E) can be reasonable, but after you have attempted to obtain justification and escalation; it is less immediately effective than C/G. Threatening discharge (H) is unprofessional and punitive, especially when the system failure is not the patient’s fault.
learningPoints: IR(ME)R justification is mandatory and cannot be replaced by patient consent or seniority pressure.
Clear, empathetic explanations reduce anxiety and help maintain trust when delays occur.
Use appropriate escalation channels (e.g., duty lead) when facing cross-department pressure to bypass safety processes.
Seek timely support from authorised referrers and administrative pathways to resolve documentation gaps.

scenario: You are a DCT in a sedation clinic. Mrs Khan (29) is booked for IV sedation for surgical extraction. The sedation nurse Lucy tells you the pre-assessment form is incomplete: it records asthma but no peak flow, and the medical history section is partly blank. Mrs Khan admits she used her salbutamol “a lot” this week due to a cold. She is tearful and says she has arranged childcare and cannot rebook. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is delayed in traffic and will arrive in 20 minutes; the theatre team is asking you to start cannulation to keep the list on time. Lucy is uneasy and says the last patient had a desaturation event yesterday. Meanwhile, receptionist Nadia says two patients have already complained about delays.

options.A: Begin cannulation and baseline observations now to save time, and plan to clarify the asthma history once Dr Banerjee arrives.
options.B: Explain to Mrs Khan that you cannot proceed with sedation until the assessment is completed, and ask Lucy to help you obtain missing details and observations immediately.
options.C: Cancel the sedation immediately and discharge Mrs Khan back to her GDP with advice to re-refer when her asthma is stable.
options.D: Ask the theatre team to proceed with another case first, and inform Nadia that the list may be delayed due to incomplete pre-assessment and safety checks.
options.E: Proceed with sedation as planned because delaying will increase Mrs Khan’s anxiety, documenting that she accepted the risks.

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to prioritise safe pre-sedation assessment and complete missing information with Lucy’s support (B). Mrs Khan’s increased inhaler use and recent cold raise concern about asthma control and peri-sedation respiratory risk; proceeding without adequate assessment would breach patient safety standards and your limits of competence.

Managing system pressure transparently is next (D): asking the theatre team to reorder cases and briefing Nadia about a safety-related delay protects patients and supports a just culture. It also reduces the risk of rushed decisions after Lucy’s recent experience of a desaturation event.

Starting cannulation early (A) may be acceptable only after you have begun the safety checks and are confident it is appropriate; doing it to “save time” before clarifying risk is less ideal, but not inherently unsafe if you are simultaneously completing assessment and can stop if concerns escalate.

Proceeding with sedation because anxiety is high and documenting “accepted risks” (E) is inappropriate; consent does not legitimise unsafe practice. Immediate cancellation and discharge to GDP (C) is least appropriate because it is disproportionate and fails to explore same-day mitigation, senior review by Dr Banerjee, or rescheduling within the service with appropriate planning.
learningPoints: Pre-sedation assessment must be complete; increased reliever use or recent infection may indicate higher respiratory risk.
Time pressure and patient distress do not justify bypassing safety checks; consent cannot replace safe practice.
Use the team to manage list flow and communicate delays honestly to administrative staff and patients.
Recognise when senior input is needed and plan care within your competence and local sedation governance.

scenario: You are a DCT in a busy hospital emergency dental clinic. Sarah (dental nurse) tells you Mr Khan is booked for an extraction and “always says he’s fine with penicillin,” but today he looks wheezy and keeps rubbing his chest. The notes from a previous trust are scanned and hard to read; the allergy box is blank. Dr Lewis (registrar) is in theatre and has asked you to “keep the list moving” because two urgent facial swelling patients are waiting. Mr Khan is frustrated and says he needs to get back to work and wants “whatever antibiotics you usually give after.” He also mentions he had a rash “years ago” with something but can’t remember what. Sarah is worried because the clinic is running late and reception (Nadia) is fielding complaints from waiting patients.

options.A: Ask Nadia to rebook Mr Khan for another day so you can keep the list moving, and proceed to see the swelling patients first without further discussion.
options.B: Pause the extraction plan, take a focused allergy and respiratory history from Mr Khan, check his observations with Sarah, and contact Dr Lewis for a quick supervisory decision before prescribing or proceeding.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction using local anaesthetic as planned, and avoid prescribing any antibiotics to reduce risk, documenting that the allergy status is unclear.
options.D: Prescribe amoxicillin ‘as usual’ to meet Mr Khan’s expectations, and ask Sarah to add a note to the record that the patient denied allergies today.
options.E: Ask Sarah to phone the previous trust for clarification of the scanned notes while you start the extraction, and tell Mr Khan you will decide about antibiotics afterwards.

explanation:
The safest and most appropriate action is to stop and clarify risk before treatment. Option B prioritises patient safety by addressing Mr Khan’s wheeze and uncertain drug history, checking observations with Sarah, and seeking timely supervision from Dr Lewis given the ambiguity and pressure to maintain flow. This aligns with NHS expectations to recognise limits of competence and not proceed when key safety information is missing.

Option C is less appropriate than B but still safer than prescribing blindly: avoiding antibiotics reduces immediate medication risk, and proceeding with an extraction may be reasonable only if Mr Khan is clinically stable and consent is valid. However, it still risks proceeding without fully assessing the wheeze and allergy uncertainty.

Option A manages flow but inappropriately deprioritises an unwell patient without assessment and fails to communicate clinically with Mr Khan.

Option E is risky: starting an extraction while allergy and respiratory concerns remain unresolved undermines informed consent and could precipitate deterioration.

Option D is least appropriate: prescribing amoxicillin despite a possible previous rash and incomplete documentation is unsafe and breaches professional integrity by shaping the record to fit the decision.
learningPoints: Patient safety overrides time pressure and list management.
Uncertain allergy history requires clarification before prescribing; do not document to justify unsafe decisions.
Recognise and act on signs of clinical deterioration (e.g., wheeze) before proceeding.
Seek appropriate senior support when key information is missing or risk is elevated.

scenario: In a community dental service special care clinic, you are seeing Mrs Thompson, who has mild learning disability and attends with her brother, Mr Clarke. Emily (dental nurse) quietly tells you that Mr Clarke has answered most questions for her in previous visits and becomes irritated if staff speak directly to Mrs Thompson. Today, Mrs Thompson needs a restoration on a painful lower molar. She appears anxious but says “I want it fixed,” while Mr Clarke insists she should have it extracted because “she won’t cope” and he has taken time off work. The referral letter from the GDP is brief and does not mention capacity. Dr Banerjee (consultant) is running a sedation assessment in another room and is not immediately available. The clinic is overbooked and reception (Olivia) has asked you to avoid overruns.

options.A: Ask Mr Clarke to stay in the room to keep Mrs Thompson calm, and proceed with extraction as he is her next of kin.
options.B: Speak directly to Mrs Thompson using simple language and check her understanding of the options, then document your capacity assessment and her decision.
options.C: Ask Emily to act as a chaperone while you request to speak with Mrs Thompson alone for part of the consultation, explaining to Mr Clarke this supports her autonomy.
options.D: Book Mrs Thompson for sedation on a different day without discussing options today, to avoid conflict and keep to time.
options.E: Proceed with the restoration immediately because Mrs Thompson said “I want it fixed,” without further discussion, to respect her wishes.

explanation:
The three most appropriate actions focus on valid consent, autonomy, and appropriate support. Option B is essential: you must address Mrs Thompson directly, use accessible communication, and assess/document capacity specific to the decision (restoration vs extraction). This supports informed consent and patient-centred care.

Option C appropriately manages the interpersonal dynamic. Offering time alone with Mrs Thompson (with Emily as chaperone) helps reduce undue influence from Mr Clarke while maintaining transparency and safeguarding professional boundaries.

Option F is appropriate because Dr Banerjee is the senior clinician available; a brief call can confirm the approach to capacity assessment, potential need for sedation assessment, and how to manage disagreement. Seeking supervision is justified given the ethical tension and potential coercion.

Options A and H are unsafe and unethical: “next of kin” does not confer consent authority for an adult with capacity, and documenting consent from Mr Clarke would be professionally indefensible. Option E is incomplete because a single statement does not confirm understanding of risks/alternatives. Option D avoids conflict but fails to address pain and consent today. Option G is punitive and not patient-centred.
learningPoints: Capacity is decision-specific and must be assessed and documented when in doubt.
Adults with capacity consent for themselves; relatives do not automatically have decision-making authority.
Manage potential undue influence by facilitating private discussion and using chaperones appropriately.
Seek senior support when ethical complexity or coercion is suspected.

scenario: During an OMFS ward round, you notice Dr Kumar (registrar) is hurried and asks you to “just copy last week’s plan” into today’s notes for Mr Ibrahim, who is post-op with a neck infection. Rachel (ward nurse) quietly tells you Mr Ibrahim’s temperature spiked overnight and he looks more swollen, but the observation chart hasn’t been filed in the notes yet. Mr Ibrahim’s wife is at the bedside and is upset because she feels no one is listening; she asks you directly whether he is getting worse. Dr Kumar looks annoyed and says the consultant, Dr O’Connor, will not tolerate delays because theatre cases are waiting. You are concerned the plan may need escalation, but you are also aware of hierarchy and the pressure to move on.

options.A: Copy last week’s plan as requested to avoid conflict, and mention Rachel’s concerns to Dr Kumar only after the ward round finishes.
options.B: Tell Dr Kumar you are not comfortable copying a plan without reviewing current observations, and ask Rachel to bring the latest obs chart immediately so the team can reassess Mr Ibrahim before documenting.
options.C: Document in the notes that the patient is “stable” because you have not personally seen the obs chart, and advise the wife to speak to the consultant later.
options.D: Step aside and call Dr O’Connor directly to report deterioration, without informing Dr Kumar, because patient safety overrides hierarchy.
options.E: Ask Dr Kumar in front of the wife whether he is ignoring the nurse’s concerns, to ensure the family sees you are advocating for the patient.

explanation:
Option B is the most appropriate because it addresses the immediate clinical risk and the documentation concern. Refusing to copy-and-paste a plan without current data demonstrates professional integrity, and asking Rachel to provide the latest observations enables a timely reassessment of Mr Ibrahim’s potential deterioration.

Option D is next: if Dr Kumar still does not act on signs of sepsis or worsening swelling, escalation to Dr O’Connor is justified for patient safety. However, bypassing Dr Kumar without first attempting to resolve it within the team can harm working relationships; it becomes appropriate if there is ongoing inaction.

Option A is less appropriate: it delays escalation and risks inaccurate records, though it at least plans to raise the concern later.

Option E is unhelpful and risks undermining trust and escalating conflict in front of the wife; advocacy should be calm and professional, with sensitive communication.

Option C is least appropriate: documenting “stable” without evidence is unsafe and misleading, and deflecting the wife’s question without providing an honest, appropriate update fails patient-centred communication.
learningPoints: Accurate contemporaneous documentation is a professional duty; do not copy plans without verifying current status.
Act on nursing concerns and objective observations when deterioration is suspected.
Escalate appropriately when patient safety is at risk, balancing teamwork and hierarchy.
Communicate honestly and sensitively with relatives without making unsupported assurances.

scenario: In the radiology department, you have sent Mrs Hassan from the restorative clinic for a CBCT to plan an implant-retained crown. Elena (radiographer) calls you to the desk and says the referral lacks justification and the field of view requested is “too wide,” so she cannot proceed under IR(ME)R. Mrs Hassan is in the waiting area and is becoming visibly upset; she tells Mark (receptionist) she has already taken unpaid leave and wants the scan “done today.” Dr Chen (consultant) is in clinic but between patients and has asked not to be interrupted unless urgent. Meanwhile, your next restorative patient is already seated with Lucy (dental nurse) and the clinic is running behind.

options.A: Ask Elena to proceed with the scan as requested and promise you will update the justification afterwards so Mrs Hassan is not delayed.
options.B: Return to the restorative clinic and ask Dr Chen to authorise and justify the scan, including the minimum appropriate field of view, then update Elena with the amended request.
options.C: Explain to Mrs Hassan that the scan cannot proceed without proper justification, apologise for the delay, and offer to rebook at a convenient time if needed.
options.D: Amend the referral yourself to include a justification you think is reasonable and ask Elena to accept it to keep the pathway moving.
options.E: Ask Lucy to tell your waiting restorative patient you will be delayed, and stay in radiology to clarify with Elena what information is missing and what field of view is acceptable.

explanation:
Option E is appropriate because it addresses the immediate system problem collaboratively: you stay with Elena to clarify exactly what IR(ME)R information is missing and what minimum field of view is required, while Lucy updates the restorative clinic patient about the delay. This supports teamwork and reduces friction between departments.

Option B is also appropriate: the practitioner responsible for justification/authorisation should be involved. Asking Dr Chen to justify the scan and specify the smallest suitable field of view ensures lawful exposure and patient safety, even if it briefly interrupts clinic flow.

Option C supports patient-centred communication. Mrs Hassan deserves an honest explanation, apology, and practical options (rebook/alternative timing) rather than false reassurance.

Options A and D undermine IR(ME)R compliance by proceeding or altering justification inappropriately. Option F escalates conflict unnecessarily and damages interprofessional relationships. Option G is dishonest and risks a complaint if the scan cannot proceed. Option H may compromise care if CBCT is clinically indicated; avoiding imaging purely for convenience is not appropriate.
learningPoints: IR(ME)R justification and optimisation are mandatory; do not bypass radiology governance to save time.
Clarify missing referral details collaboratively with radiographers to maintain good working relationships.
Keep patients informed with honest, empathetic explanations and realistic options.
Manage knock-on delays by communicating promptly with your own clinic team and waiting patients.

scenario: You are on trauma/on-call overnight. A&E has referred Tyler, a 9-year-old who fell and fractured an upper incisor. Angela (A&E nurse) says Tyler is distressed and his father, Mr Petrov, is demanding immediate treatment and filming staff on his phone, saying he will “put this online” if you don’t help. Chloe (dental nurse) tells you the dental trauma kit is incomplete because supplies have not been restocked. The electronic record shows a latex allergy banner, but the triage note says “NKDA,” and no interpreter is available even though Mr Petrov’s English is limited. Dr Fernandes (registrar) is currently managing a facial laceration in resus and cannot attend for at least 20 minutes.

options.A: Ask Angela to request security support and ask Mr Petrov to stop filming, explaining confidentiality and staff safety, while you focus on calming Tyler with Chloe.
options.B: Proceed to treat immediately using whatever gloves and materials are available, as delaying could worsen the dental prognosis.
options.C: Wait for Dr Fernandes before doing anything, and ask Angela to keep Tyler in the waiting area until the registrar arrives.
options.D: Confirm the latex allergy directly with Mr Petrov and Tyler using simple questions and visual prompts, and choose non-latex alternatives; if uncertain, treat as latex-allergic until clarified.
options.E: Tell Mr Petrov you will not treat Tyler unless he deletes the video, and discharge them back to their GDP if he refuses.

explanation:
Option A is most appropriate because it addresses an immediate interpersonal and safety risk. Involving Angela to request security and asking Mr Petrov to stop filming is proportionate, protects confidentiality, and helps create a calmer environment so you and Chloe can support Tyler.

Option D is next: the conflicting records (latex banner vs “NKDA”) create a clinical safety risk. Directly checking allergy status with simple communication and treating as latex-allergic if uncertain is prudent and aligns with safe practice.

Option B is less appropriate: timely dental trauma care matters, but proceeding with incomplete kit and uncertain allergy status risks harm. Some interim measures may be needed, but not at the expense of safety.

Option C delays necessary assessment and comfort measures; you can begin triage, analgesia planning, and preparation while waiting for Dr Fernandes.

Option E is least appropriate: it is punitive and risks abandoning a child needing urgent care. You should set boundaries about filming, but still provide necessary treatment and escalate appropriately rather than threaten discharge.
learningPoints: Manage aggressive or recording behaviour proportionately, involving A&E staff/security when needed.
When documentation conflicts, verify critical safety information (e.g., allergies) and err on the side of caution.
Start assessment and supportive care while awaiting senior help; do not delay everything unnecessarily.
Do not withhold urgent care as leverage in conflicts; maintain professional boundaries and patient focus.

scenario: You are a Dental Core Trainee in an oral surgery clinic. Dr Ahmed, the consultant, has briefly stepped out to answer an urgent call from theatre. You are reviewing Mr Khan for a surgical extraction of an impacted molar. Dental nurse Sarah quietly tells you she is worried because she remembers Mr Khan mentioning a previous reaction to antibiotics during triage. However, the electronic record only lists 'no known drug allergies'. The waiting room is full and receptionist Nadia has already informed several patients that delays are likely. Mr Khan appears impatient and tells you he has taken time off work and wants the procedure completed today. Sarah says she may have misheard but felt uncomfortable ignoring the concern.

options.A: Proceed with the extraction and prescribe the standard postoperative antibiotics as documented in the notes.
options.B: Ask Sarah to clarify exactly what she heard from Mr Khan during triage before continuing the consultation.
options.C: Explain to Mr Khan that you need to confirm his medication history before proceeding with treatment.
options.D: Contact Dr Ahmed to ask whether he would like to review the allergy history before you continue.
options.E: Document the uncertainty about Mr Khan’s allergy status and delay the procedure until the information is clarified.

explanation: Sarah’s concern introduces a potential patient safety issue. The most appropriate first step is to explore the concern further by asking Sarah exactly what she heard (B). Team members raising safety concerns should always be taken seriously in NHS practice. Clarifying directly with Mr Khan (C) is also essential, as allergy history must be confirmed before prescribing or carrying out treatment that could involve medication. Escalating to Dr Ahmed (D) is reasonable given the consultant remains responsible for the clinic and may wish to review the decision if there is uncertainty. Documenting the concern and delaying treatment until clarification (E) is acceptable if the allergy status remains unclear, although further discussion should usually occur first. Proceeding with treatment and prescribing antibiotics without clarification (A) disregards a potential safety issue raised by Sarah and could expose Mr Khan to avoidable harm. NHS professional standards emphasise prioritising patient safety over clinic efficiency and respecting concerns raised by colleagues.
learningPoints: Concerns raised by team members should be explored promptly.
Accurate allergy history is essential before prescribing.
Patient safety should take priority over clinic efficiency.
Effective teamwork includes valuing contributions from all staff.

scenario: You are working in a community dental service clinic providing care for adults with additional needs. Dental therapist Maria has been treating Mrs Hassan, who has moderate learning difficulties and significant dental anxiety. Maria asks you to review a lower molar that she believes may require extraction. Mrs Hassan becomes distressed when you enter the room and says she does not want 'any teeth taken out'. Her support worker, Daniel, insists the tooth has been painful for weeks and wants treatment completed today because transport has been arranged through the care home. Nurse Chloe quietly tells you that Mrs Hassan appeared confused earlier and may not fully understand what treatment was planned.

options.A: Explain the situation to Mrs Hassan using simple language and check what she understands about the proposed treatment.
options.B: Ask Daniel to explain to Mrs Hassan why the extraction is necessary so she will cooperate.
options.C: Discuss Maria’s clinical concerns and your assessment together before deciding how to proceed.
options.D: Proceed with the extraction because Daniel has confirmed the tooth has been painful.
options.E: Offer to arrange another appointment if Mrs Hassan does not feel comfortable proceeding today.
options.F: Ask nurse Chloe whether Mrs Hassan seemed able to understand the earlier explanation of treatment.
options.G: Tell Daniel that the clinic schedule means treatment needs to happen today.
options.H: Document the discussion and any concerns about Mrs Hassan’s understanding.

explanation: The key issue is whether Mrs Hassan can give valid informed consent. The most appropriate action is to communicate directly with Mrs Hassan in a clear and accessible way to assess her understanding (A). This supports patient-centred care and respects her autonomy. Discussing Maria’s concerns with her (C) is also important, as collaborative decision-making helps ensure the clinical assessment is accurate and consistent. Asking Chloe about Mrs Hassan’s earlier understanding (F) can provide useful context about her ability to process information and whether further explanation is needed. Relying on Daniel to persuade Mrs Hassan (B) risks undermining patient autonomy. Proceeding with extraction because Daniel wants treatment completed (D) would be inappropriate without confirmed consent. Pressuring the situation due to clinic timing (G) prioritises efficiency over patient rights. Offering another appointment (E) and documenting concerns (H) may be appropriate later but do not directly address the immediate issue of assessing capacity and understanding. NHS standards emphasise ensuring patients understand proposed treatment before consent is accepted.
learningPoints: Valid consent requires patient understanding.
Patients with additional needs may require adapted communication.
Team discussion improves safe clinical decision-making.
Efficiency pressures must not override consent requirements.

scenario: During an OMFS ward round, registrar Dr Lewis asks you to review the notes for Mr Ibrahim, who had a difficult extraction under GA the previous evening. While checking the documentation, you notice that the consent form lists only 'extraction of lower molar', but the operation note states that two teeth were removed. Ward nurse Angela mentions that Mr Ibrahim was confused when he woke up and asked why two teeth had been taken out. Dr Lewis appears rushed and says the consultant Dr Chen is already in theatre. Several other patients still need reviewing on the ward round.

options.A: Ignore the discrepancy because Dr Lewis has already documented the procedure.
options.B: Ask Dr Lewis whether the consent discussion included removal of both teeth.
options.C: Speak to Angela privately and suggest she reassure Mr Ibrahim that the treatment was necessary.
options.D: Document the discrepancy in the notes and clarify the consent details before the team leaves the ward.
options.E: Inform Dr Chen later in the day if the consent details remain unclear.

explanation: A discrepancy between consent documentation and the procedure performed is a significant professional issue. The first step is to clarify the situation with Dr Lewis (B), as he may be aware of additional consent discussions that were not documented. Recording the discrepancy and ensuring the information is clarified before leaving the ward (D) helps maintain accurate documentation and transparency. If uncertainty remains, informing the supervising consultant Dr Chen (E) is appropriate because consultants hold overall responsibility for patient care. Suggesting that Angela simply reassure the patient without clarifying the facts (C) risks misleading the patient and does not address the documentation issue. Ignoring the discrepancy entirely (A) would be professionally inappropriate and could undermine patient trust. NHS standards emphasise honesty, accurate documentation, and addressing potential consent issues promptly to maintain patient confidence and safety.
learningPoints: Consent documentation must accurately reflect treatment provided.
Concerns about consent should be clarified promptly.
Accurate clinical records support transparency and patient trust.
Team members should feel able to raise concerns about documentation.

scenario: You are a Dental Core Trainee working in a hospital emergency dental clinic. Dr O’Connor, the consultant, has asked you to assess Mr Clarke who has arrived with severe toothache. Dental nurse Emily notes that Mr Clarke appears frustrated after waiting nearly an hour because several trauma cases were prioritised earlier. During the consultation, Mr Clarke demands antibiotics immediately and says he does not want any dental treatment today because he needs to return to work. On examination you identify a heavily decayed lower molar with swelling that would normally require extraction or drainage. Emily quietly mentions that Mr Clarke told reception earlier that antibiotics helped when he had a similar problem before.

options.A: Explain to Mr Clarke why antibiotics alone may not resolve the infection and discuss appropriate treatment options.
options.B: Prescribe antibiotics to satisfy Mr Clarke so that you can move on to the next patient.
options.C: Ask Emily what exactly Mr Clarke said earlier about previous treatment.
options.D: Offer Mr Clarke a follow-up appointment if he does not wish to have treatment today.
options.E: Discuss the situation with Dr O’Connor if Mr Clarke continues to refuse recommended treatment.

explanation: The main issue is balancing patient wishes with appropriate clinical care. The most appropriate action is to explain clearly to Mr Clarke why antibiotics alone are unlikely to treat the underlying infection (A). Providing information supports informed decision-making and patient-centred care. Clarifying what Mr Clarke previously told Emily (C) may help understand his expectations and guide the discussion. Escalating to Dr O’Connor if Mr Clarke continues to refuse recommended treatment (E) is appropriate because complex disagreements about management may require senior input. Offering a follow-up appointment if he declines treatment today (D) respects patient autonomy while ensuring ongoing care is available. Prescribing antibiotics simply to satisfy the patient and speed up the clinic (B) is inappropriate because it prioritises efficiency over responsible prescribing and may not address the clinical problem. NHS guidance emphasises evidence-based prescribing, clear communication, and prioritising patient safety even when clinics are busy.
learningPoints: Antibiotics should not be prescribed when not clinically indicated.
Patients should receive clear explanations about treatment options.
Senior advice should be sought when disagreements about care persist.
Clinic pressures must not compromise appropriate prescribing.

scenario: You are assisting in a paediatric GA list in the oral surgery theatre. Consultant Dr Banerjee is supervising the list while registrar Dr Kumar is performing most procedures. A child named Tyler is scheduled for multiple extractions. Theatre nurse Olivia quietly tells you she cannot find the radiographs listed in the referral letter. Dr Kumar says the teeth appear clearly non-restorable and suggests proceeding without reviewing the images to avoid delaying the list. Tyler’s mother is anxious and asks how long the procedure will take because she needs to collect another child from school later.

options.A: Ask Olivia whether the radiographs might be stored in another part of the electronic system.
options.B: Tell Tyler’s mother that delays sometimes occur during theatre lists.
options.C: Explain to Dr Kumar that you are uncomfortable proceeding without confirming the imaging.
options.D: Proceed with the extractions because Dr Kumar is the registrar performing the procedure.
options.E: Contact radiology to ask whether the images can be accessed quickly.
options.F: Suggest briefly reviewing Tyler’s referral documentation again for imaging details.
options.G: Ignore the issue because the teeth appear clearly decayed.
options.H: Document that radiographs were unavailable and continue.

explanation: Radiographs are normally required before extractions in paediatric patients to confirm diagnosis and treatment planning. The most appropriate actions focus on locating the imaging and raising the concern professionally. Asking Olivia whether the images may exist elsewhere in the system (A) is a practical first step. Expressing your concern to Dr Kumar about proceeding without radiographic confirmation (C) supports patient safety while respecting the team hierarchy. Contacting radiology to obtain the images (E) may help resolve the issue quickly without delaying the list unnecessarily. Informing the parent about delays (B) may be appropriate later but does not address the clinical concern. Proceeding without images simply because the registrar suggested it (D) or ignoring the issue (G) would be unsafe. Documenting the absence of radiographs and continuing (H) still exposes the patient to potential risk. NHS professional practice requires clinicians to prioritise safe treatment planning and speak up if important clinical information is missing.
learningPoints: Appropriate investigations should be reviewed before treatment.
Team members should raise safety concerns respectfully.
Hierarchy should not prevent raising clinical concerns.
Patient safety must take priority over theatre efficiency.

scenario: You are working in a restorative dentistry clinic. Consultant Dr Chen has asked you to assess Mrs Thompson, who has a fractured crown on a heavily restored premolar. Dental nurse Lucy mentions that Mrs Thompson is particularly anxious because she previously had a painful dental procedure. During the examination you realise that the tooth may require complex endodontic retreatment, a procedure you have not performed independently before. Mrs Thompson asks whether the problem can be fixed today because she is travelling abroad next week.

options.A: Explain to Mrs Thompson that the treatment may require specialist input and discuss possible options.
options.B: Attempt the retreatment yourself so the patient can be treated today.
options.C: Ask Dr Chen if he could review the tooth and advise on management.
options.D: Ask Lucy whether Mrs Thompson expressed specific concerns about treatment earlier.
options.E: Offer to arrange an urgent review appointment if treatment cannot be completed today.

explanation: Recognising limits of competence is essential for safe practice. Explaining to Mrs Thompson that the procedure may require specialist input (A) ensures transparency and helps manage expectations. Asking Dr Chen to review the tooth (C) is appropriate because he is the supervising consultant and can advise on whether retreatment should be performed in this clinic or referred. Asking Lucy about Mrs Thompson’s concerns (D) may help address her anxiety and support patient-centred communication. Offering an urgent review appointment if treatment cannot be completed today (E) provides a practical solution. Attempting complex retreatment independently despite lacking experience (B) risks compromising patient safety and contradicts NHS professional guidance on working within competence. Patient expectations should never pressure clinicians into performing procedures beyond their ability.
learningPoints: Clinicians must recognise and work within their competence.
Senior supervision should be sought when treatment complexity exceeds experience.
Patient expectations should not override safe clinical practice.
Managing anxiety requires clear communication.

scenario: You are working in a GDP training placement. Practice manager Mr Lewis tells you that the schedule is running behind and asks whether you can shorten consultations slightly. Your next patient, Mrs Khan, attends with her teenage daughter Amira who is translating because Mrs Khan speaks limited English. During the consultation Amira answers most questions for her mother. Dental nurse Katie quietly says she is unsure whether Mrs Khan fully understands the treatment discussion. Mrs Khan nods when Amira translates that you recommend extraction of a painful molar today.

options.A: Ask whether a professional interpreter service is available before proceeding with treatment.
options.B: Ask Amira to step outside briefly so you can speak directly with Mrs Khan.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction because Mrs Khan nodded agreement.
options.D: Explain to Mr Lewis that additional time may be required to ensure proper communication.
options.E: Ask Katie whether she noticed signs that Mrs Khan did not understand the discussion.
options.F: Provide written information about the procedure in English only.
options.G: Explain to Mrs Khan that you want to ensure she fully understands before proceeding.
options.H: Tell Amira to translate exactly what you say without interruption.

explanation: The scenario raises concerns about valid consent due to language barriers. The most appropriate actions prioritise ensuring Mrs Khan understands the proposed treatment. Arranging a professional interpreter (A) is ideal when language barriers exist because family members may not translate accurately. Speaking clearly to Mrs Khan and confirming her understanding before treatment (G) supports informed consent. Explaining to the practice manager Mr Lewis that extra time may be needed (D) reinforces that patient safety and consent take priority over clinic efficiency. Asking Katie about her observations (E) may provide context but does not resolve the communication issue directly. Proceeding with extraction simply because Mrs Khan nodded (C) risks invalid consent. Written information only in English (F) may not help comprehension. Asking Amira to translate precisely (H) may still introduce bias or misunderstanding. NHS professional standards emphasise ensuring patients understand treatment regardless of time pressure.
learningPoints: Language barriers can affect valid consent.
Professional interpreters should be used when possible.
Family members translating may not ensure accurate communication.
Patient understanding must be confirmed before treatment.

scenario: You are a Dental Core Trainee covering the hospital emergency dental clinic during a busy afternoon. Consultant Dr Ahmed is supervising two surgeries while registrar Dr Grant is assisting with a facial trauma case nearby. Your next patient, Mr Petrov, presents with severe swelling around a lower molar. Dental nurse Chloe mentions that Mr Petrov appears increasingly uncomfortable and has been holding his jaw while waiting. During the examination you notice mild trismus and facial swelling extending toward the submandibular area. Mr Petrov says the pain started two days ago and has worsened rapidly overnight. Receptionist Nadia informs you that several patients are waiting and asks whether the clinic will run much longer.

options.A: Ask Chloe to record Mr Petrov’s vital signs including temperature and pulse.
options.B: Explain to Mr Petrov that the swelling may require urgent senior review.
options.C: Arrange immediate review by Dr Ahmed due to the spreading swelling.
options.D: Prescribe oral antibiotics and ask Mr Petrov to return if symptoms worsen.
options.E: Document the clinical findings and concerns about possible infection spread.

explanation: Mr Petrov’s symptoms suggest a potentially spreading odontogenic infection. Escalating promptly to the supervising consultant Dr Ahmed (C) is the most appropriate action because deep infections can progress rapidly and may require urgent management. Asking Chloe to record vital signs (A) helps assess systemic involvement such as fever or tachycardia. Explaining the situation to Mr Petrov (B) supports patient-centred communication and prepares him for possible escalation or hospital admission. Documenting findings carefully (E) ensures clear clinical records and continuity of care. Prescribing antibiotics alone without senior review (D) risks underestimating the severity of the infection and delaying appropriate management. NHS clinical safety standards emphasise recognising potentially serious infections early and involving senior clinicians when deterioration or spreading infection is suspected.
learningPoints: Rapidly spreading infections require urgent senior review.
Vital signs help assess severity of systemic infection.
Patient safety must override clinic efficiency pressures.
Clear documentation supports safe escalation.

scenario: You are assisting in a special care dentistry clinic. Dental hygienist Laura has just completed periodontal treatment for Mrs Singh, who has advanced Parkinson’s disease. Laura asks you to review a mobile lower incisor that may require extraction. Mrs Singh’s husband, Mr Singh, speaks on her behalf frequently during the consultation and insists that the tooth should be removed today. Dental nurse Olivia quietly tells you that Mrs Singh appeared uncertain earlier when Laura discussed treatment options.

options.A: Speak directly to Mrs Singh to confirm her understanding of the treatment options.
options.B: Ask Laura to explain again what she discussed with Mrs Singh earlier.
options.C: Proceed with the extraction because Mr Singh requested it.
options.D: Ask Olivia what concerns she noticed about Mrs Singh’s understanding.
options.E: Explain to Mr Singh that the decision must come from Mrs Singh if she is able to consent.
options.F: Arrange another appointment without discussing the decision further.
options.G: Document the discussion about consent and treatment preferences.
options.H: Ask Mr Singh to leave the room so Mrs Singh feels less pressured.

explanation: The central issue is ensuring Mrs Singh’s autonomy and valid consent. Speaking directly with Mrs Singh to confirm her understanding (A) is essential, as she is the patient receiving treatment. Explaining to Mr Singh that decisions must come from Mrs Singh if she has capacity (E) reinforces patient-centred care and professional boundaries. Clarifying with Laura what was previously discussed (B) helps ensure consistent communication and avoids confusion. Asking Olivia about her observations (D) may provide context but does not directly address the consent issue. Proceeding with extraction because the husband requested it (C) would undermine the patient’s autonomy. Arranging another appointment immediately (F) without discussion avoids the consent issue rather than addressing it. Documentation (G) is important but should follow clarification of the patient’s wishes. Asking Mr Singh to leave (H) might sometimes help reduce pressure but should be approached sensitively and not as the first step. NHS practice prioritises direct patient communication and respecting patient autonomy.
learningPoints: Patients should be addressed directly whenever possible.
Relatives cannot make decisions if the patient has capacity.
Clear communication with the clinical team prevents misunderstandings.
Consent must come from the patient unless capacity is lacking.

scenario: You are working in a restorative dentistry clinic supervised by consultant Dr O’Connor. While reviewing the notes for Mr Ibrahim’s crown preparation appointment, dental nurse Emily quietly tells you that the temporary crown fitted during the previous visit by another trainee, Dr Fernandes, came off twice shortly after placement. Emily says Dr Fernandes seemed frustrated during the appointment and mentioned that the patient ‘kept biting incorrectly’. Mr Ibrahim now appears irritated and tells you he lost confidence in the previous treatment because the temporary crown kept failing.

options.A: Ask Mr Ibrahim to explain what happened during the previous appointment.
options.B: Speak privately with Dr Fernandes to understand the circumstances of the previous treatment.
options.C: Tell Mr Ibrahim that temporary crowns sometimes fail and continue treatment.
options.D: Discuss the situation with Dr O’Connor if concerns remain about the previous treatment.
options.E: Document Mr Ibrahim’s concerns about the previous appointment.

explanation: Professional integrity requires addressing patient concerns while maintaining respectful communication with colleagues. Asking Mr Ibrahim to describe what happened previously (A) allows you to understand the patient’s experience and concerns. Speaking privately with Dr Fernandes (B) provides an opportunity to clarify the situation and avoids making assumptions about a colleague’s work. If uncertainty remains, discussing the matter with the supervising consultant Dr O’Connor (D) ensures appropriate oversight. Documenting the patient’s concerns (E) maintains an accurate clinical record. Simply dismissing the issue by stating that temporary crowns sometimes fail (C) risks undermining the patient’s trust and ignores the opportunity to clarify what occurred. NHS professionalism standards emphasise openness, respectful communication with colleagues, and maintaining patient trust when concerns arise.
learningPoints: Patient concerns about previous care should be explored.
Professional discussions with colleagues should remain respectful.
Senior supervision may be needed when concerns persist.
Accurate documentation supports transparency.

scenario: You are on call for the OMFS team during a busy evening shift. Registrar Dr Kumar is managing several trauma referrals in the emergency department. Ward nurse Rachel calls to say that Mr Khan, who had a dental extraction earlier today, is complaining of increasing bleeding from the socket. Dental nurse James is already preparing instruments in the treatment room. At the same time, reception staff inform you that a patient with severe toothache has just arrived asking for urgent review.

options.A: Assess Mr Khan immediately to evaluate the bleeding.
options.B: Ask James to prepare haemostatic materials while you review Mr Khan.
options.C: Ask reception to inform the new patient that there may be a delay.
options.D: Ignore the bleeding for now until Dr Kumar arrives.
options.E: Document Mr Khan’s symptoms and treatment plan.
options.F: Call Dr Kumar again to update him on the bleeding.
options.G: Ask Rachel whether Mr Khan has any medical conditions affecting bleeding.
options.H: Send Mr Khan home with advice without examining him.

explanation: Postoperative bleeding requires prompt assessment. The most appropriate action is to review Mr Khan immediately (A) to determine the severity of the bleeding. Preparing haemostatic materials with James (B) ensures you are ready to manage the situation efficiently. Asking Rachel about relevant medical history such as anticoagulant use (G) helps identify potential contributing factors. Informing reception about delays (C) may be appropriate but is secondary to managing a potential complication. Contacting Dr Kumar again (F) may be reasonable after initial assessment if senior support is needed. Ignoring the bleeding (D) or sending the patient home without examination (H) would be unsafe. Documentation (E) is important but should follow clinical assessment. NHS practice prioritises immediate evaluation of postoperative complications and effective teamwork during busy on-call shifts.
learningPoints: Postoperative bleeding should be assessed promptly.
Effective teamwork helps manage urgent situations.
Relevant medical history can influence bleeding risk.
Immediate patient safety takes priority during busy shifts.

scenario: You are a Dental Core Trainee working in a community dental service clinic. Dental therapist Daniela has just completed an examination for Mr Clarke, who has severe dental anxiety and mild autism. Daniela asks you to review a decayed molar that may require extraction. When you enter the surgery, Mr Clarke becomes visibly tense and asks whether the treatment will hurt. Dental nurse Sarah quietly tells you that Mr Clarke struggled during previous appointments and often leaves if he feels rushed. His support worker, Mark, says the transport arranged by the care home will leave in forty minutes and they cannot stay longer. The clinic schedule is already running late and several patients are waiting.

options.A: Explain the situation slowly to Mr Clarke and ask what specifically worries him about treatment.
options.B: Proceed with the extraction quickly before Mr Clarke becomes more anxious.
options.C: Ask Daniela about strategies that worked previously to help Mr Clarke cope during appointments.
options.D: Offer to arrange another appointment with additional behavioural support if needed.
options.E: Tell Mark that the clinic schedule means treatment must happen today.

explanation: Patient-centred care is particularly important for patients with anxiety and additional needs. The most appropriate action is to communicate calmly with Mr Clarke to understand his concerns (A). This supports trust and allows you to tailor your approach. Asking Daniela about strategies that previously helped Mr Clarke (C) uses the experience of colleagues and supports teamwork. Offering another appointment with additional behavioural support if needed (D) respects the patient’s needs and ensures treatment occurs in a safe environment. Informing Mark about scheduling constraints (E) may be necessary but should not pressure the patient into treatment. Proceeding quickly with extraction without addressing anxiety (B) risks causing distress and damaging trust. NHS standards emphasise adapting communication to patient needs and prioritising patient wellbeing over clinic efficiency.
learningPoints: Patients with additional needs may require adapted communication.
Understanding patient anxiety helps improve cooperation.
Team input can help identify effective management strategies.
Patient wellbeing should not be compromised by time pressure.

scenario: You are a Dental Core Trainee working in a GDP training practice. Dental nurse Emily mentions that a patient, Mrs Hassan, appeared unhappy when leaving the surgery after seeing another trainee, Dr Grant. Mrs Hassan approaches reception and says she felt rushed during the appointment and did not fully understand the treatment explanation. Receptionist Nadia asks if you can speak with her because Dr Grant is currently with another patient.

options.A: Listen to Mrs Hassan’s concerns and acknowledge her experience.
options.B: Explain that Dr Grant is very experienced and the treatment was likely correct.
options.C: Ask Mrs Hassan what specifically she found unclear during the consultation.
options.D: Tell reception to ask Mrs Hassan to return another day.
options.E: Offer to review the treatment plan together to ensure she understands.
options.F: Discuss the situation later with Dr Grant to clarify what happened.
options.G: Advise Mrs Hassan to submit a formal complaint immediately.
options.H: Document Mrs Hassan’s concerns in the clinical notes.

explanation: When a patient expresses dissatisfaction, it is important to listen carefully and address concerns professionally. Listening to Mrs Hassan’s concerns (A) demonstrates empathy and professionalism. Asking her what specifically was unclear (C) helps identify the issue and allows you to clarify information. Offering to review the treatment plan together (E) supports patient understanding and reassurance. While documenting concerns (H) and discussing the issue with Dr Grant (F) may be appropriate later, the immediate priority is addressing the patient’s concerns directly. Defending Dr Grant without understanding the issue (B) may appear dismissive. Asking the patient to return another day (D) avoids addressing the concern. Encouraging an immediate formal complaint (G) may escalate the situation unnecessarily. NHS professional standards emphasise respectful listening, open communication, and resolving concerns early whenever possible.
learningPoints: Patient concerns should be listened to respectfully.
Clarifying misunderstandings can prevent complaints.
Professional communication helps maintain patient trust.
Addressing concerns early can prevent escalation.

scenario: You are assisting in an oral medicine clinic supervised by consultant Dr Banerjee. A patient, Mrs Thompson, attends with persistent oral ulceration. During the consultation she becomes emotional and reveals privately that she has been under severe stress at home due to family problems. Dental nurse Lucy is present in the surgery. Mrs Thompson asks you not to record the personal information in her notes because she is worried other healthcare professionals might read it.

options.A: Explain to Mrs Thompson why relevant information may need to be recorded in her clinical notes.
options.B: Respect her request and avoid documenting the information.
options.C: Ask Mrs Thompson what aspects she is comfortable having documented.
options.D: Discuss the situation with Dr Banerjee for guidance.
options.E: Document the clinical findings but omit the contextual information she shared.

explanation: Clinical documentation must balance patient confidentiality with accurate record keeping. Explaining to Mrs Thompson why relevant contextual information may need to be documented (A) promotes transparency and helps her understand the purpose of medical records. Asking which aspects she feels comfortable recording (C) demonstrates respect for her concerns while exploring compromise. Seeking advice from Dr Banerjee (D) is appropriate if uncertainty remains about documentation. Recording clinical findings while minimising unnecessary personal details (E) may help maintain appropriate records while respecting her concerns. Completely omitting relevant contextual information (B) may compromise clinical understanding and continuity of care. NHS professional guidance emphasises maintaining accurate records while respecting patient confidentiality and explaining how information is used.
learningPoints: Clinical records should be accurate and relevant.
Patients should understand how their information is recorded.
Confidentiality concerns should be discussed sensitively.
Senior advice may be helpful in complex ethical situations.

scenario: You are assisting on an OMFS trauma clinic. Registrar Dr Lewis asks you to review Mr Khan, who attended after a fall resulting in facial bruising. Radiographer Elena has taken imaging but mentions that the images appear slightly unclear due to patient movement. Dental nurse James says Mr Khan still appears dizzy.

options.A: Ask Elena whether repeating the radiograph would improve image clarity.
options.B: Assess Mr Khan’s symptoms including dizziness before reviewing the images.
options.C: Proceed with diagnosis based on the existing images.
options.D: Inform Dr Lewis about the dizziness and imaging concerns.
options.E: Explain to Mr Khan why additional imaging may be required.
options.F: Ignore the dizziness as it is unrelated to dental trauma.
options.G: Document the findings from the examination.
options.H: Ask James whether Mr Khan reported losing consciousness earlier.

explanation: Patient safety requires careful assessment following trauma. Assessing Mr Khan’s symptoms, including dizziness (B), is important because it may indicate a head injury requiring further evaluation. Informing Dr Lewis (D) ensures the supervising registrar is aware of the situation and can guide management. Asking Elena about repeating the radiograph (A) may improve diagnostic accuracy. Explaining imaging to the patient (E) and documenting findings (G) are appropriate but secondary to initial assessment. Ignoring dizziness (F) or proceeding with unclear images (C) risks missing important clinical information. Asking about loss of consciousness (H) could also be relevant but would typically follow initial symptom assessment. NHS clinical practice emphasises careful evaluation of trauma symptoms and appropriate escalation to senior clinicians when concerns arise.
learningPoints: Symptoms such as dizziness after trauma require careful assessment.
Clear imaging is important for accurate diagnosis.
Senior clinicians should be informed when concerns arise.
Patient safety should guide investigation decisions.

scenario: You are a Dental Core Trainee assisting in an oral surgery outpatient clinic supervised by consultant Dr Ahmed. Dental nurse Katie informs you that Mr Ibrahim, who is scheduled for a surgical extraction today, appears increasingly anxious in the waiting room. When you bring him into the surgery, he says he feels nervous because a previous dental procedure was painful. Nurse Katie quietly adds that Mr Ibrahim asked several times whether the procedure will hurt. At the same time, receptionist Mark informs you that the clinic is running behind schedule because two earlier patients required longer procedures.

options.A: Ask Mr Ibrahim to explain what concerns him most about the procedure.
options.B: Reassure Mr Ibrahim that the procedure will be quick so the clinic can stay on schedule.
options.C: Explain the steps of the procedure and how discomfort will be managed.
options.D: Ask Katie whether Mr Ibrahim mentioned specific worries earlier.
options.E: Delay the consultation until Dr Ahmed becomes available.

explanation: Managing patient anxiety requires clear communication and teamwork. Asking Mr Ibrahim about his concerns (A) allows you to understand the source of his anxiety. Explaining the procedure and how discomfort will be managed (C) helps reassure him and supports informed consent. Asking Katie about earlier comments (D) may provide useful context about his worries. Delaying the consultation until Dr Ahmed is available (E) may be appropriate if the patient remains very anxious or if complex decisions are needed. Simply reassuring him quickly to keep the clinic on schedule (B) does not address the underlying concern and may undermine trust. NHS practice emphasises addressing patient anxiety through clear communication and involving team members appropriately.
learningPoints: Patient anxiety should be explored through open questions.
Clear explanations help reduce anxiety before procedures.
Team members can provide valuable insight into patient concerns.
Clinic pressures should not compromise patient communication.

scenario: You are working in a hospital emergency dental clinic. Mr Petrov arrives requesting antibiotics for a painful tooth. During the consultation he becomes frustrated and insists that antibiotics worked previously and that he does not want any dental treatment today. Dental nurse Chloe quietly mentions that the swelling appears larger than when Mr Petrov first arrived.

options.A: Explain to Mr Petrov why antibiotics alone may not resolve the infection.
options.B: Prescribe antibiotics so the consultation can end quickly.
options.C: Ask Chloe to describe the change she noticed in the swelling.
options.D: Discuss treatment options including drainage or extraction.
options.E: Tell Mr Petrov that antibiotics are the only option available.
options.F: Seek advice from Dr O’Connor if Mr Petrov refuses recommended treatment.
options.G: Ignore the swelling as long as the patient wants antibiotics.
options.H: Document the discussion about treatment options.

explanation: The situation involves balancing patient wishes with appropriate clinical care. Explaining why antibiotics alone may not resolve the infection (A) supports informed decision-making and responsible prescribing. Asking Chloe about the change in swelling (C) helps assess whether the infection may be worsening. Discussing appropriate treatment options such as drainage or extraction (D) ensures the patient understands the available care. Seeking senior advice (F) or documenting the conversation (H) may be appropriate later but are not the first priorities. Prescribing antibiotics simply to end the consultation (B) or ignoring the swelling (G) would be unsafe and unethical. NHS guidance emphasises evidence-based prescribing and prioritising patient safety even under time pressure.
learningPoints: Antibiotics should only be prescribed when clinically indicated.
Patient concerns should be addressed through explanation.
Clinical signs of infection progression should be assessed carefully.
Patient safety must take priority over clinic efficiency.

scenario: You are a Dental Core Trainee working in a restorative dentistry clinic. During a consultation with Mrs Singh for crown preparation, dental nurse Emily quietly mentions that the patient appeared confused during the consent discussion earlier with another trainee, Dr Grant. Mrs Singh now asks you several questions about the procedure and says she does not remember agreeing to treatment previously.

options.A: Explain the procedure again to Mrs Singh and check her understanding.
options.B: Ask Emily what specifically made her think Mrs Singh was confused earlier.
options.C: Proceed with treatment because consent was already recorded.
options.D: Speak with Dr Grant later to clarify the earlier discussion.
options.E: Document Mrs Singh’s questions and the explanation provided.

explanation: Ensuring valid consent requires confirming that the patient understands the proposed treatment. Explaining the procedure again and checking Mrs Singh’s understanding (A) is the most appropriate step. Asking Emily what she noticed earlier (B) may provide useful insight into potential misunderstandings. Discussing the situation later with Dr Grant (D) helps clarify what occurred previously. Documenting the explanation and the patient’s questions (E) ensures accurate records. Proceeding with treatment simply because consent was recorded earlier (C) risks invalid consent if the patient currently does not understand the procedure. NHS professional guidance emphasises that consent is an ongoing process requiring patient understanding at the time of treatment.
learningPoints: Consent must involve patient understanding.
Team members may notice important communication issues.
Consent should be confirmed before treatment proceeds.
Accurate documentation supports transparency.

scenario: You are on call for the OMFS department overnight. Registrar Dr Kumar is assisting with a trauma case in theatre. Ward nurse Angela calls to say that Mr Clarke, who had a tooth extraction earlier today, is experiencing increasing pain and mild bleeding from the socket. At the same time, another patient has arrived in the emergency department with severe dental pain requesting urgent review.

options.A: Assess Mr Clarke first to evaluate the bleeding and pain.
options.B: Ask James to prepare haemostatic materials.
options.C: Tell the new patient that there may be a delay.
options.D: Ignore Mr Clarke until Dr Kumar returns.
options.E: Ask Angela whether Mr Clarke has any medical conditions affecting bleeding.
options.F: Send Mr Clarke home without reviewing him.
options.G: Document the clinical findings after assessment.
options.H: Call Dr Kumar again if the bleeding appears significant.

explanation: Postoperative bleeding requires prompt assessment. Reviewing Mr Clarke first (A) ensures the complication is evaluated quickly. Preparing haemostatic materials with James (B) allows prompt treatment if needed. Asking Angela about relevant medical history such as anticoagulant use (E) helps identify potential causes of bleeding. Informing other patients about delays (C) may be appropriate later but is secondary to clinical assessment. Ignoring the problem (D) or sending the patient home without examination (F) would be unsafe. Documentation (G) and contacting Dr Kumar (H) may follow initial evaluation. NHS practice prioritises patient safety and timely assessment of postoperative complications.
learningPoints: Postoperative complications should be assessed promptly.
Preparation of equipment helps manage urgent situations.
Medical history can influence bleeding risk.
Patient safety must take priority during busy on-call shifts.

scenario: You are a Dental Core Trainee working in a hospital oral surgery clinic supervised by consultant Dr Banerjee. Your patient, Mrs Khan, is scheduled for surgical removal of a retained root. Dental nurse Olivia quietly mentions that Mrs Khan told her during triage that she takes blood-thinning medication for a heart condition. However, the electronic medical record only lists medication for hypertension. Mrs Khan says she took all her usual medications this morning but is unsure of the exact name. The clinic is running behind schedule because an earlier surgical procedure took longer than expected. Receptionist Rachel has already informed waiting patients that delays are likely.

options.A: Ask Mrs Khan to describe the medication she takes and why it was prescribed.
options.B: Ask Olivia what exactly Mrs Khan told her about the medication earlier.
options.C: Proceed with the procedure because the record does not list anticoagulants.
options.D: Pause the procedure until the medication history is clarified.
options.E: Contact Dr Banerjee to confirm whether the procedure should proceed.

explanation: The key concern is potential anticoagulant use, which could significantly increase bleeding risk during oral surgery. Clarifying the medication directly with Mrs Khan (A) is the first step to establish accurate information. Asking Olivia what she heard earlier (B) may help identify the medication name or indication. Escalating to the supervising consultant Dr Banerjee (E) ensures senior input before proceeding. Delaying the procedure until the medication history is confirmed (D) protects patient safety. Proceeding based solely on incomplete records (C) risks serious complications. NHS clinical safety standards emphasise verifying medication history before invasive procedures, particularly when bleeding risk may be affected.
learningPoints: Medication history must be confirmed before surgical procedures.
Team members may identify important safety concerns.
Patient safety must take priority over clinic scheduling.
Senior supervision should be sought when uncertainty exists.
